The iPhone really needs a horizontal case like this one. It's a bit long and wearing it on your hip vertically makes it uncomfortable and unwieldy when you sit down. Whereas a vertical case eliminated this problem. In terms of looks, I feel horizontal and vertical both look equally as cool but a horizontal case is a lot more convenient, at least to me.This DLO case is nicely made and seems durable but unfortunetly, there is a flaw in the design: the exposed open parts can make your iPhone very vunerable to scratches. I noticed a fairly deep scratch on the back of my iPhone right where the middle opening on this case is. It could have happened from a zipper, metal button or most probably from a rivet on my jeans coming in contact with the back of the iPhone through the slot. I am switching to another case that covers more of the iPhone.Just a side note about rubber iPhone cases: I don't like the feel of the rubber cases at all-they feel weird in the hand. And the rubber cases tend to stretch out over time and this lets in dust and grit which can scratch the iPhone. Also Apple recommends you not charge the iPhone will in a rubber case because the heat will not be able to dissipate. This means you have to keep taking it out of the rubber case and putting it back in every time you charge. You can see that these types of cases are less than ideal.

3 people in my office all upgraded together. I came from a Galaxy Nexus on Sprint, my buddy came from an iphone 5 on Sprint and my other buddy came from an Evo 3D on Sprint and got the HTC One on T-Mobile. There's a concensus amongst the 3 of us that all phones were beta tests to get to this point.Build quality: Hands down best display and build quality around. The buttons are flush or almost flush and feel very solid and the connectors (headphones and charge port) all feel really solid as well.Boom Sound: I have to say, this is a feature I didn't know I needed until I listened to music for the first time on the speakers. I used to lug a boom box (a $40 Sony from Walmart. Don't get me wrong I know it's a phone and not a full sized stereo) out to my garage to play music while i hit the bag and now I can set my phone on the shelf and it sounds just as good if not better.Software: Sense 5.0 runs amazingly smooth and offers nice improvements over stock android or the old versions of sense. I'm big into rooting my android smartphones and this is the first one that I messed around with after buying and got everything set up and just didn't care to root. I started looking at the ROMs on the developer sites and they all hae certain things not working or are the same as stock sense 5 so I figured why bother? To load a ROM that has less working features than the stock phone? Seems dumb to me. Some may love or hate blink feed, I find it extremely convenient and the operation of it is nice and smooth just like the rest of the interface.Battery Life: Nothing short of Amazing. All the Android phones I've used or seen people using up to this point have crappy battery life. This thing runs for 2 days with medium levels of usage and will do a day and a half with heavy usage. I put my phones through their paces to so I don't doubt you'll be happy with the battery.

This review is for the Obliq Slim Meta iPhone 6 Case. I personally look for the ultra-slim cases mainly to protect my phone from scratches and to give me more grip (the iPhone on its own is slippery!). I have never dropped my phone (knock on wood), so having a slim case that doesn't do much in terms of drop protection, is fine by me. This case fits that bill exactly. It is an ultra-slim case that, although it does provide some drop protection, really isn't designed for major drops. The case is made to look like it is forged from metal, but it is plastic. It has slits in each of the four corners, allowing this to be a single piece case that has enough flex to get your phone in and out of it. The cutouts are all appropriate sizes, they line up with the phone, and don't cause any interference with my fingers, or accessories (such as charger and headphones). The front lip of the case sticks above the iPhone screen every so slightly, enough to protect your screen from scratches while face down on a hard surface. It also has a rubbery feel to it, so your phone won't slide around on a flat surface. Bottom line is that this case is ultra-slim, ultra-lightweight, and aesthetically pleasing. If you are looking for scratch protection, minimal drop protection, and a nice looking case, then look no further. I was provided a free sample for an unbiased and thorough review.

Yay for this case!!

I wasn't sure about these waterproof cases. When it arrived, it reminded me of those plastic pencil cases we used to put in Trapper Keepers growing up. The plastic on the top functions as a clamp but while sticking our Galaxy S6 in, one of the plastic pieces popped off. I put it back but it was a pain since the material is clear and lining up the prongs with the little wholes was tedious. Our Apple 5C fit better and the Galaxy was a little tight. Granted both are enclosed in a case so they're thicker than if they were just naked. Not to mention, there's definitely more resistance with the cases on as the silicone material rubs against the plastic on this Haissky case. In order to test this waterproof case's capability to keep water out, I put tissue in the bag and left it in the sink for several minutes. I could see air in the waterproof case for sure and I even gave it a little squeeze to see if I could get some air out. Some bubbles came out, but no water ever went into the bag. The one thing you should be careful of is opening the back right side up. If there was some contact with water, water will get stuck at the top of the bag and those droplets will fall down toward the phone. Sure, they're just droplets, but I'm not risking it. My recommendation would be to open this Haissky case laying on it's side. And of course, since there is a gap between the Haissky case and the phone, we had to tap several times for the phone to respond. Before assuming this case will do the job every time, just make sure (as Haissky recommends) to check for leakage every time. A lanyard with a detachable clip is included in this package so you'll have the option of taking it off to use your phone. And finally, do not use this case to make calls. Hearing is an issue on both ends and that's understandable as the main function of this case is to protect one's phone from water damage and it definitely does that. And for those who are still using zip top bags, it'll behoove you to change over to this case because it won't accidentally become opened with the double locks.  Haissky sent us this sample for free with our commitment to use it, write a review and share it on Amazon to help others better understand their product.

This is the perfect case for an office environment.The Nexus 5 is a nice thin phone and this is pretty much the only option I saw when shopping for a case that didn't add much thickness or bulk. This is a true bumper and only covers the edges of the phone, the entire back and camera lens is exposed.Pro:-The rubber lip around the screen edges protect that critical edge of the glass screen area from chipping or starting a crack. In my opinion is the most fragile part of a phone that is almost all screen like the Nexus 5 is.-Very light weight case, dramatically lighter than any TPU case.-When the phone is set face down the case will lift the screen slightly off the table when face down to avoid screen scratches.-Plastic sides won't catch in your pocket and make it hard to get out.Con:-When setting the phone down face up on a flat surface the camera lens edge WILL contact the desk or whatever you set it on. The phone is designed for this and the camera has a slight raised edge around the glass but if this bothers you this is not the case for you.-The buttons on the phone are covered by rubber which makes them hard to feel the "click" when depressed.

First off, it took me forever to finally figure out how to put this thing onto my watch.Second, once I did, the royal crown didn't turn at all! It was so tight that I had to force it to move.However, after about a week and a half of having the protector on my screen, it must have loosened up a bit because then I could turn the royal crown a little bit more.Third, it holds moisture inside of the watch.I ended up taking it off because when I work out it leaves the whole thing wet and sweaty (gross).Fourth, it has a weird looking texture in the case and it makes the screen look sort of pixilated.Fifth, the charger DOES WORK with this case on. It does not interfere at all which is something I was very worried about. But not need to be worried about it charging with the case because it will!Sixth, it does come with an EXTRA screen protector in the package that just covers the face of the watch (or at least my package had that included) so I will instead be using this so I have full function of my royal crown and it doesn't attract the moisture. This made the purchase worth it because then I had an alternative since I didn't like the case.Overall, I think that it was worth the investment, especially if you're one to bang your watch on doors and whatnot like I apparently am. However, there are some fallbacks to using this product but if you can get past those things, it works pretty well.

This white/grey case came with my LG G2.  I don't like it because its inner liner is too loose around the top and bottom edges of the front of the phone.  The outer shell is not as stiff as I'd like and the matte white finish picks up colors from whatever it rubs up against.  The inner layer does a wonderful job of covering the rear buttons while making them easier to feel, though.  I dislike this case enough that even though I already have it in hand I'll probably purchase a different "tough" case, such as an Otterbox Commuter.

This case was nice and sturdy when it arrived. The colors were bold and everything was as it should be in great condition.The case goes on easily enough, although in multiple steps, and fits snugly over the phone, while still allowing you to use the buttons and other functions. The bright color makes it simple to locate and identify, and the case offers a nice bit of added protection. The multiple layers help you feel more at ease when carrying around a rather fragile looking and feeling phone. This compares to more expensive brands at a lower cost and can take a drop or impact without issue or harm to the case or the phone.That said, the rubber piece that slides over the phone started to stretch after being removed multiple times. This made it fit less snugly and allowed for some movement. It also makes getting the other pieces on correctly a little more difficult. The rotating clip was a nice feature, but it moved when bumped or pressed, which left the phone turned and in the way too many times. This was a problem playing sports, when running and even driving the car. A twist the wrong direction left it poking into my side or leg and having to be fixed.After only a few weeks of use, this was retired and replaced by an Otterbox case that worked much better for my needs.

Yet another great case! We recently just changed from the Samsung back to Apple with the iPhone 7 and I needed cases for the entire family. After looking at more cases than I ever want to count on Amazon, I decided to go with Spigen again as I have done in the past and I was not disappointed. This is really a quality case. Very simple to put on and I have found it to be an exact fit for the iPhone 7. All the cutouts are where you would expect them to be and align perfectly with all the parts of the phone. I noticed that some cases have been covering the light sensor, not this one! Really nice corner protection in case of drop and very easy to grip. Not slippy at all in the hand. I also like the fact that it has a cutout in the back for the Apple logo. I also have screen protectors on all our iPhone 7's and found that this matches up perfectly with those giving us total protection for our iPhones. I feel very confident that if I dropped my phone in everyday use that this would provide the protection needed. It also has a raised face so if you place your iPhone glass down, the glass does not come in contact with the counter at all, once again providing great protection. We also found this to be priced right! This would great considering I had to purchase 4 of them for the family. If you are looking for everyday protection for your iPhone and you want something that compliments the looks of the iPhone and is not all bulky yet offers great protection for everyday use, this is a great case that our family would purchase again in the future. You can't go wrong with Spigen!

Upon writing this, I am working on an exchange. I will update this if the exchanged product works better.This will be a 2 part review, one for the case, one for the battery.The case get a 4\5. Awesome damage protection, no built in screen protector (Which to me is a good thing), and an open back so you only have to remove the rubber otter shell to pull the battery (Vs on the otterbox having to pull off the outer shell, inner shell, and back cover of the phone.) It also has a nice, solid belt clip.A negative about this case, and why it only gets 4 out of 5 is it is no longer waterproof, due to loosing the back cover. Also, the flap that "covers" the charging port does not close properly, and more or less gets in the way with the normal flap built into the phone.The battery I received is defective, and thus the reason I'm working on an exchange. I believe if the battery would last, I would give this a 5/5 despite the negatives about the case. I followed all instructions to charge the battery, leaving it charge for many hours after the phone says it charged, however I can still not get more then a days worth of light use-age, and its actually worse then the stock battery. When I receive an exchange, I will update this to reflect my new opinion.

The Riot series cases by Caseology are unique in that they offers dual layer protection for your device without sacrificing aesthetics or adding bulk. They consist of two layers:  1) a strong rubber inner layer which includes a slight lip around the screen of the phone 2)an outer layer which is constructed of polycarbonate and includes a bumper. Combined this makes this case durable while maintaining a slim design.Please note that this case is not pink. At least not a true pink. It is more of a light coral or peachy color. Also, the back is not leather but synthetic material of some kind. I don't have an issue with that as I believe it will hold up well. I tried to get a more accurate photo of the color but it is difficult. All in all I like how it looks although I would not order it if looking for something pink. The gold accents are classy. I think the two colors combined are a nice touch. This is a case that makes a unique fashion statement.All the ports are open in the right places. The soft touch buttons function well. I'm typically a fan of cut-out buttons but this company has been winning me over with their soft button design.In my personal opinion for the current offering of $15 you can't go wrong with this case. I was provided a sample of this item in exchange for an unbiased review.

I really thought this was cool looking. I've used a number of multiple device chargers (one I dropped and broke, another I lost, and another just broke...). So, I've tested out a number of them over the years and just wanted something new and different. I love the glow it has and it is small. It works well, but after almost a month of owning it, I have been so frustrated that it topples over and ends up in my bed (it's on my nightstand) or on the floor, and the cords get tangled up. I like sleeping alone...I don't like this little guy joining me! And, if I need to access it at night and it's not in bed and on the floor instead, I don't like rolling over and almost falling out of bed to pick it up! (although, the light makes it easy to find!). There are MANY multiple device chargers out there that work very well and are also small and portable I have taken everywhere (why I dropped one and lost another...I'm very portable myself...). I looked at some I have used in the past and liked, but this one was visually appealing, had great reviews, was unique, and for about the same price. So, I went with it. Thankfully I'm still within the 30-day return window because I'm sending it back and going back to one of the other ones I've bought before that I know worked really well.

It falls out very easily. It feels like it's literally dangling on a string. It might just be this particular model. I prefer the ones that have the cords to inset into your phone so it does not eat away your battery life. So far I can't get my LG G3 to charge faster than standard USB rates with this charger. 500mA charge rate is not fast enough and I am using a charging cable rated for 2A.Update: Patozon took it upon themselves to send me another unit to try in the event the original one was defective. It is appreciated that they would do this without any effort on my part. I have continued to use this charger with the same issue and had chalked it up a fairly cheap loss and was not going to pursue a replacement. I'm only seeing my LG G3 draw at max in the 500-600 mA range which is not enough to charge my device significantly with the screen off and streaming a locally saved audio book via bluetooth to an earpiece. Patozan really seems to have good customer service and has continued to check in with me this rating is strictly for the product not the seller. They asked me to try charging a different device but I ordered this for my LG G3. I have a different product being delivered this week I will try.Update 2/3/2015: I've been meaning to update my review, I am now going full 5 stars. After trying a couple different higher amp chargers I had started suspect something might be wrong with my battery or phone that nothing would charge it faster than around 500mAh. I am now getting a fast charge from all my higher amp car chargers and I am thinking it was due to either or both of these things: A recent system update from sprint/LG (I originally put off taking the update but finally flashed a rooted version of it). The other thing I changed was the charger I was using at home. I had a "trusty" Motorola wall charger (not usb) that I had been using. I realized it was only 510mAh and a very slow charger, fine for overnight when I didn't realize how slow it really was. My guess is either the phone update corrected an issue and/or changing to the stock LG charger "untrained" the phone/battery from slow charging. I have an hour+ commute and now the only time I charge my G3 is on the way to/from work. I am able to bluetooth stream an audio book as well as run traffic/navigation apps and the battery will now get enough power to still top off.

I've been looking for a red case with a clear back for my iPhone 6s & saw this one. It doesn't come with a screen protector or screen covering (which is fine with me); & it was under $15 (which I liked), so I decided to get it. When it arrived, I instantly feel in love the deep red (think burgundy, not bright red) metallic color; & when I put it on my phone, it looked even better.I love how the small strip of black (which is the rubber piece attached to the clear plastic backing that sits under the red 'bumper' piece) lines the front & back of the phone under the red bumper. The combination makes the my gold (metallic) iPhone pop against the red/black & is really sharp looking. My favorite thing about this case is that you can actually see the red metallic color on the front, back, & sides of the phone. Most other cases I looked at, only has the red on the back of the case while the front & sides are black. (Not exactly what I had in mind, if you going to call it a red case.)Functionality wise, it does everything as described. All open ports are easily accessible due to the larger cutouts, & (rubbery) buttons are really easy to push. The case is pretty slim & hardly adds any extra weight to the phone. Once you remove the clear plastic protectors inside & out, the back of the phone is crystal clear. As for the grip, it's decent but could be a little better. There were a few occasions when I had sweaty or hand creamed hands which caused the phone slipped out & luckily fell on carpeting, thank goodness. I guess if you want a metallic look with a clear backing; you'll have to give up on the strong tactile grip. (^_^) There are a couple of concerns I do have though...One is cutout for the camera area. While the front of the case is raised so your screen doesn't sit flat on a surface; the clear back is not raised at all & will sit flat. Although the camera cutout doesn't cover the flash or lens area, the camera lens is flush with the top (for lack of a better term) back of the case. This will cause the lens to also sit flat on a surface when you put the phone down. There are other cases out where the camera cutout is slightly indented so the camera lens will not touch any surface (Otterbox & Caseology cases are a great examples of this feature); which (to me) seems to be better protection for the camera.Second, it's a little bit of a struggle to put the case on & actually really hard to take the case off. If you look at the illustrations above, you can see that the red bumper part goes over the black rubber part & "snaps" into place. The red bumper piece is a little flexible which helps in putting it on. However, when you want to take it off, there is not a lot of give to it; & you would need some pretty strong fingers nails to lift the red bumper piece up. You can get the bumper off; but if you may break some nails in the process. Having fake nails may definitely be a problem & I would highly recommend  NOT using anything sharp and/or pointy to pop it off, for fear of damaging the case & phone.Despite these issues, I would still recommend this case. The case is well made, even with the camera lens thing, & the color is really pleasing to the eye.

I ordered this because Cnet mentioned it was 80% off, down from $50 to $9.99.  Add the fact that I get free 2 day shipping via Amazon Prime and it's a no brainer.First issue I had was the shipping.  It was supposed to be delivered 2 days later, and according to USPS it was delivered as scheduled, but it didn't arrive.  It turns out USPS delivered it the following day, and when they arrived they mentioned that "it was delivered on schedule, TO THE POST OFFICE.  That meant that instead of 2 day shipping it took 3.Installation was easy enough, simply clean your hands, clean your phone surface, carefully peel back off of adhesive side and place on screen and apply pressure in a + pattern (FYI, I've installed many protectors on many phones, including invisible shields and hard vinyl protectors with both wet and dry installs and never get even a single bubble on my screen).  Installed it looks beautiful on an iPhone 5S.  Colors pop and fingerprints are much less noticeable than with a conventional vinyl screen protector.That's where the love ends.  After using a couple of days, the left side of the screen got a hazy arc which perfectly fit the shape of my palm (I'm left-handed).  It seemed as if vapor or something else had gotten between the screen protector and the phone screen, and this would not go away.  During the evening it wasn't noticeable but with sunlight it was very evident.  There was no way to remove this without taking the screen protector off and by doing so, there's no way to clean the adhesive without making marks which would be even more seen.  So after 48 hours of use, ended up throwing it away.  No way to contact the seller to ask if the problem was a defective product or defective installation from my part.I would've overlooked the delivery issue if it would've lasted more than a couple of days, but unfortunately it didn't.  If you're lucky to get one which works then you should be happy.

This was a gift for the wife.I can directly compare to the Speck CandyShell Grip, Otterbox commuter and defender, and Tech21 Impact cases.Fit is perfect of course.  No issues with buttons, lenses, sensors, etc.The Ballistic Aspira corners (shoulders) bulge out just slightly in all dimensions.  This is a big advantage, because if you drop your phone on a corner, that impact is multiplied considerably, and you're much more likely to take damage.  The down-side is that the bulging may give this one a slightly "cartoonish" look, compared to the Speck Grip.Comparing to the Spek CandlyShell Grip (which is on my phone at the moment) The Ballistic has somewhat beefier silicon all the way around.  Slightly thicker, with a higher lip around the screen.  It seems (without testing) to be more protective when I feel / examine them side-by-side (my wife and I have the same phone.)The Ballistic does NOT have a hard outer shell as some may think.  They use a thick but flexible glossy vinyl paint.  It is attractive and I suppose adds a little strength, but it's not the hard plastic of other models.  However, it does allow you to slip the phone into a pocket (and back out) more easily than if it did NOT have those painted areas, because the bare silicon is grippy indeed.Over-all, it looks good, it's light, easy in and out of the pocket, and in my opinion, I find it to be more protective than the Speck, especially in face-down situations where it has a nice lip and puffed out shoulders.Compared to Otter Boxen:  The Defender (which I own) is nice, but too bulky for MY daily use (no good w/ my uniform).The Commuter (which I own) is very protective, but heavy, and it's hard plastic outer shell is way to slippery.The Tech21 Impact case is VERY classy looking, and beats them all in the looks department.  But it's just a hard plastic shell and in my book, that's no protection at all.Yep.  I like this Ballistic Apira case very much.  When I need another case, I will look at Ballistic first, and check out some of their other models as well.  They have beefier more "manly" looking designs for those guys who don't want to walk around with big orange flowers on the phone.  :)So, why did I knock off one star?  Price.

I'd never heard of iFLASH battery packs before and decided to try them out to see what kind of power they had.  I've been testing and playing with several of them with different mAh values.  Mind you, I take the more informal approach to testing and always figure that if a battery can power up my iPad, no doubt they'll work on smaller devices such as Kindles, Smartphones, iPhones, etc. One thing I immediately noticed when I've opened the boxes was the high-quality connectors and cables. Many of the higher mAh battery packs are including the "extras" now, something I appreciate. The 30-pin Apple connector is quite solid as is the accompanying cable.  Most of the battery packs come in the standard black or white, but this one is a nice baby blue and white, a nice change.FLASH Performance:Start: 27% (4 LEDs)15 minutes 31% (4 LEDs)30 minutes 35%45 minutes 40%60 minutes 43%75 minutes 46% (3 LEDs)90 minutes 52%115 minutes 56%  (2 LEDs)145 minutes 61%160 minutes 68%  (1 LED)175 minutes 73%190 minutes 77%205 minutes  82%  (flashing)220  minutes 87%The battery finally ran out of juice, but the performance was really impressive for a 5600mAh battery pack.  Mind you, I didn't sit on it watching it every second as it charged, but was quite surprised at how hard to work to try to get my iPad back up to snuff.  It finally gave up the ghost, but it really packed an unexpected wallop.  It's a small, sleek unit and could easily be carried in a purse, knapsack, or briefcase. The top of the battery pack has the four LED indicators. Beneath that on the front side is the small LED flashlight, and one output port.  I suspect that LED flashlight would be able to stay on for more than 200 hours continuously given the power of this battery (just a guess). It's small, but would be great in emergency situations. On the right-hand side panel is the micro 5-pin input port.  The power switch is conveniently on the top face.WHAT'S IN THE BOX:1. Power Bank 5600mAh2. PSP connector3. Nokia DC 2.0 connector4. Micro 5-pin connector5. Mini 5-pin connector6. iPhone/iPad connnector7. Dc3.5 70cm transfer cable8. Quick start guide9. Blue mesh travel pouch w/iFLASH logo on a red tagSPECS:~ Weight: 123.5g (.27 pound)~ Input: Dc5V/1A~ Output:  Dc5V/1A~ Output: Charging speed for mobiles: 1000mAh/hours~ Input charging time: 5 - 6 hours~ Recycling time: > 500 times~ Six month limited warrantyPOWER INDICATORS:~ One LED light on: <25%~ Two LED lights on: 25% - 50%~ Three LED lights on: 50% - 75%~ Four LED lights on: 75% - 100%Sample provided for review.

I bought this phone to replace my Droid X2. I loved that phone alot. But after about 18 months of use, that phone had finally died off. I will start off by saying when doing this review, you do have to take in consideration that this phone has been out over 2 years now, so it will have some disadvantages.That being said there are both really good and bad aspects of this phone.One of my favorite features is the speaker phone mode; while on a phone call if set the phone face down, it will automatically switch to speaker phone. This comes in handy while driving and i use this everyday.Another feature i enjoy is the menus option. There are four options to choose from when displaying apps. "All Apps, Frequent, Downloaded & Verizon" I keep my phone set on the "Frequent" setting. It will display your 16 most used apps.The call quality on this phone is actually decent to good. In comparsion to my driod x2 people can hear me alot better. And i tend to be able to hear better.The HTC Sense User Interface is great too. I love the little weather widget. Whatever the weather is the phone screen will actually display that. Meaning when its raining the phone shows rain and wiper blades wipe it away. Really cool Feature.While the phone has some good aspects, there are also some not so great factors.The battery life, like most smart phones, isn't that great. But in comparison to my droid x2, this battery life really blows. If you barely use your phone, you will not notice a problem. Moe than about 3 hours a day, and youll be running low. The charge time also takes atleast 4 hours for me.Another problem, i encounter is that phone will freeze up more and moore with time. Which isn't that uncommon, but like others have stated, the reboot time can be about five minutes and will use atleast 15% or more of your battery life.The actual phone itself, i have noticed, isn't very durable. The first few times it dropped  or fell, even just a little bit of distance then phone became deeply scratched and dented. Not exactly sure what its made from, but quality isn't  a strong feature. I bought a snap on case for this phone and its helped abit.For the starter to average user, this phone will do the job. Just don't expect wonders to be made while using it. Good sound quality, Great user interface and Verizon wireless coverage make this phone worth while._________________________________________________________________its now 6months later. my phone is now dead. doesnt ring, wifi doesnt work, dead pixels and digitizer wiped. there are Better phones for the money.

This is by far the best hands free cell phone mount out there, in my opinion. I've bought a few different more expensive mounts, but the durability was just not there, after a few months the head of the mount would get loose or the gripping mechanism would be damaged so the mount would not grip the cell phone.At first I was a bit skeptical about this item because of the clamp design, but now that I've actually used it, it's perfect. Ironically because of its simple design, I don't see any way that this mounts clamp would brake; I mean unless of course there's an accidental damage done by myself or a passenger in my car, but other then that I feel like this item will last for ever, and for such a great price  (7.99) you cant go wrong.I'm just briefly going to mention a few other things I found were very good. The flexible arm is actually stiff enough that the phone stays right where I adjust it in all driving conditions, and the suction is made of this gel like material that has amazing grip.So yes I def recommend this item.NOTE: Also wanted to note that unlike many drivers I don't mount my cellphone on my windshield, I actually place the mount suction face down, in the middle area right by my shifting stick. It's a tiny area where I can just get enuff suction from the mount that it will stay in place. So if I can place it there, then on the glass would be a peace of cake.LAST NOTE:Long review I know, but I want to make sure this helps anyone trying to by this item. At first I noticed that the clamp would press down on the volume or power buttons but after a little adjustment of the phone placement that was fixed. The good thing is that the clamps are so strong that even if you have to hold the phone with just two of the fingers of the clamps grabbing the phone it would still be good enuff to hold the phone without dropping it. My OEM Samsung Note 4 battery was barely making it the 2/3rds of a work day after 3 years of faithful service. So I jumped on amazon to see what options I had. I previously had purchased Noot and Anker replacement batteries to replace my older Note 2 and 3 but Noot Note 4 batteries didn't come up in the search results and when I looked for Anker Note 4 they had 1 battery $15 bucks and when searching a little more I came across the ZeroLemon Note 4 battery 2 pack. The batteries included NFC and the 2 pack had a discount (maybe it was bc of Blackfriday?) making it $21 for 2 batteries, so I had to read some reviews on them. After reading some reviews - which is very annoying when amazon bundles multiple product reviews on the same listing page, in this case it seems a lot of the reviews are for an extended battery case by ZeroLemon - I decided that it was worth a risk to try a new battery manufacture and I figured with amazon customer service being very good to me in the past if I had any problems with these batteries right off the bat they would take them back and refund me no problem, so the risk was minimized.I haven't had them but a few days now but here are my findings. They both had 50% charge when arriving so Im hoping that means they were not stored in amazon warehouses for years. I put one in and used it till it died (lasted about expected for 50% charge) then turned it on again and it shut down and then I charged it to 100%. (just FYI, this is how you re-calibrate your battery) Then I did the same with the 2nd battery, used it till it died, turned it back on, it shut down and then I charged to 100%. I will use one battery one day and the other the next day to fully run them over a few weeks to ensure both are working as expected, holding charges and not swelling up etc.Over the few days Ive had them so far I will say I am satisfied. They batteries are working as expected, holding a charge well and not getting hot in use or when charging. Cant ask for much more, even from an OEM battery. I had a hard time finding warranty info on amazon.com as well as the ZeroLemon site. It seems they offer 30 day money back guarantee if you order from their site but they do not say about an amazon purchase. They included some info in the shipping package but its still not clear how long the warranty is. They offer a QR Code/Link included in the materials that you can register your amazon order at (order number, email and such info needed) and they say they will email me back if my "extended 12 month" warranty was accepted. I assume they check the amazon order number to verify the purchase and then it passes. But its still unclear if its just a 12 month or if its 6 months and when registered it adds on 12 more months. Who knows, but this is kinda important info as you can read a lot of horror stories on replacement batteries going bad, swelling up and getting hot etc a few months into use. I had to take a star off and probably should take 2 as its impt info to know and you want to be clear about that kind of info, however even if its just a 12 month warranty that is fine and about what you would expect so I'm only going to take 1 star off because for the price and the initial testing I think 4 stars is very fair and if they are still going strong after 6 months I would even update it to 5 stars.If I have any issues with these batteries I will edit/update my review.- 2 Months later update: Still working as expected. Will try to update at month 6 and month 12.- 8 Months later update: Ive been switching the zerolemon 2 pack every month, so months 1, 3, 5, 7 I used battery A and months 2, 4, 6, 8 I use the other battery "B" and Ive so both batteries wear evenly. Ive noticed a slight to moderate drop off in capacity, more so than expected so I took off a star from 4 down to 3. Moderate to heavy use the battery is only lasting a little more than half the day, so say 8am to 3pm and then I start to worry as the battery will be pretty low so I then need to charge it or switch it out with the other battery in the 2 pack, which is semi inconvenient and not up to par for the price paid. With light to moderate use and display brightness down to well below half I can make it till like 9pm. Overall there aren't many choices for these note 4 batteries and these are ok, but just OK IMO I would expect them to hold their capacity for at least a year if not longer and these didn't last but about 3-4 months of use (per battery as I stated above) and then their capacity started to dwindle. I now have to carry the extra battery with me most of the time. I do think these batteries are about as good as you will get though for the 4 year old note 4, I think I will be updating in the next year though due to not being able to go the full day with moderate to heavy use, sad but true.

I tried to upgrade to this phone from my Samsung Galaxy S5, the appeal of the larger screen and low price was very attractive. I do love the screen size, it felt good in my hands and the screen size was very nice. The Snapdragon 808 was pretty quick, didn't experience too many hiccups and being able to unlock the bootloader opens up many more options for people who like to dabble in in Android development. The bamboo backing looked really nice and classy though its not changeable and being able to add more memory via the microSD is very nice. The QuadHD screen is beautiful, looks really clear and the colors were very nice. It does come with a nice plastic bumper that protects the sides, but not the back.Having a vanilla Android is extremely nice, no more bloatware makes it that much nicer to use.Somethings that I didn't like was the fact that it got really got when using the Turbo Charger, the phone was extremely hot to touch, never had a phone get so hot before. I was a bit concerned, but it seems its normal operation. 24 Watt charger will charge the phone pretty quickly, but I guess the tradeoff is heat. The major concern was battery life, noted in many other posts, 3000 mAh is not a good size for a phone this big with such high resolution screen. I could barely last a whole day without charging, and that is fairly minimal usage, just some web surfing and maybe 20 minutes of YouTube. Another personal gripe would be the build in Motorola Camera App, unlike the Samsung, when you touch the camera screen, it takes the picture. This will take some getting used to by some people who are used to using their finger to focus first before taking the picture. Thus there is no dedicated button for taking a picture and I could not find anyway to change it. Also the lack of Qi Wireless charging is a bit disappointing, since the back is not removable, there is no way to cleanly add a 3rd party charger to it either.Pros-Price can't be beat-Beautiful large screen-Fast Charging-No BloatwareCons-Gets very hot while charging-Battery life is very anemic-No Qi Wireless ChargingI wound up returning the phone due to the heat, I did not feel comfortable with the phone getting so hot, and I did not see the advantages of the phone over my S5 and the lack of wireless charging was a bit off putting since I have several charging bases. If I had something older, I would definitely keep this phone as the price allows for more often upgrades. I have had many smartphones in my life starting with Siemens ones (OEM HTC), and have dabbled in rom customization starting with the original Galaxy and do feel that this phone is a really good bargain aside from the battery life.

When I received this product and opened the package I read the extensive instructions on how to mount the case to the bike's handlebars or crosspiece using the included wire ties, looked with interest at the instructions to link up to the Runtastic website to set up the app on your phone to use your phone as a bike computer and GPS station, and then came to a screeching halt. I could not figure out how to OPEN the case either, and there were no instructions included for this critical step in merging iPhone with bicycle. I put the thing back in the box in frustration and visited the website to see if instructions exist there for opening the case and inserting the phone. They did not. I carried the whole shebang into work today and, after passing the unit around the engineering department with no success,was ready to give up when our customer service person played with it and found out how the fake hinge slides back to open the case. With that issue resolved, I returned to the website to download the app and activate the system, but again was thwarted. I could not find anyplace to input the "activation code" and was bombarded with ways to purchase a more extensive app, a more extensive service, ad nauseum. I won't use this device. It is way too much trouble and a bike computer isn't all that expensive. I don't need the GPS -- I usually know where I am going, and I don't plan to invest in any upgrades. Further, I would have to dismantle my phone from its safe otter box to insert it in this case. I think I will just put my cell phone in the basket on my bike and use the computer already on it to keep track of my mileage, cadence and speed. Maybe this case and installation will be good for someone else in my biking circle. We'll see.Runtastic Bike Case for iPhone 4, 4S, 5 (Black)

First, this aux wideangle lens adapter is HUGE. It has a 58mm lens cap on the front and weighs more than the phone.It does work, although it has pronounced barrel distortion, making the image look like a GoPro image with straight vertical lines at the edges curving markedly.By unscrewing and removing the huge the front element, it leaves a smaller lens that acts like a macro allowing you to focus the camera within about an inch of the front element.Arrives in a nice telescoping box with excellent padding. For field transport it features a fabric carry pouch and a micro-fiber cleaning cloth.  Also has front and rear lens caps to protect the optics when not in use, although when mounted to the clip the rear lens cap cannot be attached and the rear element is exposed. The rear cap works only after you unscrew the wide angle/macro units from the clip.It attaches to the phone via a large plastic spring-loaded clip that should accommodate most phone cases. You "collimate" the lens manually after installation by looking down the lens and sliding the clip around on the phone until you roughly center the adapter over the phone's lens.It does work and delivers a wide angle picture. But the "panorama" mode on the iPhone is even wider and doesn't require a pocket-full of adapter.Fortunately, it was on sale and did not set me back much. And the macro lens might come in handy for serious closeups.

I think this phone is Verizon's best phone thus far.  It is a great phone, and is a nice combination upgrade of the top three phones from Verizon (LG Dare, LG enV3, LG Voyager).  The Blackberry Curve is VZW's top device, but I put that more as a PDA phone, rather than a regular phone with extra features (which is what the LG phones I listed above).  I've had many phones from Verizon for about 10 years now;  I have three lines in my account, and I upgrade my phone every year and the two other lines every other year.  IMHO this is the best phone to come out of Verizon.  The keyboard options are nice, though I find myself using the virtual keyboard more (I am used to the LG Dare).  I like the animated scroll (though not as nice as the HTC Touch Diamond or Pro, but again, these two are more data device/PDA phones, rather than regular phones).The screens for the LG enV Touch are quite nice, and definitely look a lot nicer than the LG Dare (and I like my Dare).  On paper, the LG enV Touch screens are better.  Both the internal and external displays of the LG enV Touch have 480x800 pixels, and 16 Million color levels (-vs- 240x400 pixels and 260K color levels on the Dare).  So it makes sense that the Touch screens look better.  (The Samsung Omnia screen is also 240x400 pixels and 65K color levels, so also inferior to the enV Touch).  If you have a chance, take a look at them side-by-side at your local store... you will see what I mean, and the difference will be quite obvious.The camera is quite impressive (3.2 MP) and the large screen size makes taking pictures a lot easier.  Daytime shots come out decent.  The night shots are not the greatest, but even regular cameras have problems with dark scenes.  I've tried the flash feature, and it helps a tiny bit (but I would never use it again. LOL!)I tested the data speed (via dslreports) and the reported speed was 812 kbit/sec, which is slower than my LG Dare (I got 1185 kbit/sec). I placed the phones on the same spot on my desk when I was running the tests, and ran one test per phone (one after the other) for 5 tests, and these are the average speeds of the 5 tests. I'm not sure why the Touch is slightly slower, given that both are EVDO Rev. A. I will next test the mobile broadband speed of both (results pending).  One huge benefit is that the "unlimited" data usage for this phone is only $15/month instead of the $30/month like the other data phones (blackberry, omnia, htc, etc.)The only negative feature I found was the disappointing battery life. I charge it overnight, take it off the charger at 7am, go to work, and by 2pm it has half the battery. This was consistent over the last 5 days I've had the phone.  If I leave the phone alone for an hour or so, when I get back to it the battery is up to 3/4 charged (I did not charge it).  I've even performed a hard reset (took the battery off while the phone was still on, waited over 30 sec, replaced the battery, turned the unit on, and charged overnight). No change in the battery life. I remember this was an issue with the LG Dare when it first came out a year ago, but the issue with the LG enV Touch is worse. I hope LG comes out with a fix for this (like they did with the Dare, and the battery life became significantly better).Altogether, I have been very happy and impressed with this phone. Having tested this unit thoroughly and extensively, I highly recommend this phone.  I am still running some more tests (bluetooth, mobile broadband & tethering, mobile web browsing, apps like VZ Navigator, etc.), and I will update this review in about a week.Having said all this, the LG enV Touch is a GREAT iPhone alternative, but is far from being an iPhone killer.  For those who are not on ATT and/or those who want to move from ATT, I highly recommend this phone.Pros:-Ext & Int screens are clear and large-Camera takes great pictures (for a phone)-FAST Mobile web...like my LG Dare-EVDO Rev. A so mobile broadband with tethering is FAST-dual keyboard (virtual and real)-decent speakers-clear sound for phoneCons:-battery life not very impressive. This is a pity, I actually gave thisphone 6 stars (out of 5) and docked one star for the battery, thus ending with 5 stars still.

This is my first IPod Touch case for my first IPod Touch. I did quite a bit of research before I decided to purchase the Otterbox Defender case. I've had arthritis in my hands since my 30's so I know there's a good chance I might drop my IPod. I read several reviews that said the Otterbox Defender case is supposed to offer the best protection for an IPod Touch.Together, the IPod Touch w/case weighs a little over 5oz. I purchased the black/red case.The 3 layers of protection consist of: 1. the black, hi-impact polycarbonate case. This is in 2 pieces, fits over the IPod & snaps closed. The black case has a clear window in the back to show the Apple logo, openings for the cameras, speaker, mic, 30 pin dock & headphone jack.2. The screen protector which is built into the black case. I love the screen protector; it doesn't hinder the touch sensitivity of my IPod or the viewing (everything looks clear & vibrant). I haven't used it long enough to comment on scratches.3. The red, impact absorbing, silicone cover which fits tightly over the black case. The silicone covers everything but the Apple logo on the back, the cameras, speaker & mic and it also leaves a small border of the black poly-carbonate case in a few places. The silicone covered volume, on/off/sleep & home buttons are responsive & easy to use. There's flap-type, lift-away covers over the dock & jack. I close the smaller jack cover first & wiggle/press it into place, then I close the dock cover.The red silicone cover has grips on the sides which makes it easier to hold, doesn't feel sticky & it's not collecting lint/dust like some silicone will. I use the Philips Fidelio DS7550 Rechargeable Portable Docking Speaker for iPod/iPhone (Aluminum/Black) & was able to dock my IPod with the case on. The Phillips Portable Speaker's dock can be pulled away from the speaker a little. Depending on the speaker dock you use, you might need to purchase an adapter to dock your IPod with the case on.So far so good. I will update my review with any new findings.I also want to mention that I don't see any sort of guarantee on this case.UPDATE: I liked this case so much that my daughter decided to purchase a pink case for herself. For anyone interested, the pink color is a hot, neon pink. Very bright.

This product is everything it claims to be. I'm going to be honest and say that these screen protectors are hard to apply, but then again, so are the others. I've applied protectors to multiple devices (iPhone, Blackberry Playbook, Leappad Explorer 2, Nexus 7 etc.) and I always use up two screen protectors in a row just trying to get the perfect "fit". I'm not going to blame the screen protector because I misjudged where the corners were or because I missed a piece of dust when I was cleaning off the screen. These screens don't bubble if you applied it correctly and if it still has some, it's most likely a piece of debris that got caught in there. That's why you have 3 tries. Once you get it all nice on your screen you leave it on there practically forever. Smudges come out pretty fast, just use an eyeglass cloth or some material that's 100% cotton. I've been using this Screen Protector for close to two months and it's gone through many hands (including, but not limited, to toddlers and babies), pockets, and purses and still looks great. When the time comes to replace my Screen Protector I will be buying these again.

This review is for the Anker Astro E1 external battery.A quick review.  The charger is 5200mAh, shiny piano black in color, and has one charging port.  I own several New Trent external chargers and will make some comparisons for those of you who may have seen or own a New Trent charger.I have bought Anker phone batteries and chargers before and have always been very satisfied with the quality of their products.  This product comes in the Anker style box with a white sleeve over a plain box.  Simple but effective, I like it.  Inside are instructions, the battery, and a cable about 2 feet long.  The battery is glossy black.  Its about the size of a stick of butter.  There is a button on the side of the battery.  There are 4 blue LED lights on the front.  When the button is pushed the lights indicate the charge on the battery.  The charging port is an IQ port which Anker says will adjust the charging output to match the input capability of the device its connected to.I tested this product on my Galaxy Note 3 while using the app Galaxy Charging Current Lite.  The maximum output for this battery is 5V, at 2A.  This will meet the maximum charging capability of almost every phone, tablet, or other small mobile device.  My first test was done with taking the cable from an old wall charger and plugging it into the Anker battery pack.  This resulted in a charge rate of 950ma, much slower than the normal 1200ma.  I then connected the cable that came with the Anker battery.  This time the charge was 1200ma.  The same as using a many chargers.  Although the Samsung charger for my phone has 5.3 volts output, I believe 5 volts is fairly common.  So maybe with a few exceptions, the Anker battery pack is going to charge at the same rate as a wall charger.  As soon as the battery pack is connected to a device it automatically starts charging, there is no button to turn it on or to start the process.  I found no way to stop the charge other than unplugging the battery.During the charge the battery became slightly warm, but no more than would be expected of an electronic device being used.  The battery charges fine with the LED lights staying on to indicate the charge level.While comparing this Anker 5200mAh battery to the New Trent 12,000mAh battery,  The Anker has about 40% the capacity, but is also only about 40% the size of the New Trent.  The New Trent has 2 charging ports, but they are dedicated 1 and 2 amp ports.  In fairness, my New Trent battery pack is over a year old.  I say this for 2 reasons.  First, these two batteries are for 2 different purposes.  Second, the Anker is brand new, my New Trent is 1 or 2 years old.  Stone age when technology is concerned.I use my New Trent battery while traveling all day and will be unable to charge my phone while stuck on a plane for 8 hours.  This Anker battery would serve a better purpose of putting in your pocket, purse, backpack, or desk drawer and charging your device once with a little left over.  The huge difference is price.  I think I paid about $65 for my New Trent, while the Anker is $16 on Amazon.  If you own or have researched portable batteries, then you know different sizes are made because not everyone has the same needs.The Anker Astro E1 with 5200mAh should serve you well for general purposes.  Its not a battery you want if you need power to your device all day long while being mobile.  But I believe for the other 95% of the general public it will serve them well.  For $15, you really cant go wrong.  It has a good capacity, is good quality, and super customer service if anything goes wrong.  I attached some photo comparisons between the Anker on the right and the New Trent on the left.

NOTE: Several versions of this selfie-stick/monopod are grouped together under this listing; my review is for the 188 (basic) version.What's included in the package:* Monopod/Selfie-stick* Cell phone attachment clamp* GoPro attachment mount* Camera attachment mount (it has a screw which can accommodate a regular camera or the cell phone attachment which screws on)* Velvet drawstring bagThis particular version doesn't have Bluetooth or 3.5mm jack for snapping photos. Basically, it's just an extensible pole and you'll either need to use your own separate remote control or the camera's timer function to get the shot. For me, that wasn't a problem.First, the construction is amazing. I expected it to be thin flimsy pseudo-metal/plastic, but the pole is entirely metal and thick and *strong*. Even extended all the way, there is no bending, even with a nearly 2lb camera attached, and the clamps don't budge. It feels similar in construction and thickness to one of the legs of my full size pro tripod kit.Even though my primary use for this isn't as a selfie-stick (more on that in a sec), I wanted to test out how well it could work with my cameras. Without a case, it fit an iPhone 5, 6, and 6 Plus without a problem. However, when I put my iPhone's case on--which is admittedly thick--it didn't clamp as well and, frankly, didn't feel secure. So, if your phone doesn't have a case or if it's using a thin case, then it should be fine, but if not, you might run into some problems.But as I mentioned, I'm not using this as a selfie-stick, I'm using it as a monopod. And this is where this thing shines. On the bottom, there's a screw hole which allows me to screw on a standard table top tripod. On the top, there's a standard screw adapter which allows you to screw on a camera. Initially, I was worried using this with my almost 2 lb Samsung, but this selfie-stick held up no problem. There's also a knob at the top which you can loosen in order to reposition the camera 180 degrees. (You can also take the knob completely out if you wish to switch to the GoPro attachment.)Overall, this is a great option if you're looking to take tabletop photos or videos and need a much taller angle without taking up a ton of room. In the month I've been using it, I've not encountered any problems and it's holding up very well. But I'll update this review should anything change, but in the meantime, if I've overlooked something or if you have any questions about my experiences with it, please leave a comment and I'll do my best to answer.Disclosure: Sample provided for review.

Ive been using this battery since March 2015.  After the initial 12 hour charge out of the box I did 6 additional charges of 2% or less back to full.  I normally rate products on a scale of 1  7  based on the following:  The Product Quality, The Product Playability, and The Product Pricing.  My rating in each area and overall rating are as follows:The Product Quality: Some have complained about the buttons on the side being too sensitive.  They are a little, but this has only been an issue every now and then.  No adjustments were needed other than how I hold the device when increasing the volume.  I had to make sure I wasn't accidently hitting the power button on the other side with another finger.  I wasn't too fond of the lack of battery protection on the back of the device.  The rubber coating is all that protects the otherwise exposed huge battery.  There is a hard cased battery cover for use with the regular battery within the ZeroLemon case.  I have dropped the device a few times and the battery has not sustained any major damage.  Other than that the product is made quite well.  I give it a 5 out of 7.The Product Playability, how easy it is to use, and does it perform as expected has pros and cons.  Pros: The battery does seem to last for several days.  I didn't track onscreen time, just how I used the Note 4 on a regular basis.  With my usage I get anywhere from 3 to 4 days depending on how I use it on those days.  The 1st day I am usually down to 60 or 70% battery on average, the 2nd day down to 20 to 30%, the third day either 10 to 15%.  If it is 15% or above I will usually let it go another day which usually takes me down to 1%.  I love the usage and how long it lasts.  On extremely heavy consecutive days of usage I can get 2  2  days without a problem and still have at least 15 to 20% of battery left.  Cons: The battery doesnt calibrate quite accurately and I have done it several times.  When I get down to 1% that remaining 1% can literally last for a half day or more.  It may sound great but it is not a realistic expectation of battery life left.  When calibrating the battery for the first few times I watched 2 whole HD movies, each over 2 hours, on Youtube all whilst the battery stayed at 1%.  Impressive, yes, but also misleading.  In addition, the Note 4 doesnt allow use of the camera when the battery drops below 5%, so I don't have access to one of the major features of the Note 4 when the battery is low and that condition may last for a long time.  I have two regular Note 4 batteries I use as backups but when I use these (If I am out and about and my battery drops to an unreliable 1%) I have to recalibrate the battery all over again when I switch back to the ZeroLemon battery.  I give the playability a rating of 5 out of 7.The Product Pricing: I paid 59.99 for the backup battery, the case for the Note 4 and battery, and the belt clip.  Its a great price but I cannot seem to find any of these items to purchase individually.  My belt clip broke and I searched online, including the ZeroLemon website, to no avail.  Overall, the price is great so I give it a rating of 6 out of 7.Overall rating: 5 out of 7.

First, about T-Mobile's service: it usually sucks. I live in a metro area and get full strength reception on my Nokia Lumia 920 4G Windows Phone, Black (AT&T) (simply the best smartphone!), but can barely get 1 bar on this phone or other T-Mobile phones (Alcatel, T-Mo's own brand, etc.) -- and that's when I stand next to my windows! If I move to the bedroom, forget about it, there's no bar and I can't make or take calls. Calls drop frequently, too. This is why T-Mobile's network is always ranked dead last nationwide. The reason I chose T-Mobile for my family's prepaid needs (prepaid is great for keeping track of the kids) and reception generally seems okay outdoors, which is fine for my needs.Next, about the phone. It's small and cute and has good battery life. Calls, when I can make them, come through loud and clear. The screen is decent for its size. But the UI has a distinct Europe bend which means it's not all that friendly to American users. For example, I have small hands but those buttons are even too small for me. The tools menu is a mess and cannot be customized. Worst of them all, the calendar's functionality is simply crazy! Weeks start on Monday, so I always misread the calendar. The way appointments are kept is simply pure evil: get this... when you have multiple events on the same day, you cannot view them all on the screen! It only shows one at a time. For some reason you sometimes can suppress event notifications but other times cannot. If you can overlook these weird UI shortcomings, it's a solid phone for making calls. So I finally decided to migrate my kids' simple calendars to my Nokia 920, much to their consternation.In summary, if you just want a basic prepaid phone, this works, and it's really cute and pocketable and tossable, unlike smartphones. UPDATE:After having this POS for over a year now I have to down grade it to 1 star. This keyboard is the worst in the world... It sucks terribly!!! Very fustrating...I should switch to another KB I know... No more samsungs for me!!!S***!!! LOL!!!This is my updated review of the Samsung Epic (LOL!) 4G Touch smartphone. After having it for 3 months now, I can give a much better review of it and it's flaws.I've had to downgrade this phone to 3 stars and it's lucky it's getting that. I have 3 gripes with this so-called "Super Phone!"1) The Google Navigator GPS. This thing is a joke! I have to use it at work because I drive for a living. To behonest I would say it works 75% of the time. That other 25% it has a hard time locking on to a Satellite. This is a known issue with this phone that they have not corrected. Even with ICS 4.04 or whatever number it is. Or it gives me a wrong location for the address I punched in. That is Google's fault. Not too sure who should get the major blame for this Google Navigator, Google or Samsung. I have a friend who has a Motorola Razr and she has the same problem. Just not as reliable as my 5 year-old Garmin GPS. Very fustrating to say the least.2) This doesn't happen very often at all, but last week I was trying to surf the Internet with it and I was trying to scroll down the webpage and it kept going back to the top. Extremely fustrating.3) The virtual pain in the a** keyboard. I've never liked these to begin with. But I thought with this phone having a 4.5" screen, that it wouldn't be a problem, but it is. You pretty much have to have Mickey Mouse fingers to type accurately with these keyboards.My first smartphone was the HTC Incredible. Nice phone but it would go buggy while surfing the Net as well. In fact it was much worse. It would start typing letters by itself while I was composing emails and texts. Really weird.All I have to say is, is that they certainly have not perfected the smartphone yet. Most people are paying over $100 a month for these phones. Especially if they are with that ripoff carrier that carries the Incredibles. Fortunately I'm under my sister's family plan and I pay about one third the normal cost for unlimited everything.P.S. There is one significant plus. The phone tethers onto my laptop without kicking me off the Internet, unlike my Droid Incredible 3G used to do constantly. Tether.com is the way to go on that one. Just a one time $29.95 fee and your good. I can PC game online with it, watch Netflix movies with it, Youtubes, etc.I hope this review was helpful.UPDATE 07/01/12:I just wanted to add an update to the Sprint Samsung Galaxy SII.I've had it for one month now and I am very happy with it. The operating system with all the screen menues are so fluid and smooth. You can really feel the power behind the 1.2GHZ Dual-Core processor. On top of that it has an independent GPU (Graphics Processor) as well.Some folks have complained about the location of the power button when they are accessing the volume buttons, inadvertently shutting the phone off. I've experienced this only a few times at the beginning. But now that I'm used to the phone, that doesn't happen anymore.Also, at the beginning the Galaxy SII seemed kind of big for my tastes, but like other reviewers have mentioned, it grows on you. It now feels perfect to me. I kind of like it without the phone case on it, but I would hate to drop it and scuff it up. So the case stays on, which is low profile anyway.I've upgraded my four star rating to a five star rating because after one month of owning it, it has proved to be an excellent smartphone from Korea giant Samsung. I like their other products as well. LCD/LED PC monitors and flat panel TV's.The only downfall is Sprint's 4G Wimax coverage which could be better. I've noticed that their 4G coverage is excellent in commercial areas, but lacks considerably in residential areas. The perfect scenario would be the Samsung Galaxy SII with the Verizon network with Sprint's unbeatable Everything Plan. But unfortunately, we don't live in a perfect world. :-(06/13/12 Review:I won't go into a lengthly review of all the features that this phone has, because there are plenty of good ones to read.I've had the phone for two weeks and it works flawlessly. Some folks nit-pick about it only having 800X480 resolution, but believe me, I see no difference between it and phones that have 1280X720. With that Super Amoled Plus screen, everything is crystal clear and sharp with the fonts and the videos look like 1080P. Very impressive to look at. I also think I just found my new E-reader.When I was shopping around for a new smartphone, I told myself that I wasn't going to get a big slab one. I was looking at the Blackberry 9930 and even came pretty close to getting the iphone 4. But those phones with their tiny screens and non-adjustable fonts were just too small to deal with. I mean I could read them, buy I said to myself, why? (And oh by the way... Apple is coming out with a larger screen. 3.95" worth... lol. Wow... that's going to make a real big difference). On top of that, they are both 3G.Was even looking at the HTC Evo 4G. The only problem with that is that there is no Sprint 4G LTE right now. So your stuck with 3G again. Besides, 4.5" is my limit. It's been posted that it does take a while to get used to the size of this phone... but it is very thin and light. Small profile.WARNING: I know that T-Mobile is a good carrier... but stay away from their version of the Samsung Galaxy S2. It has a stronger processor at 1.5Ghz, but when I was in their store checking out the display model, it kept freezing on me. I also met a co-worker with the T-Mobile version and he said his does the same thing. So far, no problems with my Sprint version doing that. I also heard that smartphones do this because we don't turn off our phones at night. This is actually what the T-Mobile rep told my co-worker.So I went with the $49.99 Amazon Wireless special on my sister's family plan. I only pay $30 a month for unlimited everything. Used to pay $82 a month with my Droid Incredible with only 450 minutes with that rip-off carrier. Sprint's 4G Wimax coverage could be better. They say they are improving it, but with them coming out with their 4G LTE... who knows.

As soon as I saw the Amazon Fire, I began searching for an affordable cell phone that had internet access and a keyboard. When I saw this Samsung phone for $4.99 (plus $4.99 s/h) at ShopCellDeals, I could not believe what I was seeing! Not only was this exactly what I had dreamed of owning someday but TracFone had triple minutes forever! I could not pass this up and ordered it immediately! Then when I went to TracFone's website, they had a free Samsung but a different model so I stuck with the order. The phone came within two days. What a beauty! I called TracFone to activate the phone. No waiting for a technician, no loud music while on hold. I did not get put on hold the entire half hour it took to get the phone set up. The technician spoke well since I am hard of hearing. I had some difficulty locating the numbers he needed. I should have spent more time familiarizing myself with the phone first. The technician was very patient and told me that he would see that he would get me setup. When he completed my setup, he told me that I had 60 minutes plus 10 bonus minutes. I wanted to add more airtime and he assisted with "how to". I then added 60 minutes that were trippled to 180 minutes with five months to use the airtime before I would need to add more.And then... somewhere along the way, I lost 60 minutes! I dreaded having to phone TracFone expecting a lot of resistance and long waits on hold. That wasn't the case! I had an immediate representative response and she said that she would assist me. My figures and her figures coincided so she told me to "wait one moment" while she resolved the problem. The entire conversation took nearly thirty minutes. I was never put on hold. She then told me that she had restored my missing 60 minutes and sure enough, when I looked at the phones remaining airtime, it was there! She then assisted me with a technical problem.I'm very pleased with the phone. Pleased with ShopCellDeals prompt shipment. Very pleased with TracFone.What I have yet to figure how to do is to log in to my GMail account. Have I allowed enough time to get a connection? I did a Google search and it appears that I am not alone. One thing I have learned is that the miinutes of airtime do not allow for anything on the internet to happen quickly. I suppose I was expecting something along the lines of Amazon Fire smartphone!Even if I never learn how to connect to GMail, I can at least use "text messages". That's something I could not do on my existing cell phones unless I used the numerical keypad. I have no patience for such as that!I'll rest today and approach the internet dilemma tomorrow. If I can connect to GMail and Amazon, I'll be very, very happy! The main reason for a wireless internet connection is that if I were to lose electric power, I could still connect to the internet. I'll return later to let you know how and if I solve my internet connection problem. My phone does connect to the internet, it just won't let me sign in to GMail or navigate the web.Meanwhie, I love this beautiful cell phone (I like to "pretend" that I have a smartphone!). Is this an inexpensive item for me, a senior with limited income? Yes. I figure that the monthly cost will be less than $7.00. Since I have other cell phones, this Samsung for other than "texting" and (hopefully), the internet, I would use one of my less expensive cell phones.

These glasses were also not expensive enough for me to be bothered to return so I will look for someone who can use them and give them away to them. If I am not sure of something next time I will ask the seller before I make the purchase.UPDATE:I just tried these out with a phone with 720p resolution and the stereo ( jpeg files transferred from my computer downloaded from the internet to phone) I used were out of focus with hardly any 3D effect at all. I think because the cards were not a very high resolution to begin with I did some research since trying them and the 3D pro's say you must use stereo views with high to very high resolution (which most cameras produce today) to be sharp and have good to great depth of field.NOTE:I did purchase a different pair from another seller their name is- Starlight Pocket Virtual Reality VR Headset  3D Portable Goggles for up to 4" - 6" iPhone or Android Smartphones - Higher Quality Google Cardboard VR  Handheld for Use Over Eye Glasses) Which work much better because they have a way to adjust them (forward/reverse, up/down, and lenses adjust left/right) and they were clearer but again not crystal clear you need to make sure the stereo SBS jpegs are high to very high resolution preferably at these resolutions (1280720 being the lowest), 1366768, 1600900, 19201080, 25601440 and 38402160. Also try to use a high resolution phone like a Samsung Note,or a Samsung Galaxy 4, or better or an iPhone, or other phone with similar resolutions. Printed stereo cards should be at least 1200x1800 at 300 dpi or better. It is much better to have a higher resolution because if you need to trim the picture in software to the size you need you will still have great resolution. However if your jpeg is to small and you need to make it larger you have a problem you will lose much resolution and picture quality may be poor.

I got on Amazon with the intent of writing this review: Tracfone seems to ride on Verizon's towers, so it works better than any other no-contract phone in areas where Verizon is dominant. The phones are great, with all the basic features, and very sturdy.  I've dropped mine several times and it keeps on working well.  They're reasonably priced, and I note Amazon's prices even with added shipping are better than Wal-mart's or Tracfone's. Over the past 12 months I've spent a total of $360 for phone and cards. I saw on Tracfone's website an 'online only' offer to change my double minutes phone to a triple minutes for life phone for $30.  Great. But in fact it's cheaper to purchase a new phone with triple minutes on Amazon than it is to buy that card plus airtime.In the past year my computer has never allowed me to go online to purchase anything on the Tracfone website. I thought the problem was my laptop.  But my desktop blocked it also.  Windows won't let me email them either. I'm assuming the problem is over-vigilant anti-virus programs in my 2 computers.  But calling or texting customer service is just not wise.  There are language problems, and endless loops of annoying music.  I've been transferred through 3 levels of 'customer service', first when I tried to switch the phone from Verizon, then when I tried to buy air time.  Each time I eventually gave up and waited until I was near the store where I can buy aircards.  That's 25 miles away.Imagine my surprise to find Amazon has a decent selection of tracfones.  I can order on Amazon in a snap. These are basic phones with useful features.  You control the useage, and you always know how much time you have available. My work involves a lot of texting, and texts are cheap on tracfone. Try it. Order it on Amazon (where internet purchases are easy!), or pick it up at your local department store (where it will cost a little more--about the same as it costs on the tracfone website).  Just stay away from Tracfone customer service and you'll be fine.  I give the phones 4 stars.  They're fine, tough basic phones.  I give customer service 0 stars.  The composite score is 2 stars.

This large capacity charging unit is simple and easy to use with a convenient indicator light. It comes with about 50% battery life and I plugged it in overnight to complete charging (as per instructions). As some reviewers have mentioned, this package contains only the USB cable and the power bank itself--no wall unit adaptor so you'll have to filch one from your cell phone etc. Once charged, there are three outlet ports so you can conceivably do a simultaneous multi-device charge. In roughly 10 minutes this power bank took my Note 5 from 33% to 44% and I was able to use my phone and charge it simultaneously afterwards. One thing I haven't figured out is that the charge will stop sometimes when you are actively using the phone--and I don't know why. In order to restart it, I unplugged it, then plugged it back into a new USB outlet port. Another time I plugged it in, it didn't charge and I just pressed the indicator button to get it going...Other times i just charges as soon as I plug it in. In the long run I prefer the "smart" capability of this unit to auto turn off so my phone battery isn't ruined, but this is inconvenient if I'm expecting to use my phone and haven't noticed this discrepancy. The lack of adaptor and the cessation of charging lead me to deduct a star. On a minor note, I really like the available colorways for this product. Black + orange = Go Beavers! The white and orange is attractive as well. This device works well and holds a lot of charge. It's also reasonably priced, though a bit heavy. I received it at a discount in exchange for an honest review but had planned on purchasing one in this price point beforehand, so i would definitely recommend it.
