I wanted to upgrade my Motorola Bravo (purchased through Consumer Cellular) to an Atrix 4G, but no one would confirm that it was fully functional on thier network...with the ability to use their data network being at issue. Consumer Cellular doesn't require a data connection for smart phones, allowing the use of wif-fi instead; however they offer a small 3G data package at a low price...for those times you happen to not be near a free wi-fi.Because I already had one of their phones, I was able to add a "New APN" through the phone's menu (Settings, Wireless & networks, Mobile networks, Access point names, menu) by coping what was in the Bravo phone. Here is how one needs to fill those fields in:Name: Consumer CellularAPN: att.mvnoProxy: proxy.mvno.ccmobileweb.comPort: 80MMSC:[...] Amazon doesn't allow a web-link in their reviews, but that is required here. See below for some hints.MMS proxy: proxy.mvno.ccmobileweb.comMMS port: 80MCC: 310MNC: 410APN type: default,mms,supl,agps,fotaAPN protocol: IPv4For the fields not listed above, they are left "". The MMSC web-link above includes the words "mmsc.cingular.com", after the usual "http...",Getting voice connections only required removing the sim card from the Bravo and putting it in the Atrix.So far, I'm very happy with this phone, with the Atrix being an improvement over the Bravo, having a better camera with a flash, faster processor, more memory, an added rear-facing camera, slightly larger screen, and the Gingerbread 2.3 OS. After 3 weeks of using the phone, I have never had to reboot it, as I sometimes did with the Bravo's 2.2 OS.I've now purchased the AT&T Laptop Dock for Motorola ATRIX 4G - Retail Packaging, the Motorola Standard Dock for Motorola ATRIX 4G, and the Motorola HD Multimedia Dock for Motorola ATRIX 4G-Motorola Retail Packaging and found a use for each. The standard dock turns the phone into an excellent, inexpensive clock and radio for my desk. On trips, I'll always take with me the Laptop Dock. Although it doesn't have the full capability of a laptop, it is so close that at times I could not tell the difference; yet it is so small and light weight that I'll certainly enjoy the convenience of it...plus it boots up much faster than a laptop. I found that I could attach the Multimedia dock to my TVs and use their large screen as my monitor, but you need to purchase a HDMI to HDMI Type D Micro Cable (6 Feet / 1.8 M). The included bluetooth mouse and keyboard and also work with the lapdock, especially the mouse making a nice accessory for it. The USB ports on the two docks provide added capability to the Atrix 4G, including the ability to use any USB/wireless mouse. Both the lapdock and multimedia dock allow watching web-based video on a larger screen; however, there is a noticeable, but acceptable (if it is your only option) uneven motion in the video. However, both video, music and photographs stored in the phone play very well from the phone onto the TV, with the phone making a very nice control device.Now if I can only find a use for the Motorola Vehicle Dock for Motorola ATRIX 4G with Rapid Car Charger - Retail Packaging...Update: A reviewer on another site pointed out that "older SIM cards (maybe 3-4 years old)" may also need to be replaced with a newer SIM card for the data connection to work. After 3 months, my Atrix 4G continues to work great, with the Standard and Laptop Docks being my "must have" accessories.Update2: Be careful and not get the international version of this phone....I could not get it to work with Consumer Cellular. I purchased a 2nd phone for my wife from Unlocked CellPhoneKings (recently changed their name to Breed), and they shipped the the only version they said they had in stock...the international version. My first Atrix 4G also came from CellPhoneKings. The international versions comes with a Great Britain Plug, an adapter, and an Android 2.3.4 operating system (vs 2.3.6 for the US version). I could not get it to work with Consumer Cellular which doesn't support international use on their network.

PROS:Combines an iPod/iPhone (30-pin) charging dock with a USB charge port and AC outletWide, stable baseAC extension outlet and USB charge port work perfectlyCONS:iPhone not always reliably secured/charging in dockVery large footprintThe dock obscures most of the home button, making it inaccessible when it is in the dockNo LED charge indicatorThe RCA PCHSTAB1R 30-Pin Charging Dock is definitely a great concept item.  It combines a charging base for your iPhone4S/iPod 4th generation (or below) and also has a handy AC extension outlet and a USB charge port to boot.  In this world of expanding technology, a device like this is ideal for me: I can charge my work iPhone4, charge my personal flip phone, and plug in an extension set for my home telephone all on my night table and using only a single device.  This sure beats having three cords dangling by my bedside and the need for jury rigging the dual outlet so I can plug in three devices.  In concept it's good.  Sadly, the iPhone does not sit securely in the dock and therefore doesn't always charge reliably.The device is simply packaged with an insert that is supposed to ensure a good fit for an iPod or older iPhone.  The device is larger than you might think.  It's base is about 5 3/4 inches wide and 3 1/4 inches wide in the middle.  It tapers to about 4 inches in width as it rises up to 2 inches high.  This isn't a small and wimpy device.  It will take up a little desk or night table real estate. The unit also has a little heft to it, something that I think makes a lot of sense: it is sure on its four rubber feet and will not easily tip over or slip. On the back you have an AC extension outlet with a ground and a single USB port for charging.  If the RCA guys really wanted to impress, they might have put two USB ports on the back--there is surely enough room--but this is a minor issue.The iPhone or iPod is simply inserted in a well on the top of the dock.  Another minor issue: the home button is obscured by the upper lip of the dock.  Granted, this is not a dock/speaker type of device, but if the well were simply shallower, the button would be accessible.The major problem with this device is that the iPhone dose not 'click' securely into place, as it does when you connect its usual 30 pin connector.  I tried this with the iPhone in the minimalist, Incipio rubber case that I use and with the iPhone out of the case.  I tried using the insert and adjusting the backrest on the insert, still no luck. If I press the iPhone in place (with or without the rubber case) I get that satisfying beep and the screen flashes on.  Still, if I wobble the iPhone just a bit, the battery icon fluctuates between charging and not charging. You have to jimmy the iPhone a bit and make sure that the battery icon shows the lightning bolt to ensure that the thing is charging.  The first night I used this, I placed the phone in the dock and went to sleep.  In the morning, I realized the iPhone had slipped and didn't charge overnight.  I have tried the dock with and without the supplied insert and with and without the case on--it doesn't seem to make a difference.  The phone will definitely charge in this dock; however, the phone is still able to slip out of place easily if I bang the night table.  It would be nice if the device had an LED on the front to indicate whether the iPhone was charging or not.Most nights, I have been using the dock, but if I want to ensure a charge or will have the phone plugged in longer than one night, I simply use a 30 pin cord and plug the iPhone into the USB charge port on the back.  This works just fine, but it negates the need for having this kind of dock.Once again, this is a great concept, but the implementation is less than stellar.  The device works after a fashion, but because the iPhone can slip out if you are not extra careful, I am giving this only three stars.

UPDATE 02.24.13: In less than a month the touchpad has completely stopped working. The lamp was not moved, bumped, jostled, moistened, or otherwise abused/damaged. Unfortunately because this lamp died in less than a month, which is simply unacceptable, I had to downgrade my star rating. Since it stopped working while the light was still on it made me realize something else--there isn't an alternative way to turn this lamp on/off (either by a small switch on the bottom/back/tucked up near the light bulb) which is a huge design flaw IMO. My original review is below.ORIGINAL REVIEW BELOW:Out of the box the lamp was easy to set up (as any lamp should be)--just flip the shade the proper way, add a bulb, plug it in, and you're in business. The lamp itself was much lighter-weight than I expected (light enough to be easily tipped if bumped) and I wish it were made of sturdier stuff (the screw which locks the shade in place was actually chipped), so I wouldn't recommend this lamp for a highly trafficked area of a child's room because if someone squints at it a little too hard it might topple over. That said, it would be a good lamp for a nightstand.It's not powerful to brightly light an entire room, but with a Philips 60W Natural Light bulb it provides ample light by which to read or do my sudoku/crosswords (and keep your partner awake if s/he is light sensitive). It doesn't have a standard on/off switch, but a touchpad on the base. I've had no problems with lag--once I've touched the base the light is on/off within a millisecond or two--but I just find the location a bit inconvenient because I have an alarm clock which sits in front of it and I must reach behind it (blindly) to get to the lamp's base. So, it's important to note where the lamp will be placed and whether anything will be blocking its base.The charging dock is convenient. I'm able to charge my seven-year-old 5th generation iPod Video and my 4th generation Nano without problems (both of which some charging devices refuse to even acknowledge, considering they're in their twilight years as far as technology goes). And I can charge my iPhone 5 no problems using the USB port in the back, however, the USB charging port is a bit on the inconvenient side unless you intend to leave in the charging cable (which are often proprietary and device specific, so being able to swap devices is a pain unless you can find a universal one with various adapters). Not to mention that not all USB charging devices are supported--I could charge a number of bluetooth and wireless headsets, but my ASUS Android tablet was a no-go regardless of the USB setting (if you're using a Kindle or Nook, you should be fine, though...at least according to the included booklet). So, for me the rear USB charging port falls into the category of "feature creep" and probably would have been better swapped for a speaker instead.Overally this is a good nightstand lamp (not an ideal livingroom/den table lamp), but there is room for improvement--especially with such a high MSRP. Right now it's simply a glorified iDevice charger that offers a bit of light.I hope this review was helpful to you, but if I've missed something or you have any questions about my experience with the product leave a comment and I'll do my best to answer.

I have used popular (and spendy) BT headsets from Motorola, Plantronics, Jawbone, and other major manufacturers, and have to say this Bose headset is by far my favorite to date. While it does lack some bells and whistles (some of which can be very useful), it has better incoming and outgoing sound quality than any headset I can compare it to, and it also supports A2DP which I consider to be essential in a headset. I listen to audio books and music for hours a day and I don't want to have to switch between a wired and wireless headset depending on intended use.As others have mentioned, the max volume for this Bose is much louder than what I have found elsewhere, and the fit and design of the earpiece make this headset usable in environments where I just couldn't use another BT headset (e.g., mowing the lawn or driving in the car with the window down). Additionally, at any volume, the sound is crystal clear and never sounds forced or like the miniature speaker is about to blow.Also of note is the usability of this headset to make calls in noisy environments. I haven't tried to use it to make a call while mowing the lawn, but it does supremely well in windy conditions or in crowded and noisy environments. My primary reason for switching to this from the Jawbone ICON I used previously was the continual feedback I received that people couldn't understand me during a phone call. Having a headset that is only good for listening, but not for speaking defeats the purpose of a headset all together. Since getting this headset, I haven't had one single negative comment, and have had a couple of people ask if I either abandoned my old headset all together and just reverted to using my phone, or if I had a new headset (because my end of the call was so clear). I have even been confident enough to step outside in a stiff wind to take sensitive calls which wouldn't have even been an option before. That's good enough for me!

The charger looks and works fine, but the detection range was a problem for me.  I have an AT&T LG G4 with the proper Qi sticker.  Found that the only way this charger works is with a naked phone.  If I placed <1mm of paper in between the phone and the charger it will not charge.  Once the charger detects the phone then the distance can be increase dramatically, but it does not help me.  Charger works and charges as expected.  Tested another 3-coil stand with the same results, then used a Samsung Qi puck and the detection range is about 1/2 inch or more.  What is the difference?  This stand has 3 coils and consumes 2A.  The Samsung has 1 coil and consumes 2A.  My guess is that the 3 coils divide the power to the point that it affects detection.  Fine is you are using a naked phone, but I am returning this charger.

For those who have provided negative feedback on this case, everyone can have a different experience with smartphone cases and having various opinions allows the rest of us Amazon users to determine if this product is a good fit for our needs.  However, if your negative feedback is because you dropped your phone and the screen cracked, then you probably should just go with an Otterbox.  Don't blame a product with a bad review that doesn't claim to protect the screen - this is a wallet case!  There is no part of the description indicating it will protect your phone's screen.I have bought many KAVAJ cases at this point.  Some for my personal smartphones and some for friends and family.  The build quality on every case has been near perfect.  It arrives to you packaged very nicely and has that sweet smell of leather when you take it out of the tissue paper wrapping.  It definitely gives off a vibe of quality for a really affordable price point.The iPhone 6/6s fits snuggly in the case. It doesn't feel like it could fall out and requires a bit of effort to remove it if you wanted to.  It wraps around the corners which can perhaps save a scratch or dent on those corners, if you were to drop it.  The open cutouts over the buttons, ports, and switches, make it easy to use your phone as if the case wasn't even there.  I have had other cases where I need to dig into a hole or lift a flap to get to them.  Not fun if all you want to do is switch to vibrate mode.  KAVAJ was intelligent in providing this design if you desire your case to be about convenience and not protection.The sleeves for cards are initially very tight.  Getting two cards in takes a little work, but after a few days, the leather breaks in a bit and getting the cards in and out becomes fairly simple.  Make no mistake, 2 credit card sized items is about all you will be able to fit in there.  This is not a full-blown wallet replacement.  For me, my drivers license and credit card are the perfect combo for those times I want to carry minimal items with me.  I can see some people might want to carry more, but that is not what the case is designed to hold.I am currently using the black case on my Space Grey iPhone 6s.  I have had the cognac case in the past and it shows too much dirt for my liking as you wear it in.  Some people like the distressed/dirty leather look, but I do not.  The black case has not gone down that path at this point for me, so I am very satisfied with the lack of wear and tear I have seen.  My phone goes in both my front and back pockets all day and I am constantly taking it out and putting it back in.  For those who are seeing the edges wear or the seams break, I am not sure what you are doing differently than me with your phone, but I certainly have not seen anything like that.The KAVAJ logo is engraved into the back of the case and I can understand that some people don't like it.  For me, I am so happy with this case, I don't mind advertising.All in all, this is the best case I have ever owned for any smartphone and probably my favorite purchase ever on Amazon.  In my OCD-like search for the perfect case for me, I have tried many different wallet cases and leather cases.  I have even spent over $100 on a custom Vaja case before, and I prefer this one over it far and away.  This case remains #1 on my list, and it's not even close!

I've had a lot of different experiences with a lot of different phones. Coming from the days of the blackberry curve line, the last experience with blackberry being the curve 3G series, I took a leap years ago to the iPhone 4. I've always been a very basic, budget minded and simple smartphone user; and the iPhone met all of my needs simply. Then, I started to see the growth of the smartphone market around me; and after owning an iPhone 4 for a solid two years with no complaints, I took the leap to android finally with the galaxy s3. The galaxy s3 was an amazing phone, and to this day I love android more than any operating system, but I found myself missing the simplicity of IOS. Within the past year I've gone back and forth between platforms, switching on and off between my iPhone 4 and my samsung galaxy s3. I had at some point upgraded to an iPhone 5C, but was severely disappointed with the lack of change or innovation from apple; so I gave the phone to my father and went back to my iPhone 4. My iPhone 4 fell to its demise in the summer, and my samsung s3 is still 100% functional but has cracks across the screen. I like to try new operating systems and experience new innovation; and I was kind of tired of having an expensive $600 smartphone when I didn't really need all of the gimmicks and extras at the price. I was at walmart recently and noticed the lumia 520 on sale for $50, so I went for it in order to finally try the windows experience.Having owned the Lumia 520 for two months, it was an incredible and solid phone. It really was one of the best phones I had ever owned, and I loved it so much more than any iPhone I had owned, but I still love the android ecosystem more than any other platform. A few weeks ago I saw that at&t was giving a trade in at $200 for an iPhone, so I took my demised iPhone for trade in and walah, $200! So, I decided to not spend the entire gift card on anything flashy or fancy, but instead I put $100 of the gift card towards this gem of a phone.This phone is a TRUE and amazing upgrade to the 635. I see a lot of mixed reviews on how people don't know if it is an upgrade or not in comparison to the 520, but it really is. The 520 is an amazing phone, but if you can spare the money you should get this one instead. Yes, there is no dedicated camera key, there is no ambient light sensor; but those are just really the two main knock offs from the 520. The build quality is amazing compared to the 520, it looks like an iPhone 5C but looks and feels more solid. The battery life on this phone is also spectacular, with average-heavy usage I get at least two days out of it. Sometimes if I barely use the phone also, just a few calls, texts, emails per day - I can get almost 3-4 days of usage. The battery is larger than the 520, which is why the battery life is a lot better than the 520. The screen resolution may be less than the 520; and other people have reported how the screen is almost the same size, well it basically is. The screen real estate is larger on the 635 because of the on-screen buttons now compared to the physical buttons on the 520, but the screen is actually a tad bit bigger; and to be honest the ClearBlack technology makes it a lot sharper and brighter than the 520. I could keep going into an in depth review, but basically this is a very amazing, nifty, and speedy device. I love the windows software, even though it has some limitations, it has no quirks, glitches, and is even more fluid in my opinion than the IOS software.The software on this phone is spectacular, the build quality is fantastic, the screen is clear, the on screen keyboard is so easy to use, everything about this phone is simple and awesome. After using high end smartphones, this phone has made me reconsider why would people spend all of this money on these high end devices when you can have something affordable and simple that does all of the same things. Yes, it doesn't have a front-facing camera or the best high end back camera, and I do love taking pictures of scenery, etc. But for a 5 megapixel shooter with no flash, this takes really good pictures. Just, get this phone you won't regret it!

Purchase this phone for the wife and she is very happy with it. I am also impressed with its features. It does take a little time to understand the phone's hardware and operating system. It is a shame that some would blame the phone, but ignore that it is in fact their ignorance that is causing the problems.In conclusion I rate the phone 5 stars.Now on the other hand, the VM service which is owned by Sprint only runs on Sprint's network service with "NO" roaming allowed. Although VM claims there is no fee for roaming, the fact is that since "no roaming is allowed" thus the reason for the "no roaming fee"; nice marketing gimmick.  This means that if your area is weak for the Spring signal network or you go out of the area, you will have no service. If you had a regular Sprint phone with Spring service then roaming is enable, and if you are out of their service area then you would be roaming with no extra charges. This fact was discovered when the wife switched from Sprint to VM. So be prepare for this no-service problem.The new updated $35 ($10 increase) monthly plan is still a nice price. It would have been nicer if there were more minutes added to the plan, and if roaming were allowed with the $10 increase. Maybe in the future.In conclusion, if you do not travel much out of the Sprint service area and have a good signal in your area you will be fine.

I've had several Rokform products. I had the original aluminum case for my iPhone 4, then moved to the poly case for the ability to mount it where I needed it. When I moved to an iPhone 5, I ordered the Rokbed for it without hesitation. I recently traded my iPhone for the Galaxy S3 and made sure to add the Rokform to the order so I could have it right away. As with all of the other Rokform products, this one doesn't disappoint. It fits extremely well, looks nice and is rugged. It doesn't add a lot of bulk to the phone and doesn't have any play or slop in the fit. It mounted to my existing RMS firmly and also fits the Rokform Bike Mount on my Trek.I actually love their products so much that I typically base my phone-buying decisions on whether or not I can get a Rokform case for it.My only issue with this case is with the magnet. Because of the large size of the S3 and the position of the magnet, my phone won't stay horizontal. It wants to flop over. In vertical mode, if I don't stick it perfectly vertical, it has a tendency to want to flip over. This isn't a huge deal as I don't really use the magnet all that much, but it's worth mentioning. With the iPhone versions, the phone stayed completely horizontal and just had a more solid magnetic attraction to metal surfaces. I'm not sure if they could remedy this in future phones with a larger magnet or dual magnets or something, but it's not a deal-breaker at all.I hope this review is helpful and if you have questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out!

Read the notes that are provided for your download because it will explain how to clear any previously incorrect drivers you may have attempted, then install the correct driver you just downloaded and I now had a usb-serial option in the CHIRP menu for downloading(cloning) the radio.Hope this helps others avoid the extra frustration I endured. The iClever M1 3200 DPI gaming mouse simply put is outstanding.  Ive been using this mouse with my laptop running Windows 10 for six weeks now and its performed flawless and Ive had no issues!  Also, I use a Macbook at work with the MX Master and honestly, the M1 performs on the same level as the MX Master.  The mouse itself is glossy on top and has black soft material on the side for gripping while in use.  Also, the USB cable is braided and the lights on the top and side of the gaming mouse give it that extra wow factor and I truly enjoy the lights.  From a design standpoint, the M1 is on top notch and still using it to date and I give it 5 stars and heres why.PACKAGING// iClever has taken a simple approach with detailed information placed on the sides of the box and just a few pieces of tape and you have everything opened and ready to go.  Some detailed information is on the back of the packaging that might be helpful from a spec standpoint.  The USB connected mouse includes some instructions, plus a disc for software install.  Setup is simple and I ran into no issues, even running it on my Windows 10 laptop.FUNCTIONALITY// The M1 gaming mouse feels solid in the hand and is fairly compact.  I have large-medium hands and its vert comfortable when using it for extended periods of time.  You can tell the iClever took their time to design this mouse as it features a combination of glossy and soft touch materials.  The LED lightning throughout the mouse is definitely a nice touch and I really do wish more mice would include this feature as its sort of like using a backlit keyboard.  The USB cable is braided and feels extremely durable.  Once plugged in, install some software and setup up your settings and you're ready to go.  For work, I have to use a Macbook and choose the MX Master by Logitech and the M1 is on par with this mouse in terms of clickiness of the buttons and smooth movement of the mouse.  With its up to (built-in) 3200 DPI, you almost guaranteed fast and efficient movement each and every time.  Additionally, the nine programable buttons are the perfect touch for gaming needs.  Im thrown back with the M1 mouse and I can see long term usage of it for many years to come.FINAL IMPRESSIONS// Overall, Im extremely impressed with this gaming mouse and after searching through Amazon, I ran across iClever and happy I did, since this mouse has performed flawless for the past six weeks and seriously I have no complaints about it.  As mentioned, to date I continue to use the M1 and its my favorite wired gaming mouse to date, after coming from an older competitor's wired mouse.  Id definitely put this M1 3200 DPI mouse on your wish list and itd make a great holiday gift for anyone.  You won't be disappointed and my rating is a perfect 5/5 stars!!!Note, I did receive this product on behalf of iClever for an honest consumer experience and everything in my review is my honest opinion and usage of the mouse since Ive received it.

I had a chance to review a hands free kit and I wanted to see the quality of it.  I put this hands free kit to the test. Im an audio engineer so I ran it through a test to see what the frequency response is. In the picture I have attached my test. I ran pink noise through the Bluetooth speaker and got some interesting results. The big spike at 1K is right where people talk and our hearing is the best. There is a huge dip right in the 2K range. That 9 dB drop is a difference of 3 times the volume drop. What that means for the layman is that it's going to sound a little bit funny. I tried different angles and that is not from fazing or if it is I would bet it was the big chunk of plastic in front of the speaker. The 2-watt speaker in this device isn't the loudest thing in the world but I do like it for directions. Playing music on this will lead to a distorted sound so this will not benefit you if you are interested in a bluetooth kit for music only.When looking at this speaker for phone calls, however, it serves its purpose. It works fine for calls and is easy to pair. The Magnet on the back is a cool touch but may be a problem if you hit a pot hole or have a rough drive. I don't like that you cant have the speaker charging while it is hanging on the visor. The micro USB connector is on the back instead of the side. I wish this was a little bit louder as I don't have the quietest vehicle in the world.I purchased this bluetooth speaker kit at a deep discount so I could offer my unbiased and honest review of it. All opinions are truly my own and it is my pleasure to be able to offer others my thoughts so they might be able to make informed decisions as to whether products are appropriate for them.

This phone is pretty much the best mid range smartphone out right now for Q1 2015. I got to try out this camera from a friend whose father works for Samsung and I decided to tinker around with it for a while. So here is my initial thoughts on on the Samsung Galaxy A5.__________Design: This phone from Samsung is special. That's because this one houses and aluminum body unlike Samsung phones that we have seen to this day. One of Samsung's biggest complaints is that their phones though at premium prices, lack a premium feel. This is kind of odd because they decided to make their mid range phone feel more premium. Is it me or something seems off here? I am pretty sure that Samsung will adopt the aluminum structure for their later phones and this is just a sneak peak into it.Despite the aluminum body, it felt really good in my hand and I didn't really notice the weight of it. I am a tiny person and often struggle with heavy objects but no problems here. The buttons feels nice to press and everything works responsively._________Processing Power: This little thing comes with the Snapdragon 410 processor with 2 gb of ram. Well in human terms, it basically means the specs are pretty decent for the price you are paying for it. Navigation is nice and snappy which I really like and there seems to be no hic ups with using TouchWiz with this device. I know that Samsung's UI often drains the smoothness of some of their phones, but I had no lag when I was using this thing. The 2gb of ram are a nice addition making multitasking fun instead of frustrating and gaming does well here too._________Display: This phone comes with a 5 inch 720p AMOLED display and though the screen specs are dated, I am happy to say that it looks great. Colors pop out and look really nice on the screen. You can also correct the saturation on this phone which is a plus because samsung tends to over saturate their images but it does make colors pop better.All in all it is great screen for the price.__________Sound: The sound is probably the biggest setback of this phone. There is one speaker at the back of this phone and lets be honest, it ain't that great. You also won't get any stereo sound effects coming out of this thing. Possibly the biggest setback of this phone.__________Camera: This guy houses a 13 mp shooter and it takes pretty good photos. With good lighting and camera placement, you can get almost dslr like shots from this. Low light photos however can be a bit washed out and grainy.The 5 mp front facing camera sound great from the spec sheet but pictures come out washed out in my opinion. The sensor on the camera is simply not good enough to capture a lot of light which iphone camera's are so famous for achieving.__________Conclusion: The Samsung Galaxy A5 is the most premium midrange phone out there. You will not be disappointed with this phone even when you just paid half the price from current flagship devices. It has a nice premium feel and the aluminum body will certainly attract the jealousy of your friends.

The Otterbox Defender case is designed to do one thing - protect your iPhone from hazards. I have yet to see a case that does it as well as this one. Impact protection is superb, with a plastic shell and a rubber exterior layer, this case could really save your bacon. A built-in screen protector is included - thin enough to allow screen swiping with no interference, while helping to keep dust and dirt out of the phone. The only issue I could see is one common to heavy-duty cases, there is limited room around the power plug. If you use any kind of charging stand you may have trouble plugging it in. Like all other Otterbox products I've tried, this is extremely well designed and built. It also comes with a belt clip - while I prefer holster type cases like this, I really didn't bond with the 3-point clip. It takes two hands to take it out of the case and it adds a lot of bulk to the case. However, if you want a case with a holster, you cannot do better than this one.

If you're someone who spends a lot of time around water, protecting your phone might be your topmost priority. Whether you hike, kayak, swim, fish, etc... water can be a pretty big problem when it comes to technology.I've read about other "waterproof" cases that fail to make the grade. If this case wasn't genuinely waterproof, you could ruin your phone completely. Because of that, I wanted to test this case out before trusting my phone with it. I placed a sheet of paper in this case, sealed it, and then stuck in in my sink underwater. After three hours, I came back to find that the paper was completely dry. I pressed on the case, squeezed, and tried to stress-test it, because no water was able to get in. I felt safe to try it again now with my phone, and it worked great. Underwater, I was able to use the screen almost as if it weren't wrapped in plastic at all. Of course, behind the plastic barrier, the screen isn't as responsive as usual, but everything but the most sensitive movements are still usable.This is the first brand of waterproof case that I've tried, but I'm wary of other products. I've read a lot of bad reviews of leaks and such. Luckily, this case seals shut really well, and I would trust my phone in it any day. Tested with an iPhone and a Droid RAZR.[Note: the author was provided this product from the distributor in return for a review]

I purchased a Mophie case for my HTC One about 7 months ago and ended up replacing it twice before I gave up. The tech at Verizon recommended me trying this pack out. Most reviews were in favor of it so I thought I would give it a shot. I could not have been happier.  Pack is a tad on the bulky side for most of this type of packs. It weighs 1 pound 1.1 ounces. It's just under than 4 1/2" long, 2 3/4" wide and 7/8" wide. Pack has a nice black or white hard surface top and bottom with a brushed stainless wrap around side. It has 2 charging ports both will charge at 2.1 Amps, however it will only charge at 2.1 Amps with one port being used. When both ports are used, it will charge 1 Amp. Pack has a micro USB port to charge it. It does not come with a cord or wall charger. You will need to supply your own. Power button on the side shows the Packs capacity. It has 4 green LED lights. 4 meaning fully charged, and 0-1 respectively. 12,000 mAh battery, this pack, packs a ton of power.Pack does take on average 5-7 hours to fully charge itself. Keep that in mind when you need it. I recommend a full overnight charge if you plan to use it.It's been 3 months and all my testing on this is done. Below will be the results. This was a blast to do! All phones/tablets were charged while locked and powered on. Wi-Fi was left on and all email/text/twitter updates were left on, to update in real time. This was done to simulate real world use. Now lets get to the good stuff!Test #1: HTC One Battery 4% Pack 430 minutes in Phone 24% Pack60 min = 46% Pack 490 min = 66% Pack 3120 min = 81% Pack 3150 min = 100% Pack 3Test #2: HTC One Battery 5% Pack 360 min = 45% Pack 3120 min = 84% Pack 2145 min 100% Pack 1Test #3: HTC One Battery 5% Pack 1115 min = 78% Pack 1155 min = 100% Pack 1Test #4: HTC One Battery 5% pack 136 min = 29%Average around 2.5 hours for a full 100% Charge. I was able to get 3.3 full charges out of this pack.Test #5: Samsung Galaxy Tab 2 10.1 Battery 5% Pack 4120 min = 51% Pack 2200 min = 77% Pack 1315 min = 100% Pack 1HTC One 9% Pack 170 min = 48%It took over 5 hours to charge my tablet with about 40% left to charge my HTC One. Not the best results, but this tablet does take an abnormally long amount of time to charge.Test #7:Apple iPad 2 Battery 11% Pack 464 min = 43% Pack 3124 min = 71% Pack 2190 min = 93% Pack 1206 min = 100% Pack 1Apple iPhone 4 Battery 16% Pack 146 min = 65% Pack 1194 min = 100% Pack 1Apple iPhone 4 Battery 10% Pack 140 min = 61% Pack 0It took 3.4 hours to charge the iPad 2 with almost a full 100% charge and another 50% charge for the iPhone 4.Test #8: Samsung Galaxy Note 3 Battery 8% Pack 4148 min = Battery 100% Pack 2Test #9: Samsung Galaxy Note 3 Battery 10% Pack 2143 min = Battery 100% Pack 1Test #10 Samsung Galaxy Note 3 Battery 5% Pack 234 min = Battery 49% Pack 0Average around 2.5 hours to fully charge the Note. I ended up getting 2.3 charges on the Note 3. Not as impressive at the HTC One, but the Note 3's battery is 3200 mAh and the HTC One is 2300 mAh.The Tab 2 10.1's battery is 7000 mAh and the iPad 2's is 6930 mAh. Keep the capacity of your device in mind when charging it on the pack. Charging two devises at once will greatly increase the charge time of your devices. I like to take the phone and place it in my front pocket with the juice pack in the rear pocket. I found a short micro USB cord that is around 2 feet long. I can charge it while I am fishing, hunting or doing a non-aggressive activity.I did one more test on the juice pack. I fully charged it and left it for just at 2 weeks with no devices attached. At the end of the 2 weeks, it still had all 4 LED bars showing. I then did the test on the Note 3 without topping the juice pack off. I would assume like any battery, it would decrease over time. That will have to come in another review.To close this review, I can't say enough about this juice pack. I love it. Looks clean, smooth lines and works great. Don't be mislead by the pack being able to charge your smart phone 8 times. My guess it that would be something small like an iPhone 4 that is not fully drained.

Although a car battery can provide plenty of DC power to run electronic devices, it's at 12 Volts, and most small devices like smartphones and music players take 5 Volts. You can use an inverter, which turns your 12 Volts DC into 110 Volts AC, and then use your normal wall wart adapter to take the voltage down to 5 Volts DC. Or you can use this, and save all the power that would heat up your inverter and wall wart.I don't know what is inside this device, but it seems to be fairly efficient. It puts out up to 2.1 amps, which at 5 Volts means it can deliver 11 Watts. If you use the 2 amp port, it does get a little warm, but I'll bet the efficiency is up near 90%. Although the charger has two ports, it can only deliver a maximum of 2.1 amps, so you do have a limit on how much charge it can deliver.New cars seem to have charging ports like this built in. But for those of us with older cars, this is a handy device. It looks well-made, and although not particularly breath-taking in its appearance, it does its job well. Can't ask for more from a charger.Note that the charger comes with no cord or anything other than what you see in the product picture.

This is an exceptional charger with a feature that everyone needs. The feature is PowerIQ charging technology for each of the 6 ports on this charger. That means that you can plug in any type of device and this smart charger will set the charging current to match the maximum charging rate that the device can safely handle. Many people don't realize that device manufacturers play games by wiring their devices and cables differently so that a charger that is designed to charge an Android device will not quickly charge an Apple device. Manufacturers like Apple, Samsung, Android devices and Chinese smart devices have different circuitry in them and if the charger doesn't match their circuitry they can charge at a slower rate. The IQ port doesn't care what type of device you plug into it because it adapts its charging rate to match what the device wants by sensing what type of device is plugged into it.Excellent charging device for all my USB devicesI would recommend this product as a value for the price! I did receive a slight discount for honest feedback.  If you are in the market this product could work well for you! You won't regret buying these I assure you.

This is pretty nifty.  There are 3 cases, and each one has little moveable separators that can be velcro-ed into place.  This is kind of like a nice camera case.  The padding is fairly thick, and you don't have to use the little separators if you don't want to.  I bought this set for my Surface Pro 3.  Since the SP3 only has 1 USB port and only 128 gb of space, I have a lot of things to plug in, including a 4-port USB hub, the SP3 power supply, an extra hard drive, another extra hard drive (somehow I ended up with 2 - go figure) extra USB plugs for my phone & Kindle, reading glasses with a case (I'm getting old), an extra power bank for my non-SP3 electronics.  There are a couple of miscellaneous other things in there as well - cords and twist-ties and stuff.  If I tried, I think I can manage to get everything I want in there as well as my fold up, over the ear, Bluetooth headphones.The stitching is nice and tight.  The zippers run smoothly.  Each case has a little handle that I don't think I will ever use, but it's nice to have.  This is soft sided, but fairly sturdy.  They are more crush-resistant than crush-proof.  There is also a "free" clear passport cover which is kind of useless, but hey, it's free!This set really does hold a lot of stuff.  I haven't yet worked out how I'm going to configure everything - I keep playing around with these, which is fun.  (Sad, but true.)At the time of this writing, this set was $41.99.  You can get this a little cheaper through other sellers, but not much.  I would have thought that $42 was a little pricey for 3 small bags, but after looking around Amazon, it's not bad.  However, if they get this set down to $25, I would get a second set.  These really are handy.

The price of this 4-Port USB international wall charger sounds steep on first examining it, but you get "a lot of bang for the buck" in this device. There is a great deal that I like about this device and, for me personally, it has no negatives. These are the features that I find excellent:--The design itself is very attractive. The device is made of a very high quality, shiny plastic. I got the white with pink trim. It is labeled "rosy" color, and it is a deep, bright pink.--The prongs fold up, to make it more portable, and it comes with interchangeable plugs for the UK, EU, and AU, which is very handy for the international traveler. It comes in a small, sturdy cardboard box, which I am keeping to store the extra plugs in. If I had one small semi-complaint it would be: Please make a nice, attractive storage box worthy of this terrific device. (Though I do realize that if the manufacturer does that, the price would, of course, go up a bit.)--As the name implies, it has four ports, which allows you to charge up to four different types of devices at the same time. The ports are clearly labled as to function with two of them delivering 1.0 A of power, suitable for smart phones, and two ports offering 2.4 A of power, suitable for tablets and other similar devices. I personally only own an iPhone and no tablets, but I charged my iPhone, my Kindle Fire, my MP3, and a portable speaker all at the same time with no issues. They charged much more quickly than with my other brand of 4-Port USB.--This device has a small LED light which comes on whenever it is plugged in. It does double duty as a night light when you are charging devices overnight.--There are multiple safety features, including: over-current protection, short-circuit protection, overload latch-off protection, and voltage protection.--This device is ETL and CB certified.--It comes with a 12-month warranty.--It has an "intelligent IC" feature which serves to deliver the fastest possible charging speed to your devices.This device would make an outstanding gift for friends and family of all ages.

Fits like a glove, provides lip to protect the screen if phone is set face-down. There's a significant depth to the back of the case so camera lens is well protected, though this leads to the hole for the fingerprint scanner feeling quite deep (scanner still works with no issues). Power and volume buttons are solid. I don't think it'd be possible to accidentally press them in a pocket. Gives a very solid overall feel to the phone and makes it easier for me to hold (I have somewhat large hands). The back is smooth, but provides much better traction than not having a case. I don't feel like I'm going to drop the phone or have it accidentally fall out of my pocket while sitting any more (basketball shorts are slippery). Some kind of texture might have helped more but it would likely collect pocket lint. The carbon fiber bits are more subtle than they looked in the pictures here. I bought this for function, not form, but it's definitely a good looking case.

After reading many, many reviews of other cases, I was very discouraged and wasn't sure what to buy. Then I saw this case and decided to take a chance on it and am glad I did. It's exactly how they describe it. The case actually looks and smells like real leather (some people might find the smell objectionable but I don't - it's like a nice piece of luggage). The phone fits snugly in the plastic holder, which has generous cutouts for all the functions. The case will hold your drivers license and one or two credit cards and still close with the magnetic latch without bulging. There's also a pocket you could stick a bill or two in. If you put a credit card in the top position, it will cover the cutout for the selfie camera, but I'd open the case to use that anyway. The strap is removable, which is nice because I probably won't use it. The case has enough heft and texture to make holding the phone a lot more secure than holding the slippery phone itself. It's not going to provide much moisture resistance but should provide some protection in a fall, assuming the case doesn't open and the phone doesn't come out or land on its face. I got another style of Toru case for my wife and she really likes it. Given all the horror stories surrounding other cases, even name brand cases, which were rushed to market, it'd be worth your while to look at these. Definitely worth the money.

When you first look at the price you do not believe that you will be receiving a quality product.  We were pleasantly surprised upon installation.  Hubby's vehicle is a '98 and does not have navigation.  He is able to now use his Samsung Note 3 with google maps while hand-free.  The sticky suction mount works flawlessly on the leather textured dash.  I was worried that it would fall when he was going over pot holes or the arm would begin to flap up and down when the vehicle would shake.  None of that has happened!  His phone is rather large, yet the mount effortlessly supports the weight in either landscape or portrait mode.  It is wonderful how the phone is now able to register the vehicle's MPH while in motion and navigate with voice activation.This product is high quality and is mounted on the right side of the steering wheel.  One issue is that when we removed the mount the sticky suction is no longer sticky.  This can be remedied according to the directions by cleaning the disc surface and re-activating it's lack for a better word (stickiness).Highly recommend for older vehicles that do not have all the bells and whistles or base models that need navigation or hand-free operation of their cell phone.  This will hold even the largest screens and not shake.  The product has been continuously used for three months and has not loosened as of today.  Great!

I have read a few reviews on this case stating that the case blocked the home, back, and menu buttons.  This is a complete lie!  The truth is the back and menu buttons on the Galaxy SIII are hit too easily without this case and the case by no means blocks access to these buttons.The black and white color combination of this case matches the SIII white color scheme exactly.The case is solid and sturdy and even though I have read some reviews stating the case was bulky, it was obvious to me that they had never had a bulk case before because this case is quite slim in comparison to other cases I have had.  Besides your are never going to get a good case without adding some thickness to the phone and I think it is a small price to pay for piece of mind considering I paid $300.00 for my SIII.The belt clip is strong and the phone feels totally secure when it is on my belt.The only thing I do not like was the screen protector that came with the case.  It does "cloud" up the high definition screen of the SIII.  It is too thick and makes using the touch screen more difficult.  That being said, just do not use the screen protector that comes with the case, buy some HD screen protectors and you will be very happy.  I have read another review on this case that stated the screen protector that came with the case has to be used because it clips onto the phone before the case is attached and the case will not fit properly with out the screen protector that is supplied.  This is also a lie.  I have taken the supplied screen protector off and am using the rest of the case with no fitment issues at all.  As a matter of fact it is easier to install the rest of the case WITHOUT the supplied screen protector and it fits perfectly.To be quite honest if you are looking for an affordable case that performs like a case that costs 2 to 3 times more, look no further, just pick the color you want and buy it.  This case is the best I have found for my SIII.  You will not be disappointed.

The Native Union iPhone Dock and Handset (sorry, I don't have the energy to type the entire name!) is sleek and sexy. Of that there is no doubt. But what purpose does it serve other than to look impressively swank and modern on your desk?After all, you can easily hold the iPhone in your own little hands. You probably don't need a handset or yet another docking device to charge or one more Bluetooth connection to your iPhone. And if your iPhone resides in a case, you can't use the device as a charger or dock anyway unless you remove it from the case. So I remain mystified as to the value of this creation.On top of this, the included multi-language user manual leaves out important details. For example, which adapter is for the iPhone 3GS and which is for the iPhone 4? Neither are marked and the instructions are silent on the topic. You have to guess from the adapter's shape. (Hint: The 3GS adapter is rounded while the 4 is squared.) Furthermore, the AC power brick needs the proper AC plug adapter attached to it. It is not immediately apparent how this task is to be accomplished. Nowhere is there a mention of how to install or remove these adapters. We are supposed to know or guess.Maybe this will change tomorrow, but today the Native Union iPhone Dock and Handset sits on my desk iPhoneless but looking sleek and sexy. In my office at least that's about all the purpose it can serve.

Great, it's big and bulky, but that's necessary for containing a ZeroLemon battery (optionally).  It's a good thing it had instructions because there's a rubber wrapping layer over a hard plastic shell that comes apart using two different sets of tabs, some small ones for a tight hold and some large ones for strength.The screen protector is reasonably good quality and easier to install than most, but the cut-outs for the sensors and speaker at the top versus the cut-out for the button at the bottom don't really match up well.  You have to get the left edge of the cut-out for the button as close as possible without actually being on the button in order for the right edge of the top cut-out not to cover the proximity sensor.  The screen protector itself seems to do very little though since it still gets fingerprints, and is even more apt to scratch than the screen since it's made of much weaker materials.  The edges fit under the edges of the case though (just barely) so it works very well with the case itself.The case is very good construction and would protect well against a fall.  The button covers on the outside eliminate any feedback, no matter how minimal it already was, from the physical buttons though (just like any other case).  The little covers for the headphone jack and the usb jack are really just flaps that don't really do much in the way of protecting them.  They're almost impossible to open one-handed as well, even the directions instruct you to use two hands when opening them.  Without them though the thickness of the rubber would create a funnel for dirt directly into the jacks and would leave rough edges on the sides of the case that would make it catch on everything.The "stand" is really just the belt clip on the belt carrier.  This is clearly shown in the product description, but the downside of it is that in order to keep the "stand" open, the belt clip itself locks when opened all the way.  If you aren't intending to use it as a stand but are merely trying to remove it from your belt/pants/pocket, it's possible to accidentally lock it open while removing it.  This could also be termed a "feature" though as it does make it easier to remove from your clothing with the clip locked open.  Unlocking it requires a good bit of grip strength though as you have to push the belt clip open slight more until it hits its actual limit, then push open a slightly flexible heavy duty locking mechanism.The phone case locks into the the belt carrier very solidly, with a slight indent in the side of the case fitting with a slightly protruding slightly flexible heavy duty flange.  Unfortunately they decided to put an indent in the correct position only on the left side of the phone (there are two indents on the right side but they're off center so they don't line up).  If you wear the belt carrier on your right-hand side, you either have to put the phone in face up if you want the connecting flange facing up, and then have to fight to unhook it with one hand,or put the connecting flange down and the phone in the carrier face down (preferred and easier to work the flange with one hand), but risk dropping the phone when unhooking it from the carrier one-handed.

Honestly, I only bought this case because I am currently waiting for the Urban Armor Gear one to come back in stock, and I wanted a cheap one in the meantime to tide me over. Color me surprised when I realized what I received in the mail. The cutouts are perfectly-aligned (albeit there is an extra one where there would normally be an IR blaster, so I'm not sure why it was included in this case), and the case fits the phone very snugly. The plastic feels solid enough to take an impact, though I would have preferred it to cover the corners of the phone rather than leave those just covered with silicone. My favorite part of this case might also be a reason why others might not like it so much; the sides of the case extend higher up than the edges of the phone screen. This is excellent for protection in drops (the screen will not make contact if you drop your phone on a corner, unlike most other cases such as the Otterbox, where only the top and bottom of the phone have the case extending higher than the phone itself), though it does hinder the ability to use the edge screen by a significant amount. You have to really try in order to use the edge features, especially if you have larger fingers. This does not bother me, but I don't really use the edge features often anyway, so it's not a deal-breaker for me like it may be for others. Overall, a great case in terms of protection for the price, and the color perfectly matches the pictures.I got this at a discount for my review

I learned on my first tablet, 9 tablets ago, that a case is a must. I had to pay to have 2 screens replaced before I came to the realization  that a case is NECESSITY to keeping tablet ownership costs in line. Not to mention starting over reloading a tablet after a reset or worse, losing all the data not saved to micro SD.I knew what to expect with these Amzer Gel cases. I had one on my Nexus 7 (ORANGE)  and I still have a Galaxy Note 8.0 (GREEN) and now a bright  FUSCSIA) for the new 12.2 Note Pro.Construction features: All the corners and an *X* cross support on the rear of the case offer a true drop Cushion feature. They are reinforced with perhaps triple material in the high prone impact areas. So good in fact, that my Nexus 7 on top of the fire place mantel, survived a fall to the stone harth. Why, the cat didn't think the tablet should be in her favorite spot. Amzer gel cases have cushioned my tablets on every occasion of mishap. Too many I am embarrassed to say. Here is another great feature or two. The case wraps around toward the screen yet still allows enough room to access the extreme edges of the tablet screen surfaces. Plus that area from the perimeter edges of the case (where you hold it) have a molded texture in the case gel. This provides a gripping surface which makes it possible to hold even if you have damp hands or your hand cream is still fresh.I also like to keep a special after market S Pen on my tablet cases that have the pen. I found that making a small hole in the side of the case with a paper hole punch, makes a perfect sized hole to dock the pen to the case by the pocket clip on the side of the pen. Especially on the 12.2 Note Pro, as it comes with mini Note 3 phone pen?I have never had a tablet crack from a drop using an Amzer case. Three years and now 3 tablets using that case.I give it 4 stars only because the case does not fold over the screen. Now granted it would require a strange mishap for the tablet to some how land directly on the screen?  I must also mention that when the case wraps around over the edges  it does have an extra thick front boarder on the case.I actually  have dropped or a cat has launched a tablet more then once and it has landed screen down. However the extra thick front gel has always protected. 4 1/2 Stars would be more fair to the protective qualities of the case. BTW, I weighed and compared my Note 8.0 Amzer with the leather fold over case and found that it to be lighter.

The directions that came with the protector were very clear. I took one of the other reviewer's advice and hinged the sticky strips from the bottom of the phone rather than the side. After using the various wipes and dust remover, and aligning the glass, I just let it drop gently onto the front of the phone. I didn't even have to touch the glass. It just zipped, bubble-free from the bottom all the way to the top in about 20 seconds. Done.The glass is slightly thicker and less flexible than I would have expected, but this is probably advantageous with regards to drops and spills. It's also about a millimeter less tall and wide than the phone's glass surface. (I measured with a caliper.) This is not a deal breaker. It's probably a tolerance consideration that makes it easier for most people to arrange the glass so it actually fits on the phone's surface.It's way smoother than the plastic protector that came with the phone. In my mind's eye, I have the image of Smeagle from Lord of the Rings stroking the One Ring and saying "My Precious"...It's hard to smear this glass, even with filthy hands. It wipes off easily.The container it came in was rigid, probably to protect the glass during shipping. It's made of cardboard, but has some foam lining. The packaging and the other contents that came with the glass are themselves award-worthy design. Well done, Sparin.

In an age where things are much more commonly charging via a USB cable, I wanted to reduce the clutter at my power strip. This solves the problem quite nicely.It's supplying adequate power to the following three devices: 1) A Chromecast Audio, 2) a Fire TV Stick, and 3) an Amazon Basics amplified HD antenna (the older model). Power requirements are a concern of course, it can do up to 2.4A to each port for a total of 8A. The charger for the antenna did a max of 0.5A, and the two other devices had chargers that did a max of 1A each.In this role, I really can't complain. The audio on my chromecast feels a little cleaner, and the reception on my antenna seems just a little bit more stable (anyone with an HD antenna knows what I mean). Of course that could all be psychosomatic, but I think it's just the result of having a cleaner power source powering these things.Finding a 4 port charger isn't the easiest thing to do. They're all 3-port and underpowered, or 5+ ports and overpriced. This was perfectly in the sweet spot.

Im not sure if this is fair to say, but iPhones are kind-of weak.  I have had to replace the screen on my wife's iPhone 3 different times, because, they just don't hold up to any sort of impact very well.As a result of getting tired of replacing screens that still broke despite having what I consider "decorative" cases on her phone, I got her a gawd-awful case that protects it like Fort Knox.  But good lord was that thing ugly.  It was some sort of Otterbox knockoff that protected it very will with a form-factor of zero.  Did I mention it was ugly?After a while of us both grumbling about it, I decided to start looking around again for something both protective and attractive, and found this Buddibox Shield case.I have not had it long, and will update this review if I change my mind over time.  But here are my initial impressions.It is a hybrid case, with the rubber cushioning on the inside, with a hard case on the outside.  Visually, it's a very nice looking case, and a massive improvement over the last monstrosity..It fits the phone perfectly, with no gaps or areas that are too tight.  All the buttons work well through the rubber layer.The case has a kickstand on the back that I was pretty skeptical about.  It just seemed like something to break off or flap around and get in the way.  But it stays tucked in there really well, and actually requires effort to open, so you won't be accidentally "deploying" it.  And if you watch vids or read a lot, it would probably be pretty handy.As for protection, I think that sort of a direct impact to the screen itself, this case should protect it pretty well.  The case has a good amount of screen liftoff (I don't know the technical term for that, but I mean if you were to set it face down, how much clearance there is between the screen and the surface), and fully encased in the TPU should give it good shock protection.The only potential downside I see to this case is the hard plastic wraps around the corners.  It looks great now, but I have a feeling that after a few drops that will change.  I prefer when Hybrid cases have the rubber bumper corners that take the brunt of the impact, and keep the case looking nice too.  But cases are cheap, and if it has to sacrifice itself to keep the phone in good condition, that is what really matters.All in all, I am happy with this new case, and hopefully it will protect as well as it appears it will.  If not, and I have to buy yet another screen, I'll post back.*****************************************************Disclosure:  I received this phone case as a free sample for evaluation and review.  I am not a journalist, but I am kind-of acting as one in this capacity, and I do believe in Journalistic Integrity.  My agreement is for a thorough review, but not necessarily a good review.  I will not rate a product 5 stars if I do not feel it actually deserves it.  I will degrade my rating if it fails to live up to it's claims or my standards.  Fake reviews tick me off, and I refuse to engage in unethical behavior for free stuff.*****************************************************

This phone is giving me a constant error message that reads: SD Card Was Unexpectedly Removed. And all the photos and apps on the SD card are not accessible until the phone is restarted. Works fine for a while, then it happens again and again. Brand-new SD card, a SanDisk 32GB Class 10. I've owned a lot of Android smartphones and I've never seen anything like this. Same thing happens when other SD cards are tried. Works for a while, then the card crashes. If you Google this, you will see it is apparently a common problem with Motorola phones.ADVICE: If you buy this phone, DO NOT buy it directly from the Motorola website like I did. Motorola does not make it easy to get a replacement phone. They will try to charge you. Buy it from Amazon where returns are simple and easy.Original review: This is probably the best "cheap" smartphone you can buy.I bought three of these for my family and took them to Cricket Wireless for a very good unlimited data, multiple-phone deal.I have no complaints about the phone except that I've only had it for a week and It needs to go back to Motorola for repairs.The edges of the screen and the earpiece/speaker are wide and very deep set. These areas collect lots of dust very easily.I tried to use a very soft toothbrush to GENTLY clear the dust from the earpiece and it immediate punctured the thin waterproof membrane that covers the earpiece. The waterproof membrane covering the earpiece is thinner than paper. It's like rice paper.So the phone still works, but the waterproofing has been compromised. And it happened very, very easily.So it will be $70 to ship it back and have it repaired. No fun.Motorola gives ZERO instructions regarding how to keep the phone clean, but my advice is DO NOT try to brush the dust off.Ever.The waterproof membrane is super-delicate and really should be covered by a grille of some kind. It's a design flaw in my opinion.Other than that, I'm happy with the phones so far. Best deal in mobile!

This is a power unit that holds a lot of juice for many portable devices.It comes with the unit, a USB cord with an open plug, and a series of plugs to connect to your device - iPod/iPad, micro USB, mini USB and 2mm round. It took awhile to get this charged up plugging the provided USB cable into my USB wall charger. You have the option of charging it with your computer as well. I think it was at least 10 hours of charge time but I eventually went to sleep and it was fully charged in the morning so I'm not sure when it hit its peak.It has two USB ports and one input micro USB port to flip your cable for charging the unit. There is an easy power button with three light indicator for remaining power on the unit. Three lights indicate more than 80% power remains, two lights mean 30-80% and one light means less than 30% power remains. I wish there were actually four light indicators but three will do.The unit has a solid feel. I used it to charge a 30% battery level Droid Incredible 4G, which took about 80 minutes to charge fully. I also used it to take a 17% Kindle Fire to full battery power in just over an hour. It sill had two out of three charge lights available. I then charged an iPod classic with about 50% power left and that was about the time it needed recharging.I was impressed with how quickly it charged these devices.If you've ever run out somewhere and forgot to fully charge a device, you know how convenient it is to have something like this in your briefcase. If you have ever been caught in a power outage, you know how frustrating it is to get to a power source to charge what is probably your only form of communication for awhile.It would have been nice to have a carry bag or pouch to contain the unit, cable and tips but it's easy enough to find something in my closet.I was provided a unit for testing and evaluation.

I chose the GGS over a Nikon screen protector, because I read many complaints about the Nikon protectors scratching easily. The GGS LCD screen protector is crystal clear and inconspicuous on my camera. The smaller protector that covers the LCD in top of the camera looks exactly like the LCD frame and is completely unnoticeable. The protector for the large LCD on the back of the camera also looks just like the frame, but it covers the "Nikon" logo below the LCD and replaces it with a smaller GGS logo. I can't say how these fare with long-term use yet, but the protectors seem hard and durable so far. They look nice. And they are securely affixed.The GGS LCD screen protectors are ultra-thin optical glass. They are 0.55mm thick with at least 90% light transmittance. They consist of 6 layers: an adhesive layer, an anti-crack layer, a color layer, and a transparent layer, sandwiched between two protective layers. (The adhesive and color layers only have adhesive and color around the edge.) 3M double-sided adhesive around the edge on the underside adheres to the frame of your camera's LCD. If you want to remove the screen protector, the manufacturer recommends using a very thin plastic card or similar device to slide around the edge to loosen the adhesive. Prying it up by one corner may break the glass.The GGS screen protectors were easy to apply. I sat near a window with bright daylight streaming through, so that I could see clearly that there were no dust, oils, or fingerprints on the LCD. You peel the protective plastic from the front of the screen protector, then peel the adhesive backing off, line it up, and stick it on. Press around the edges with a lens cloth to ensure that the protector has adhered well. There was one snag: When I removed the adhesive backing from the large protector, some dust appeared on the underside. I think this was due to static electricity. So I had to wipe that off with a lens cloth and make sure the underside was perfectly clean before I stuck it on the camera. It was fine, but make sure there is nothing on either side of the protector before you apply it.

The Mifine 5.2Amps / 26W 3 USB car charger adapter will be a valuable addition to your car or your travel bag for use in rental cars.  There are a lot of things to like about this charger.There are 3 USB charging ports.  One is 1 amp and two are 2.1.  The face of the charger is cleverly designed so that it is easy to distinguish which slot is 1 amp and which are the 2.1 amp slots.  The face is a triangle and the top slot is in a horizontal position while the 2.1 slots are below it in vertical positions.  You will never have to squint to see which slot is which.  No more guessing if you are plugging your device into the correct slot.The car charger is very neat and attractive.  Mine is a polished black color with chrome type trim.  It fits neatly in the cigarette lighter slot of the car.  The charger is only 2.75 long and the face is 1.5 high  so it really is a fairly small footprint.The 1 amp slot will charge most older devices and the 2.1 amp slots are for the newer electronic devices  smart phones, MP3 players, tablets, digital cameras, etc.This is a handy little device and I like to keep an extra spare in the car.  It has smart power technology that prevents over current or over charging.I was provided a sample for testing/review purposes.

I received this unit for review from the iFLASH people but I did not promise or offer to produce a 'good review' in return for a free product. It just happens that this version is all-around good and the very few negatives don't amount to much - fingerprint magnet is really the only one I can think of.So here it is why I love this model (I do): HIGH CAPACITY: at 8400mAh it can charge a phone a couple of times, perhaps more so, once you fill it up with juice you can simply drop it in your bag and it's ready to use when you need it. COMES WITH ITS OWN CHARGER: most chargers on the market rely on you providing your own wall charger, which is not hard but, still, the one included is a welcome frill and it just happens to be smaller than most third party so I'm glad it's included. INTERFACES GALORE: read Amazon's description but, just in case you don't want to bother, the iFLASH comes with a modular system that has a USB Type-A at one and it allows you to attach any of the supplied interfaces at the other end such as Mini USB, Micro USB, PSP (true), Nokia DC 2.0, iPhone/iPad (old style) and, of course, you can supply your own wire if none of these fit your device. DUAL CHARGING PORTS: they are the standard 0.6A for most phones and 2.0A for tablets but also usable to charge a phone faster. And they could be used concurrently (very nice). COMES WITH A BAG: this is very important to me. You are not going to take everything with you but the nice black bag can easily hold the iFLASH, the wall charger and the few wires and plugs you may need for your devices. CHARGE INDICATOR RINGLIGHTS: they light up when you press a button and 4 blue rings mean 100% full, 3 rings mean 75% full and so forth. LITTLE LED FLASHLIGHT: just in case. Who knows, you may find yourself in complete darkness and this just might save the day.And, again, no significant negatives. Yes, it's not going to win any prize at an industrial design competition but even the looks are quite decent. If you are looking for a charger that works well and comes with everything you need, this one is very good choice. I didn't measure how many minutes it took this iFLASH to charge my phones or little tablets but charged it did and it did it quickly and, once all I needed was in the bag already I didn't have to waste time looking for little wires or plugs.

This is a great bargain for those that have hesitated for a long time to upgrade. At $65, including shipping, it demonstrates how inexpensive these devices are to manufacture.  The reseller, Aplusacc, Inc., must have these flying off the shelves. With a 200 minute card in included, purchased separately it would be $39.95, this makes the phone only $25!I have been with Virgin many years and have noticed the move to get customers on some kind of plan since Sprint bought Virgin USA.  My Rumor 2 was having charging problems and I was told only 2 phones fit my plan and they were out of stock.We had some problems with TracFone with my wife's phone (LG 840G)  but maybe they are listening.  My representative spoke slowly and clearly.  She repeated back everything phonetically.  Although advised that it could take up to 2 days, the change went through in about a day.  I would advise purchasers going to TracFone to check their phone service with their current phone.  As the message said, when your current service ends, you are ready. (If your text fails to go through and there is signal or you try calling your number and there is a strange tone instead of a ring, then it is probably ready.)  Then it was simple to dial the *XXXXX supplied in a email or in manual, and the message says you are connected and the phone starts its programming from the new service.  It reboots and you are ready to make calls and text.  Triple minutes, texts and data were all displayed. Although I will probably never use the full texts allotted, I will look into what I think was a buyback program.I like the Wi-Fi only update option.  Downloading popular apps like Office Mobile, Outlook, OneDrive, Adobe Reader and TracFone Account were easy.  These were all done on home Wi-Fi and used no data time.  I recommend, as others have, to download the manual and familiarize oneself with the functions.  Tapping the functions, like the camera resolution and changing to SD storage were needed from being used to cursor and a manual key click.  It has the auto update for photos to my OneDrive, which is nice.  The browser wheel with right swipe for home and bookmarks on the right side was also a difference.  These phones are portable handheld media and computer devices.  Give it too much to do an you may have to wait, just like any netbook or  laptop with a less powerful processor and memory.  But, it is all many people need.I do have one caution, Google.  If you look at the updates that come for Chrome and other applications (Chrome and others are from Google that makes the Android operating system), you give up a lot of information to use the applications and services.  Just be selective to see what each application takes from you before saying Yes.  This includes the Google Cloud printing application.  A freebie does test pages on three laser printers but costs $12.95 and may be worth it for some.Overall I think this will do nicely for a few years and the accessories will make that possible.  An upgrade to a year plan will be made before the supplied 90 day card is up and will probably do the web specials, as we did for my wife's.  There is rollover if the full amount is not used in the year.  We had to purchase a small card and the annual fee service.  As we are not big talkers, this placed my wife's monthly charge at less than $10/mo for 2 years.  Based on current usage, I will be under $20/mo for a year and have over 3 GB data available for a 1000 min web-card, which is less than the Virgin plan that had no  high speed data unless I upgraded phone and plan for over twice as much.  This and service phone even bested AARP's discounted carrier by a large margin and Walmart Strait Talk plans..None of the media phones actually have a big enough screen for me to do serious work, so I will still rely on my laptop.  However, for small spreadsheets and document viewing, it has come in handy.  It is more powerful that this might indicate, it is a preference based in personal tastes and visual perception.  Although set to normal, it appears that people need better than 20/20 vision to use the small type setting.  The coverage may be an issue for some.  The keypad access to voicemail, pressing "1" till voicemail activates, is something to get used to but I would still recommend this to anyone that has hesitated to upgrade.Accessories included spare batteries and charger, case/holster and car charger, all recommended and available from Amazon.
