I received my new Galaxy Note 4 Case by Spigen today and I have to say it is of great quality but I feel that it may not calm any fears you might have. Spigen is a great company and I actually have several of their products. I honestly would not have ordered this had I not already gave them a try. The reason I say that it will not calm any of your fears is because if you are switching out your case from a bulky protective case to this one you will at first glance notice that it is visibly thinner. However, I will cover this further in the review. First, the packaging was sufficient. The case to the product is of hard plastic so there is no way it will be damaged in the shipping process. Also, it did not take much to open the box to the case. There are two stickers holding the clam shell type packing together and I was able to have it out of the packaging in under a minute. Inside the package you will find a guarantee card that shows you that it is the genuine article. There are companies out there that try to replicate the products made by Spigen and for this reason the company has set up a website where you can check if you received a bogus knock off or the real deal. Also, on the phone case is a seal of authenticity on it to further demonstrate that you are in fact receiving a quality product. The case itself as I said may not calm your fears about it providing enough protection. For my wife she was a little worried about this because she went from a worn out OtterBox to this thin looking case. I can tell you first hand that Spigen makes quality products, and that the phone cases that they are selling are of the finest construction. The technology that is out there now does not require your phone case to be as bulky as it once was. There are companies out there that know you will pay a lot for protection and if the wheel is not broken why fix it? This is their mentality, why are OtterBox's so bulky? Because they know you will pay for something that looks like it will protect your phone, and rather than stick with the times they will continue to peddle the same things because they know you as a consumer want it. I am here to tell you that this case works. It not only protects from falls, and drops but also from things being dropped on it. Now this case does not have a screen protector, and when I first went with Spigen I was a little concerned about that also, but you can see that most cases that have a screen protector is just a thin plastic film and it actually does not do anything at all. I personally paired my Spigen case up with a glass screen protector and those will crack before your actual screen will. Don't stick to what you know because there are better products out there like this Spigen phone case. You will be glad you made the switch to a thinner and better case.***Please note, I received this product at a discount for my honest review. I stand behind my review, and this product regardless of any discounts. ***

This is exactly the type of Qi charger I was looking for. I have the Google Qi charger that Ive been using since I had the Nexus 4. The problem I had with that is it can be finicky about placement of the device. Also, if it is at all remotely dusty, the phone will slide down off the charger.I just got the Nexus 6 and while it was working with the previous charger, placement was really tough to figure out since its a single coil. Also, the phone being so big, it kept sliding off the puck. I wanted to get something that could hold up a phone this size(or any phone for that matter) in a vertical position.After reading some of the comments at people saying this didn't work for the Nexus 6, I ordered it anyway knowing it should work. Clearly none of the people who commented that it didn't work don't have the phone. The Nexus 6 started charging immediately when I put the phone on the stand. Just for kicks, I tried putting it horizontally too. This was a bit trickier because the charging coil on the phone is actually lower on the back and not centered. Because of this, I only had to slide the phone over a bit on the stand and then it started charging right away.I like that this comes with two cables; one for wall charger and another for a computer USB. I also like the charging indicator on the front that changes from green to blue to indicate that its working(even though the phone signals that its working too). If you think its too bright then its not hard to cut a little slip of electrical tape to cover it up.Again, I want to let anyone know that is wondering, it WILL work with the Nexus 6. Not only that, I have a Spigen case on my phone and it was charging with that on it too!EDIT: Ok, I changed my review because, well, I think I reviewed the product too quickly.Yes, the charger shows that the Nexus 6 is charging whether its placed in vertical or horizontal position. However, it doesn't seem to have enough power to charge the phone. I put my phone on the charger at 19% battery and 4 hours later it was only at 20%. This is very disappointing and I apologize if I misled anyone with this review. Looks like Ill be on a search for something else at this point. This product did seem to work fine with my Nexus 5 and 4. However, the placement of the charging coil in the back of the Nexus 6 apparently is in a position not conducive to charging correctly with this device.

This is by far the easiest installation of a screen protector I have ever done.  The kit comes with a wet wipe to clean the phone, as well as a microfiber cleaning cloth - not the kind that you get with a pair of glasses, but an actual little piece of chamois microfiber.  I followed the instructions and installed this in seconds with not a SINGLE bubble.  I did not get the bottom aligned completely right.. it is slightly off center on the home button, but not so much that the glass overhangs the button.  It is worth noting that the top does not look like the photo -- there is not a small cutout for the speaker, but rather a large square cutout that leaves that whole section open.The glass is SUPER crystal clear.  More than any plastic cover I have ever had.  The test of the cover will be durability.  Will the clarity hold up to use to justify the cost which is 7-8 times more than a typical plastic cover.  Time will tell, and I will do my best to update.

This is a great headset in general, yet I give it only three stars. Here's why...When the speaker in my cheesy Motorola E815 dumbphone failed, I bought this top-of-the-line Plantronics Voyager Pro to work around it. The Pro is comfortable, sounds good, holds a long charge, and doesn't fall off my ear. However, it kept disconnecting from the Moto. I wrote to Plantronics, and was told that my phone was an antique (I knew that) and had an old version of Bluetooth that the Pro did not support. The Pro was too cool for my phone!Though disappointed, I tossed the Pro in a drawer for a few months until I got the current iPhone 4. The Pro works great with it! I leave it on my ear all day, grabbing calls without fatigue.Yesterday I downloaded the NPR Radio app for the iPhone, looking to flood my mind with food for thought. I fired it up, waiting for sophistication to pour out of my Pro and into my ear, and... nothing. The tiny speaker in the iPhone was chirping away, but the Pro did not speak.Turns out the Pro doesn't have Bluetooth A2DP, which it needs to play anything besides phone calls. Now, my Pro isn't cool enough!The point of a wireless headset is to transmit any and all audio to and from a mobile phone, and the Pro ought to do this but doesn't. It's one of the most expensive headsets available, and it's beautifully designed, but it doesn't work with an older phone and it doesn't work (beyond phone calls) with the world's most visible smartphone.Conclusion: if you want one headset to do everything, don't buy this one.

I have a Trident Kraken on my GS3, was excited to see a release for the GS5. Overall the case is good and appears that it will do it's job well. As usual Trident the mark for protecting my investment, however I am disappointed with a couple of things:1. The built in screen protector. I find that it allows dust into the home button area and at the top. I hate this on the Otterbox Defender on my work Iphone.  I was hoping that it was like the Galaxy family predecessors and would be open int he front so that I could try some Zagg glass. Saved me some money in the long run, but I think the feel would be better with the glass.2. The advertising on the Trident web site is a little confusing. I thought it was going to have 2 cases in one, like the previous models. In fact when I ordered the case last week, that was what the web site advertised. When I opened the package I was a little surprised that this case was only one layer with some padding glued into it. In fact the web page is still misleading because one part of the description states that it is one piece now, but the little icons indicate that the case is a two part case.3. The volume rockers do not work very well with this case. I can press hard and the button might work a little, and then I have to use the touch screen to make further adjustments. This is a little frustrating as I cannot adjust the volume levels or make adjustments through these rockers while it is in the case.4. The USB port cover could be a little wider. As it is now the flap interferes with the opening of the GS5's own cover. This is a big problem with short nails.5. The belt clip is bulky like the Defender series as well. It also doubles as a stand and the loop that goes around your belt is on the side that flips out, so if you press the case in the wrong spot while on your belt there is no positive locking onto your belt.The Good:1. I must say that I definitely like the change to the case as far as the snap fit goes. On the S3 model I went through a few front halves because the small tabs broke fairly easily. This has three pins on each side and very positive snaps on the top and bottom of the case.2. I also like the way the clip was moved to the middle. On the S3 toward the lower portion was kinda a pain in the butt and interfered with some of the mounting solutions that were available.3. The case is well made and does a great job of protecting the phone. There is enough of the silicone type material to slow sliding down, but not enough to make it difficult to carry in your pocket.

I had a TechMatte tempered glass screen protector for my HTC One M9 and it was bubble-free with a Spigen case! It's a different story with the HTC 10 full coverage glass protector. But, as others have noted, if you add a case (I use a Spingen Slim Armor) it can cause new air bubbles to form. The protector can be removed multiple times to remove bubbles, but in the end it may be impossible to be bubble-free with a case and the glass will crack if you try too hard and bend it. Don't forget to use the two lighted buttons at the bottom of the HTC 10 as well as the camera at the top to align the glass accurately. Is it worth the hassle? YMMV. Remember, even if you do get it bubble-free with your case, you might have to start all over again if you ever remove the case.

As a long time new trent owner, i had very high expectations for this battery pack, as it has a long history of predecessors to live up to. i have to say new trent not only didn't disappoint but rather surprised me with many new updates that have not been seen in any of their previous battery packs. there were a lot of similar features and a few new ones that i have truly made this pack my new favorite.i want to start off first explaining my typical phone usage. when i travel, i use my iPhone (now the 5s) for everything. just coming back from a Disney World trip, i put the phone through an amazing stress test. i run the following apps at all times: 1) my disney experience, which uses GPS at all times to find out where you are in the park, helps with reservations and reminders based on dining and attraction locations / times 2) disney touring plans, which also uses GPS and constantly talking to servers to compute wait times and optimize your trip around the parks 3) foursquare: to follow my 30 or so checkins a day 4) facebook, to keep track of my social network, as well as the usual maps, messages, phone, email camera etc. typically i will take about 500-750 photos and videos a day as well. so as you can expect, battery toll is very high. therefore i rely on backup batteries to keep me going and connected at all times. as well my day typically starts at 8:00 am and goes through 11:00 pm or later.CAPACITY:the 11,000mAh super capacity was a blessing in disguise. with previous models i found myself recharging the battery on a pretty frequent basis. with this capacity, i was able to keep the battery going over 2 days between charges. on a regular day, i would let me phone get down to under 20% about 3 times a day, so as you can see, i counted at least 5-6 full charges on the new trent even before having to recharge it. amazing. i didn't use it much for my iPad, but i am confident that it could have handled the additional load just fine.PHYSICAL SIZE:the battery was a little larger than i expected, but was still able to stick in my back pocket comfortably and keep the cable tethered to my phone in my front pocket. the weight is appropriate for the size. the photos make the battery look a little smaller than it really appears in person, but again, it wasn't outrageously large and was still very portable to carry both in my pocket or to stick it in my sling pack.PHYSICAL FEATURES:like other new trent devices there are 2 ports on the pack, clearly labeled for phone and tablet. i typically would plug the phone into the tablet slot. though it made the phone a little warm during charging, it did charge the phone up very quickly (20% to 100% in less than 2 hours or faster) with heavy use while charging. the battery itself has a good premium feel to it. the plastic used in the construction seems very sturdy and has a beautiful matte finish to it. the logo is de-bossed on the front, which helps its premium look. the design of the power remaining light is very clean and easy to see. some of their older batteries use individual LED lights that aren't very impressive. the LED strip across the front is a great addition and makes is very easy to see remaining capacity at a quick glance, not to mention that it just looks great too!POWER BUTTON:i am breaking this out separately as this has to be my FAVORITE feature of this battery. in the past, with the older battery packs, when your device hits its capacity and the battery senses that there is no more power being drained, it auto-shuts off. though this is a good feature, i find that by the time i get to my device it already had dropped 10% of battery as it was back running on its own power. this unit has a always on method of charging, much like when you plug into a wall unit. this assures that your phone / iPad / headphones / sports fitness band will remain at 100% until you are ready to pull off the tether! again, with previous battery packs, if there isn't enough power being pulled, such as from bluetooth headsets or even my nike fitness band, the battery would shut off. with the always on behavior, even the lower powered devices get a full charge now! a MAJOR PLUS PLUS PLUS!!!RECHARGING:due to the size of the unit, a full charge does take about 10 hours, but its well worth it for the amount of power you get back from the unit. in the past, the units either had a built in plug or had a separate power adapter. new trent took the leap of faith, with this device to make it use USB to charge from your computer or from a wall usb adapter. i find carrying an extra usb cord is much more convenient than some of their power adapter in the past. i would like to see a built in plug as well, but that is a minor suggestion.SUMMARY:the new trent nt110t is my favorite battery pack, hands down from all the previous models. its form factor is compact enough, the finish is premium feeling, the capacity is strong enough for 6 or more full charges and the always on behavior is an amazing addition to an already powerful solution. i would surely recommend this battery for any family traveling with more than one power hungry device.

We bought this originally for travel but have since decided it will be staying in the house (and we will likely be ordering more)- We have several charging stations in our home but they are bulky and take up a lot of counter space and do not offer two 2.0+ USB charging outputs.Pros:*Size- At about 4"x3"x1.5" it is compact enough to store with a tablet case, in a glove compartment, purse, or carry on luggage.*Finish- While it has a metallic finish, it's not the fanciest metal or material. It seems to be a sturdy plastic- which could be a con, but for me indicates that's how the manufacturer keeps this functional and affordable. I've had no cracks or scratches yet and it LOOKS slick, so I'll keep this uspdated should it cause an issue.*Design- This comes with a white charging cable that is just over 18" long and has a velcro tie back (another nice touch that makes it great for travel and portability.) The cord may seem short to you, and you wouldn't readily have another cable to swap it out and give you more length, so this could be a con. Also, I wish that the cable was black, but that's a design preference.*USB Ports: There are 4 ports- one could easily use this to charge and apple and android product at the same time- here you can see we are charging a USB bike light, iPad, and two Galaxy S4s. The ports fit snug. There is one 2.1 (top port), 2.0, and then two slower 1.0 charge ports. This indicates the speed of charge you should expect. The top 2.1 is comparable to the fastest port on your laptop, and seems to charge just a tad faster than my Samsung Galaxy S4 manufacturer charger. For those more technical 2.1 equates to about .75% per minute, or 45% per hour- the 1A port will yield .50 per minute or 30% per hour.Cons:*Cable- This is not a standard power cable so you likely won't have one laying around if you wish to extend the length, which feels a bit short to us.*Instructions- There are none- this is not really an issue with a product like this, but there's a funky tab button on the back corner we can't figure out what it's for, so some extra functionality may be lost from ignorance there.When it comes down to it, for the price, this is an excellent deal, you'll be hard pressed to find such a professional looking 4 port charger with 2 2.0+ speed ports in this price range. In addition we own a few other Poweradd products and they have really held up over time.*Design- The material looks slick but is made from more of a hard plastic, rather than metal reinforced panels.

The iZeeker VR Kit is a simple, incredibly inexpensive implementation of the Google Cardboard standard. For the uninitiated, Google Cardboard offers a low-cost method of providing a workable virtual reality (VR) platform for use with our existing Android- or iOS-based smartphones. The iZeeker offering supports smartphones up to 6 inches in size.In the package you get the cardboard enclosure with optics and a simple velcro head strap, as well as some optional padding. Since the viewer is open on the sides, you can easily plug in headphones. As usual, my test platform was my Apple iPhone 6 Plus, and the Google Cardboard app, as well as the Vrse app. Unlike several of the much more expensive headsets I tried, when you run the Google Cardboard app and scan the QR code on the enclosure (there's also NFC support via an included sticker for Android devices with that feature), the iZeeker is immediately recognized as a 2015 Google Cardboard compliant device. In fact, perhaps even more importantly versus those other headsets, which seem to put most of their R&D into their plastic enclosures, this has a pseudo-button that is somehow recognized by the VR apps. In other words, for just $12.99, you get 100% functionality. Incredible.Although it took me a few minutes to figure out how to insert and enclose my phone, once I did, it fit just fine with my bulky Rokform case. So not having to remove my case is yet another plus versus those other headsets that cost many times what this does.If you're looking for a Google Cardboard device, look no further than this. In fact, if you want to save even more money, you can get the Yellow version for $1 less. Perfect.Thanks to iZEEKER DIRECT for the discounted review unit.

iBlazr 2s marketing video shows that the flash is timed via bluetooth to fire when the iphone camera takes a picture.  In actuality, the iblazr is on a delay and always fires at the same time.  If you use image stabilization, which takes the photo only when the iphone is steady, then it rarely times the flash and the photo shutter at the same time.There is the option to leave the light on.  In which case you just paid $59.99 for a light that doesnt work as a flash and will continue to shine in your subjects eye.  Keeping the LED on the entire time then makes the Bluetooth feature a moot point and unnecessary.Their support team said, As to returning - due to a Concepter warranty and returning policy, we replace and accept returning on units ordered only via [...] and the other resellers are obliged to replace and accept return by their own.They gave no attention to what I described and asked for a video showing the problem.  I said I would help them by posting a product review on youtube.  That way people will be fully informed on what to expect.  The customer service rep accused me of threatening them and trying to shame them through revenge.  Bottom line, they need to improve their product, tweak their commercial so it doesnt make it seem like it completely synchronizes with the camera shutter, and lastly, work on their customer service skills.

If you're like me and you're kind of old school in how you like to carry your phone on your hip or on the outside of your pocket, rather than in your pocket where coils, lint, dirt, keys or anything else can scratch it up, then this is the thing for you. The first thing I tried when I got this was to see how wide the holder actually is and how thick of a phone you could plop in there with a case on it. I have an iPhone 6 with a battery case on it, so it's a pretty thick phone in total with a good bit of weight on it. I opened the clasp up from the magnet inside it, which I was very surprised because the magnet in the locking mechanism on this is actually pretty strong to keep itself clasped. I stuck my phone in there with case and all and it had absolutely no issues accommodating my phone and the case with a little bit of room to spare. The elastic band on the sides of it gives it a little bit of room to flex, to keep it small enough for thinner phones and flex enough for thicker phones. I don't know if you could get an iphone 6 plus in there, but I kind of feel like you would have enough room for one. I don't own one otherwise I would just simply test it. The lining inside isn't soft, but it isn't something you would feel like it would do any damage to your phone sliding in and out of the holster. I really like the fact that it has the belt loops on it, and also the hook / clip on it to add a little versatility to where you would like to carry it on your body. It's very simple, very stylish, and very attractive in all of it's subtlety. I really enjoyed using this holster for my phone. You can easy slide your hand and phone in and out of it. This is a pretty conventional power bank in terms of features. If offers 2 USB output ports (0.6A and 2.0A) an integrated LED flashlight and a charge level "gas gauge." It offers a total of 8,400mAh capacity.What sets it apart from other power banks is its style - it is very nice looking! It's a high gloss black with a white accent strip on the side. The corners and sides are rounded, so it feel nice to carry and slips easily into a pocket. The charge level indicator consists of 4 blue circles on the front. It's probably the best-looking charge level indicator I've seen so far.The size is reasonable for its capacity - it weighs 6.5 ounces, and measures roughly 4 inches long by 2.5 inches wide. It's somewhat thick (3/4 inch).The build quality is good -- very solid feeling. The base is flat so it will stand up on edge securely. The two USB output ports are firmly attached with no wiggling even when I stressed the attached cables. The micro USB input for charging is also securely fastened.It comes with the usual variety of adapters and connectors so it can be used with pretty much everything. It also includes a wall adapter plug and a decent carry pouch.It does not have an auto-on feature. You have to push the power on button when you want the attached device to start charging.Given that the output is limited to 2A, this is not a good choice for tablet charging. But, given the 8,400mAh capacity, it really is more geared to phone and small device use rather than tablet charging. So, I won't hold that against it. Note that while the product listing states "2.6A output" that referrs to the combined total output -- one 2A outlet and 1 0.6A outlet. The max it can put out to a connected device is 2A.What I will hold against it is, ironically, related to one of it's major pluses - appearance. While the glossy black looks great, it's also a major fingerprint magnet. And I mean major! While the gloss looks great when it's clean, it's not nearly as good looking when it's all smudged up (which happens quickly). A matte finish would have been a better option.I'll nick this a half-star for the fingerprint attraction feature, but will round the overall rating up to 5 stars since it does everything else very well. Well worth considering if you're looking for a decent capacity power bank for phone use that is well-built and looks nice. It's not the one to get, however, if you have a major problem with fingerprint smudges.[sample provided for review]

If you have a fast wireless charge capable phone (currently all of which are manufactured by Samsung) like the Galaxy S6 Edge+, Note 5 , Galaxy S7/S7 Edge , Note 7 then you would definitely want to invest out a fast wireless charger such as this Nanami Qi compatible fast wireless charger which is significantly cheaper than the OEM charging mat that Samsung offersFast Wireless vs Standard Wireless Charging-------------------------------------------------------Wireless charging certainly has its appeal being able to charging your phone simply by placing it on a stand/mat without worrying about cables. However practical usefulness the standard Qi 1st gen chargers (which is still the current standard BTW) is hampered greatly by the painfully slow charging speeds as regular Wireless chargers are ususally only capable of outputting 5 watts (5V @ 1 A) to your phone when they are capable of as high as 16 Watts (Quick Charge compatible phones) . To remedy this Samsung introduced the Fast wireless charging technology which when connected to a compatible Quick Charge/Fast Charge adapter is able to output almost double the power at 10+ Watts wirelessly . This turns out to be 40% quicker than standard wireless chargers and should be able to fully juice up your phone in about 1.5 hoursDesign---------- The Nanami wireless charger is of the desktop stand type , which In my opinion is better than mat type wireless chargers as you can acutally use your phone for looking at notifications or watching videos while charging without having to lift your phone- The charger has a soft foam padding at the bottom where you phone rests to prevent scratches. There are anti skid pads at the bottom as well- There is a soft glow LED indicator which momentarily turns blue when you connect the charger and then goes off. It glows green while its charging/fast charging.Idle Power Consumption-------------------------------An important factor for wireless chargers is that they consume some energy even when they are not used . I used a KillAWatt P4400 power meter to measure the idle consumption of this Wireless charger (see my video)Idle Power Consumption under no load = 0.4 WattsApprox Annual Energy costs = $0.50 / Yearly (when idle)Using it------------- My Samsung Galaxy S7 Edge had no issues identifying it as a Fast Wireless charger and it was able to fully charge my phone from 10% to 100% in about 1.5 hours- Because of the desktop stand design , its almost impossible to place the phone incorrectly on the charger and it works every time both in landscape & portrait orientation- Even with extended fast charging the charger does not get hot which is critical to the long life of your phone's batteryThe Good-------------- Great OEM like fast wireless charging performance at half the price. Its able to fully charge my S7 Edge in about 1.5 hours- Runs cool , does not generate a lot of heat while charging- Desktop format means you can use your phone without interrupting charging when compared to mad/pad type chargers- Not too bad on idle power consumption either- It is well balanced and supported, it does not tip over when the phone is placed in portrait modeThe Not so good-------------------- The only criticism I can levy against this charger is that I personally didn't like the big bold 'FAST CHARGE' text on the front face, to me it makes it look a little bit less elegantFinal Thoughts--------------------If you are looking for a fast, inexpensive wireless charger I can definitely recommend the Nanami wireless charger is it provides OEM like performance which does not run very hot , a better form factor ( desktop stand vs mat) at about half the price of the OEM offering. Just remember that to get fast charging , you need to conect it to a fast or a Qualcomm Quick Charge charger .Disclosure : Review sample provided by seller at no cost . Ting is an MVNO. Simply put that means they resell services off major provider networks, in this case Sprint and T-mobile. You can use Sprint or T-mobile devices. If you're looking at this SIM then you are most likely thinking T-mobile which I think is a wiser choice. Sprint changed something recently that makes it harder to get used Sprint devices onto Ting. T-mobile is nearly a sure thing. Regardless, when looking at a device wether used or new make sure to check with Ting if it will be compatible. Or simply buy from their store.The benefit of Ting is MUCH lower rates for server, better customer service, wider range of devices and networks, no contracts, tethering on any device, pay for what you use. Since there are no contracts you pay for your device up front. This means something used from eBay/Glyde/Amazon or pay full price for a new device. I'm using a OnePlusOne and my wife has a Galaxy S4.I've been a customer since 2012. Ting's customer service is better than all the major carriers, and better than most other companies even outside cell service. My bill averages $40/month for 2 smartphones. My wife and I use wifi as much as possible and Google hangouts for text and calls. A couple times a year we get the bill down to $30, especially when I'm not traveling and don't need data as much. Our largest bill was $65. With this card you can use T-mobile's network which I have switched over to, my wife is still on Sprint. There are spots where I can 5 bars and she gets none and vice versa so the provider is hit or miss depending on where you live. And both Sprint and T-mobile have horrible networks outside of metro areas. I bought a Verizon pre-paid phone for anytime I'm somewhere rural like on a road trip. Just something to keep in mind but that's true regardless of the carrier. Verizon just has a larger stronger network.If you are already with Sprint or T-mobile there is no reason you shouldn't switch. You can even bring your existing device. Even if it costs money to break your contract you will still save all of that in the long run.
Would recommend to anyone, great product and fast shipping.

I was shopping for a phone for my father in law to replace his old fashioned flip phone but I didn't want to spend a lot of money and I ran across this phone by coincidence.  I was looking for something that was a good starter phone in the smart phone arena that had similar specs as the samsung galaxy light which is a very good branded starter phone.  I had purchased one for my mom and I played with that phone and was very impressed with the android jelly-bean platform as it compared to the apple ios.  The only problem with that phone was that it was pricey to purchase out right at $240 for someone that would never use all of the smart phone features.  However, it was a good gamble and my mom is now crossed over into the 21st century.  But with regard to my father in law, I wanted something equally impressive but pay less, so i was looking for similar specs.  Now I was skeptical of BLU because most of the off-brand android stuff that has been put out there is usually junk so I wanted to see this before I bought it.  I  noticed that the local tigerdirect had them in stock so i went out to look at it.  Now this phone is not out on display so you have to ask for it and they will pull it out for you.  Also, with any purchase from Tigerdirect, once you buy the phone, you cannot return it.  So lots of gambles here.  But since the price was about the same as on Amazon, i went ahead and purchased it for about $120.  This phone is everything it says it is and more.  It has the same look and feel as the samsung with some pretty impressive bells and whistles.  The only thing I find that it lacks is screen resolution but this is how they keep the phone down in price.  So long as you aren't looking for a retina display to watch movies, this phone has all of the features you would want int a phone.  it is fast, large screen, good operating system, and overall a good starter phone in the android arena.  For the price, it is fantastic.  The dual sim slots are bonus for those that want to travel abroad or simply have a work phone and home phone in one unit.  It comes with a case and a screen protector which only adds to the value.  The sim card is a standard sim and not one of the micro sims so it is was easy to take the old sim from the flip phone and just install it in the BLU phone.
This product performed the way it was designed.  It met my expectations and is a great product.  I am very satisfied.

The other day I accidentally dropped my ZX2 on the floor and surprisingly, when I took it out to inspect it, it was completely undamaged. It slides into the case easily snapping in and remains on, holding tight once inserted. It adds minimal bulk to an already large and heavy digital music player and is almost unnoticeable. When the ZX2 is lying flat, screen side up, you can see that the case adds an extra 1.5mm rim surrounding the large screen on three sides and then running across the bottom of the ZX2. The case also adds additional protection to the earphone jack by surrounding it with about a millimeter of rubber and covers all four corners top and bottom.So why four stars? The case covers the buttons on the right of the player and although it does have buttons on the case which overly the ones on the ZX2, they are difficult to depress requiring a good deal of extra force (especially at the top power button which restores the screen) to get them to work. As I placed the ZX2 into the case as soon as I got it, I initially thought that the hard pressing of the buttons was a design flaw but recently, I had it out of the case to add additional music files to the micro SD card and discovered that they require minimal force making it much easier to use. As I also have an Astell & Kern AK120 which also has transport buttons on the side, I was used to the ease required to get them to function. The Sony ZX2 doesn't have a volume knob as does the AK120, so whenever you want to adjust the volume you have to press the aforementioned buttons which are less responsive with the case on.That said, for $10 you definitely get your money's worth, protection for your ZX2 with minimal bulk, I just wish there was a way to make the buttons easier to push.

I wanted a dock to make my life a bit easier when working out of my office and listening to my iPhones iTunes library through by studio speakers.I did not need it so much for the ability to charge my phone, but I liked that it had this feature also.So here is what I've come to find...I tried to hook the dock's audio out port to my speaker system, and to my surprise, it did not work!Just for the hell of it I plugged the power connection port into an outlet to see if this would make it work. Yup! This dock needs the power connected for it to output the audio through the unit. Once you plug the power in you can then unplug it and the audio will still come through the unit (until you disconnect your iPhone of course).This being said, I winded up leaving the power connected at all times.The dock seems to charge my phone pretty slow. I think this may have to do with the fact that my screen does not go to sleep when I connect it to this dock unless I force the screen off by pressing the power button. But even when I do this, it charges slower than usual.Once the connection is made and the audio is being sent through the Dock you CAN NOT adjust the volume! It automatically is sent through at 100% volume. It does not matter if you had the volume on the phone set at 40%, it will still come through at 100%. There is no volume adjuster on the unit itself and pressing the iPhone's volume buttons does nothing at all when connected to the dock. This was a bummer since the volume adjuster on my speakers are difficult to adjust (behind each individual speaker on my studio desk which I can not move).My work around for this was to use Monster 123872-00 iSplitter Shared Headphone Adapter.Now I have it working the way I originally intended, however, the unit itself is so light in weight that you have to hold it down when removing your iPhone from it. I am going to install some Velcro strips to get this dock to stay down on the desk. If that doesn't work I will liquid nail the sucker down. lol.This Dock worked with both my iPhone 3GS and my iPhone 4.I selected this dock because of all the bad reviews posted on the other docks, but I am guessing this is pretty much in the same boat as the others. I am surprised there is not a decent iPhone Dock out there without so many gripes.Another thing to mention is using this dock with a phone case. It worked fine with my iPhone 4 in its case, however, it would not fit using my 3GS with my Incase CL59055 Slider Case - Black. I would have to slide the bottom part of the case off for it to fit into the dock. I fortunately have another case for my 3GS that fits into the dock. I like this case as well and felt like it was time for a change anyway.All in all, you get what you pay for and it does work with some minor work around depending on what you want to use it for.

When I was looking at cases for my Xperia Play, there were many choices. At least a couple of them looked remarkably similar. One was priced at 75 cents. The other one (the one I bought) was $4.48. I really only wanted a simple black case, nothing fancy. So, I ordered the higher priced one thinking that it would possibly be a little better quality. Come to find out that it's the EXACT same case that was for sale for 75 cents. AND, when I came back on here to write this review, I found that the price for the one I bought has now "suddenly" dropped to $1.45. What's up with that? Seems like shady marketing to me.As far as the case itself goes, like I said, it's a simple black polymer case, no fancy design. It seems a little rigid to me and I can imagine the side clips breaking off at some point. The question is when. It fits well, and doesn't seem to hamper the function of any of the buttons or plugs. The game-pad still slides out easily and doesn't get "jammed" or anything. The hot keys below the screen are a little more difficult to push now because the top part of the case sits so close to them. But it's not too bad, I think I can live with that. It does add a little bulk to the phone but really not that much. I have big hands anyway so that's a plus for me. It also makes the phone much less "slippery", also a plus.Overall, I would recommend this case to others, depending on what price you end up paying for it. Do your homework before ordering it. You might be able to save yourself a couple bucks.

With tablets using USB power sources, along with some newer laptops, low amperage phone-style chargers are no longer sufficient.  In some cases, using a device with it plugged in won't even result in breaking even - the device is losing power faster than gaining it.  Modern USB chargers generally address this issue.  In this case, TopG designed the ports to output 2.1A, 2A, and 1A.  That is enough to charge a couple larger devices and a phone. Some chargers can output higher amperage to a given device, which is worth considering if you only need to charge one thing.I wouldn't normally consider a car charger sleek, but this one is.  The metal accents and bold red paint make it fit well with a car's aesthetic rather than the standard utilitarian block of plastic.  It is one that I wouldn't mind leaving plugged in all the time, and the light isn't too blinding.In short, a charger that looks good and is capable of charging relatively large devices.Thank you to TopG for the review unit.

I found this to be a great case for those looking for a good amount of protection with little bulk. These two-part cases are the types that I usually recommend to family and friends who tend to drop things a lot. Its a great compromise between soft-shell shock/drop protection and hard shell rigidity and durability. My previous go-to recommendation for the iPhone 4/4s was the Incipio Silicrylic. The version for the 5/5s started to go downhill in quality slightly in my opinion. I think elago did a good job with this case and its my current recommendation for a good shock absorbing case. I do feel like there are a few areas which could be improved upon though.I tend to really like elago designs because of the simplicity and cleanliness. They seem to try to keep bulk at a minimum while still giving the most protection possible. This case is no different. The design is smooth and attractive and keeps inline with Apple's style. The materials are high quality with the rubber portion being not too hard but not soft and floppy either. The hard shell is thin and fits perfectly within the soft portion. One thing on the materials which could be improved upon is the flashing (the extra material which often creates a line where the molds meet). Mine seems to be a little sharp in some areas, most noticeable  on the bottom of the case near the lightning port. The reason its so noticeable for me is because I tend to rest my the bottom of my phone on my pinky and any time I adjust the position I feel it on my finger. Its possible its just my sample but its something Id recommend elago keep an eye on. If you already have one where this gets a little sharp just take an exacto blade and run it down the line to smooth things out.The buttons are nice and responsive but do take some getting used to. Reason being that traditionally you press the bottom from all the way on the side of the phone. The buttons on this case kinda lean forward a bit and are more responsive a little more toward the front. Im not entirely sure what the reasoning is for this. It could just be style and creative design more than for a specific functionality advantage. When I first put the case on though I might be bothered by it, but after a few days its become the norm. Im still not sure that Id say I PREFER this style for functionality but its not necessarily a hindrance either.The ports are pretty well protected and I do like that this phone will fit on most docks with ease. Unfortunately my custom-made car dock is not one of them because the bottom of this case is actually a little on the thick side. But my sony and iHome docks have no issues. There also should be no issues what-so-ever with third party cables. That being said. The headphone jack might be just a little tight for some larger headphone jacks or jacks with a 90 degree elbow. This is mainly due to the amount of protective material on the bottom and not necessary the size of the opening. It is something to be aware of though if you have some headphones with a massive jack.Overall, I think this is a good case and one I have no trouble recommending. The caveats I pointed out definitely won't effect everybody and the flashing issue could have just been my sample. All of this being said, I don't drop my phone a whole lot so Ill actually be going back to my Elago Glide. That one fits my dock in my car and is just about the right amount of protection for me.Pros:Soft and hard case for protection and durabilityClean smooth designAdds little bulkLip on front protects screenFully open camera portCons:Buttons might not be preferred for someMaterial a little thick on the bottom which might affect some headphones or docksFlashing a little sharp on the bottom of my sampleThis product was provided to me for evaluation purposes. I am under no obligation with the manufacturer or seller and my reviews are honest and unbiased.

I LOVE THIS PHONE. I have been eagerly waiting for this day. After trying out and loving the Note 4, but then learning about this and returning the Note 4 and using a crappy feature phone while waiting for the Note Edge to arrive, I can finally say it was worth the wait.I've only had a little time to use this, so this is just my general overview. For technical specifications and professional reviews, there is always "Google it."To make this review as efficient as possible, I will do a quick run-over, and also a comparison with other phones you might be most interested in comparing it to, especially the Note 3 that it replaces, the iPhone 6 Plus, the LG G3 since it shares more than a few similarities, and of course, the Note 4.Physical:* The Note Edge is basically a Note 4, except for that edge portion - if you've held the Note 4 before, then you'll feel right at home. The Note Edge is just a smidge shorter, wider, thinner, and lighter(a little of this is because the Note Edge has a 3,000mAh battery vs 3,200mAh for the Note 4). The specs are: 151.3 x 82.4 x 8.3 mm / 174 g for the Note Edge.First of all, in the grand scheme of things, Samsung has finally paid attention to its best aesthetic competitor HTC, and followed suit with a real aluminum metal chamfered edges, and not the fake aluminum of the Note 3. It looks great. View it from the top and bottom edges, and you'll see an even more striking design change and upgrade. View it from the bottom edge up especially and you'll see what I mean. Here is the first Samsung with truly beautiful edges - I would have never thought I would say that!Comparing it with each other, the Note Edge definitely gets the nod in terms of the style and sleekness - people have already asked me what phone I had as I went into the AT&T store to get my SIM card initialized. For sure it will be a great conversation piece and attention grabber!*The very next thing you'll notice once you turn it on is the screen. the LG G3 was the first QHD screen for America from a big company, but I can say that Samsung's AMOLED has come a long way, and is now the best on the market. The color gamut, saturation, contrast and black levels have always been great, but Samsung has also come a long way in terms of color reproduction, and especially making the screen still looking great in direct sunlight - it's only been two years since the Note 2, but Samsung has made amazing progress. QHD means 2560 x 1440 resolution for a 5.6" screen means 525 ppi, and Samsung's is the brightest, clearest, and best screen on the planet right now. The "Edge" screen adds another 160 pixels to the side(160 x 1440).*Other notable improvements are:- Processor: upgraded Exynos and Snapdragon processors depending on where you buy it, upgrade GPU for you gaming aficionados. The Note 3 was one of the fastest and most powerful for its time, and is still one of the fastest phones right now(!), and yet the Note 4 is even faster! Swiping through screens, switching between apps, running a dozen apps, playing processing-intensive games, they all feel like a breeze.- Upgraded cameras: 16MP on the back, 3.7MP on the front(instead of 13MP and 2MP). You can now record video in 4K mode!- The pen/stylus is now made by Wacom, the famous stylus maker. Hardware and software improvements have made it more usable than ever.- Samsung got rid of the USB 3 charging port of the Note 3 in favor of the more universal mini-USB 2, but improvements have made it possible to charge the battery from 0%-50% in 30 minutes. The battery is 3220mAh, up from 3200 in the Note 3. Both will get you through a whole day of moderate usage with a bunch to spare. I've never had it lower than 20% remaining using it from 10am-10pm with moderate to heavy usage. What you should know if you didn't already is that the screen uses the most battery. Keep leaving it on and it will drain fast, especially if you're playing a game. Other big battery hogs are leaving your GPS radio on - even if you are not actively using it - and apps that continually access your phone - like if you set your email to instant retrieval instead of every few hours.By itself, this phone is a beauty and the beast in many senses of the word. But how does it compare with the other phones, and is it a worthy buy or upgrade?In comparison with the iPhone 6 Plus:If you want a giant phone, the iPhone 6 Plus is a very intriguing option. If you're an Apple person, you're going to get this phone, that's just the truth. It has a beautiful 1080p screen(a very first for Apple), it's fast, and finally Apple has a phone that all Apple lovers who have been craving for a huge phone to have. The Apple ecosystem is still superior in terms of apps and userability. On the other hand, if you like Samsung, then you should absolutely get the Note 4. Samsung now has an almost perfect big phone, and each phone from now on will only get better. You will never regret getting this phone. If you're in the neutral, then it depends on your lifestyle. If you just want a big phone for everyday use, I personally think it's easier to use Apple phones. Everything is just right there - the learning curve is not very steep(until you get into iTunes and want to export stuff, but that's another issue). But if you want more control over your phone and you're more tech savvy, there is nothing better than the Android ecosystem - it has the best of all worlds, and for those more tech savvy, it's actually easier to use than Apple phones. As a user of both ecosystems, I can attest to this personally.In comparison with the LG G3:I wanted to include the G3 because both share the QHD-resolution screens. But the G3 is more compact, with a slightly smaller 5.5" screen. But because its bezels are so amazingly thin, it feels a lot more compact(narrower) in the hand than the Note Edge. So if you still want a big phone, but feel the Note phones were just a tad too big, then the G3 has that perfect combination. It also has one of the best screens on the market, in LCD form, has a beautiful design, and the LG quality.In comparison with the Note 4:Personally for me, there is no comparison - I'm keeping my Note Edge as my main phone. It has a smaller 5.6" screen, but that curve and design makes it oh so unique - it's the most unique and best-looking phone for me by far. Some will complain that with that form factor it will be very hard to have a case that can fully protect it, but so I will just be extra careful with it. Cases(both tight-wrapped and "folio" styles) have already came out for this phone.In comparison with the Note 3:This might be most important comparison, because you own a Note 3 and you want to upgrade to the next Note. Moving from the Note 2 to Note 3 was easy for me, because my Note 2 had performance issues that my Note 3 doesn't(it would slow to a crawl when too many apps are installed/used, and when your text history is too large texting crawls to a stand still), plus the 1080p screen on the Note 3 is 10x better than the 720p Note 2's, not in just resolution, but brightness, color reproduction, sun-light legibility, etc. The differences from the Note 3 to the Note 4 is less pronounced, since at this size QHD over FHD resolution is not so pronounced - although if you put them side by side you will notice the difference.My advice is, if you want something similar to the Note 3, then get the Note 4. If you want something almost totally unique, then definitely get the Note Edge. Or just wait until the Note 5 next year - the grape vine already has it pegged as having a 5.9" 4K screen(2,160 x 3,840 resolution).For me, the Note Edge is the most unique-looking phone in many years, even more so than the LG Flex and Samsung Galaxy Round. With its powerful hardware, and futuristic stylish looks, it will last you for years down the road and make you less inclined to upgrade your phone next year or the year after, and that in itself is another plus - it will save you money!DEFINITELY RECOMMENDED!***Please note(no pun intended), that with these international versions, you might not get the best LTE/4G speeds, because that more depends on the country where you live/travel to and their telecom providers than the phone itself - although the Note Edge has one of the most complete sets of radio bands of any phone out there. Check the specs, and then research the telecom providers of your country/country of visit.

I've purchased this case ahead of the iPhone 6 release to get an idea of the size of the device. I will be updating this review as soon as I get my hands on the iPhone.I've played with Notes and LG G3s before but it's just not the same thing. I even printed the cutouts that were floating around the Internet with the iPhone's dimensions (you can find that here http://ios.wonderhowto.com/how-to/which-iphone-6-size-is-best-for-you-use-our-printable-cutouts-find-out-0157311/)The iPhone 6 Plus is only 7.1mm thick and weighs only 172g. Where as the LG G3 is 8.9mm thick and weighs 149g and the latest Samsung Galaxy Note 4 is 8.5mm thick and weighs about 176g.It's truly amazing for Apple to design such a thin and lightweight phone with an all aluminum frame, and did I mention it's only 7mm thick? That is insane! It allows for many case makers to design nicer looking things without adding too much bulk (although I'm sure OtterBox and Lifeproof will find a way to make the phone look like a brick).I'm not expecting something out of the ordinary with this case, after all it was only $11. But at least it'll help me decide whether the iPhone 6 Plus with its 5.5 inch display is right for me.I will be getting one of the Apple Leather cases as well, but they go around for $49, ouch (and the shipping date already slipped 3 weeks)************************UPDATE #1 9/13/14**********************The case just arrived! I bought the Satin Silver version and it truly does look like the one in the picture. The color is not off by any means.It's very thin and the texture isn't grippy, like at all, but it doesn't feel like it's gonna slip outta my hands either.The buttons and camera cutouts look great, unlike some Chinese cases I've seen on eBay.There's also a little transparent film covering the round area where the Apple logo will be, it's a nice touch and it'll prevent the aluminum from scratching.Now comes the waiting game.The expected delivery date of the iPhone 6 Plus is the 19th, so I can't really comment right now on how it'll look with the phone inside.I just hope this case allows me to use third party headsets with large 3.5mm jacks (like Audio Technica and Beyerdynamic headphones). So I'll definitely update this review when the phone arrives.Overall it seems like a great temporary case, and I plan to use it at least until Belkin or Incipio launch their new cases.I'm not disgusted by it and it doesn't feel like a cheap $.99 case. So good job Spigen!

First off, you need to understand that PU leather is polyurethane over a thin leather backing, so don't expect this to be solid leather. That's ok, the material is durable, and for the price, quite high quality. This really is a great case. It comes with a stylus, a screen cleaning cloth, and a screen protector. Personally, I would not use a plastic screen protector over hardened glass, but it is there for folks who prefer to do so. The case is flexible, can stand up vertically and horizontally (for watching movies), and holds a couple of cards as well as your i.d. Not only that, but you can, indeed, slip in a couple of folded bills behind the cards for those times you need real cash. The cover is held in place by a rare earth magnet which works really well and keeps your phone protected. Still another reason not to use the plastic screen protector, but that's just me...I wondered whether you could hear phone calls with the solid face of the cover closed over the phone, and was pleasantly surprised when I found that the sound comes through just fine. Worst case you might turn the volume up a notch or two, but in my case, I found that unnecessary. Overall, the product fits my iPhone 5 just fine, protects it very well, and looks very impressive. I really like this particular case, and I still have it on my phone even though I have dozens of other cases to choose from.Disclosure: I was provided a sample in exchange for an honest review

This review should taken with a grain of salt.  I've used iLLumishield screen protectors before and haven't had more than the usual issues I have with any brand of screen protectors.  In general I feel they are good and have good products.  This product was a bit different and I think it is in large part due to the device.This comes with 3 clear protectors for the top and bottom screens of the New 3DS XL as well as a wipe cloth and a card for getting rid of bubbles, standard stuff but it was nice that it included 3 of each.  Applying the bottom protector was fairly normal and easy.  The cutouts were done well enough and logistically I can see why the protector doesn't cover the screen from edge to edge considering that the screen is depressed in the system a bit.  Also, the actual display doesn't fit the whole screen anyways so the protector covers the entire used area.Applying the top protector was another story.  I ended up using all three protectors and even my final install was not where I want it to be.  There is a fair amount of dust and for a few different reasons.  Let me just say that I've done about 40-50 screen protector installs on phones, tablets, watches, portable game systems, etc...  I want virtually no dust and I will tolerate no bubbles.  In my years of screen protector installs I have gotten to a point where using the steamy bathroom method and dust removal stickers, I can do near perfect installs every time.  This protector is fairly thick and I am fine with that fact because I don't require this to be a thin protector.  Also, due to the thickness, it is easier to keep out and get rid of air bubbles and the piece or dust or two don't create air bubbles as much.Applying the protector was easy at first as it always is, however, when you get to the point where you have to remove the top layer covering the protector is where I repeatedly ran into issues.  It does not remove from the protector easily and when you do finally get it to peel, it lifts the protector and sucks in dust and causes air bubbles.  Fine, you can just use tape to peel back the protector and use dust tape to remove the dust and reapply, but this is where it gets tricky.  The screen protector is harder than normal to peel back, especially because the screen is depressed a bit into the system.  This makes fixing the problems very problematic and on my first install, cause me to bend the protector where it would not lay flat again.Once the protector is on, it is very hard to peel back and this makes it very hard to correct the mistakes that come about from the top layer over the protector being also difficult to remove.  With any dust or issues, the 3D screen can make these errors very visible and unacceptable for my preferences.  Basically, the inset screen, the difficulty to remove the top layer, the 3D nature of the screen, and the difficulty to peel back the protector to fix the install make this a very mixed recommendation.I have very high standards for screen protector installs because I am OCD and by the third install of the top protector, I am not totally satisfied.  I got it to a point I can live with.  The bottom screen looks great and is protected but the top screen has some dust that is still a bit visible when using the 3D screen.The fact that this comes with 3 sets for each screen, is reasonably priced, and ideas of perfect installs may vary, I can recommend this if you are extremely patient and just want protection for your screen.  I haven't tried other protectors for the New 3DS XL so I can't compare it to others but just be clear on the issues so you can make efforts to avoid them if possible.  My photos show the alignment and cut of the protectors and you might be able to see some imperfections and dust.

I like this cable compared to other ones mainly because of its lighting-up with charge status indicator. It's awesome to have, especially in the car (because daylight is nonexistent at night) so that I know where my phone is and how the charging is coming along in the car. Great for anyone who charges their phone in the car... or even just at home when you want to add a little bit more to the daily charging routine!What I do have to say about it, though, is that the charging can be very finicky when it comes to smartphones. Smartphones are getting more complex so much that charging could be more complex than simply plugging in and leaving it alone expecting a full charge. My Nexus 5 does have some background processes I know of and I try to keep it in light, but, at times, red (charging) lights would flicker along with the blue light in the background. The red stays on when it's charging, as supposed, but also when the screen is on. I'll make it clear now that the device is charging, but I know people will probably lower their review because their phone goes back into charging when the screen is on and the device is in use. It would be nice if a disclaimer came along with the device that says something about charging.That being said, particularly for Android users, don't expect "AC" on "USB." It's still USB, but you're getting power more than a standard USB cable. It's still not AC. Remember that.Otherwise, I like this cable with an awesome design!

First, reality check:  Kevlar and gorilla glass do not make the Razr indestructable.  However, being super thin, the Razr can wear some beefy protection and not feel like a brick in your hand.  The Commuter does it for me.  The interior silicone wrap serves as a bumper and shock absorber, while the hard case makes an already sturdy phone feel rock solid.  With the commuter case, the Razr becomes about as thick as a naked Droid X or Bionic.  I also find the phone stays in my pockets a little better with the case on--without, it is so thin and slippery, it can slide out of a pocket if you sit the wrong way .All the ports are covered but easily accessible by silicon flaps that tuck into the hard shell, except the SD/SIM card slot.  This to me is the one down side--you have to removve the case to access the SD card.  If this fixed, 5  stars easy. The power and volume rockers are completely covered, yet still easy to press.  The screen side has a silicon edge around it, making it impossible to lay the glass down on a flat surface.  This screen bumper is far enough back to not interfere with the touch screen itself, so you get protection without messing with functionality.Overall, great protection that does not look like Fort Knox on a phone, yet still keeps your Razr safe.

Comfort: My main problem with this headset was that it is not very comfortable on my ear.  When I first got it, I couldn't wear it for more than 5-10 minutes.  Now that my ear has become accustomed to it, I can wear it for an hour or so.  It just takes getting used to.  However, I wish I could rotate the headset without rotating the earhook like some bluetooth headsets.  In order to have the headset have a tight grip on my ear (and not fall off), I have to rotate the headset so that it points upwards towards my eyebrows instead of downwards towards my mouth.  It's not really an inconvenience per se, but it looks pretty uncool.  Perhaps I have misshapen ears.Audio: The sound is fantastic.  Only sometimes does it get staticky in my pocket, and not often enough to notice, at least on my end.Ease-of-Use:  This headset was easy to pair with my Motorola KRZR and the main things I do (hanging up, answering calls, and adjusting volume) are simple and intuitive.  Only problem is that if you don't hold buttons for the correct length of time, it can perform the wrong function.  For example, pressing the call button once activates voice dialing, for 1 second redials your last call and 3 seconds turns it off.  I've done my share of accidentally redialing.Battery: I was disappointed when I learned the battery wasn't rechargeable and it instead uses a AAA battery, but the lifetime is very long.  It's supposedly 30 hours or so.  I can't verify that, I just know I've been using it for two months now without replacing it, and it's just a regular Energizer battery.Durability: I carry this around in my jacket pocket most of the time and it holds up well.  I do find myself accidentally turning it on when I lean against something, but I just need to be more careful.

After all the cases I have tried lately I wasn't expecting much out of this one. When it arrived I was surprised to see it was better quality than I had assumed. For the price, it's everything you could ask for. It has two credit card slots (I was slightly disappointed since there is room for more) and an area just behind that to slide cash, receipts, etc. The closure is magnetic but it doesn't turn the phone off like some covers do once closed. Again this is all reflected in the price point here. The phone is held inside a soft plastic case attached to the wallet. It's definitely not silicone but some soft plastic that will provide adequate shock absorption. All buttons are properly exposed and charger and headphone cutouts match up. The camera initially appeared to be covered just a tad by looking but once I tested it out with a photo there was no sign of camera obstruction. The color I chose is a true tangerine on the outside with fuchsia inside and bright pink plastic case the phone goes into. Nice colors.Though I am unsure how much wear and tear this case can take I am going to continue using it and update once I have had more time to really see how it reacts with everyday use.The only negative I have is the same with every item I've tested lately: flimsy lanyards. The piece connecting that to the wallet is not much thicker than nylon kite string. It could quite possibly be thinner than nylon kite string. What is up with these cheap unsecured looking lanyards? I don't feel safe dangling a $900 phone from a nylon string. That's all it is. I wish companies making cases and phone covers would realize the importance in lanyards being tough and durable. A case is only as good as the lanyard it dangles from. So please - remember that.I received my case at a slight discount from its current price. In return I am providing my honest review. All in all it would have been 5 star but then came the flimsy lanyard I have had issues with in the past. I would still recommend this to people. I do however advise finding a better lanyard.

Meh. I was a fan of my Otter Box case for my HTC Evo 4G LTE a few years ago, but this case isn't awesome. There is a big enough gap between the "window" of the front of the case around where the 3 "physical" buttons are than quite a bit of lint, dust and dirt get trapped between the phone screen and the "front shield window" of the case. As a result, the case "clear window" gets distorted and "un-smooth" very quickly. Also, any mositure at all tends to sneak into the same gap between the case window and the screen itself, so there are tiny "puddles" of moisture that often render certain areas unresponsive to touch, nearly forcing you to take the case apart and wipe the screen and case window clean whenever it rains, or "sweats" (like at the beach, or in a mist of any kind).As with all OtterBox cases I've used now, its very challenging to pop off the case open and clean the phone screen and case window. Also noteworthy, the tiny little "clips" that hold the front and back pieces of plastic casing together beneath the rubberized "wrap" are very brittle after awhile, and crack and break, so there are tiny plastic pieces "floating" inside the rubber covering, reducing its resistance to water damage. The Pelican brand case I had for my old Samsung S5 Active was considerable easier to open up to wipe off, and trapped particles and moisture FAR less than the OtterBox Defender cases do. Also, the black plastic "holster" for this case does NOT have an opening for the camera, and the holster is more difficult to attach and remove than it should be, in my opinion. All in all, this case is moderately annoying already, having had to pop it open 4 times to clean it up / empty it out in only a months time.While I give OtterBox a lot of props for making a very durable case, my Pelican brand case for the Samsung S5 Active was every bit as durable, and much easier to clean, with a lot less frequency in need (about once every 2-3 months, as opposed to once a week) Lastly, the Pelican has a lifetime warranty, where OtterBox now only honors 1 year warranties. The case is okay (meh), but I do feel like I could have done much better for the price with a different brand.

I'm not a negative reviewer (you know there's always ONE!).  I tend to like the things I buy from Amazon (check my other reviews).  But this...this was a problem.  I bought this because I had purchased a leather band (also on Amazon) and didn't realize I was buying JUST the band - no adapter attached.  After fiddling with the microscopic screws to get this attached to my watch band, I was pleased since I knew I'd never have to do it again - the band was to stay attached to this adapter.  All was great...for about three weeks.  I didn't wear this band every day (I change bands according to what I'm wearing or how casual or dressed up I am).  I was at the gym getting changed into my gym clothes in the locker room (carpeted floor - thank God) and when I removed the watch, one side just came off and my watch dropped to the floor!  I didn't panic...I could see that one of the tiny screws obviously had come undone and I knew they had sent me extras.  When I got home, I reattached the loop with one of the additional screws, checked to make sure all was tight and secure on this and the other three screws, put my watch back on and went on with my day.  Later that evening I was at a restaurant.  I lifted my wrist to see what time it was and POP!  The loop had come off again!! (same place)  This time the watch happened to stay on my wrist while I caught this and put the watch in my pocket.  I paid a LOT of money for my Apple Watch...I don't need to see it bouncing off the floor of a restaurant - or worse a sidewalk or parking lot.  My advice?  Find a band that has an adapter already attached.  This is difficult to put on and, well, there's the fact that your watch could go flying off at any moment.  I'm not saying spend the big bucks on an actual Apple band (that's just ridiculous) but I do NOT recommend this product.

The NT120C CarbonPak is a high-capacity (12,000mAh), dual-port (5V/1.0A, 2.1A) backup battery pack that is a perfect companion for mobile devices.I have had great results so far with the CarbonPak, and I look forward to continuing to put it through its paces and testing its performance.Physical (5/5):For portability of ease-of-use (which are among the top criteria for a backup battery), the CarbonPak is hard to beat. It is compact, relatively lightweight (especially for the large power capacity), and charges with the ubiquitous (near-standard) micro-USB connection and 5V input. This is pretty convenient when traveling, as you won't have to bring a dedicated charger/power adapter or even a dedicated cable that would add more to the packing list (and would just be another thing to forget and leave behind).The CarbonPak does have some heft to it, but it gives it a premium build-quality feel. The case is sandwiched by glossy, black plastic. This is a bit of a fingerprint magnet and while I don't think that is as much of an issue for a battery pack (versus a mobile device's screen, for example), I would have liked to see a soft-touch finish to match the matte surface of the sides of the device. This could have also been a way to provide some traction/grip when the CarbonPak is placed on a flat surface. As it is, the CarbonPak slides around a bit too easily, which can be a problem if your USB cables are particularly stiff and/or short. The CarbonPak doesn't have any feet or ridges on the bottom, so I suspect that the surface will become scratched and scuffed with repeated use (I've already noticed some smudges), dulling the glossy finish.Function/Performance (4/5):I've used the CarbonPak with a number of my mobile devices:* Samsung Galaxy Note II* Apple iPhone 5* Apple iPad 2* Iogear GBHFK231 Bluetooth Hands-Free Car KitThe CarbonPak had no problem charging these devices, and it performed well using a single port, and using both ports simultaneously.A note about the instructions: they lack detail about operating the device. Granted, there is nothing too complex about the operation of a device with a single button, but the instructions should clearly state what the function(s) of that single button is(are). I noticed that the CarbonPak does NOT provide power upon connecting a device via USB. The button DOES need to be pressed to start charging any connected devices. Initially I had thought the button was just a way to check the battery status (it DOES temporarily turn on the LEDs indicating the amount of charge/power remaining); the button does not have a power (or on-off) symbol (such as the standard IEEE 1621), which would have made its function clear even without instructions. It seems to only function as an ON button. I tried holding the button to stop charging a connected iPad, but it kept on charging. I disconnected the iPad and the USB cable and held the button again, but the CarbonPak still charged the iPad when I reconnected the cable and iPad. I suspect that it just has an automatic timeout feature that shuts the battery pack off when no devices are detected; it would have been nice to have a `hard' shut-off, though.The button is nicely recessed/indented, and it is easy to find by feel without having to look for it.Sort of a minor point, but the output USB ports seem to be oriented upside-down. USB ports (A-type) are usually oriented so the USB symbol on connected cables faces upwards, opposite to the way the USB ports are on the CarbonPak.Something else that could be made clear in the instructions is the LED battery status indicators. Is each of the four LEDs imeant to represent 1/4th of the battery's power? i.e. does the LED indicator drop to 3/4 when the battery charge falls below 9000mAh/33.3Wh. Are there still 3000mAh/11.1Wh left when a single LED indicator remains lit? I suspect it's meant more as a general guideline, though.Overall (4/5):The CarbonPak is a great travel backup battery, and it performs well in single output mode (on either port) as well as dual output mode. However, I am still assessing the capacity because it seems like the amount of power supplied to my devices (after only a few days following a full charge of the CarbonPak) fell a bit short of the 12,000mAh/44.4Wh rating, but I need to find a more accurate and complete method of testing this.

Zerolemon was gracious enough to send me this product to review. This is my unbiased and personal opinion of the product to help you and other consumers to make an informed decision to buy the product or not.Pictures tell a thousand words! I have uploaded a lot of photos of Zerolemons Tempered Glass for the Apple iPhone 6!Pros: Rounded Anti-Chip Corners, Crystal Clear Screen, Oleophobic Anti-Fingerprint Layer, 9H Surface Hardness, Perfect Cut Outs, Slight Leveling at the Home ButtonCons: NoneWhatever: Trapezoid shaped cut out for the proximity sensor, Not an edge to edge tempered glass due to curve design of the Apple iPhone 6First off, the screen for the Apple iPhone 6 is curved and most screen protectors will not reach edge to edge. This screen protector is no exception. Secondly, I highly recommend tempered glasses over the conventional PET films. Tempered glasses are almost superior in every way and Zerolemons Tempered Glass for the Apple iPhone 6 is no exception.Installation of the Zerolemon Tempered Glass was simple, easy, and bubble free (refer to the pictures for installation instructions). I did not notice any screen degradation or rainbow effects for a truly clear display. Furthermore, I did not notice any decrease in screen sensitivity while using the Zerolemon Tempered Glass. Another feature of the Zerolemon Tempered Glass is the oleophobic layer which helps prevent fingerprints sticking onto the screen.I did notice something that was very unique to Zerolemons Tempered Glass as the cut out for the home button is leveled off. The leveling of the Zerolemons Tempered Glass allows for easy use of the tempered glass by decreasing the drop from the screen protector to the home button. Furthermore, the cut outs for the tempered glass were spot on. I did notice the cut out for the proximity sensor was in a trapezoid shaped instead of the normal circular shape. This isn't a problem but found it to be out of the ordinary.Overall, I really like Zerolemons offering. The tempered glass is definitely designed with usability and protection in mind with the 9H Hardness rating and various other features. I have no reservations recommending Zerolemons Tempered Glass for the Apple iPhone 6.
