I was dying to get away from my contract plan (Galaxy Note 2 on US Cellular) and spotted this device in the store one day.  Never before did I imagine a functional Android phone could be maintained for as little as $7/month on a cellular network that is very reliable.  Yet, here it is...and I highly recommend this product to anyone looking to test the Android waters without spending a fortune.Let's be clear what this is not: A high-end phone with a data plan that allows you to stream audio and YouTube like it's going out of a style.  It is not a Samsung S4 with a snappy processor and hi-res camera.  If you're looking for that type of Android experience, you'll have to spend hundreds more -- even on a prepaid device.  What the Valet does do, however, is remarkable for its specific price point.The 1 GHz processor can run basic apps just fine.  Anything that is demanding (like games or HD video) will obviously challenge the Valet's hardware.  Just out of curiosity, I played Temple Run Oz since that game has fast-paced 3D graphics and relies heavily on accurate touch response and tilting.  To my surprise, the game was actually playable on the Valet.  The frame rate was sluggish in brief instances, and the overall display wasn't visually stunning, but the swipes and tilts registered quite effectively.  Considering this is a bare-bones mobile device, it was a bit surprising to see this game perform adequately.The Valet's battery life is typical for Android.  The call quality seemed passable, but the speaker volume is a little on the weak side.  Most of the specs on this phone are running average to below-average compared to other Android devices.  One pleasant surprise was the inclusion of a notification LED.  Even some mid-range phones lack this important feature, but the Valet does have it.  It also accepts MicroSD cards up to 32 GB, in case the 4 GB card (included) does not get the job done.I was also pleasantly surprised by the amount of on-board storage.  Most entry-level prepaid devices don't have nearly enough internal storage capability.  The Valet boasts 4 GB internal, so you should have enough room for all your important apps and even a little extra space for non-essential ones.  Once again, for a sub-$100 Android device, you certainly can't find too many faults here.The biggest downside is the camera which is barely useful for anything but the simplest photos.  There is a flash, but no auto-focus.  The image quality might be okay for MMS messages.  However, anything beyond that purpose is going to be a disappointment.  This is not a huge deal-breaker for me personally since the phone does well in other areas.It is Tracfone's tremendous value which has made the Valet impressive to me.  Not everyone needs a high-end mobile device that costs over $100 per month to operate.  In fact, most people could get by with far less and not suffer any ill consequences.  This is the market segment that is all too often ignored by the big carriers.  If you're someone who has sat on the fence about getting a smartphone because of cost, the Valet has taken away your very last reservation.  Plans can run as little as $7 per month, depending on your usage patterns of course, and are extremely flexible for most basic-to-moderate cell phone users.  As an added bonus, the Valet is powered by Verizon's well-respected CDMA network meaning you won't have to worry much about "dead zones" and dropped calls.I couldn't give this phone a five-star rating due to some technical shortcomings, but it has earned a solid four-star review in my estimation.  Simply put, in a dollar-to-dollar comparison, the Valet on Tracfone gives you more than most every other prepaid offering out there right now.

I tested this quite fully before reviewing, but strangely enough not with the S7 but S7 Edge.The Speck Products Pocket VR PLUS CandyShell Grip Case for Samsung Galaxy S7 though enjoyable to use does have strangely limited design purposes.  To start, I don't know why they would limit it to only be specifc to the S7 (regarding the Galaxy model).  The S7 Edge without a case is similarly sized to the S7 with the CandyShell Grip Case.  I actually knew someone who works a cellphone shop so I was able to test it for specifically with an S7 and the case.  And oddly enough, it functions quite the same.  The CandyShell Grip Case allows the S7 to sit more firmly and with less wiggle than the S7 Edge by itself, but both environments work.Due to the obviously open nature of the Speck Pocket VR I won't knock it for those aspects.  And because it obviously lacks a head strap I won't knock it for that either.  This to me seems not to be geared towards gaming at all because you will have to hold this to your head all the time.  And though you can hold it in one hand and perhaps use a separate controller, I just don't see it being used in this way.  I do see it being used as strictly a VR video viewer.  Its lack of a dedicated button doesn't detract from the score because you can just stick a finger in and tap the screen.Rating the Jaunt VR app it's an adequate experience.  The app seems to only provide online content via its own portal.  You can't access and view any kind of downloaded local content.As a whole I can't really rate it that high.  Initially I was going to give it a 4 but based on my time of review and the price I had to knock it down to 3 stars.  Other reviewers have mentioned a price range of around $25 or so.  At the time of my review the Speck Products Pocket VR PLUS CandyShell Grip Case was being sold at $69, basically $50 above the case alone.  Now I'm not sure but I think the lenses are plastic, albeit good optics for plastic lenses but for me this meant this VR experience was an additional $25 above what I would expect.  I have a VR headset that is in transit, it has a head strap and a Bluetooth controller and it was less than $25.  The Speck VR has fixed optics, my VR headset has adjustments for eye width and focal depth.So, VR experience is enjoyable to use with the Speck Pock VR but as for value given pricing as of this review, not so much.

These are excellent cables and they are in compliance with the USB C spec. Spec-R and Current monitor verified.

Works great with a Qi charger. I installed in seconds using the existing back cover. There is a very very slight bulge but it is hardly noticeable. The charging works great, although the device does seem to get very warm on some charges.

I can say without a doubt that I highly recommend this product. Each end is strong and have a good plastic boot to keep it from shorting out. The look and feel is very high end.But the most important thing would be that the cable actually passes a CheckR test. This means it is properly wired for a Type C device. This is very important, because most cheap cables due not fully meet the standard. One issue I often see is a USB 2.0 cable doesn't stop a USB 3.0 port from charging at a higher rate than is safe. This could cause a device to be overpowered, overheated and potentially cause a battery to burn out or explode.I use the CheckR app available in the Google Play store on my Nexus 6P to test the cable. This doesn't test for every possible issue with a cable, however it does catch most issues. So I can't guarantee the results. However I use this cable on a daily basis, as it is currently is in my travel pack.I did receive this item for free in exchange for an honest review. I do this because I have seen many good reviews of type c cables that look good, but then I purchased and it didn't work properly.A little backstory on me. I have worked in technology for over 10 years. I have many Android phones, tablets and other devices, since the first Android phone the T-Mobile G1 (HTC Dream). I love the new Type C design, however with anything new, you find a lot of bad accessories that don't work right. You can be assured this product isn't one.If my review helped you at all, please click the yes button. It would be a great help to me and I will love you forever.

I bought mine as an impulse buy from a local store because it was on sale and I was fed up with the way the cord on my Plantronics BackBeat Go2 always sticks to the back of my neck and pulls one of the earbuds out of my ear every time I turn my head. One thing I really like about this headset is that the earpiece is like the old school, 'micro-speaker' shaped earbuds of the late '90s-'00s instead of those little, silicone tipped things that are too small to stay in your ear. The included "large rubber" retention thing is a little uncomfortable. I've found that the plastic over-the-ear clip is more comfortable for extended use.I have a pretty big smartphone and this headset prevents me from having to pull the phone out of a pocket to answer a call, but unfortunately, it still requires me to interact with the phone if I want to pause/resume audio playback. While it's great for listening to podcasts, it's disappointing that I still have to dig the phone out of a pocket every time I want to hit the pause button. This is the best phone case ever!!  The wristlet broke off though.  I would like to have that back but I have still used this case over a year without it.  Inside the zipper has 2 sides and plenty of space.  There is enough space in the card slots to fit a couple cards.  The leather is nice. The phone fits in the case perfectly and wraps around the front edge of the screen to protect those highly vulnerable corners.  I wouldn't have a case without that.  It also has a magnetic back to attach it to the inside of the case.  At first I was very leery of this but it turns out that it has never failed me. I love that I can detach it from the case to hold it to my ear. When I'm on my motorcycle I can magnetically attach my phone to the tank to use my GPS and to keep it secure.  Before I had thought of this, I lost my wallet and phone off my bike on an interstate at 80 mph. It laid there for an hour before I found it by using the "find my iPhone" app.  The phone did come apart from the case and I found both on the edge of the interstate about 5 feet from each other.  My wallet had a few scuffs and so did the back of the phone case but my phone was perfectly fine.  Not even a scratch!  I get compliments on it all the time and my mom just recently bought one because she liked mine so much. It saved my IPhone 6s so it was well worth the value!

I have two phones I use for videos and needed two holders. My first one was by Vastar and I bought this one as a backup. The two holders appear identical except this holder would not accept either my Moto-G (3rd Gen.) or Moto-E phones whereas the Vastar would. A check with a caliper showed why. The Vastar can accept a phone body up to 11.5 mm thick whereas the WizGear is limited to 9.5 mm. Unfortunately this value is not specified in the sales description and the difference makes this holder unusable to me. The difference is in the extra thickness of the padding on the rear of the holder which I do not see as necessary.If your phone has a curved back side, buyer beware!

First impressions:The case feels tough, yet soft on your phone. The outer plastic is hard and the surrounding shell is soft, but not too soft. Some may complain about the edges of the rubber shell being too "sharp", which they are, but that's the design of the case. You will notice it if you hold your phone in weird ways for long periods of time. The case is very sleek looking. I've noticed it has maintained the thinness of my phone very nicely - it's not thick like other slip on cases. I have big hands and really don't enjoy the use of my iPhone 6s without a case on it but this fits the bill nicely.Drop performance:I'm not about to test performance of my personal device with this thing, but from the feel of it, it can definitely take a drop without harming your phone. The case does have an ever so slight lip around the edge to help keep the screen off whatever it is you place it on.Design: Sleek, edgy, modern. I love the look of it. It shows off your apple logo too, which I'm not always a fan of, but it works here and is no different than your phone without a case, right? The holes are well constructed for the camera and logo. The volume buttons feel good when clicked and there is enough space to move your vibrate switch, but not accidentally. The ONE thing I am not a fan of on this case, in particular, is the power button. I never knew how much I turned my phone on in the dark. This case has raised power button so it can be challenging to locate it in the dark or without looking, a feature I would have liked.Overall, this is an awesome case for $15 - feel comfortable knowing that $15 will protect your $700 device adequately with style.

WARNING if your screen has scratches on it, the ones you feel with your nail it most likely will still be visible with the protector over the screen.NOTE the screen of the Note 4 is rather curve at the edges, this means the FLAT protector WILL NOT adhere to these areas.GREAT NEWS the areas not adhered to are just outside of the usable screen. I got carried away with pressing the edges down and cracked/broke the edge... Where it will not adhere so no big deal.CASE USERS like the slim design by SAMSUNG the protectors are slightly smaller than the screen so it avoids not fitting cases. Also to lesson the chance it will break. It took a lot of force to break the edge part I broke. More than what normally would ever be placed on it.TOUCH response is 99.99% spot on. So no noticeable difference in touch response. The thickness is slightly ever so slightly above the home button. That still works 100% for finger print use.Selfie/front camera has a cutout the ear speaker has a cut out and as for proximity sensors they work 100% .PRICE - Hard to best. I mean you get 5 tempered glass protectors. Easy to install. I used the three guide stickers on the TOP edge after aligning tape over the top of the protector fold over phone top and stick to back.... One each top side one in middle. IMAGINE a trap door, or flip up cover. This allows you to flip up the protector and pull the adhesive film off and let the protector lay flat it will nearly self seal with ZERO bubbles.ALSO CLEAN YOUR SCREEN USING A GLASSES LENS CLEANER AND A LINT FREE SOFT CLOTHOnce the protector is sealed press from the CENTER out - THE EDGES THAT DON'T SEAL are outside the viewing area and WILL NOT BE NOTICEDThe rainbow effect is on the right side about one MILLIMETER wise the entire length. But you have to be really looking for it. AND it is 99.99% out of view. Not a big deal reslly.Why don't tempored glass protectors come form fittable to the entire screen...If you dislike the rainbow edge, you most likely will not like that when off or a dark screen any scratch at just the right viewing angle will be seen also... This might not be for you.As for $50 options, $30 options they do the same thing as these from what I have seen in person.Plastic sadly due to that sexy note 4 curvy glass will also have trouble staying stuck around these areas also. And, good luck with bubbles. I have yet to get a 100% bubble free  plastic prtection.With this tempered glass design I have a 100% bubble free install doing it myself.Also Google about 9H and the hardness of Gorilla Glass and glass in general. You might be paying tons more money for something physically not possible to provide. GOOGLE hardest glass possible.I choose this set of protectors based on actual understanding of what, why and how it works.You don't need a protion harder or tougher but able to absorb and deflect the damage from making it to your screen. Much like a helmet does, and a car number does, these things are durable but not indestructible.Imagine something that tough failing trying to get it off to replace it later.The idea here is a shield that you replace if it takes on damage.The lifetime replacement that some makers offers is nice. But, I have 4 more times I can protect my screen. Not pay shipping, not need to wait for shipping and in effect have the same protection for much less, and no wait.

I have never used car grips before - most of my cars have that textured dashboard surface. Also, it seems like every suction cup device I have tried for showers and whatnot always hold for a period then several days later I hear a crash and sure enough, the suction grips have given out.However, the Adura U-Grip Plus has a lever activated suction cup - that terminates in a wide diameter sticky rubber ring. On my 2013 Accord, the clock/dash monitor area has a  perfectly smooth area, and enough where I can mount the phone without blocking the monitor - and is also out of the way from blocking part of my view out the front windshield. The grip can also be used on the windshield by the way.The phone gripper has the same type of 'sticky' rubber inside the spring-loaded clamp; the sticky surface does NOT come off on the phone - nor the dashboard or windshield for that matter. There's no loosey-goosey about this at all - the suction will NOT degrade and release later as is common with most similar devices.If that's not good enough, they supply an optional secondary 3-M disk that will allow the suction to work with textured surfaces as opposed to a smooth area.I am really very amazed at how well this holds - the phone is FIRM inside the clamp area and the suction portion feels like it's permanent to the car - but if I hit the release switch, comes off easily with no residue. I even for argument's sake slammed my car over a few speed bumps with no effect to the phone or the Adura grip.This is well worth the money - I completely recommend this one for the Lumia 1020!!

I have a new Samsung Galaxy Note 4 which I want to charge wirelessly. The only problem is that if you order the official Samsung wireless charging back cover for the Note 4, it adds extra depth to the phone and makes it unable to fit in most standard Note 4 protective cases. (and many elastic arm running bands and holders for mountain biking)  Which is where this little baby comes in. You simply pop off your back cover, apply this wireless charging receiver to the contact points inside (it may sound complex but it is extremely easy, and instructions are included) and pop your cover back on. I am happy to report this wireless receiver does not cause the cover to bulge out or ripple in any way. And that means you can then fit your Note 4 in any regular protective case (like the official Samsung ones or a third party model, like Otterbox).  But the real question is how does it work. I can report that it does indeed work reliably but with a great deal of excess heat. (this is a side effect of resonant electromagnetic induction) I decided to pair this wireless receiver with the TechMatte QI wireless charger (singe coil) and the combination does work but with the battery rising almost 30 deg C. One reason I like this pairing is that I still get to keep my official Samsung protective case on my phone while wirelessly charging. And the time it takes to go from a battery at 10% back up to 100% is about 5 1/4 hours. I lose a little bit of the quick charge ability offered by the micro-USB wired cable but then I do not have to place pressure on the USB slot. The next morning, the phone is back up to 100%, and I get to keep my protective case on. (have only tried with the official Samsung Note 4 case)Are there any downsides? Well, ...the battery does heat up by 28.4 deg C on my Note 4 during the initial wireless charging throughout the night and lithium ion batteries are sensitive to heat exposure. (as are the electronics of the phone itself) (this is a side effect of near field resonant electromagnetic induction) Will this decrease overall battery longevity? Or, more importantly, damage the half life of the Note 4? Obviously it will, but by how much? The typical rule in electronics is that for every 10 deg C rise, the average reliability is decreased by 50%. Your average smartphone is designed to last between 3 and 5 years if not dropped. (this is part of the "designed obsolescence" strategy all major American carriers and hardware firms adopted from the Hong Kong market roughly 10 years ago) Since the battery (and the internals of my Note 4) are rising by 28 deg C, then this is a decrease of up to 87% (this follows from 0.5 almost cubed) or roughly a usable life of perhaps four to eight months or so, on average, if you left your phone on the charger all day long, every day. (which obviously is not realistic) For a normal usage pattern where you charge your phone from roughly 11 PM to 7 AM and then use it for the rest of the day (so, 33% of the day is spent charging), you find the same half-life multiplied by three. If you can afford to replace your battery and potentially your phone every 21 months or so, and a large heat build up is not a concern, then this wireless charging receiver may be for you. However, if you want your phone to last beyond two years or find your phone model getting very warm, then you have the Amazon return policy at your back.So far, for me, this is about 3 stars out of 5. It does work and it will recharge your phone but at the risk of a great deal of heat build up during every charging cycle. I primarily only use this charger now at my office if I need to add a bit of charge for the commute back home. Your mileage may vary though (especially if you have a phone whose battery is not replaceable)

Bought this for my wife to replace a Plantronics Voyager 510 that she has used for a number of years.  Started using the Plantronics Voyager series with her because she would get echo using most other bluetooths at the time.  She has never had an issue with the older headset.  She has said she would like to easily know how much battery life is left, has been one of her biggest complaints, plus feeling like a nerd with the boom.  (Got over that as you would guess)This Plantronics delivers for her.  Not only does it state how much battery is left when you hit the "call" button but it is also displayed on her iPhone 4 (Verizon).  Another problem she had with the old bluetooth was not remembering she had been using it, had taken it off and could not hear folks when they called because the bluetooth was still connected and sending the  audio to it.  With this bluetooth it senses that she has removed it and automatically transfers the audio to the handset.  Another frustration solved for her.  Folks talking to her are surprised when she tells them she is on a bluetooth headset.  They report that it sounds great and would never have guessed she was on a headset.  She keeps telling me how much she appreciates this headset.  She is on conference calls for an hour or more at times and it is never uncomfortable, like some others have been.  Folks have even asked her for what she is using so they can get one.Only negative I can come up with is the charging cable.  Plantronics as gone to a proprietary connector for charging.  Comes with a very short cable with a magnetic style connector (like some Apple devices) instead of plugging in a USB charger style cable. Only real issue is the length of the cord, the magnetic connector will prevent damage from plugging plug out/in wrong.Bottom line "Happy wife, happy life"  ;-)--bill

I've read a few reviews on here and it seems like there's a common issue, though I have not experienced that with my purchase.I own two other cases, Spigen and Diztronic, and was wondering why none of the two brands had a case where it shows off the "Nexus" logo. Enter the Ringke Max.I ordered a white one. I saw the textured plastic and instantly thought it was cheap unlike Spigen's and they cost about the same. It wasn't a deal breaker though. I examined the clear plastic that displays the logo and how it fits onto the TPU cover and noticed a notch where it should sit in place. Only one end has it so you can only place it in one direction. The white plastic shell snaps over the TPU cover and provided a good enough seal to prevent the clear plastic from moving. (Perhaps a change in design?)I really like that the bezel that goes over the camera is black and not white like Spigen's case. Great two tone to compliment my Midnight Blue N6. I love the fact that the buttons are raised and that they are separated, unlike Spigen's. (Spigen's design is clean, but it takes some time trying to differentiate between the buttons.)  Another cool feature would have to be the loop at the bottom right of the case. I attached my "Kill la Kill" charm onto it. Haha!One of the greatest features of this case has to be the caps for the 3.5mm audio jack and the USB port. With my Spigen and Diztronic cases dust accumulated at these locations. Now there's minimal dust build up with the Ringke Max case. The caps aren't a bother to me. I had a very cool clear TPU case for my iPhone 4s that also had similar caps and it was an ingenious design.What surprised me most about this case is how slim it is. The description about it being slim was not a lie. I was honestly expecting it to be bulkier like Spigen's case which claims to be a slim fit. My Diztronic case is the slimmest, IMO. This isn't too far from it.I have had no issues with grip or how others have described it to be "slippier than an uncased Nexus 6." Pulling it out of my pocket, watching a video, walking while using my phone - no issues with grip.I am very satisfied with my purchase. I didn't use the screen protectors as I bought a pack of Spigen's so I can't comment on those. Price was a tad high and the finish of the shell cover could have been better, but I will still give this a five star rating.My only concern with the white shell and the texture and finish Ringke chose to go with is that it is bound to get dirty. Marks, scuffs, oil/grease - these might be tough to clean off. So far so good though.

Normally I wait a week or more to write a review but I couldn't for this product. I purchased this phone new in 2014, it is the 2013 non LTE model. I really wanted and needed a better android camera phone,  so with the dump off of the old models I took a shot on this phone. That review won't be for months as it has many plus and minus' that I need to work out before I decide how much I like the Samsung Galaxy Zoom. Unlike the phone that this houses there are no mixed emotions about this case. It is spectacular! I had ordered a Skinomi skin and this case the day I put the order in for the device. The case did not arrive until yesterday, giving me 10 days of use with just the Skinomi applied. The phone is very "pretty" when you first see it. Very sleek. Unfortunately very sleek also translates to very slippery. Unboxing it and looking it over, in just the first few moments of my ownership I gave myself a couple near heart attacks as it would slip from my grip causing me to catch it in the nick of time. I immediately skinned it with the Skinomi and that helped a bit. Still with that there was always a lack of confidence doing any operation of the device with only one hand with the skin alone.The case arrived last night and right away I could see it was something special. I got the black and it glistened through the matte packaging it was in. I pulled it out and it felt solid even without the device in it. I slipped the phone into the case and when I say slip I mean it. It slid right on with a little pop at the end that made me know it was on. Perfect fit! Lens cover on next, another perfect fit.  I immediately fell in love with what this did aesthetically for this bulky and slightly awkward looking device. They say black makes you look thinner and wow this case sure did that for this fat phone. The best part is it makes you want to hold the phone. I didn't even like picking it up to use before this case. The device is awkward and unbalanced. The case with it's amazing gripping/ finger traction powers make that a non issue. Before this case I never knew how to set this thing down. On the lens side and scratch the lens or on the screen side and scratch that. Both fixed with this caseHolding it with one hand is simple and easy now.The Achilles heal I felt was the huge lens sticking out is now protected by a very nice lens capThe screen protection is even better because there is a slight raised edge that does not interfere with use but now I am able to lay it down on that side and the screen never touches a surface.I have purchased many many phone cases over the years and one thing I always think is "Did this designer even TRY their case before they went into production?" Not at all with this case. The person/people who made this clearly used it and took that experience to the production model. It actually corrects many weaknesses this device has coming out of the box. If Samsung really wanted to move any of these they would have supplied this case in the box with it. It is a must have for this device.

I bought one of these for my wife and one for myself back in May and I must say it has not been a good experience.  Our biggest gripe is that the Moto E is EXTREMELY slow.  It is hard to tell what the bottleneck is, but any level of multi-tasking will bring this phone to an absolute crawl.  I typically use Endomondo to track my walking and running while also listening to music stored on my phone with Google Music or streamed with Pandora.  So far I have not yet been able to run both apps without one or both crashing or the phone spontaneously rebooting.  If the phone is updating any of its apps, you might as well sit it down and walk away for a while because it will be mostly unresponsive until the updates are complete.  Switching between apps takes far longer than I would expect from any phone in 2015.  I have tried clearing cache and doing factory resets on both phones several times since we got them but have not seen any improvement in responsiveness.Another extremely annoying issue is that apps moved to the SD card are moved back to the internal storage when they are updated through Google Play.  So, if you plan on moving apps to the SD card in order to save space on the meager internal storage, be prepared to move your apps from internal to SD each time they update.The Moto E isn't all bad though.  The battery life is very good, there is virtually no bloatware and the built quality is very sturdy for a budget phone.  Motorola is also doing a great job keeping their devices on the latest stable version of Android.  I wish I could recommend the latest release of the Moto G over the Moto E, but it has the same CPU and memory bandwidth as the Moto E so I'm concerned that it might be a terrible multi-tasker as well.  I replaced our Moto Es with the Asus Zenfone 2 (purchased here on Amazon for $200) and found it to be a much better performer and better value overall than the Moto E.  Not a surprise really since the Zenfone's CPU is much faster and it uses much faster RAM.

I decided to try out the Spigen Rugged Armor along with the Verus High-Pro Shield and Supcase Slim Hybrid case over the course of a few weeks. Below are my Pros/Cons list along with some comparison shots and my final recommendations. Please note, my Nexus 6p was fitted with a Pleson glass screen protector during testing.Spigen Rugged Armor+ Easiest case to take on and off+ Curved area around fingerprint reader+ Compatible with glass screen protectors+ Correctly sized USB, headphone, and camera cutouts- Worst grip in the hand of the 3- Power and volume buttons fairly stiff/difficult to press- No alternative colors/optionsVerus High-Pro Shield+ Good grip with the back texture, well spaced buttons+ Color options that can be stylish+ Compatible with glass screen protectors+ Correctly sized USB, camera cutouts- Difficult to take on and off- Outer rim made of fairly cheap material that feels like it will chip/show wear and tear quickly- No curved area around fingerprint reader (uncomfortable to press)- Headphone jack area quite narrow/incompatible with some headphonesSupcase Slim Hybrid+ Most protective of the 3 (has a hard plastic back)+ Best grip in the hand with rubber sides, easy to press buttons+ Color options available+ Correctly sized USB, headphone, camera cutouts+/- Looks like something out of Deus Ex- Bulkiest of the 3 (makes your phone feel much bigger)- Not compatible with all glass screen protectors- Difficult to take on and offFor the clumsy/all-terrain user --> Supcase Slim Hybrid: Hands down the best protection you can get, and has the best grip to boot. Just make sure you get a very accurate fit with your glass screen protector if you're planning on using one with this case.For the style conscious --> Verus High-Pro Shield: The fake brushed metal look on the back actually helps make it easier to grip and the colored outer rim makes it easy to show off and make your phone stand out and feel a bit different.For the average consumer --> Spigen Rugged Armor: Hands down the best overall pick. Offers a great balance of protection and has great spacing on the cutouts. The carbon fiber inserts aren't for everyone, but after switching around between the 3 cases it's the one I just plain loved using most.Please feel free to ask any questions and I'll do my best to answer them/take additional photos on request.

[See edits below]While Tile is new to Amazon, I've had a set of four for many months now as I backed Tile when it was crowdfunded, so I have quite a bit of experience with it. And, earlier, I got several Stick-N-Finds, which are similar Bluetooth trackers, though there are differences.The Tile tracker itself is a bit chunky but not so much so that it inhibits its usefulness. I have one on a keyring for my shed lock, as I sometimes misplace that. Another is in my camera backpack and a third in my briefcase. Truthfully, I don't tend to misplace the latter two, but sometimes when I am at conferences it's helpful to find where my briefcase got moved. I have tried both Tile and Stick'N'Find as luggage trackers, and in my experience that's not a good use of them as by the time the app sees that the tracker is in range, I already have my suitcase. (What works better for me in this situation is Trakdot Luggage Tracker)Some things I like about Tile are that it is sealed and water resistant, and that the app is easy to use, including the option of taking a picture of the thing you've attached the Tile to. The Tile app gives a relatively coarse indication of how far you are from the selected tag, or all of them - The Stick-N-Find app is more detailed, though its radar-like display could mislead you into thinking it knows direction as well. On both you can activate an audio alarm, but the Tile's is very faint and you won't hear it unless it is exposed and near. (The Stick-N-Find, with its speaker exposed to the outside, is much louder and also flashes a light.) The Tile offers a "lost" mode which is said to allow it being "found" by other Tile users. I'm somewhat skeptical of how useful this is in practice.The sealed design of Tile, while it does add some ruggedness, also means that the battery is not user-replaceable (unlike Stick-N-Find.) The battery is said to last about a year, after which you have to return the Tile to the manufacturer for replacement. (I cannot find, on the Tile web site, any hint of how much this would cost.) [Edit July 2015 - Tile sent me an offer for replacements at $12 apiece plus shipping. The app is now reporting that at least one of the tags' batteries is low, though it doesn't say which one. All four of my tags are still working so the battery life is clearly more than a year.]One feature Stick-N-Find offers that Tile does not is an alert when the tracker comes into range. But Tile also remembers the last place the app saw the tracker, which Stick-N-Find does not. It's a rather coarse location (street and city), but it's better than nothing. Stick-N-Find also offers a "leash" function which alerts you if the tag goes out of range, Tile does not. (But Tile could add these features in an app update.)One interesting difference is that Stick-N-Find has updatable firmware - mine have had several updates. Tile doesn't seem to offer that (or at least there have been no updates in all the time I've had Tiles.)Neither Tile nor Stick-N-Find are perfect. They each have pros and cons, but they both serve the same general purpose. Look at them both, as well as other Bluetooth trackers out there, to see what works best for you. If you have questions about Tile, feel free to ask in the comments and I'll be glad to help if I can.Edit: December 10, 2014I have three Tile tags in use and they are all still working well, but I did have an opportunity to contact Tile support about a problem I was having with the app (which since resolved itself.) While I got an automatic acknowledgement of my request, I never heard back from them even after two weeks and a subsequent inquiry. That there is no community support and the company support seems lacking troubles me.Edit: December 11, 2014Daniel from Tile did respond to my support inquiry today.Edit: February 14, 2015My Tiles are still working fine. The app (at least on iOS) has had some user interface improvements, but no added functionality I can see.Edit: July 23, 2015All four Tiles still working, though I am getting a low battery warning. Replacements are now available for $12 each (at least that's the price as I write this.)Edit: August 27, 2015I replaced my initial four Tiles whose batteries were running low and got four new ones. But then Tile notified me that I was going to receive a "surprise". This ended up being a set of four "Generation 2" Tiles. Physically, they look identical to the original version. The only way to distinguish them is the model T1003 on back, compared to T1001 for the original.The Gen2 Tile adds two things. First, the sound is "3X louder", Whether it is 3X or maybe 2X I can't tell, but it definitely IS louder. One thing I noticed, which I had not earlier, is that the bottom surface (opposite the large "tile" lettering) is the sounder, and both generations are much louder if the bottom is in open air rather than laying on a surface.The second feature is "Double press the "e" to ring your phone." This works - if your phone is in range, a loud musical tune will play continuously until you unlock the phone. I like this.Tile has also added the ability to find your phone through their web site - this is independent of the Tile version. Still no "leash" mode, though.

I bought this a while ago and have been waiting for the company to make it right ever since. Maybe I got a bad batch. Maybe I suddenly couldn't do with this product what I had done before a others. Maybe the God's conspired against. Nevertheless, this company (who offers a LIFETIME warranty on this product) eventually just ignored me when I would not work for me.Now before I continue, let me say that I still have the exchanged emails & the requested photos (proof) that I sent to them, which included a copy of my original purchase invoice from Amazon.I ordered these for the new Moto E (1st Gen) that I had just gotten to use with my Tr*****e account. Including phones for my wife & my son, I had installed this type of product 6 times before. Frankly, I thought this would take care of all our phones. Much to my chagrin, my first attempt at installing this on my phone (in a closed bathroom a recommended), I couldn't get the first shield to adhere without scores of bubbles that I could not press out. So, too, the second and third films. Bubbles + bubbles that I could not get rid of.But it's under warranty, right? So I contact the company. I don't complain; I only tell them what happened. Maybe I'm doing something wrong that I not seeing. Please advise, I ask. A few days later, I get an email asking me not to give a bad review. Please give them an opportunity to resolve the situation and would I please send pictures. I send pictures of the three failed results.  A few days after that, I get an email saying they got the pictures, can I send my original invoice. I send them a copy of my Amazon invoice for the product. After that, I wait. And I wait some more. Then I just forget about it & order a flexible glass protectors that installed flawlessly on each of the first tries.It's been almost 4 months since the original purchase and I just again today thought of it. I could leave it be, or write the review. I HAD to write the review. If you have a company that offers a warranty, you should abide by that warranty, even for a $7 product! They can do nothing for me now as I already have screen protectors for all three phones.I never asked them to replace the product. I only asked them to advise me (as I may have been doing something wrong). Instead they choose to, ultimately, ignore me.

I like how this case is protective -- more so than most battery cases, which are typically made of hard plastic.  There's some give in the material in addition to reinforced corners.  The case is one big piece and uses the "give" in the case to be able to apply the case by first sliding the lightning port into the charging port and then pushing in the phone.  However, the power button is not as responsive as I would have liked, which prompted me to try to remove the case.  It's very hard to do, and I felt like I was going to bend my phone in the process.  So on one hand, this is good, because you know your phone and case won't fall apart if you drop the phone.  It's really secure.  And the material has enough give that it's likely to distribute the shock.The case has the ability to charge "wirelessly" on a pad.  I think it's a secondary feature.  I would really like it to have come with a longer charger cord so that I could plug it in next to my bed and then have the pad set on my night stand.  This is where I could really see this wireless technology really shine.  However, the phone has to be aligned properly on the pad.  there are two sections on the pad, which correspond to two contacts on the case.  For some reason when I charged it last night, it didn't completely charge. so not sure why.Like all battery cases, it adds heft to the phone and makes it challenging to use with one hand, but again you have to look at the opportunity costs of having the case and how it will serve your needs.  If you prioritize thin and light all the time, this case is not for you, but if you're a power user, it's worthwhile to look into this case.I received this product as a sample for my unbiased opinion

With every new mobile gadget, there comes the need for a new screen protector. I carry my phone in a pocket full of coins and keys and I can not imagine using it without a screen protector. Since there are plenty of different brands but not a single brand makes screen protectors for all new devices, every time I buy these protectors it is from a different company. But almost all are the same with very similar package contents, installation guides and the level of protection they offer. This one is not much different either. However, due to my previous positive experience with this company, this time I chose their product directlyFirst of all it works okay and is easy to install. Therefore I rate it five stars, but after in depth analysis and comparing it with the previous protectors I had, I have to say that I have some reservations on this rating:Pros:-Fits well (not covers the entire screen but covers 99.9% which is a good thing since the protectors that aim to cover everywhere are very difficult to fit and the edges are real problem.) This one got on in a simple one time try.-Bubbles are not nightmare. Again, some models get bubbles in and are almost impossible to remove. This is not one of them. With the little tool they send it took me less than 30 seconds to remove every single bubble.-Crystal clear vision with absolutely no comprimise in image quality but it comes with a cost (see cons sections,)-Relatively okay value for money. Not the cheapest but not the most expensive either. I think it is OK priced.Cons:-Lacks an instruction manual. Actually there is an instruction manual but it is not about how to install but about how to write a review on amazon. Installation is left to a website link (which is okay but still not very handy)-The screen cleaner, they sent is like a joke. Before installation, you need to remove dust and grease and make your screen look like just out of factory. There you need a microfiber cloth which is normally provided in the package. This one has something like that too but the least quality disposable one and it did not help me clean anything. I had to use one of the existing lens cleaner clothes I had.-It is a finger print magnet and has no antiglare capacity. This is how the absolute image clarity is obtained. After trying several different screen protectors, I figured out that you either get matte which is free from finger prints and glare but the image quality is a bit comprimised, or you get extra clear and that suffers from fingerprints and glare. So, no matter where you buy, the story is either ways.-The additional dust remover sticker is too strong and should be used as the last option since it may leave some marks behind on the screen which can be more troublesome to remove. A microfiber cloth in a stable indoor environment (not much air circulation) is all you need for the dusts.In conclusion, I liked the fact that it worked flawlessly and was not a pain to get it working. I still have two more in case the one I put gets scratched over time. There were times I had to send back screen protectors, compared to those experiences this is a smooth one and therefore I rate them with 5 stars. But as I said I have some reservations and I hope the manufacturers are reading this review and take it as constructive feedback towards better products in the future

This is a review of a few items I just bought. As they are all related and being used together Im going to paste the same review to each product.Recently my Samsung S5 has been draining badly as the OEM battery started to come to the end of its life. I started looking around for a replacement and decided to get an extended battery. I have been using a wireless charging pad and I wanted to continue to charge with this method as I have several pads and even a wireless charger for my truck. The charging receiver I have been using was not going to work with the extended battery so I opted for one that plugs into the bottom charge port of the phone. Im not crazy about removing the door from the bottom of the phone but I cant seem to find a way to get everything I want no matter how many products I buy.What I settled on was the PowerBear 7800 battery and the wireless receiver listed in the link below, unless this review is the one for that receiver. I got and installed the Powerbear battery and two back covers. I first installed the wireless receiver and then popped on the hard plastic back plate. I then tested to see if the phone would charge on my pad. It did, on two separate brands of pads without issue. I then added the protective soft rubber back cover and tested the phone on the pads again. To my surprise it started to charge with both cases on it.The battery and case backs feel of high quality and I really like not only how the two back plates look but feel. The extended battery and both plates do not add that much weight to the phone and the cases do not make the phone very fat. I have been using a LifeProof case up until this point so Im used to a larger phone. This case gives me plenty to hold on to with my man hands and still can be stowed in a pocket with ease.To make up for the lose of my Lifeproof case I also purchased a set of the Moko waterproof cases. They were really cheap and seem to be made well enough for my use. I wanted something that I could use on my boat and motorcycle. These fit the bill and the phone with both back plates fit in the case easily. I find that once you place your phone in the bag you can press the air out to get a vacuum seal. This makes using the phone much easier. This is also the same company that I bough 2 wireless charging phone holders for my vehicles. They work very well and are much better than constantly dealing with a charging cord.I have not had the battery long enough to speak to its power and life. If it fails me or does not deliver the powder advertised I will be sure to update that review.http://www.amazon.com/Rerii-Universal-Wireless-Charging-Micro-usb/dp/B00YGV76LK?ie=UTF8&psc=1&redirect=true&ref_=oh_aui_detailpage_o00_s00http://www.amazon.com/PowerBear-Samsung-7800mAh-Extended-Protective/dp/9707716371?ie=UTF8&psc=1&redirect=true&ref_=oh_aui_detailpage_o01_s00http://www.amazon.com/Universal-Waterproof-MoKo-Armband-Devices/dp/B01AJP3F7A?ie=UTF8&psc=1&redirect=true&ref_=oh_aui_detailpage_o00_s00Update: 11-5-16I recently took a trip where I had a almost 3 hour plane ride. I was watching movies the entire flight and before while waiting to board. I had the screen on full. I didn't have to worry about my battery at all. It lasted all day and I still had plenty of juice when I was ready to settle in the hotel room.I have also been able to use my phone while the screen is on full while listening to Pandora and using GPS on my motorcycle. I have not even come close to running the battery down yet.When I get up in the morning I don't even have to think about a charger and I do whatever I want on my phone without any worries. On a typical day of use I will end the day with still about 70%. On a heavy day after watching a movie and having the screen on full I might be in the 30%s. I have to really try to get in much lower than that in a day.It was a great by and a great battery for travel.

I tested the eBoot Dual USB 3.9 Amp (20 Watt) SMART High Capacity AC Travel Wall Charger with my Samsung Note 4. I did test it using the Super Charger port, just out of curiosity. I've used several rechargeable power packs, most requiring additional cords for different devices but never one that had near all the cords attached like the myCharge Peak 6000 and never one that could recharge every device I connected to it. I was very impressed with charging abilities of this ultra portable power pack. It charged every single device I attached to it.Highlights:- The power pack has three notification modes: Voice, Tone or Silent. The power pack comes with Voice mode on by default. Additionally the led indicators on the side flash the power levels when a device is connected or when the level check button it pressed. When in voice mode, every time a device is attached a voice notifications tells you that it's charging and what type of device is connected i.e. Apple, USB or Micro.- A level check button on the side when pushed will tell you how much power is left by either voice or tone, depending on mode selected, and the led indicators will flash the corresponding bars: 5 bars = 91% - 100%, 4 bars = 71% - 90%,  3 bars = 51% - 70%,  2 bars = 35% -50%, 1 bar means the power pack needs to be recharged with less than 25% power left.- Recharges fast. It took about one hour and 20 minutes to get the power pack to 51% - 70% charged once it reached 1 bar and the power pack's voice prompt indicated that it was time to recharge. The manual says it will take up to 4 hours to charge via wall outlet and 13 hours via standard laptop USB port with onboard USB cable.- Can recharge the power pack via wall outlet or the attached USB cable through your computer.- Compact. This power pack is the size of a portable hard drive.- Has a USB port and three charging cables attached: MicroUSB, standard USB and Apple(not the new iPhone 5 one) connectors.- Can charge up to 3 device at the same time with Apple devices charging first since these devices are high-powered with exception of the Nano then USB connected devices and the microUSB devices.- I was able to charge an iPhone 5 from 82% to 100% in 20 minutes, Logitech K800 Wireless Keyboard from 1 bar to fully charged and iPhone 4 from 51% to 91%, which is when it ran out of charge, right out the box, and this is all after I ran around spot charging the devices listed below.Items I could charge:- iPhone 4- iPhone 5 via the iPhone 5 cable through the onboard USB port.- iPad 1- iPad 2- Logitech K800 Wireless Keyboard- Kindle Touch- Sony Reader PRST1- Nook Simple Touch- Nook Color- Sony Ericsson Hi-Fi Bluetooth Stereo Headset- Jabra Drive Bluetooth Speaker- Motorola S305 Bluetooth Headset- Motorola S10-HD Bluetooth Stereo Headphones- Sony NEX-F3 CameraETA:- Samsung Droid Charge- Samsung Galaxy S4As you can tell, I went around trying to find anything I could to charge with the power pack. I'm thrilled that I didn't find not one item myCharge couldn't charge.I'd recommend the myCharge Peak 6000 power bank to anyone who wants to cut down on the cords, power bricks and adapters that have to carried around for most portable devices, especially while traveling, that's truly compatible with nearly all chargeable devices.

Imagine yourself at the edge of the Grand Canyon, or standing on the Eiffel Tower looking out over Paris, and you want to send a selfie to your friends to make them weep with envy. The process is fast and relatively painless... once you've figured out what the process is. Here's the process in a nutshell:1) Turn on the monopod. There's a switch at the bottom. If the blue light is flashing it's charged. If not, use the enclosed USB cord to charge it.2) Pair it with your phone. This means turning on the Bluetooth function if it isn't already on, and having the phone scan for available devices. Your phone should find it pretty simply. Mine did.3) Mount the phone in the cradle. If it doesn't fit securely you can use the gasket, though I have no idea how it works. My phone didn't require it. Good luck with that.4) Attach the cradle to the arm.5) Set your camera to "Selfie!"6) Fiddle with the length of the arm and angle of the cradle until you're happy. This could take an hour if you're picky.7) Once you've got the photo you want framed in the camera, press the conveniently located button on the handle.8) Marvel at how well that really works. You're welcome.

I used a Contour Showcase on my iPhone 3G. When I upgraded to iPhone 4, I was looking for something similar: hard plastic or rubber case with a belt clip holster, that was less bulky than an Otterbox. At my time of purchase, there was no Countour Showcase made for iPhone 4, so I needed an alternative. This case had good reviews, so I gave it a try.I am pretty happy with this case. I find it very easy to insert and remove the iPhone from the holster with one hand. I am not seeing any of the stiffness or wear problems reported by some other reviewers.Here's why I deducted 1 star from my rating:1) The swivel on the belt clip is not tight enough. It is easy for the holster to rotate on the clip by accidently knocking it with your arm.2) The top clip on the holster doesn't snap down over the phone on its own, when the phone is returned to the holster. I need to push the clip down to make it snap positively over the phone. I am worried that the phone may fall out of the holster if I forget to do this, but that hasn't happened yet.3) I use a Power Support anti-glare anti-fingerprint screen protector, which is quite thick. I had problems with the screen protector fitting into top portion of the case. I needed to use an X-Acto knife and sandpaper to remove some of the case material in order for the screen protector to fit into the top. I emailed Splash Products support about this, and their reply (which came instantly BTW) said their case was not desgined for a thicker screen protector, and I should use the (non-fingerprint-resistent) protector included with the case. So if you want to use an anti-glare screen protector, you might want to consider a different case, or else be prepared to make some modifications.All-in-all a good case and I am happy with my purchase.

I owned the original Galaxy Note, plus I owned the Galaxy Note 2. Thus Ive been using big phones, or phablets, long before it was cool to do so. I still recall people calling my Note 1 an iPad Phone and snickering. Oh how times have changed.I was excited to get my hands on the Galaxy Note 4. I figured it would be everything I liked about the Note 2, plus it would be bigger, faster, with a better screen, and with a better camera. The Note 4 didn't disappoint. Here are my thoughts on the phone: Pros:- Gorgeous, big screen. Best display on any smartphone- Fast performance- Stylus pen support- Good camera (low light not as good as the iPhone 6 Plus though)- Great battery life- Can basically replace the need for a small tablet Cons:- Maybe a tad big for some- Pen is good only if you bother to use it- Cost more than other good options- Would have kept it if it had software like the Droid Turbo/Moto X Bottom LineI really, really, really like the Note 4. Its really the best big phone you can buy. I prefer it to the iPhone 6 Plus, even though I also like the 6 Plus very much. The most compelling features to me is the big, gorgeous screen and the awesome battery life.I did however return the Note 4 in exchange for the Droid Turbo. The Turbo doesnt have as good a screen or as good a camera as the Note 4. The Turbos screen is smaller. The Turbo doesnt offer pen support. I actually would recommend the Note 4 over the Droid Turbo to most people.So why did I trade the Note 4 for the Droid Turbo? Moto Apps!Once you embrace Moto Assist, Moto Voice, Moto Display, Moto Actions, and Trusted Devices, you won't use a phone that don't have these apps.While I do feel like the Note 4 is the best smartphone available, I encourage people to try the Droid Turbo or Moto X and give the Moto Apps a spin. You may also decide that you don't need the best phone.

I just got this phone as a replacement for my Curve 8300.  Boy, was I impressed.  It runs fast, keeps up with my typing & you can do most anything on this phone.  Email set up is a breeze & I love using the custom ringtones for people in my address book.  You can easily create your own wallpaper & songs for it and upload without confusion.The downsides are:  No video and I DETEST that the memory card is behind the battery, making it take forever to power down and access. But I loved it so much, I bought another.  I checked out the new Bold but was unimpressed by it's bulky size and the flat keyboard (makes it hard to type) - this phone is MUCH better!

I love this device. I got it to evaluate it for my van, which doesn't have any bluetooth capabilities. So I needed that so I didn't get a ticket using my phone, and so I could listen to music. It works extremely well for that. Easy to use, and has quite loud alerts, which I like since the vehicle is pretty noisy. The big simple multifunction button is also easy to use without looking at it.I was thrown initially because it talks about a magnetic suction base, but I didn't see it. That's because the magnetic cup fits so tight you don't see it at first. It holds really tight, which is great. Would like to get a spare, but a tip: you can use a regular magnet if you've got one laying about.Another super plus of this device is the power. It is actually USB powered and comes with a 12v to USB adapter, which thoughtfully has a second port. It will run off any USB port, and only needs the 1 amp flavor, not the 2.1 amps required to say fast charge an iPad. And since it's USB that means you can plug it into any of the now common LiOn USB rechargeable batteries that are out there and run it off that instead of 12v if need be.Why would you care that it can run off USB? Ah...that means it can run be used outside your car. I am using it to run a pair of computer speakers from my iMac's bluetooth. And I used it outside my van to power a set of battery powered speakers, with both the speakers and the TT-BR03 running off the same LiOn USB battery. Yeah, I could have bought BT speakers I guess, but I don't have them. So this works great for that, and uses a paltry amount of power. Getting a gender changer for the mini stereo plug might be handy if you want to use this as I have.I found the bluetooth super easy to set up on an iMac, Samsung Galaxy Pro Tab, and an iPhone 5. I did notice with both the iMac and the iPhone that after the initial pairing I had to delete (forget) the pairing once and add it again to get a good connection, but it's worked flawlessly since. Switching back and forth between streaming devices is as simply as pausing one and starting the other. If I have Pandora going on the Tab Pro and get a call on the iPhone I'm in business; the ability to be paired to two devices is fantastic. Now either I or my passenger can play music without a lot of fussing around, yet still get calls. Not all aftermarket BT receivers do that.Aside from a couple typos, and the misundertanding about the magnetic base, the documentation is easy to follow. It's small and unobtrusive. Note that it requires the more recent bluetooth; an old iPod Touch I had would pair, but no audio. The iPod doesn't have the right bluetooth (4.0). Used it with Skype on my iMac and Google Voice/Hangouts on my Galaxy Tab. It also worked fine with iTunes Radio, advancing to the next song via the button on the TT-BR03.You can get a lot of mileage out of the TT-BR03. In the house, battery powered, or in the car or probably even some other scenarios. I love it when a piece of kit can be using in more than one way.

a few months ago, I bought this battery case: http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00FZ3OY0Y/ref=oh_details_o07_s00_i00?ie=UTF8&psc=1I used it for a few days and liked it, but then a friend told me he was having serious battery issues so I gave it to him. Since then, I've been researching battery cases for my 5s and found the Maxboost. It seemed to be the right balance of features and price. I've used it for a couple days and I'll be returning it and buying the one linked above.PROS- I did not experience any signal loss like other reviews.- beautiful, minimalist design blends in perfectly with the phone and at a glance, you can't tell there's a case on it.- nicely slim and light; a friend commented that it's the slimmest battery case he's seen.- it 'feels' expensive due to its material, a type of plastic that is rigid but feels soft to the touch. this also makes the case have a matte finish which some people might really like.- the charging works as promised, which is very cool: when you plug the assembly in with the included cord, the case charges the phone first and then the case, so they're both ready to go when charging is done.CONS- that 'soft touch' plastic makes the case very slippery. some people might like that material, but I don't.- the bumper is also make of that plastic, which is an odd choice. bumpers can be purchased as a separate accessory and I've never seen a bumper made out of this type of material, because it's slippery. 'normal' shiny plastic has a more tacky feel against skin yet can still be made to look nice.- that bumper doesn't quite snap on completely at the bottom corner near the headphone jack. like other battery cases of this design, the phone docks into the battery back and then the bumper keeps the whole assembly together. the bumper is held on with tiny tabs, and the one on the bottom near the headphone jack doesn't grip the bumper, resulting in an assembly that isn't as 'tight' as it should be.- the kickstand is really just silly. it's VERY difficult to snap open (and if you have no fingernails, forget it), and once you get it open, it feels like it will snap off with very little help. it bears mentioning that the kickstand does NOT work in portrait mode; it only works when the phone is in landscape mode. that behavior is common to all cases of this design.- the micro USB port is very close to the bottom, on the side of the case. if you hold the phone in your right hand, you will not be able to hold it comfortably while it is charging. I believe this is a design flaw; the port should have been on the bottom in the same place as the lightning connector on the phone.- the bumper has cutouts to access the phone control buttons, but the thickness of the bumper makes the buttons difficult to get to.I know there are certain sacrifices one makes when using a case like this (weight, thickness, button and headphone jack access) but there are other battery cases that get the design closer to a better balance. This particular case is going back.

Ok so I do really like it, its pretty easy to put as long as you follow the instructions, the worst part for me was the speaker clasp as its rounded follow by the small little strips for the side of the watch which I felt disappeared right before my eyes before even installing them as they are really thinlol. The fronts are very easy as well as the rear as long as you align the 2 noise cancelation holes properly. My complaint is that like everyone else has said, I had to press my cradle super hard to get it to close, at least I did the first few days. Due to the constant pressure closing the cradle the skin started getting indentations and lifting at the edges. My solution was (a bit too late) to remove the rear which allows the right amount of space to close the charging cradle but the damage is done and I just request a replacement. If you feel like you need the rear protector I found a way that works for me to charge without forcing the cradle shut. Hopefully this makes sense to you.What I do is pick up the open cradle flat in my hand, I put the watch dead center of the cradle, as I hold the cradle from underneath while the watch is centered, I ever so slightly pull the watch band down as I push up on the cradle. You will see the watch sink deep into the cradle, while maintaining that slight pressure close the lid, you will see it will close with no effort. I discovered that a bit too late but now I know for my next skin. Another way would be to place the watch in the cradle and with your pointer finger under the cradle and your thumb on the screen of the watch through the open window of the lid squeeze gently, you will see the watch sinks in. While maintain the light pressure just close the lid, no forcing the lid shut needed. I hope I helped someone.

I just received this phone and I am very happy with it - with a couple of caveats.  The build quality is typical HTC - near perfect - and the screen is superb.  And, finally, I have an Android phone with decent battery life.  I have both the Desire HD, with awful battery life, and a Desire Z which is only moderately better.  I fully charged mine upon receiving it, and have used it for three+ hours, and it was at 71% when I just checked it this morning after having it on standby overnight.  Couple of issues though - but still a five-star rating:  Mine came with Android 2.2 and an over the air update is not available (at least in Germany where I live) to upgrade it to Android 2.3/Gingerbread.  And, the touch screen is less responsive as my other HTC phones let alone my iPhones.  This problem isn't a deal-breaker - but I wish someone had mentioned it earlier.  One minor potential issue is how easily the battery cover slides up and off - probably not a major issue but be aware that it slides off fairly easily.  I have seen some complaints about the quality of the Nexus One's being sold lately. Mine came from The OEM Shop (via Amazon) and it was in absolutely new condition with US accessories.  Last, but not least, I ordered this phone in order to have a less expensive phone (all of my other HTC phones and iPhones have been over $500) to take on my walks without fear of breaking the bank if I drop it or lose it.  It fills this need perfectly.*************************** EDITED ON JUNE 8, 2011 TO ADD LONGER TERM IMPRESSIONS ************************************I've had this phone for almost two weeks now and I continue to be thrilled with it - especially given that it's quite a bit less expensive than newer (but also very nice!) HTC phones.  The battery life continues to impress me - I can get 2 and 1/2 days with data turned on - which is impressive compared to my Desire Z.  I have not had any problems connecting to both my hidden and visible wireless access points at home - and I've easily connected to free wireless at two locations on my current job trip in Germany.  I have to say though that I really miss HTC Sense - especially the weather/clock widget - and I'm still irritated that I can't download Android 2.3.x here in Germany.  I have downloaded Angry Birds Rio and Plants-vs-Zombies and both play great and playing either of them doesn't take too much battery life.  I am convinced that for the price you cannot go wrong with this phone and continue to highly recommend it.

This case is inexpensive but will protect an LG Optimus F3 T-Mobile phone well. It certainly offers protection for all sides of the phone, if you have a separate screen protector installed too.Pros:- Fits snugly and holds tight- Good protection on the back and four sides.- Wraps around the front but doesn't interfere with the screen or button operations. Provides good screen protection by protruding above the screen all the way around the edge of the phone face.- Has soft rubber on the corners and a hard case on the sides and back.- Good kickstand that locks in both the open and closed position.- The kickstand can be used with the phone in portrait or landscape mode.- Covers the volume rocker but I actually think it is easier to use the volume controls with the case on.- Covers the power button but again I think it is easier to use the power button with the case on.- Has openings for the micro USB connector, earphone jack, microphone (in the right position for a T-Mobile phone), speaker, and camera.- It has a nice design and is easy to grip.- The slightly rough surface of the hard part of the case does not show fingerprints.- Fits the T-Mobile version of the LG Optimus F3 correctly with 90 degree upper corners and the rounded lower corners.Cons:- The case makes the phone nearly double the thickness of the phone itself.- Adds significant weight to the phone. Not double but perhaps 30-40% more than the phone itself.- This case is not a snap on/pop off affair. I found I had to start by removing the hard plastic back, install the soft rubber part on the phone, then work the hard plastic case on over the soft rubber part. It required me kneading the soft rubber under the hard case until it fit properly. Removal was also a matter of working the hard cover off little by little.Conclusion:If you're looking for a case that will protect your device, with a kickstand, this might be the case for you. If the extra weight or thickness is an issue then you might need to look elsewhere. Overall I'm surprised how good a case this is for just $5.We will be buying two more LG Optimus F3 phones for family members this week. I will show them the case and offer to buy one of these cases for each of them. It will help that we can have three phones with three different colored cases so we won't inadvertently pick up the wrong phone and head out the door with it.Personally I rarely drop my phone, keep it in my shirt pocket, and even after a year or two my phones look like new. I will likely try this case in my pocket for a few days but will remove it due to the extra thickness and weight. The one reason I may keep the case on is I like the kickstand for watching videos.

I purchased this black case for a MetroPCS LG Stylo 4.  I understand AT&T's Cricket is also coming out with the same phone with just more ram (3 gb > 2gb)... So it should fit that one, too. This is a review about a case, though, not the phone; which blows my LG G5 out of the water, loving it so far.This phone came out last week and so the case choices are limited and the reviews all seem like they're written by the people selling them, so I took the plunge and want to let you know this is a legit review for an "ok" case.It's a dual layer case with a softer flexible black matte TPU inner lining and a plastic shell snaps onto to the backside.The plastic shell has a been covered with a matte coating on the exterior facing side that is slippery and will suck up and smear oily finger juice from your toddler or french fries.  I would have rather they just have left it normal uncoated plastic as it makes it difficult to grip the phone. It makes it slippery... But I've had this issue with other phone cases, too. Anker makes a case for my iPhone 8 plus that was just as slippery.A pop socket or maybe a flat magnet plate (for use with car mounts) on the outside might help with grip.If you use a magnet car mount you could potentially hide the metal plate under the plastic shell if you don't want to have that plate rubbing against your phone (a plus for this design).It also adds a decent amount of thickness to the phone.  I am considering using this case without the plastic piece (i.e. just the inner TPU). When the shell is off it's actually a pretty slim case with reinforced top, bottom, and corners.I frankly don't think there are any better alternatives out there at this time since the phone is so new.  Going to roll with this for now until something better comes out.  The case adds a significant amount of thickness to the phone but I'm confident that it will protect it well.Edit: the tempered glass screen protector went on well and I have no issues with it so far.  Feels like the OEM glass.Edit: second edit... I took the screen protector off.  The protector only had adhesive on the edges of the tempered glass to affix it to the phones glass. Unfortunately for this design if you get even a tiny bit of water under the glass you are toast... It will leave a water stain or worse because it gets under the glass. I think it is a problem with the LG screen which they intentionally made curved for whatever reason.  Took off the plastic shell and been using just the inner TPU.  Works well.Edit: it's been two months.  looks like the manufacturer is merging reviews from older cases and different phones - sketchy.  I don't really like the case anymore ... i am finding it much more bulky than the other phone i carry (my work phone) and i'm actively looking for a new case.

I own a few Lumsing batteries and they're always great quality products. This one has a great metal casing that has a brushed texture. I love the way this feels in my hand. It's a tiny bit taller than my iPhone 6, which is nice. Typically batteries of this capacity are short and thick. This slides nicely into the bag I carry to and from work without adding any bulk. I love that it charges via lightning cable because that's one less cord I need to carry around. It takes about as much time to charge the power bank as is typical with batteries of this capacity (a few hours). It definitely is at the 1200mAh capacity they advertise because I've charged my phone from about 40% three times and the indicator still shows 3/4 dots (so 75% full). Having two USB ports is more useful than you'd think. I have charged an iPhone and iPad simultaneously and there was no slowdown in charging speed. Finally, contrary to another review here, I'm using a 3rd party (gshine) lightning cable to both charge the battery and my phone and have had no problems. So as long as you're using an apple certified lightning cable (you should be anyway), it'll work just fine. I highly recommend this product.

The nature of my work requires that I must have enough power for my phone - an iPhone 6. I ordered this, and it does increase the size and weight of the phone significantly - the trade-off to get 3600 ma of power. It works: when you see the phone battery level decrease to nervous levels, hit the button on the back. And there are LEDs to indicate the levels of charge on the battery/case. It seems to retain the charge for days, which is great. To charge the case and phone requires a micro USB plug - not a lightning plug. So you can use all those micro USB cables you most likely already have... but you'll need two cables if you want to charge your phone separately.A big plus: it's sturdy - I managed to drop the phone in the case flat on pavement (ugh) and aside from a couple of nicks on the back, it was fine.Negatives: aside from size and weight - which means it doesn't fit in my geeky case that clips on my belt - is that the opening for the lens / flash should be larger. When using flash, you get about 3/4 of the shot illuminated - the smallish opening cuts off a side of the flash, especially noticeable on a horizontal shot. So when I do use flash, I have to consciously crop the shot so that it is evenly lit with the flash... or worse, take the phone out of the case. Now I understand why some of the other cases I've seen have big openings for the lens/flash! Almost tempted to use my Dremel to enlarge the opening... but nah.And - the enclosed directions are very vague on how to actually get the case on the phone. The case is in two pieces - you need to pry off the front portion at the seam to pull it away from the back / battery part of the case. Be very careful, that front piece seems flimsy and delicate. Since I don't use the case all the time, I store it with the front piece snapped on because I'm afraid I'll break it if left loose.Finally, that button that you press on the back to activate the battery is very, very small... and on my black case, very very black. It's there... just have to hunt a bit for it.All in all - good battery backup, some issues with the flash coverage, and be careful snapping the front of the case on and off.

I got into the Smartphone game late in life. I couldn't justify spending $600 on a phone. That would feed my family for a while. But everyone told me I needed one and I caved to peer pressure. This is my third BLU phone. I'm content. I don't have anything to compare this one to other than previous BLU phones. My first one succumbed to the washing machine. Grrrr! My second is still working, but encountered a chair roller and now has a giant crack down the screen. It works just fine, but the crack drives me nuts.  I bought this one, because it was 4G LTE. What I should have realized is that 4G doesn't matter at all when you live in the sticks and you're lucky to get reception standing on your roof or that one tiny spot in the driveway. I have an AT&T GoPhone plan.I really enjoy this size. Any bigger and I wouldn't be able to hold it comfortably. Any smaller and I don't think I'd enjoy watching Netflix in bed or playing Candy Crush while waiting for dinner to cook.The calls don't seem quite as clear as my last BLU and I'm slowly adjusting to the on screen navigation buttons. It seems responsive to light touch. The speakers don't seem to be as loud as my last BLU. I haven't played around with it much, so maybe I don't have the settings right. I've been using my old phone for it's alarm. I'm hoping I can fix the issue with a simple setting adjustment.A big improvement over my last phone is the battery life. I'm regularly getting 3 days out of it, playing games, a few phone calls, and some light texting. I suspect even with heavy usage, I could get at least a full day.The camera seems pretty good for a cell phone. Once again, just comparing to other BLU phones. I can't say it's huge improvement over the last phone, but it's definitely better.Price is the biggest issue at play here. I'm getting a phone that will do everything I need it to do and more for $100. I don't have nightmares about it breaking. I can afford to replace it if something happens. It may not be as fancy or as fast as a Galaxy or have an iPhone camera, but it's $100. I have no complaints.
