This is an excellent dash/window mount for holding your smartphone in its carrying case or bare, your choice. I have a leather case for my iPhone 5 and it works just great in this holder. Check out the video to see what I mean. I was more than a little concerned about a dash mount working on my textured dash, but it works perfectly. I like the double sided sticky disk adapter they provide, so you can first mount the disk, then mount the holder itself. The vacuum seems to be unnecessary, but adds considerably to the strength of the hold on the car. The soft grip on the phone is very nice, too, as it won't add any scratches to your precious smartphone's case. I was able to pick a spot on my dash that had a very slight curve to it, and the entire assembly appears to be there for the indefinite future, just what I wanted. When Koomus asked me to review a sample, I was reluctant because those vent mounts and CD mounts can do damage to your car if you aren't careful, and minor stress on a vent can cost you hundreds of dollars in replacement materials, believe me, I know from personal experience. But this mount goes directly to your dash or window, and that's fine as far as I'm concerned. I accepted their offer and couldn't be more pleased. If you want to mount a smartphone in your car, this is the way to do.

I bought this power bank as a Lightning Deal during the Black Friday sale last year. Having used it for several months now, I am very pleased with this product. The power bank is roughly the size of a small paperback novel and weighs less than a pound. The package included the power bank itself, a heavy-duty felt carrying case, and a micro-usb charging cable. There was no wall-charger included, but it will work with pretty much any standard phone charger or computer usb port. As with any kind of rechargeable battery, it's always best to plug it in and give it a full charge before using it. After I received mine, I plugged it in and let it charge overnight.Given the high capacity of this battery, it WILL take a while for it to reach a complete charge. That is to be expected. The input is rated at 1.5A, so it charges 50% faster than devices that only have a 1A input. Charging from 0 to 100% will take about 15 hours, provided that your wall charger puts out enough amperage.The power bank has a sturdy aluminum housing and seems to be well made. I really like the fact that it has a digital readout showing the exact percentage of charge remaining. There are 3 usb outputs, all of which are rated at 2A. I've only ever needed to use 2 at once, but it can charge my phone and tablet at the same time with no issues. The rated capacity seems to be pretty accurate. It can charge my phone about 5 - 6 times or my tablet about 3 - 4 times before it runs out of juice.PROS:+ Very high capacity+ Three 2-amp usb inputs for charging multiple devices+ 1.5 amp micro-usb input charges the power bank 50% faster+ Digital display shows the percentage of charge remaining+ Rugged construction+ Holds a charge even when not used for a couple of monthsCONS:- A bit larger than expected- Does not charge itself when a cable is plugged from the output to the input (Just kidding! I saw something similar in an old Pink Panther cartoon when I was a kid.)For some bizarre inexplicable reason, this review isn't showing on my profile. What's up, Amazon???

Anker, Anker, Anker. I wish Anker made ALLLL batteries! Not only do they give an EIGHTEEN MONTH WARRANTY on batts, their service center has never let me down! I currently own 5 batts and 2 chargers from Anker! They are like clockwork. All positive here, with the exception of just one comment to them:Their charger uses a red/blue light system that is house within the same display. So a dead battery registers RED and a charged battery registers BLUE... however, as the battery charges it displays a scheme of colors that goes from pink to purple to light blue to dark blue. While this is visually appealing and unique, you really must have a system to know when the battery is fully charged. My system is that I pull the battery down a little from the contacts, and if it stays the same blue as when the battery was in contact, then it's charged. This comes from almost 3 years of experience using these chargers.A better design would be to not only separate the light indicators, but only light the blue LED when 100% charged. I noticed that the design of the charger has gone through some revisions from the first one I purchased... here's a hint from a loyal customer for the next rev! ;)

Remarkably Good screen protector. I recently bought a Casio Protrek watch, and knowing it will possibly be exposed to the elements, I wanted a screen protector for it. These were the only good ones I could find after searching. I must say that initially I was a little put off by the price. I am ok with paying 7$ for a tablet screen protector - but for a watch ? I decided it was worth protecting anyway.After receiving the product, I was lazy to apply it for a while, what with applying screen protectors being a sacred event, much like a surgery :D - you have to first scrub yourself, have all your tools in a contaminant free zone, and then really focus. So, I typically tend not to want to get into that frame of mind.While applying it, I followed the instruction video on youtube, and read the instructions carefully on the back of the packaging as well. I first washed my hands with soap, made sure the table was clean, cleaned the face of the watch with the provided microfiber cloth. I then sprayed the provided application solution onto my fingers, and proceeded to carefully remove one of the two films from the provided sheet - I agree with some reviewers in that it was a little difficult to remove the film, but definitely not terribly hard. At this point, I had my heart in my mouth though! I could see finger prints on the film. I was like, s***!! I then proceeded to spray more application liquid onto the protector itself, and positioned it on the watch - at this point, I was not sure if the fingerprints would stay or disappear. I was half thinking I should throw away the film, and use the other spare. However, I did read someone's review that said this should not be a problem, and things will be fine, once the application liquid evaporates. And boy, am I glad that I didn't throw it away. I left it alone for more than 24 hours (more out of fear of finding out I screwed up), but then when I looked at it later, it was just the most beautiful perfect application! Bang up job by Klearkare!!You should note that it would have been impossible to apply a traditional screen protector onto the watch without getting my fingerprints on it. (there is hardly much area to get hold of an edge.)One thing I am disappointed in though - the video seemed to suggest there was another layer on top, and I was unsure if it was there or it wasn't - I needed to be sure in case I needed to remove it. However, looks like there was no layer on top in this case at least. Just the film on the backing.

This is a nice holder to mount your phone to your car's windshield or to the dash. Many people do this as they use their phone to listen to music using Bluetooth or they use their phone as GPS. The holder has a built in suction cup for your windshield mounting and features a dial to create the suction force to mount it. Make sure that you thoroughly clean your windshield before you mount the holder.The hold features a hip ball joint that rotates and allows you to adjust the phone to the proper angle for easy viewing. The phone holder also rotates 360 degrees so you can set any angle you want. The holding mechanism is well padded to protect your phone and to also hold it securely. It adjusts to fit phones 2 inches to 3.3 inches wide. It also comes with an adhesive pad so if you want you can mount the holder onto your dashboard of your vehicle. I have posted a video of the holder and its features and I hope it is helpful to you.I used to travel all over the world and the USA and carried a portable GPS with me so I could find the places I was going to quickly and easily. Because of that I became aware that it is illegal in 28 states to use a windshield mounted device and many other states have limitations on where you can mount such devices. It is also illegal in 6 provinces of Canada. Make sure that you check out the laws and requirements for your state to make sure you can use such a device and where to mount it. Take care also that you know the law in the states where you are traveling.I like this holder and the price for it is good. I am going to use it to mount my iPhone to my desk so I can use it daily and listen to music with my Bluetooth headphones. Maybe I'll stop knocking it off every day onto the floor.I rate this product with 4 stars.

The Skinomi TechSkin Samsung Galaxy S6 Edge+ Screen Protector arrived promptly and was nicely packaged in a cardboard container with the instructions on the inner leaf. Included in the package is a cleaning cloth, 2 screen protectors and a small spray bottle of cleaning solution. A small piece of paper directs you to a website which has an instructional video. Before the application you wash your hands and wet your fingertips with the included solution. Next, you clean the device with the provided cloth. Next, apply a thin layer of the solution on the adhesive side of the screen protector. Note: not a lot of solution is required. Next, you apply the protector to the screen. I used a credit card to carefully remove the excess solution and bubbles away from the center toward the edges. The instructions require you allow the screen protector to sit for 4 to 5 hours before turning your device back on to allow the film to set properly, which found a little annoying. Instructions did say that a hair dryer on a low setting could be used to expedite the process. Small microbubbles and streaks were reported to disappear within a few days, and this was what I experienced. The thickness of the protector feels very study and makes the cover otherwise unnoticeable. It conforms well to the shape of the phone and provides adequate space for a back cover. Despite its rather difficult application, I highly recommended this product. I received the Skinomi TechSkin Samsung Galaxy S6 Edge+ Screen Protector at a significantly reduced cost for the purpose of reviewing it and providing my honest opinion.

The E LV Defender Hybrid for the Samsung Galaxy S7 made a good first impression with its looks, but (what are for me) its functional flaws reduced it to one star. If what troubles me doesn't trouble you, however, it might deserve even four stars.The deal-breaker for me are the three deep cut-outs at the bottom of the case. (Please see the photograph, below.) I often hold the S7 upright with three fingers along one side and my little finger supporting the S7 on the bottom. In this position, it is actually painful to move my little finger back-and-forth across the bottom of the case.Additionally, the volume buttons are not as responsive as I would like. And the raised dots on the long sides of the case detract from the case's sleek, business-like appearance without, because of their positioning, adding any grip or other functional benefit that I can perceive.Apart from these design flaws, the case itself appears to be well made. Although it's called "shock proo,f" and "defender" may be intended to call to mind the Otterbox Defender, while it should offer more protection than a bumper or thin-fit case, it doesn't strike me as particularly heavy-duty in that department.Please note I received the E LV Defender Hybrid free of charge in exchange for my honest review, which I've tried to give.

PROS:These are good cases that add almost zero weight to the phone.  They aren't quite hard plastic, but aren't soft gel cases either.  They are a nice compromise between the two.  The sides have rough areas made to help grip the slick S3.  They are extremely easy to put on and take off.  The lip on the face is just high enough so that when you lay it face down, the screen doesn't touch the surface.CON:The cut-outs for buttons and holes are not perfectly placed.  The only place I have noticed this hurts is the USB/power connection.  Not all mini-USB adapters have the same size housing at the end they plug into the phone. Some are very tiny, some are relatively large.  You'll either have to find a smaller one (I'm using the charger that came with my old HTC Evo) or cut the small bit of plastic between the existing hole and the face.  I cut mine out with a pair of small wire cutters so that I can use chargers with different size housings.The CON was only enough for me to deduct one star from this product.  I have two of these, one smoke and one clear, for a white S3.  My wife has a pink one for the Galaxy Nexus. I've only had a little time to use this, so this is just my general overview. For technical specifications and professional reviews, there is always "Google it."To make this review as efficient as possible, I will do a quick run-over, and also a comparison with other phones you might be most interested in comparing it to, especially the Note 3 that it replaces, the iPhone 6 Plus, the LG G3 since it shares more than a few similarities, and even the new upcoming Note 4 Edge.Physical:* The Note 4 is basically a Note 3 - if you've held one before, then you'll feel right at home. It's just a smidge taller, narrower, thicker, and heavier. But the main thing you'll notice is the design difference. Looking at it straight down onto the screen side, you might not notice much, but set it down and view it on all sides and you'll see a much better difference. It's like having a same car but the 2015 version over the 2014 version - at first you don't see any difference, until you go up close and realize the little things that make it really stand out. First of all, Samsung has finally paid attention to its best aesthetic competitor HTC, and followed suit with a real aluminum metal chamfered edges, and not the fake aluminum of the Note 3. It looks great. View it from the top and bottom edges, and you'll see an even more striking design change and upgrade. View it from the top edge especially and you'll see what I mean. Here is the first Samsung with truly beautiful edges - I would have never thought I would say that!*The very next thing you'll notice once you turn it on is the screen. the LG G3 was the first QHD screen for America from a big company, but I can say that Samsung's AMOLED has come a long way, and is now the best on the market. The color gamut, saturation, contrast and black levels have always been great, but Samsung has also come a long way in terms of color reproduction, and especially making the screen still looking great in direct sunlight - it's only been two years since the Note 2, but Samsung has made amazing progress. QHD means 2560 x 1440 resolution - 515 ppi - and Samsung's is the brightest, clearest, and best screen on the planet right now.*Other notable improvements are:- Processor: upgraded Exynos and Snapdragon processors depending on where you buy it, upgrade GPU for you gaming aficionados. The Note 3 was one of the fastest and most powerful for its time, and is still one of the fastest phones right now(!), and yet the Note 4 is even faster! Swiping through screens, switching between apps, running a dozen apps, playing processing-intensive games, they all feel like a breeze.- Upgraded cameras: 16MP on the back, 3.7MP on the front(instead of 13MP and 2MP). You can now record video in 4K mode!- The pen/stylus is now made by Wacom, the famous stylus maker. Hardware and software improvements have made it more usable than ever.- Samsung got rid of the USB 3 charging port of the Note 3 in favor of the more universal mini-USB 2, but improvements have made it possible to charge the battery from 0%-50% in 30 minutes. The battery is 3220mAh, up from 3200 in the Note 3. Both will get you through a whole day of moderate usage with a bunch to spare. I've never had it lower than 20% remaining using it from 10am-10pm with moderate to heavy usage. What you should know if you didn't already is that the screen uses the most battery. Keep leaving it on and it will drain fast, especially if you're playing a game. Other big battery hogs are leaving your GPS radio on - even if you are not actively using it - and apps that continually access your phone - like if you set your email to instant retrieval instead of every few hours.By itself, this phone is a beast in many senses of the word. But how does it compare with the other phones, and is it a worthy buy or upgrade?In comparison with the iPhone 6 Plus:If you want a giant phone, the iPhone 6 Plus is a very intriguing option. If you're an Apple person, you're going to get this phone, that's just the truth. It has a beautiful 1080p screen(a very first for Apple), it's fast, and finally Apple has a phone that all Apple lovers who have been craving for a huge phone to have. The Apple ecosystem is still superior in terms of apps and userability. On the other hand, if you like Samsung, then you should absolutely get the Note 4. Samsung now has an almost perfect big phone, and each phone from now on will only get better. You will never regret getting this phone. If you're in the neutral, then it depends on your lifestyle. If you just want a big phone for everyday use, I personally think it's easier to use Apple phones. Everything is just right there - the learning curve is not very steep(until you get into iTunes and want to export stuff, but that's another issue). But if you want more control over your phone and you're more tech savvy, there is nothing better than the Android ecosystem - it has the best of all worlds, and for those more tech savvy, it's actually easier to use than Apple phones. As a user of both ecosystems, I can attest to this personally.In comparison with the LG G3:I wanted to include the G3 because both share the QHD-resolution screens. But the G3 is more compact, having smaller bezels and a smaller 5.5" screen. So if you still want a big phone, but feel the Note phones were just a tad too big, then the G3 has that perfect combination. It also has one of the best screens on the market, in LCD form, has a beautiful design, and the LG quality.In comparison with the Note 4 Edge:Personally for me, there is no comparison - I'm going to get and use the Note 4 Edge as my main phone. It has a smaller 5.6" screen, but that curve and design makes it oh so unique - it's the most unique and best-looking phone for me by far. Some will complain that with that form factor it will be very hard to have a case that can fully protect it, but so I will just be extra careful with it.In comparison with the Note 3:This might be most important comparison, because you own a Note 3. Moving from the Note 2 to Note 3 was easy for me, because my Not 2 had performance issues that my Note 3 doesn't(it would slow to a crawl when too many apps are installed/used, and when your text history is too large texting crawls to a stand still), plus the 1080p screen on the Note 3 is 10x better than the 720p Note 2's, not in just resolution, but brightness, color reproduction, sun-light legibility, etc. The differences from the Note 3 to the Note 4 is less pronounced, since at this size QHD over FHD resolution is not so pronounced - although if you put them side by side you will notice the difference. My advice is, if you resell your Note 3, then absolutely upgrade to the Note 4. If you can't, or can't upgrade and have to buy the Note 4 at full price, then I would say to wait until the Note 5. Or do what I will do, and get the Note 4 Edge.By itself, the Samsung Galaxy Note 4 is a definite recommendation. As an upgrade, it is still highly recommended, with caveats. I will add to the review once I have used it more.***Also note, that with these international versions, you might not get the best LTE/4G speeds, because that more depends on the country where you live/travel to and their telecom providers than the phone itself - although the Note 4 has one of the most complete sets of radio bands of any phone out there. Check the specs, and then research the telecom providers of your country/country of visit.

Benson here again, continuing my reviews of USB Type-C legacy cables, adapters, and accessories. Today I am reviewing LightningKid's USB Type C (USB-C) to USB 2.0 Type A Charging and Sync Cable.Let's check spec compliance :I'm using my trusty Chromebook Pixel 2015 with the Chromium Twinkie USB-PD Sniffer, available on Amazon from Plugable : Plugable USB 3.1 Type-C (USB-C) Power Delivery SnifferFor my first test, I've got the LightningKid USB 2.0 Type-A to Type-C cable plugged into an Apple proprietary Type-A charger, the stock iPad Air 2 charger.The Type-C end is plugged into Twinkie, acting as a pass through to Chromebook Pixel 2015. Picture attached!First let's run the ectool command from Pixel's command line to check what Pixel thinks is happening on its USB-C ports :localhost / # ectool --name cros_pd usbpdpowerPort 0: SNK Charger Proprietary 4719mV / 2000mA, max 5000mV / 2000mA / 10000mWPort 1: DisconnectedSo far so good. Pixel identifies the charger as a Proprietary type adapter, and has negotiated 2A 10W of charging.Let's see what Twinkie (the USB PD sniffer) says :> tw ccCC1 = 418 mV ; CC2 = 15 mVThe tw cc command reads the current voltage values from the CC pins. As you can see here, CC1_PD pin has a value of 418mV. Referring to the USB Type-C Specification Table 4-25 Voltage on Sink CC pins, this voltage falls right in the middle of the range allowed for vRd-USB, meaning that yes, this MicroUSB adapter has the correct 56k pullup resistor!One more command :> tw vbusVBUS = 4767 mV ; -2034 mAThe tw vbus command uses twinkie's current and voltage meter ability. As you can see, the laptop is pulling just around 2A of current at 4.767V from the Apple adapter.For a data test, Ive hooked my Nexus 5X to my Chromebook Pixel via the LightningKid Type-A to Type-C cable to test large file transfer via MTP. It just works, no problem!Now for more subjective judgments of this cable : The Type-C plug on this cable is a stamped metal part that's been wrapped around to the shape of the plug. It does have a seam on one side. The tip is plastic. Overall, good, but not great, quality here.In conclusion : A high quality charging and data sync A-to-C cable from LightningKid! The cable is a great safe way to charge and sync newer USB Type-C devices such as Nexus 6P/5X or Pixel devices with your old Type-A equipment. It is also future proof for fast charging USB Type-C devices to come!

I would like to start out by talking about the 5600mAh model. This is perfect to carry in your pocket or leave in the car. It not only has the support to charge your phones, tablets, and other electronics, but it also comes with a flash light built in. It's decently bright so you can use it to find your keys, or even find your phone when it's dead and isn't lighting up anymore.The 5600mAh came with a long USB cable and a short USB cable that had a universal adapter kit. This kit comes with everything you will need to charge your device, except for the iPhone 5 new cable. This means they give you the tools and all you have to do is open up the box.Out of the box, the 5600mAh was able to charge 4 phones one after another before it needed to be plugged in and charged itself. This Power Bank has a lot to offer especially on the go, and it's so easy to use. Plug in your phone with the given USB cords and then plug into the Power Bank. Hit the button that is on the top and you will see the blue lights, and you are done. let it go and charge like the wind... if that makes sense. The battery app on my phone stated it was charging at 52% an hour, if that is accurate. I am not sure about the battery apps validity.The next model I was given to try is the 10000mAh. With two USB ports for dual charging, this unit is impressive! Out of the box we charged two phones at once and we even charged our phones days later without having to recharge the unit. The battery charge this Power Bank holds is great because you want to rely on something that isn't going to die after charging one or two devices.The 10000mAh also came with the appropriate cords for all of the devices, except for the iPhone 5 cord. On the back of these devices they mention only a few ideas of what devices can be used. I want to inform you that you can use these Power Banks on Tablets, Phones of all sorts (not just exclusive to iPhones) and any other device able to be charged with the USB.Both of these Power Banks can easily fit inside of your pocket, and of course they can fit in the car or a purse or a fanny pack for anyone who still rocks those. They are easy to charge, and they charge very fast and efficiently. You don't have to be glued to a wall because the cord only goes so far, you don't have to stand up whenever you get a text message and the wall outlet is clear across the room.Overall these products impressed me! It's a great idea and Easy Acc takes full advantage of it. They want to make sure you keep your devices healthy with a full charge and on the go. Some cars do not allow devices to be charged off of the cars battery when it's off, this will allow you to charge your batter long after you car dies on the side of the road.One more bullet point, if you read my review on Easy Acc Bluetooth Speaker, now you understand why pairing these great devices would bring you much happiness. You can charge the Bluetooth speakers while it plays, and since these Power Banks hold such a long charge, you have a potential of days and days of music binging

I have a couple of phone cases; most of them have a different function for my need of the day. I have ones with clips and some with wallet cases and then I have one case that is similar to this one; just a simple clear back case for everyday use. I actually really like that case too but the only thing about it is my phones screen has no protection. I love the simple and thin design but I fear dropping my phone face down will crack the screen on even the shortest of falls. You can find the case I am taking about here: iPhone SE Case, PLESON [Tou] iPhone SE 5SE Case, Ultra-Thin Crystal Clear Case Lightweight/ NO Bulkiness/ Shock Absorption/ Scratch Resistant Soft TPU Protective Bumper Case for iPhone SE 5SE (2016)This case, which I received for free in exchange for my honest review, has a lot more lip to the edge over the phone front than the other for which I am grateful. Of course, if I drop my phone face down on anything pointy there will be no saving it but if it falls flat I think this case would have a little more forgiveness than the other. I added some pictures so you could see the lip over the front.Though this case better protects the screen I don't think it has as much give on the sides as the clear case I have. When I press in on the sides I don't feel as much cushion and therefore it has less shock absorbency. I love the colors and all the buttons are easily pressed and all the proper holes are cut. It fits my iPhone 5 nicely and I like that not only does it have some height above my screen it also comes over the sides of it a bit further, if nothing else it gives me piece of mind. I do really like it, I just don't recommend dropping it off any buildings. ;)I have found that when I need to charge the phone while in this case I have to use an Apple Lightning cable. The other cables I have are too big around the actual connector and the plastic piece won't fit inside the case's hole so the plug can't reach the port. I works fine with the actual Apple cord just not the aftermarket cords I have.

This Roocase looks outstanding and complements the classiness of the s7 edge.My biggest pice of advice if you are going to use this case however is to SUPER GLUE the case in place which removes the removable feature but the entire wallet case assembly doesnt get in the way when taking pictures so it was well worth the piece of mind of not having the phone fall out (more on that later).  Keep in mind that the case itself (which houses the s7) won't protect the phone very well (it leaves the edges/corners bare) so having the phone fall out will almost certainly damage your expensive phone so I HIGHLY recommend you glue in place if you are planning on using this case. In fact, I was toying with the idea of getting a different s7 edge slim case that covers the corners and super gluing that into it rather using than the case it comes with. I used loctite super glue (can find it here on amazon or any hardware store like home depot/lowes) and now have absolutely no concerns of my phone falling out.Gluing is a necessity because, as other people have mentioned, there is a high likelihood or your expensive s7 edge of falling out and potentially shattering your screen/damaging your phone if case is held horizontally (phone screen pointing down). When I tested this over my bed it took one jerking motion with the screen pointing down for it fall out.  In addition, since the phone case is attached to the wallet via a magnetic, it could be easily knocked out while you are fumbling around trying to take out a credit card or something.  However, I should mention that it works well when shaking phone up and down with the case held vertically (phone screen pointing perpendicular to the ground).Conclusion: 5* if and only if you glue the phone case to the wallet.  1* if you don't glue the case to the wallet because of the potential of seriously damaging your phone.Note: Picture of case super glued to the case. Would never hold the phone like this otherwise because the magnet isn't strong enough to hold the weight of the phone as this angle (what I referred to as screen facing the ground) in my review.

I made the jump from Apple (I had the iPhone 5C) to this and I am VERY glad I did.  First, I really like the phone itself...it has a battery life almost double that of my iPhone 5C which I thought was very good.  Second, the screen is on this phone equals if not exceeds the iPhone 5C.  The fingerprint unlock scanner works perfectly.  The cameras are SPECTACULAR.  I have never gotten such good photos out of a smart phone before and always used a dedicated digital camera for more serious stuff, but this phone just about replaces that device.  The phone is larger without being too large and makes texting far easier than it was on the iPhone 5C.  The CPU in the Moto G4 Plus blows away the one in my previous phone and the rotational sensor works in a far more timely fashion..the iPhone 5C just would not rotate without an additional twist of the wrist most of the time.  All in all, I am very happy with my phone and believe Motorola has a serious hit here.  Only $300 for a phone of this quality?  This has to be the best bargain on smart phones.

Ths is by far my favorite case with card storage for the iPhone 6 Plus. With my iPhone 5s, I used an Incipio Stowaway that I loved but the iPhone 6 Plus version fell short and I was scrambling for a similar case. Verus, Spigen, CM4 and Silk make cases with card storage that I compared.The Silk case is by far the most lightweight which is essential with the already heavier iPhone 6 Plus. Even if you only use one card, it will not fall out of the card storage section! On the other hand, the Verus seem to offer better card storage because their slide and/or storage door feel like they would be more sturdy and protect your cards. However, both the slide and storage door functions can act up. Meanwhile, the Incipio promises 3 card storage but only if you're okay with a door that sticks up a bit and looks like your cards can fall out. The Spigen is by far the best but it is over an ounce heavier than the lightweight Silk. The CM4 case stacks up great against this Silk case but asthetically it leaves much to be desired and anyone can see your cards when you make a phone call.This case feels great in your hands. It's not slippary at all and the card storage nook allows you to put your finger there while making a call so you don't have to wrap your hand around the entire iPhone 6 Plus. It also comes with a screen protector!

Versus (VRS) Design makes good cases.  I've tried another offering from them and came away reasonably impressed.  This High Pro Shield model has a stylish "stealth" look to it and promises "Military Grade Protection" so it seemed worth a shot.This thing looks pretty cool in person.  It's subtle but finished nicely.  The metallic feeling of the rim and the textured grain of the back feel good in the hand and are definitely less slippery feeling than the naked S7.  The case fits very snug on the phone, it's easy to get on, but can take a little work to get off thanks to the two-piece construction of the case.  You might consider some other options if you find yourself (for some unknown reason) frequently removing your phone from the case.  You should do fine if, like most people, you leave it on once you get it on.Several reviews pointed to the button covers as a weak point on this case, noting a rattle and/or buttons falling out.  I can confirm that there is a slight rattle to them when the phone is in the case, but only when I am vigorously shaking the case trying to make them rattle.  I was also able to pop a button out by pushing it from the back side when the phone was out of the case.  It was easy enough to put back in and no lasting damage is done.  I don't see how these could fall out during normal use, as there's no lip or edge for them to snag on.  I'll keep an eye on it though and post an update if they end up falling out.Also note, this case did not prevent either of my wireless Qi chargers from working.It's hard to verify the "military grade" protection claim, but it does seem like VRS has selected good materials and done some sculpting of the TPU layer on the inside to give it maximum flex and bounce.  I feel like this case offers a bit more protection than offerings of similar size, but it's not a huge difference.  Still, if you want good protection from bumps in a manageable case size, this should be strongly considered, especially if you like the sleek modern look the case offers.** Disclaimer - I received this item at a discount in exchange for thoroughly testing the product and writing a comprehensive, honest, and unbiased review. I enjoy trying new products, testing them out and sharing my experiences on Amazon so other buyers can make informed purchase decisions.

THIS IS UPDATE TO EARLIER REVIEW WHICH I RATED AT FOUR STARS FOR THE CLEAR CASE - See that review below ***Just received the Black case after first having ordered the Clear and being disappointed in that choice for one of our two new iPhone 6.  I upped my rating to Five stars for the case now.This Black case is a great choice for the Space Grey iPhone.  It looks very sleek and professional with the black front of the phone, and maybe even stealthy is another descriptor for it.Fits perfectly just as the other case and it's really nice to have that clear back panel to show off the space grey finish for a two-tone appearance for the phone.It also seems that the Black bumper material is different and way better than the Clear bumper.  The Black bumper feels slightly softer and potentially more shock absorbent than the Clear bumper which felt ridged and potentially brittle compared to this.  The Black bumper also has a slightly better tactile feel.The button covers are more responsive on this case.  The shut-off button on the clear case was stiff.I couldn't exactly fault the manufacturer for my initial poor choice in color for the case with a space grey phone, but this one is now perfect in color compliment and a better case all around.Original post below:*********************This is a decent case and the iPhone 6 fits perfectly in it.  We bought two of the clear cases and buyer beware.Yes the backs of all the cases are clear, but the Clear case bumper or edge is not really clear or translucent.  It's more of murky or milky clear, almost appearing white.  It actually looks great on my wife's phone as it has a white face and gold back.  The silver and white phone may look good in this case too.I have the space grey phone with a black face and it really does not look good at all in the Clear.  That's not Supcase's fault exactly.The disappointment in the murky bumper is that it obscures the visibility of seeing the sleek appearance of the metal, regardless of its color (grey, gold or silver).  It would be nice if the Clear was totally clear on the bumper edge too.  That would really let the phone appear as if it were encased in glass or ice.The last comment is that the front of the case does provide a very slight raised edge to give the screen some protection if laid face down on a surface.  The edge could be ever so slightly raised further to give better screen protection if the phone is dropped.The colored bumpers are what you see is what you get so this is not an issue for those.  I should have gone with a colored bumper.  Don't make my mistake.

I'm the happy owner of a half-dozen or so Anker products. Their customer service is the best. I desperately wanted to love these earbuds, but the problems I had with them were enough to cause me to return them. The sound quality is okay. The battery life is fantastic. The fact that they're Bluetooth LE compatible is awesome. However, the cons include:* Occasionally the audio would drop out for a second, even when my phone was just a couple of feet away.* The volume is too low. Inside a quiet room it was fine, but when I stepped outside or inside a restaurant, I'd have to crank up volume on the phone and the earbuds all the way up, and it still wasn't what I'd call loud.* It only came with a cable, not a wall plug. That meant that I'd have to swap out the cable for one of my existing USB wall plugs or buy another one.* The charging cable is too short (about 1.5 feet).* The charging cable uses a proprietary clip to attach to the earbuds. That means that if the cable gets damaged or lost, I'd have no way of charging the earbuds. It also means that I'd have to carry the charging cable everywhere with me, for those moments when the batteries suddenly die. I wish the earbuds simply accepted a standard microUSB plug; I have a bunch of those laying around all over the house, office, and car.* The buds didn't stay in my ears, even with the flat rubber "wings" that came with them. The right bud, particularly, would come loose. I'd have to keep pushing them back into my ear.* Player controls weren't intuitive to operate. Tapping the "+" and "-" buttons work as you'd expect to control volume. However, to rewind audio you have to long-press the "+" button, and to fast-forward the audio you long-press the "-" button. Those should be reversed.I hope they go back to the drawing board on this one, because it's got good potential. If they simply added a microUSB port, I wouldn't mind it if they got rid of the charging cable altogether.

I've never used a battery like the Incredicharge I-5000 before, but I'm so glad I have one now. I have a Kindle Fire and a Kindle 3G. They sometimes run out of juice when I'm on the go. I simply plug this little battery in and recharge them. It's so convenient. It's about the size of an empty wallet, which makes it completely portable.So far, I've found that if I really want to fully charge my Kindle Fire with the Incredicharge, it takes hours and completely drains the Incredicharge. I can use the Fire as it's charging, but I don't if I want to get the full charge and get it more quickly.The Kindle 3G charges faster than the Fire. We went camping last night. I plugged in the Kindle 3G, tended a fire for a short while, got ready for bed and before I knew it, it went from fully discharged to half charged. I'd say that all took about an hour and my Incredicharge still has 3/4 charge left. Even though it's fully capable of completely recharging my Kindle 3G, I used it more like a backup until I could get home. I never know when I'll need that spare battery for some other device. It's a precious commodity!The Incredicharge set comes with a short USB cord and seven different mobile phone connectors:- Sony Ericsson- iPhone- Nokia- mini USB- PSP- Samsung- micro USBAnother extra tidbit, it comes with a built in LED flashlight. It's just one LED light, but nevertheless helpful in a jam.To recharge the Incredicharge, simply plug it into a USB port on the computer or a powered USB hub. It can also be plugged into the USB wall plugs. It just can't be plugged into an outlet directly. For some, this may be an inconvenience.My only disappointment is no connector is included for the Nintendo DS Lite or 3DS. We need to supply our own DS Lite or 3DS USB cords. Basically, we can use any USB cords, including the one that it comes with. The unit has two USB ports that can be used at the same time. Another added convenience.If they could include a pouch for all the pieces, that would be icing on the cake. I love this charger. I take it everywhere with me. Silly me, I'm letting my gadgets run out of juice just to have an excuse to use it. Overall, a solid product.PROS:* small, compact, portable* two USB ports* comes with many different adapters (see above)* LED flashlight* can fully charge a Kindle Fire* can recharge the Incredicharge by connecting to a powered USB port* can use own USB cord to charge devicesCONS:- cannot recharge Incredicharge by plugging directly into an outlet- no Nintendo DS Lite or 3DS adapter included- no pouchThis product was provided for review by JR Closeouts; but it's been proven so useful, I went and bought another one as a gift._____________________________________UPDATE 5/26/12: I have been using this battery regularly to recharge my Kindle 3G. I just set it and forget about it. The battery recharges the Kindle 3G until it's finished charging and then turns off. I can get two full recharges on my Kindle 3G before the Incredicharge is completely depleted. What a wonderful product.

I am a fan of the Blackberry OS 10 and own a Z10, which I like very much, except for its completely inadequate battery life. The Z10 battery died during the day after starting the day fully charged once too often and so I ordered a Z30 from Amazon. As luck would have it, I received the phone in time to take to NYC (my wife and I go to the opera 4 or 5 times/year). We had a dinner reservation after the opera and were a little late heading to the restaurant and so I tried to call them to let them know we'd be a bit late. The call connected, but the person who answered could not hear me. Yes, the mute was off (I am a retired computer professional -- 50 years doing OS work and running software projects; I am not a novice). I tried again. Same thing. I ended up making the call with my wife's Android phone. Later at the hotel,  I tested the Z30, calling my wife's phone. Same problem. Oddly, I can make voice recordings with the phone, so it's not as simple as a dead mike. It also works correctly on speaker phone. But if you do not set it to "speaker", the other end will not hear you, though you do hear a faint echo of what you say through the earpiece. A little Googling reveals that this is a common problem with Z30s, so caveat emptor. Mine is going back tomorrow and I will simply have to put up with the Z10's lousy battery life (I do have a second battery for it, but it is really a pain having to change batteries mid-day and make sure two are charged; despite the excellent software and UI, I think I'm beginning to see why Blackberry is not gaining ground: hardware design problems compounded by sloppy quality assurance).

It works, but I have some reservations about automobile usage.  I would definitely use this if going on a long trip or frequent long commutes if the area was in question.  I've never actually had that issue, so I may opt to get the home adapter kit for this to use at my house.  It's too darn cold outside right not to try this in my car, so I connected it to a USB port on my desktop computer.  I ran the antenna and put it up on the window sill, and my little LG flip phone went from two to four bars.  I wanted to try it on my BLU GSM radio based phone, but without a sim card it showed no bars before or after, but the fact that a six year old phone got a boost was a good sign it does the job.A couple of concerns, one it mentioned not for areas that exceed 150 degrees Fahrenheit.  I'd hate to on a daily basis have to take down the unit and carry it inside, unscrew the antenna to disconnect etc.  The temperature limit is going to be exceeded often even in the Northern United States, maybe not right now in February, but soon enough.  Cars can easily obtain 30+ degrees hotter than that limit sometimes even with the window cracked.  My GPS often gets hot when I parked at a restaurant for lunch on my Chicago commute.  I've had power amplifiers burn up on my dash and even some in dash because of heat.  Now, this company is said to make boost units for outdoor and indoor ATM's and such, so why make something to be used for cars, that with even normal use is likely to overheat and break?  Next, the side stays on this pull off and are adjustable to different phone widths, My BLU phone was already at the outer limit, I suspect there might be wider phones that would work.  That aside, my LG flip phone was much too small to fit even the lowest width adjustment.  There is a growing trend of people wanting some of the older smaller more portable and more battery efficient phones, this would not work for them.  It might however extend the battery transmit life if you talk a lot for the bigger phones.Recommended, for long trips, home use, but frequent small commutes, I think it becomes a problem to maintain properly.  FYI, typo in the quick start guide/manual on item six it says 3G S instead of 4G S.  Maybe a reprint edit of an earlier model, or just a fat finger typist.

I didn't plan on owning this product.  I have a Palm Pre and was just fine plugging a male-to-male 3.5mm connector into my phone to listen to podcasts in the car... until the phone got stuck in headset mode one day... well that's another story.  I have an aftermarket Sony Xplod radio that doesn't have Bluetooth, but does have an aux input.  So when I saw this product, I was pretty excited.  I read the reviews and saw the issues other people had with hissing and noisy calls, but figured I'd give it a try for a little over $20.  When it arrived, the instructions are little more than pictures. There isn't anything in the included instructions about pairing the remote with the cigarette lighter portion - I had to find that through this forum and through the Kensington FAQ.  However, it paired with my phone extremely easily, and after pressing the CENTER of the remote it also paired with the cigarette lighter.  The attached cable that is "cut off" in the pictures was just a little too short to reach from my cigarette lighter to the aux input on my stereo, but there was an included extension 3.5mm wire that allowed it to reach, although now I have this coil of extra wire under my dash. Sigh.  So, the moment of truth- play music through the phone.  Yes, it was very quiet and yes, when I had to turn up my stereo to hear the music, there was a loud hiss.  So, I was on the phone to customer service.  They needed a number from INSIDE the remote (not the cigarette lighter portion) to send out the ground loop isolator, but they agreed to send it out free of charge.  A few days later, I noticed that the volume on my phone's music player wasn't turned up... for some reason it reset to a low level when pairing with the Bluetooth?  But when I turned the phone up, I could turn my stereo down, and the hiss all but disappeared.  I will say that the sound is a slight bit "muddier" than the direct connect, it seems like the LiquidAux (or my phone, no way to tell) doesn't output very low frequencies as well, so the bass isn't quite as defined.  Again, that is before I adjusted the car stereo's equalizer to compensate.  I plugged my phone's charger into the charging port on the side of the LiquidAux and that worked fine.So I've owned the product for about three weeks now, and there are a few quirks I've noted that some others have as well.The first is powering on. As others have noted, there is a bit of a "thump" sound through the speakers when the LiquidAux finishes pairing with my phone.  The louder you have your stereo turned up, obviously the worse this is.  But it lets me know that I can start my phone playing.  Of course, I can also have my phone playing through its built in speaker when I get into the car, and as soon as power-up/pairing occurs, the sound will automatically start playing through the speakers.  There is also a slight thump between tracks.The second is the remote.  It takes at least a full second, nearly two, between when I push a button and when the phone does something.  A little annoying, but again, could be my phone's issue, not the product.  Easily overcome by the cool factor of being able to control your music (pause/play, skip) without touching your phone or the stereo.  It won't fast forward or rewind tracks on my phone, just skip them.  I have to press the play button twice to get the phone to play after I start the car.  The mounting strap doesn't really cinch down tight, so the remote was constantly tilting in and out on my steering wheel.  And, when I was doing a lot of steering, my hand would sometimes accidentally hit part of the remote and skip back or forward - not fun when you were in the middle of a 30 minute podcast!  So, I decided to mount it on my turn signal stalk.  Now there are no accidental hits, and it stays put... just looks a little odd, but not any more odd than the big windshield washer paddles on the top of some US car stalks.  Sometimes it doesn't work at all... I can't figure out exactly why but it has something to do with starting the music playing using my phone instead of with the remote, and it has something to do with switching the car off but leaving the key in the ACC position.  It is quite rare that it doesn't work, about twice a week, perhaps.  I've tried pressing the center of the remote, but nothing.  But, if I power on/off the LiquidAux, it works fine again.Finally, the phone.  Admittedly, I didn't buy this product for its ability to take calls.  Really only needed the bluetooth music portion.  I had assumed that if I also had my bluetooth phone headset on, my phone would know which was which and switch when a phonecall came through.  But alas, the Pre holds onto the LiquidAux for dear life.  So when a phone call comes through, you press the phone button on the remote or on the LiquidAux, and, voila, crystal clear sound through the stereo.  Rejoice!  Umm, but, the tiny little mic on the product is behind my shifter, and just inches from the transmission, so the faster I'm going, the less likely anyone can understand what I'm saying even with shouting.  But if the key is in ACC or the car is idling, there are few complaints.  For those who are also wondering how to switch calls to a much more understandable bluetooth headset, it's a pretty complex process that you probably don't want to do in heavy traffic.  Basically I have to switch the phone to speakerphone, hold the power button on the LiquidAux until it beeps through the stereo (I don't know how some other customers missed this, but it tells you when it switches off or on with an audio tone), then turn on my bluetooth headset and hope that the phone sees it and pairs with it before the other party gives up waiting... just kidding, but it can take a little while to pair depending on your phone/headset combination.  If I have my headset on already, I still have to tell the phone to pair with it once it loses the LiquidAux.... but again, that might be a Pre thing.Overall though, very very cool to be able to leave my phone in my pocket, get in and start the car, and 10 seconds later with the push of a button, hear music from my phone as if it were built into the car.Anyway, I'm still waiting on my ground loop isolator to see what issues that fixes - will try to repost when that happens.

I have always wanted a battery case but never could fork over the money to buy one. This is a GREAT product and I am glad I've come across it!It is very simply packaged with straightforward instructions on how to setup, charge and use. I now carry it in my purse as a backup for whenever I need a charge and I'm away from a charger.After unpackaging I immediately connected it to my phone and turned it on. It has 4 LED lights on the back to signify the case's charge level and it lit up as fully charged. At 2:24pm I had 45% battery on my phone. By 4:06pm my phone was at 100% and the case had 50% charge. So it looks as if the case is good for charging a dead phone to 100% battery in one charge lifespan.PROS-Fast charging, both the phone and the case.-Clear indication of how much charge the case has, bright LED lights.-Does not obstruct rear camera.-Side buttons fully functional. Right off the bat, lets talk about radio transmitters. I'll try to keep it short. This is all based on my being a certified Circuit City (RIP) mobile electronics specialist for 9 years and car electronics "buff" for 18 years.In roughly 2005-2006 at the height of satellite radio, the fcc cracked down on all fm transmitters because they would interfere with other cars near you if they had their frequency tuned close to the same frequency as your transmitter.Before this they were really good quality and suitable for all but the fussiest people (audiophiles).After the new restrictions, They were terrible. I mean terrible, like sometimes we had to try different stations because the sirius satellite radio wouldn't transmit the 3 feet from the console to the radio.While the technology has improved tremendously, it is still not the quality or a first choice for most people as far as  sound quality. And No one should go in expecting direct blue tooth, auxiliary cable, or cd quality. I would say it is close to broadcast radio sound quality.Now, I reviewed this product knowing that, and instead focusing on how this model fits into that category.Upon receiving this transmitter for review, the very first thing i noticed, nice and big in the upper right hand corner is a 100% satisfaction Guaranteed.  That's nice, hard to find. Inside you find the unit, instruction booklet and small remote. For some vehicles i could see the remote being needed because of the placement of the cigarette lighter, but for most, probably not needed.Installation is a breeze also. What i didn't see mentioned, You should first use your cars radio and scan for a station that is silent or all static, to help eliminate interference with the transmitter. (if you usually commute a large distance you may have to try a few different ones to find one that doesn't pick up farther from your home.) We usually start looking in the 88.___'s they are usually pretty empty. Once you find that just hold the arrows on the unit to tune the unit to that station found on your car radio.Connect your device via bluetooth or aux cable. You can even use a thumb drive (flash drive) in the usb port to play files stored on it. But wait there's more.... It also features an sd card reader for whoever in the world might use that. (most of us have some laying around from when we used digital camera's and not just our phones) Speaking of phones, you can take and make phone calls also, however in my 2 test vehicles 1 a 2000, the other a 2014, The radio interference made the call nearly useless.I did dock this unitKeeping in mind that fm transmitters/modulators sound quality is lacking,no matter if you spend $150 or buy this.  I scored this item 4 stars, simply because it performs very well given its limitations, and packs a lot of features you don't get with the other brands ive seen and used.I knocked it a star because of the screen, it can be rather hard to see in the daylight, and could stand to use different contrast.

This is a tempered glass screen protector that is much thicker than other screen protectors I've used. It still does not cover the iPhone 6 PLUS surface completely, only the LED part of the screen so it will be more visible on the phone and the edges left uncovered may attract dust if there is a gap between the protector and your case edge. It provides sturdy protection as it is a thick protector and keeps your glass screen free of scratches.As with any screen protector to install it flawlessly some steps need to be followed. I have gotten better with practice. Here is what to do:1. Work on a table in a very well lit area so you can spot teeny-tiny dust particles as they result in frustrating bubbles on your screen protector that won't go away. Lay a clean white sheet of paper down so you don't attract extra dust from table surface. Put phone on top. Make sure your hands are clean and dry. The package suggests washing as you can have surface oils and minute dust particles on your hands.2. Wipe off the screen with the provided alcohol wipe and then with the microfiber polishing cloth included with the package. Anything else leaves tiny fibers on the screen. For good measure start with back and then clean the front so there is no dust anywhere. Lift off any remaining dust or grime with the included dust remover tabs.3. Carefully align the protector to the phone surface. Then, once you're sure the cutouts on protector match up the home button and top cut outs, peel off the backing and realign. Now start laying it down. Since this is a stiff screen protector you will be forced to apply it in one step. Do do this very, very slowly or you'll trap too much air under the protector and realign as you place it down.4. Once the protector is laid down on the surface, let it rest for 1 minute - do NOT apply pressure yet - as the adhesive goes to work slowly covering the screen, you'll see it starting to stick. After a minute, squeeze out any bubbles with the included microfiber polishing cloth, and clean off the screen.I had to lift off and replace this screen protector twice as it is exactly the size of the LED screen on the iPhone 6 PLUS and even if it a little off it shows up when the phone is lit. The second time I did it slipped a little downward and obscured the home button ever so slightly which started giving me problems with Touch ID. The third time was a charm but I managed to get one dust particle on one edge that I did not see and clean off. This protector doesn't really get bubbles since it's stiff but you see the adhesive makes a fine design wherever there is a dust particle trapped underneath.You'll be happy to know if you do need to realign it lifts off clear without a problem and the adhesive does not leave a residue.5. All done.  Enjoy!So now more about the protector. As mentioned earlier, this protector is a bit smaller than needed for 6 plus. It covers the screen but does not cover the entire glass on the phone - the edges are left bare on all sides, it just covers the rectangle of the screen itself. Another Urban Armor Gear protector and an Amazon Basics protector were the same size. Not sure why, as they could certainly have been a little bigger. It also does NOT have rounded glass edges like the product page suggested as it is not big enough to go to the sides of the phone. The dimensions of the iPhone 6 protector and 6 PLUS protector that are shown on the product page are EXACTLY the same so that may be the reason this protector is a little small for iPhone 6 Plus.It came in a foam sleeve but there were already minor scratches on the protector top, wish they could have put another film on that side.The cut outs on this are correctly matched to home button and for the top 2 cut outs. It does not interfere with regular operation of phone or make it less sensitive if you're using fingers. I tested it on many apps and the feedback seemed the same as other screen protectors. I was worried about this one as this protector is much thicker than others. It does make it a little less sensitive to a stylus similar to other screen protectors. If you want to draw with a stylus on their phone, a bare screen will always respond better to pressure if you're trying to do light and dark strokes.It catches more dirt and fingerprints than the bare glass or maybe they're just more visible. I have to constantly clean off my screen. The included microfiber cloth is handy to keep around as it helps keep your phone clean. A spritz with a lens cleaner solution every now and then keeps it shiny and new.Overall, it protects my phone screen more than the thinner protectors but it is so much more visible, like glass on glass - particularly due to its smaller size. The edges may also collect more grime due to this and I may need to go back to the thinner screen protector that was almost invisible on my screen. The tactile feeling of this protector is more like glass, right now I am liking the smoothness but it has to remain scratch free to feel good.

To avoid glare & reflections and use my phone in a rental car, I was looking for a mount which I can attach & remove easily ==> CLIP TO VENT looked like a reasonable idea. This mount can fit air vents but there are limitations. The blades in these vents can be quite deep and the mechanism used by this mount provides a sturdy grip but it is designed to a range of fan vents -- but it won't fit all. In the rental cars I've used it in, I was able to attach it to only 1/2 of the vents, the others had such odd shapes or positions, the mount would not fit.Another positive point for this mount is the increased distance it can provide from the dashboard ==> more room to tilt the phone and room to attach a USB cable to power the phone while driving. Again, the details will depend on your car & dashboard but there I had no problems.The strong points for this mount are the larger room for adjustments which allowed me to avoid glare & reflections and at the same time, there also was room for the USB-cable. Battery life on devices are becoming bigger and last longer, but they don't last forever. This Battery Pack by Maxboost is one of the greatest solution you can use to recharge your devices.Let's start off by the physical appearance of the battery pack. It has 2 usb ports, one producing up to 2.1amps and the other 1.0 amps. In most cases the 1.0A is sufficient enough to charge most phones. The leisure of the 2.1A as an addition is a plus for this battery case. That 2.1A can charge pretty much all tablets that currently on the market. The Otterbox Commuter series is a hard shell case which protects the back and sides, but leaves the screen exposed. It does ship with a screen protector, which protects it from scratches and abrasions, but does not protect it if it is dropped (on the screen). I have a similar case for my HTC Droid, and have been keeping that inside another (soft neoprene) AmazonBasics External Hard Drive Case case as a result of the lack of protection for the screen. This case is nicely fitted, with an opening for the camera lens. It also makes for faster access. Nothing needs to be removed to use the phone, camera or screen. I just do not get the logic of leaving the screen, which is the most delicate part of the phone, exposed. If you share my feelings here, get the Defender OtterBox DEFENDER SERIES for Samsung Galaxy S6 - Frustration-Free Packaging - Black. It is essentially the same case with a hard shell which covers the screen (but which needs to be removed to use it).I generally carry my phone in my bag or briefcase, and so a hard shell case like the Defender makes sense for me. My husband carries his in his hands or pocket, and so prefers a slimmer case. I personally do not like this style of case as well myself, but my husband does. If you are more concerned about the size and weight, the Commuter series is a slimmer, lighter case, but only because it does not have a cover for the screen.

This case looks good and works well too.  This is the third case I've owned with the same basic design.  I like the design because it provides protection for the screen when the case is closed.This case really is made of genuine leather!  The other products I've had said they were made of genuine leather, but they were "PU Leather" which is supposed to be plastic bonded over leather, but is more commonly just plastic.  Either way, PU Leather starts to crack and peel in a few months.  The genuine leather in this case is supple and not super shiny like some leather covers are, and it smells of fresh leather.This case has a hidden magnetic closure and it works OK at holding the cover closed.  It will keep the cover closed in your pocket or purse, but not much more.  The magnetic closure does a really good job of holding the cover open when you flip it all the way around to talk on the phone.  Shieldon makes versions of this case with magnetic flap closures, but my experience with similar cases with the flaps is that they do a better job of holding the cover closed, but they constantly get in the way.This case has two slots for cards (credit card, driver's license, etc.).  I put a credit card in each slot and they fit so tightly that it is difficult to get them in and out.  They might loosen up over time.  The instructions say that if you put in more than 1 card in each slot, the magnetic closure won't work because the extra thickness will keep the cover from closing all the way.  I found that with just one card in each slot, the closure is much weaker than it is with no cards,.  Of course, it might not be a good idea to put credit cards with magnetic stripes right next to that magnet closure.The plastic casing that the phone snaps into holds the phone very well.  It needs to provide a little more protection across the the top and bottom of the phone, though.  They can't put more material around the headphone jack, but they could make the corners thicker.The "kickstand" feature works correctly right out of the box.  The other cases of this style that I've owned were so stiff that they had to be wiggled back an fourth hundreds of times before they loosened up enough to stay folded on their own.

This is my review on the Yubi 3.1 Amp Dual USB wall charger. The charger is designed in a very neat way, it is in the shape of a rectangular prism, where on one end is the company logo and on the other are the two USB Ports which clearly indicate which port is a 2.1 Amp output and which is the 1 Amp output. Now if you don't know much about what these numbers mean they are basically charging outputs, smaller devices such as iPods, cell phones, etc charge at a pretty "normal" rate of about 1 Amp. The other port is for larger devices such as iPads, kindle fires, basically any device that has a pretty large battery that would take an extremely long time at 1 Amp. I tested this with both phones, and tablets and have found that the 2.1 Amps definitely makes a huge difference in charging time, the high output is fantastic because it eliminates the need to leave tablets recharging for such a long time and the beauty about this charger is that it can charge two devices at the same time without losing charging speed. I really like this product, it can be great for travel or regular home use where you need to make the best of outlets and get two USB Ports instead of one. One last neat feature that this charger offers is that the charging prongs fold inward and conceal themselves for ease of carry during travel, definitely a nice addition that makes it easier to carry around!I rate this product 5 stars out of 5 stars!I hope this review has helped you out!I was provided this product for testing and evaluation, my review of this product is completely honest and unbiased and the words stated above are my own.

After seeing how flimsy cover is for the USB charging port on the Galaxy S5 I became concerned that with repeated opening and closing the cover would suffer from fatigue and fail.  It turns out from reading many reports on the various forums that this concern is grounded in actual fact and this is a real problem.Before the wireless charging back was available I purchased the Trendon charger and 2 batteries here on Amazon.  This is also a great product, the batteries seem to be as good as Samsung OEM batteries and the charger while slower than the OEM charger does the job nicely without overheating the batteries.Once I received the wireless system from Amazon (the charging pad and charging back) I have not had to change batteries once.  It is so convenient to top off the battery charge that it's never an issue.  It is nice to have the extra batteries though if I am out in the field and working extensively, it's a good feeling to know that I have plenty of backup power.The wireless pad is rated at 2A and seems to charge very quickly.  It is powerful enough to charge through my Tech21 D3O Tactical Case. And it is just as fast with the protective case on the phone as off.  Or so it seems.I love this system and now that I own the wireless system I could never again be without it.  It is fantastic and highly recommended.  Funny thing is before I owned the system I thought it would be a superfluous waste, why not just plug the phone in and forget about it.  Or swap a fully charged battery from my Trendon kit (also purchased here on Amazon - charger and 2 extra batteries).The reason this is not superfluous is convenience and safety, it's super easy to "top off" the battery and the risk of damaging the charging port cover is eliminated.

Testing this as a free product from the Amazon Vine programFirst question as always, would I buy this?Resounding yes, funnily enough I was just going to buy this very case when it became available to test.Don't let the $10 price tag put you off, an OtterBox it may not be, but for the price it offers great protection.Dual layer with soft rubber inner and hard shell provides great shock and drop protection.High bevel on the leading edges should be enough for good screen protection, but I would always recommend a solid glass protector anyway. Well, you get what you pay for. I own a few OEM HTC batteries because the HTC Rezound is a major battery hog, so having a spare ready-to-go is a must.This charger will NOT fully charge it to 100%.I've tested it many times. You will notice one bar missing on the battery level display.All the batteries I've charged with this only charge it up to 85%.However, if I charge my phone with the USB, it will fully charge the battery to 100%. It is definitely an issue with the charger and not the batteries.85% charged battery is better than nothing, but just keep in mind, you get what you pay for. The charging rate for this charger is also much slower than if you were to charge via USB directly with your phone.There's a large bright LED - it will be lit BLUE when you put it on the socket without a battery mounted; when it is charging, it will turn RED. When it is "fully" (more like 85% charged) charged, it will turn BLUE again.I really wish there was a reliable fully chargeable separate battery charger for the Rezound batteries, but these seem to be the only ones on the market.

7/18/16I am only at unboxing, it is charging, but this is initial review.This is not my first ZeroLemon.  S3, Note2 are my predecessors.  This seems no different.  Besides no removable battery, this is as close as you can get to the same style.  Clip on case with TPU wrap around case.I have also purchased the Incipio offGRID 3700mAh with Qi Charging and have been using that for roughly 3 weeks now.  It is nice, but the ZeroLemon ZeroShock feels much nicer (again initial review, not gotten to put it on yet as it's going through initial standard charge).  It is bigger, but not by much.  I have dropped the Incipio and it has snapped apart and in one case, the phone flew out.  I know this won't happen with this one as it's the same style as my last ones that survived countless abuse.This is not like the ones I've seen in videos with the "u" connector to connect the battery and phone as it's a solid piece, also i need to look into a belt clip.  I'm one of the few it seem to use them, but for office use, I love them.  This actually looks nicer than I expected and have seen.The only plus's I have for the Incipio is the wireless charging, and even that isn't a rapid charge wireless.  I do kind of like the soft round of these back of the Incipio case as well.I will do MUCH more edits to this as I use it. I had many more pictures, but limited to 20.Day 1 Grade: B (anticipation and time of charge is killing me!)----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------7/19/16Put the case on after an overnight charge.  The TPU case is nice and snug!  The phone went from feeling like a TOY out of the box, to a respectable phone with the Incipio, to a VERY secure and well protected beast!Few initial thoughts:1. I am unsure what the gripe about the flaps not staying shut as they have little "tabs" to hold them shut.  They seem like they would hold up just fine.2. There is front facing speaker "ports" on the bottom.  I am unsure why there is one on the left as it does not connect to the right side at all through a channel or anything.  Maybe aesthetics?  There is a few speaker holes at the bottom as well.  I've yet to really try to crank the sound with this case on, but looks like it will be more than adequate.3. There is the recessed 3.5mm headphone jack.  I am wanting to find an adapter that sits flush with the case so I can just leave it in and still use any headphone (rarely do as I find myself using bluetooth FAR more often), but the adapter that comes with this is just odd (Incipio case comes with the same cable).4. All buttons are very "substantial" as they rightly CLICK when you press them.  Both Volume buttons, Power button, as well as the Battery button on back.5. I'm still torn on if I like the LEDs and button on back or front.  With Incipio, I wish they were on back as the wireless charging means you see the bright LEDs all night.  With this, I don't need to worry about wireless charging, so I can keep the screen face down as I had in other cases, but now these LEDs are on back.  This will require more digging to see which I like.  It almost seems as I wish they were reversed -ZeroLemon on front and Incipio on back.  BUT with this case, I don't need to worry about blocking the sound if I do lay face down at night (as I had previously mentioned), there are speaker holes on the bottom (as well as front facing).6.  Case feel is VERY beefy!!  I no longer fear a drop as I did with the Incipio!  I do with the bottom "bezel" looked a bit more high end as it looks cheap almost.  That however, is the extent of my "gripe" as I LOVE the size, look, and feel of this case.  I'm not sure why, but I LOVE the "ZEROSHOCK" etched into the right bottom side of the TPU case.  I normally hate more logos, but it's done just right!  Hole at the top for the microphone is perfectly aligned as well.More review to come as I get to use it more.  Initial thought at this point: FLAWLESS, save no wireless charging or belt clip.Gay 2 Grade: B+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------7/20/16I have gone through my initial phone battery and now seeing how long I can go without touching a charger.  I am a HEAVY user, charging my phone every night, all night.  But this time I want to see how long this will last with actual, heavy use. l also do proper "taining" for battery cells -Charge 100%, use 100%, repeat.  This does make a difference!  When wife and I both had extended batteries (ZeroLemon) for our S3's, I would get 1 1/2 days regularly with heavy use, if she made it one day it was a feat... because she did not cycle her battery as you should.Now for my continued thoughts and findings:*When charging from the battery, the LEDs stay lit.  This was kind of useful when walking around a dark room and not wanting the blinding camera LED, but I do wish they went dim.  This is a very minor "gripe".*Sound is very nice out of this with the bottom and front facing speaker "grills" (although still unsure why the left front facing one is there)I have emailed support inquiring about a belt clip as well.  That, to me, is a big one!  I love the belt clip, especially while in the office and running back and forth.  Not to mention its use as a stand.  *I have tried my Note2 ZeroLemon belt clip, it is right depth and length to fit the phone, but Note2 is too wide.  SO CLOSE!Day 3 Grade: B+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------7/21/16Got 2 full days of GPS to and from work, with BT streaming, LOTS of surfing.  Just normal use.  I got 2 FULL days (up at 6, bed at 11).  Roughly 2 full charges on initial run.  Quite pleased.  That said, I did notice the charging flap issue a bit after all night of charging.  It is closing fine, but had to leave it pressed in for some time before it would stay.  Odd for their products as the previous ones I've had held the flap shut with the case holding it snug in the "ridges".  This version does not have those on either side of the tabs.  Put those back in and this goes away!Also, I decided to put a screen protector back on.  "Galaxy S7 Screen Protector Glass (Full Screen Coverage), amFilm Bye-Bye-Bubble Samsung Galaxy S7 Tempered Glass Screen Protector [NOT S7 Edge] Case Friendly Screen Protector 2016 [Lifetime Warranty] "  https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B01CDVZAH6/ref=oh_aui_detailpage_o03_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1is the one I use.  GREAT CS, good item, has fit with every case wife and I have used (4 total).  This fits this case with ease.
