First off, I want to say that I got this phone to simply text and make phone calls. I do NOT go on the internet, take photos, or anything else. I just needed a very basic phone for communication only.Anyway, the keyboard on this phone is excellent! I used to have an LG Xenon, which had a full keyboard. Because it had a full keyboard, I texted extremely slowly. As soon as I got this Blackberry Pearl, however, I was able to text very quickly. Each key has 1-2 letters on it, which makes the keyboard a lot smaller and therefore easier to text. It has a dictionary, so it knows what words you are trying to text MOST of the time. Sometimes it gets it wrong, but most of the time I have no issues. The main word it mixes up is "see" and "are".When it comes to phone calls, I never have an issue with that. The battery lasts throughout the entire call. I make calls that are a good 2hours sometimes. The Blackberry has no problem with battery life. The sound is perfectly fine. I can hear the other person very clearly. My calls are NEVER dropped on this phone. It is great.Once in awhile I will take photos. The photos are good quality, but they honestly do not compare to my digital camera. If I need to take a quick snap shot, I will use my phone. If I need to take a good quality photo, then my digital camera is the answer.One thing that bugs me about this phone is the alarm system. You are limited to ONE alarm, which really stinks because I could really use at least 2 alarms.Lastly, the main reason I am giving ths onlly 3 stars is due to the fact that the faulty USB port makes it difficult to charge at times. My phone will be nearly dead, so I go to plug it in and the phone does not seem to realize that it is plugged in. I take it out, plug it back in, remove it yet again, plug it back in once more, and get nothing. I will remove the battery, place it back into the phone, and still get no charge signal. I then decide to remove the battery, remove my SIM card, replace the battery, try yet again, and SOMETIMES I will finally get a charge signal on the phone. It's one of those annoying things where you have to play around with everything until you can finally get it to charge up. I am not the only one with this problem. If you look it up online, other people have the same exact problem with their Blackberry Pearls. It is a result of the USB port being faulty, which makes sense because when I plug my phone in, the USB port seems to be slightly movable, when it should be solid and stable.Anyway, if the USB port was not an issue, I would have given a higher rating. It is a great phone, but you may want to consider getting a battery charger, just in case you have a tough time struggling with the USB port like me.

This is a very nice set of 3 spare USB chargers.  Mine have a stripe that is a nice shade of green.  The best thing about these is that they are more affordable than some are.  You can use these to charge just about any electronic gadget you have, such as phones, tablets, speakers, headphones, smart watches, etc., as long as they can be charged by a USB port.  If your gadget has a cord with a regular size USB on one end of it, you should be able to charge it with these chargers, as long as it is capable of being charged.These chargers seem to be of equal quality to a name-brand charger I paid quite a bit more for.  The output of these chargers is 1.0A, so even though they will charge a more power hungry device like an iPad, the charge may take longer to complete, since an iPad would prefer over 2A; however, if your device is normally charged with 1A you probably won't see any time difference between the charger that came with your device and these chargers.I charged 2 Bluetooth speakers and a smart watch with these chargers with no problems.  I would recommend these if you need some extra USB chargers for "just in case."  Again, they are more affordable than many.  I saw the same type of charger at a national chain store that's known for low prices, and they were well over $5 each there at the checkout counter.I received a discount on these chargers in exchange for my honest opinion.

Wow. I wasn't sure if I would like this case, as there are so many now and they always seem to have some annoying problem. However, this Lenmar case is the best I have seen by far. Especially if you are switching over from Android and have a bunch of micro USB car chargers and stuff, it's nice that this case works with those. There is a nice segmented green LED display on the back of the case that shows how much battery power is remaining and it is easy to turn on and off to charge the phone when needed. After a couple weeks, I am still extremely impressed with this case, and have recommended it to a lot of coworkers already. I use a hands free all the time, so I welcomed the chance to see how this one performs. I find it to be attractive, with a good feature set (if one's phone supports them), and incredibly great for hands free use.How well it works: Excellent in all respects. The sound is clear and loud at both ends. This is the best speakerphone I have used.  I think the speaker size makes the incoming call sound better. I have an Android HTC Amaze 4G, running on T-Mobile.Design: The hands free device is a handsome black with silver appointments. It clips onto the windshield visor. On the lower side (as mounted on the visor) is a large round speaker. There is a ring surrounding the speaker that one can press to activate the voice functions. A small button functions as the answer / hang up / redial switch. There are two buttons on the left and right underside (close to the windshield) that control volume. On the left side is the on/off switch and the small microphone hole. The right side has a mute button and the charging port.  Overall, this unit is a lot thicker than my Motorola Roadster 2, to leave room for the speaker.Included items: The hands free comes with a single micro-USB cord and a cigarette lighter USB charger. Documentation is sparse, but adequate to set up the device. A slightly more in-depth manual is available online.Features: The device includes an auto on/off, using the sound of the driver door to function; it works really well. It will connect to voice commands if the phone is so equipped. The hands free also includes the capability to put a call on hold and then retrieve it (again, phone dependent). In fact, a lot of the features of this device are dependent upon exactly what kind of phone one is using. The device will support having two phones paired to it, good for those times when the wife is driving my car. One of the big advertising points is the "2W" speaker. It has support for phone audio, which is fine for GPS, but kind of silly to use for music of any sort (although it sounds good for such a small speaker). The phone has simple (but very good) voice instructions to accomplish basic tasks. For example, one can press the ring and tell it to pair with a new phone, or call out the battery level. This is common with every Jabra hands free that I have used lately, and it makes it easier to do basic maintenance tasks.Drawbacks: The online manual does not include the meanings of the various colors for the light that is in front of the answer button - the images are there, but all images are identical. Not too big a deal, but still, they should have been more careful with the documentation.Summary: The best I've seen yet. Highly recommended.

In the package you will find a mobile grip 2 holder, a mounting pedestal with adjustment ring, and 80mm adhesive disk along with an alcohol cleaning pad. Mounting pedestal is designed with a multi-surface sticky suction base and an adjustable mounting arm with an universal 17mm ball joint connection. The specific model I received was MG279 with a deluxe enhanced suction base which I found to be the strongest I ever tested. I tested it on different smooth and textured surfaces, in vertical and horizontal and upside down positions. It was amazing how it got attached to any surface like a cement, and after a number of tests when I felt the sticky base was not as strong anymore - I just rinsed it under a warm water to rejuvenate it's sticky force. As a matter of fact, Arkon even included a warning with their detailed instructions to use provided sticky disk if you have a soft vinyl or fine leather dashboard surface so you don't damage it or leave a permanent imprint. Of course, sticking it to a windshield is another option, or if you visit Arkon webpage you find a dozen of other mounting options with everything from a drink cup to a seat rail or headrest and bike handle mounts.The mounting arm has a multi-angle adjustment which is locked in place by easy grip thumb screw, and the universal ball joint connection allows 360deg rotation and compatibility with other accessories using the same joint. Of course, the star of this car mount is the new Mobile Grip 2 holder. I can tell you with certainly, it's probably one of the most minimalistic and slimmest low profile mounts I have tested. The spring loaded mechanism allows single hand operation by inserting the phone angled and pushing to extend it. The side grips are made out of rubbery material that will not scratch your phone/case, and they have flexible tips with ridges for a more secure hold. On the back where you have a connection to universal ball joint and a thread for adjustment ring, I was very pleased to see a permanently attached rubber insert. One of the problems with ball joint mount is that friction of plastic on plastic can loosen up the connection, especially under a weight of the attached phone. Here, by adding a rubber insert Arkon enhanced the grip to make it extra secure once you tighten the adjustment ring.You can clearly see they paid attention to every single detail, and design feels very solid. One detail you simply can't miss is a little rubber stopper on the side of the mount. Once you remove it, you realize right away that you are dealing with universal 1/4" camera mount screw opening for use with most of the camera tripods. Even so I used my own for testing, you can find Arkon offering a similar design in their catalog. The screw hole is all metal, no worries about stripping a plastic thread. It worked quite well and considering very slim design of MG2 the rear camera on my phone was not obstructed and ready to be used for picture or video taking. Btw, this is also an advantage if you want to use your phone as a dashcam mounted on your dashboard.Overall, I was VERY impressed with this Mobile Grip 2, and for the price of under $20 it's one great value especially if you choose MG279 model with deluxe sticky suction mount. The versatility of its slim minimalistic design, the quality of the build, the easy of one hand operation, the ability of keep your rear camera unobstructed, and flexibility to route power cable and having all the power/volume buttons in the open puts it on my list of high recommendation. As a matter of fact, I find this Mobile Grip 2 to be equally acceptable for use in the car and at home on any surface and any room. And of course, 2 year warranty is just a bonus!

This is definitely the best universal bracket I've used, and for an awesome price! I own 2 BMWs (E46 sedan, E86 Z4 M), and have one installed in both for my iPhone 4 with bumper. For a universal mount, this bracket is extremely low profile and looks like it's made for the phone. It fits perfectly with the bumper on, and it also fits my BlackBerry Curve or Style without adjusting. Adjustments are simple, just slide out the arm out and move it to the next notch. The notch system is way more secure and sturdy than those spring loaded clamps, I really hate those bulky clamp mounts.I suggest getting a Pro Clip mount for your car, then a slim swivel adapter, then attach this bracket and you're all set! For my Z4, I have the mount right side up. For my E46, I have the arms upside down so I can slide the phone UP into the holder, and when I want to quickly remove the phone I just pull the phone down.

About a month ago I was offered a free Kingston MobileLite Wireless G3 Pro 64GB unit for review and I accepted.One of the issues with current smartphones and tablets is relatively limited onboard storage. This is especially true for Apple devices, none of which have any kind of on-board removable storage option. Some of the most popular Android devices have removable storage and so do Windows devices, in the form of MicroSD slots and cards. No matter whether you have Apple, Android or Windows devices, the Kingston MobileLite Wireless G3 can drastically supplement and increase the storage of all portable devices that can access standard SMB network shares. While the MobileLite Wireless G3 provides storage that is not technically on-board your phone or tablet device, it is as portable as the mobile devices themselves and can be used while on flights, trains, automobiles or just about anywhere, including places where no outside network signals are available. I consider it to be portable local cloud storage. As it is not actual cloud storage, it does not use your mobile data plan. Additionally, the MobileLite Wireless G3 has a 6,700mAh lithium-ion battery which powers both the MobileLite Wireless G3 itself and can also power or charge your phone or tablet multiple times. It can also power portable USB hard drives which can also be accessed through the MobileLite.The MobileLite Wireless G3 has a full size SD card slot, which can be used to access SD and MicroSD cards directly, and a USB type A port, which can be used for USB memory drives, USB card readers and USB hard drives. The MobileLite G3 can read/write from/to SD/MicroSD/USB Memory sticks/USB Hard drives formatted with FAT, FAT32, NTFS and exFAT file systems. Additionally, some models of the MobileLite Wireless G3, including the Pro model I was given for review, include 64GB of internal storage. You can access a card in the SD slot, a drive plugged into the USB port and access the internal storage (if equipped) at the same time. This is accomplished through the onboard MobileLite Wireless G3 WiFi. The MobileLite Wireless G3 is equipped with 2.4GHz 802.11b/g/n and 5GHz 802.11ac networking. If you have a modern phone or tablet equipped with 802.11ac, you'll benefit from super fast transfers, pretty much limited by the speed of whatever storage device you plug into the MobileLite G3.As I have Android devices, my review will focus on my experience using my 2014 Samsung Galaxy Note 10.1. The MobileLite G3 is managed though the "MobileLite Wireless" app (red icon), available for download from the Google Play store. Kingston should have named the app "MobileLite G3" to avoid any confusion because Kingston has another app named "Kingston MobileLite" (blue icon) which will not work with the MobileLite G3. When you first use the MobileLite G3, it will start up with an open network. If your wireless device is already on a WiFi network, you will have to manually disconnect from that network and select either the 2.4GHz or preferably the faster 5GHz (if you have 802.11ac) "MLWxxx" access point. Then start the MobileLite app. From the MobileLite app, you can set up security on the MobileLite G3 wireless network access point. You can also browse the whatever storage devices you have plugged into the MobileLite G3 - they are listed and accessed individually. You can also initiate a back up of your phone or tablet's camera roll, contacts or calendar. While the app can do all of these things, I do not like the design of the app, and prefer to access the MobileLite G3 using a dedicated Android file manager like Root Explorer - which luckily, you are able to do.One of the cooler aspects of the MobileLite G3 is that it can be used to bridge to your home internet connection so you can access the internet through the MobileLite G3 without having to disconnect from it and reconnect to your home wireless network. This can be done either by setting it up to access your wireless network using the MobileLite app, or you can actually plug it into your home network as it has a built in ethernet port. So technically, it can be used as a portable wireless access point to a wired network.The MobileLite G3 is charged though the built in MicroUSB port. The MobileLite G3 does not include a charger and assumes that you have an available USB charger.In my opinion, the documentation is poor. Its fine for a person like myself that knows my way around things, but a novice will very likely have trouble with this device.One thing you cannot do, and I was surprised at this, was to plug the device into your computer directly via USB to access the built in storage or the devices plugged into it. It does not mount when plugged directly into a PC. It only charges.In usage, I was able to stream episodes of various programs and movies I had copied to an SD card in full 1080p HD, no problem. Playback was smooth and glitch free. Seeking was almost instantaneous. Because it was so fast, I never felt like I was accessing an external device. I also used it with a portable, USB hard drive. I had no problem accessing all of the files I stored on it from my tablet. In my opinion, the MobileLite G3 works really well.Overall, I like the Kingston MobileLite G3 Pro. It does what it says it does. I can see many ways in which it will be useful to me when travelling, both expanding the storage that my phone and tablet can access and for it's ability to power and charge them. It can also be very useful to photographers, for example, to make photos or video carried on an SD or compact flash card (though a USB card reader) instantly available to multiple people, wirelessly, while on a shoot. I think the weakest points are the documentation (or lack thereof) and the design of the Android app.

I was provided a Steepower 12W / 2.1A Dual-Port Car Charger with Built-in Micro USB Coiled Cord for testing and review and was asked only to give my honest review, so this is what I found.What You Should Know Before You Buy: "Dual-Port" for this cable means one built-in Micro USB cable and one normal USB port, not two actual ports. Had no problem charging two phones at once, including an iPhone 5. Had no problem charging an iPad. Each port has a maximum of 1 Amp output. Has a built-in cord with a Micro USB plug, so no extra cable needed for Micro USB devices.Detailed Review: Look - The product came in a hanging display box with product information on the package. Inside the box was the Car Charger and a small instruction manual (which is mostly warranty information).  It is a standard looking car charger with a coiled cable with a Micro USB plug and a standard USB port for charging other devices.  It has an illuminated blue ring on the front of the charger.  It looks good with a nice, solid build. Use and Performance - I went on a road trip this weekend with the family and replaced my usual dual port AmazonBasics car charger with the Steepower for the trip.  I used it the entire weekend to charge my iPhone 5 from the USB port and to charge my wife's Micro USB port phone with the included coiled cord.  It worked perfectly for both with consistent and fast charging results.  I also tried plugging in our iPad 2 and it also charged just fine.  The coiled up cable is convenient when you need it and stays out of the way when you don't.  Everything worked great.Conclusion:Overall, the Steepower 12W / 2.1A Dual-Port Car Charger performed perfectly during my road trip.  It had no problem charging two phones at once and was also able to charge our iPad.  The coiled cord made it compact for car use.  The performance is great and it is built very well. If you need a Micro USB car charger with an extra USB port for an additional device, this one is worth a look. Highly Recommended!

I recently have tried a variety of headsets, including the Plantronics 510 the Jawbone, and the Motorola H700 and H710, all to replace my trusty, old, cheap and very durable (so durable I gave up waiting for it to break, so that I could find something with better sound quality) Motorola H300.Among all these, I decided to keep the Motorola H710. It is _nearly_ perfect in all aspects, the only minor drawback compared to its predecessor, the H700, is that it has a micro-USB charging connector rather than a mini-USB one (I have tons of mini-USB peripherals, including my cell phone, camera, etc...)The Jawbone CAN be a good and comfortable fit, if the anatomy of your ear agrees with it. I was lucky that way, it was actually the most comfortable headset I have used. Even for me, though, it was not "instant love", it took a bit of training to learn how to put it on correctly in a hurry (i.e. when receiving an incoming call). The ear loops (4 different ones included in the package) are very stiff, and I can see how they would easily break after a while. The sound quality was excellent while in my car driving - even across a noisy concrete bridge there would be no way for the other party to tell. Also, incoming sound quality was great - the volume adjusts based on background noise, and the ear plugs are rubberized and block most other noise. It is also a good-looking headset; it matched my phone (a black Nokia N95 8GB) quite well. My main two issues with it were the charging connector (a proprietary one, and only a USB charging cable was included; also not that easy to plug/unplug), and the fact that the power-on time (from off to connected) was a bit too long (timing it, I got anywhere from 9 to 13 seconds..). I prefer to keep my headset turned off when not in use (battery life, connection-time issues.. etc.)The Plantronics was also great with respect to sound quality; I would say though that it did not quite beat the Jawbone while driving in a car.. (I did not try either one while walking outside in wind though). I did not quite like the large size though (my pockets are full enough already with my cell phone, keys, wallet...), and as someone mentioned, it would seem that it could easily wear out after prolonged pocket-use... Finally, I did not like the proprietary charging connector. (Give me standard mini-USB please; or else micro-USB will work too).The Motorola H700 and H710 were for me the answer to nearly every grievance I have with bluetooth headsets. You unfold the microphone boom to power it on (and automatically answer any incoming call); fold it back to power off. Simple, elegant. No confusion about whether or not the headset is already powered on or off when an incoming call arrives (I.e. no "Do I need to hold the power button down for a while to power it on, or a simple tap to just answer?" quandries). The power-on-and-connect is also one of the fastest I have seen on any headset (about the same as my old H300, and way faster than either the Jawbone or the 510). Sound quality is exceptional in either - though maybe more so with the H710 (better noise reduction, and, like the Jawbone, it adjusts your ear volume automatically to accommodate for background noise). Since you would normally keep these turned off, as opposed to leaving them on standby, a single charge will normally last you for weeks and weeks (depending on usage of course). Another plus is that they use industry standard charging connectors (mini-USB and micro-USB, respectively), so you can easily share wall/car/computer chargers with other peripherals. Finally, they are an easy fit - no one should have issues getting them on in a hurry (though for me, I actually preferred the "firm" fit of the Jawbone...).My only issue with the H700 (and this has been fixed in the H710) is an extremely short range; if I left my phone in my pocked while talking on the headset, I would hear some subtle crackling; take the phone out and hold it in my hand and it was OK. While OK for "car use", I ended up returning it and getting the H710 mainly for this reason.Good Luck in your own quest for perfection. (Nerdvana?)

I don't like to have thick cases on my phone, primarily because I slide it in/out of my back pockets a lot. Bulky = awkward to be able to do that. OtterBox cases have been excellent in years past, so I thought I would give this one a shot, with the thinner but protective case. As such, it works quite well. The shimmer of this particular case is much more emphasized on the case than what shows in the photo. You can see through the case to the name on the back, but you're peering through quite a bit of glitter. The case is sturdy and seems to be doing it's job well, though thankfully, I haven't yet had need of the protectiveness. The only downside to this case is that the buttons are a pain. Trying to double-click the power button to take a picture is difficult and awkward, and the volume buttons cannot easily be done with just one hand. Were it not for the button difficulties, I would have given the case 5 stars. However, they are just too much of a struggle to easily use. Overall, however, this is a decent case that protects without being terribly bulky. If you don't mind the button issue, then this case would be worth having.

Cell phone wallets are my absolute favorite type of cell phone cover. I feel that they actually offer more protection for my phones than regular cases as they close and cover the screen of the phone as well. This is especially good for women who keep they cell phones in their purses where they may come into contact with keys or any number of items capable of scratching or damaging your screen.Most wallet phone holders are opened like a book with a strap that goes around the case and snaps closed either in the from or in the back via a magnetic button and they are fairly bulky. This wallet phone holder is much different in that it conforms to the shape of your phone and there is no overlap. It is all in one piece. The case and the wallet. The phone is simple to place in the case and simple to take out if needed. There is a lot where you can put credit cards or drivers licenses or even money, if you wanted to travel light and not have to take your entire purse along.This is a very nice case that offers great protection for your phone.

Since I never use the CD player on my car, I found that this particular mount worked out pretty well in my unique use case. As far as mounting goes, it does its job fairly well, and passed the test by surviving a four-hour drive through desert roads last week.The mount works via a strong rare-earth magnet attached to the mount's face (I have no idea how this harms the phone long-term, however) and the mount itself comes with two sets of plates to use, one of which was a flat rectangular one that I slide below my iPhone 6's case. The mount is sturdy and holds the phone surprisingly well.One caveat though: if you have a navigation system, the wing nut used to secure the rubber part that goes in the CD tray blocks your view quite a bit. The Moov Now is essentially a "tracker" that works with the smartphone app to provide training goals and tips. The app is like an overlord trainer that tries to push you harder to reach your goals. Unfortunately, it is mainly focused on running with little to no training guides/exercises for biking or swimming. The data collection for running is OK at best. I wouldn't replace my Garmin with the Moov Now. The Moov Now really isn't even a fitness tracker, it is more of a tool for use with the app so you can use various exercises/training routines to improve your running. The biggest drawback is I need to lug around my smartphone to run the app while I wear the "tracker". Or I can just use my Vivoactive HR on my wrist which is an activity tracker and GPS tracker and forgo the "tips" that the app provides.Overall: 4/5 stars

The result is I can look them and the audience in the eyes instead of looking down or up like most people do during web conferences. I agree with some of the reviews that this case isn't exactly what you would expect from the pictures and description, but it is still a great case for the money.  The color is NOT black, but more of a very dark, off-color blue.This is NOT a shock-proof case intended to provide any significant drop protection!  What it will protect from is "rub wear" from being in your pocket or purse and setting the phone down in between use (or small drops.)  The case has a decent thickness bumper around the entire perimeter for corner protection.The front has just enough of a lip around the screen to protect the glass when face down unless there is something like gravel on the surface.  You will find the same lip of protection on the back surrounding the camera lens - enough to protect from the thousands of times you're going to be setting the phone down.  The cutouts for the stylus, mini USB, and earphone jack are over-sized allowing easy access.  The power and volume button covers are perfectly recessed and function with tight tolerances, but some may not like that you have to hunt for them being recessed.This case feels good in your hand and is not slippery or difficult to hold on to when in calls.  It is not so "grippy" that is will interfere with sliding the phone into your pocket.Bottom line, I do recommend this case for basic protection of your Note 4 - can't beat it for the price in what I've seen so far.  I'll be reviewing some of the other cases available when they arrive.

I bought that Kensington LiquidAUX unit over a year ago, I don't think Kensington's even making it anymore... I tried to find it again recently and couldn't find it for a decent price. I bought it for right around $40 as well, I think the MSRP was no more than $50. Now that I've found it again I'm almost tempted to pick it up from Amazon in case mine ever breaks or as a future gift.The Kensington unit has a couple of advantages over similar units, besides just being way cheaper than some of them. Did you notice that remote pictured on the product page? Yep, it's a wireless remote with Play, Forward, Rewind, and call buttons. The whole unit got a lot of bad reviews when it launched because of the iPhone/iPod's crappy Bluetooth stack (no support for skip/rw BT buttons) but they work very well w/any phone with proper A2DP support. I used the kit with a Sony Ericsson dumbphone before upgrading to my HTC EVO.Initially the call button didn't do anything when paired with my EVO but after the Froyo update (which brought a wealth of Bluetooth stack improvements) it now works like any standard Bluetooth hands-free dialing button. The remote uses a small watch battery and I've only replaced it once in over a year of use (cost me $0.50). The main Bluetooth receiver unit plugs into your 12V adapter obviously so you never have to worry about recharging it, that's another advantage over the Scosche.The mic isn't that great though... I drive a fairly small and fairly quiet car (2-door '07 Civic) and I still struggle sometimes when speaking over it, if you speak loudly it's fine for brief calls but for any call that's gonna take more than 5 min. I just pick up my phone... Or I put it on speaker so I can slip the phone into my shirt pocket and use it's microphone, the EVO's speaker is loud enough.The sheer convenience for music playback is great though, it remembers my device (whether it be the EVO, an iPod touch, or my old phone) just fine so I never have to re-pair it, and it can remember multiple devices as well. It can even pair to multiple devices at once, when I had the SE phone I'd pair it up for phone functions and I'd simultaneously pair the iPod for music playback.I used to have a dock and use a standard 3.5mm cable to the aux-in and whatnot but the convenience of just getting in the car and hitting Play on the remote is just awesome. It comes with a lil' strap/holder so you can mount the remote on your steering wheel if you want btw (I velcro'd it down so it looked sleeker and less aftermarket tho).It also comes with an extension cable for the 3.5mm cord (tho the port is right below the 12V port in my car), AND a 5V USB port in case you want to charge any device, so you don't have to unplug the unit, it basically doubles as a car charger. Audio quality is great, I compared it to a straight wired connection and I could only detect a slight dropoff in bass, no skipping or hiss and volume is loud enough and can be independently controlled on the phone or your stereo (except w/an iPhone/iPod which locks the volume, again, crappy BT stack/implementation on Apple's part).All in all, I highly recommend it, for music playback primarily (in a car that lacks built-in BT obviously). For having long convos thru the built-in mic, not so much.

This phone does exactly what I wanted it to do: provide me with a way to use an Android phone app on wi-fi so I don't burn up minutes on a SIM card. Yes, the keys are very tiny. And it does have a limited  internal memory. But I didn't buy it to use it as a computer - I have a tablet and two computers for that. I just wanted a cheap, unlocked phone that could maybe do a few tricks if I was bored. And this one delivers!  If you're comparing it to an I-phone or some other (expensive) phone, yeah you're going to be disappointed. And programming it is a pain - the keys are so small. But for a first smartphone, you can't beat it. The sellers were very quick to respond when I thought I had a problem with it out of the box. They offered me a refund/exchange immediately, very promptly and with absolutely no problem. If you want a phone that sings and dances and does everything else besides make phone calls, buy a more expensive phone. But if you just want a decent smartphone that is unlocked and has wi-fi, this one will do just fine. I have yet to try it with a SIM card, so I don't know if I will experience the issues with connectivity (AT&T) that others encountered. The provider I plan to use uses T-Mobile's network and their prepaid plans are MUCH better than anything the big guns offer so I'm going to give them a try. It did come preloaded with  the Android 2.1 OS package so that was a plus. The screen is crisp and beautiful, by the way. I think it's a very user-friendly phone, once you get it set up with those tiny keys. I love it! Five stars for being a generally good bargain, and easy to use too!UPDATE:  Just wanted to check back in and let everyone know that this phone worked perfectly just as soon as I inserted and activated my SIM card. I'm still very happy with it generally and think it's a great buy. I was able to unload my Big Bloat cell phone company and go with a much smaller cell phone provider  at about 15% of the cost of the Big Bloat company - and no "required data plan", either!!!! This phone is definitely worth the money and will SAVE you money too if you do a little research and hook up with a decent cell phone provider.  I have had no issues at all with this phone and would recommend it highly. But be aware that it does have the little tiny keys so it probably would not be easy to navigate if you have big fingers!

PROS:PROTECTION:  This has to be Excellent.  This is not something you can roll up... it is a thicker glass like object.  If you expect your phone to get some rough treatment... this seems to be an excellent choice.  If you have shattered your phone glass more than once... get this one.APPLICATION:  EASY... so easy.  Trust me... take your time, follow the EASY instructions.  Do it under a nice strong desk lamp and be sure to use the sticky tape to remove any stray pieces of dust before removing the film and putting it down.SMOOTH SURFACE:  Some of the better softer shields have an orange peel like surface that detracts from some of the detail of better pictures.  Kind of makes them look a little grainy.  This one does not... (However, see below)WARRANTY:  Excellent  but not had a chance to cash in on it.Small scratches do not seem to interfere too much.CONS:BLUE HAZE in Direct Sun or Bright light.  The glass has a blue tinge to it and looks like a piece of plexiglass over it.  In lower light it is clearer though.  This phone has an excellent display.  in light this screen protector detracts a little.  It is not too noticeable after a while.SUPPORT:  I am not sure about support.  I have tried to contact them twice from the address given at their website, but no response. (5/31/2016 and 6/3/2016)  There was one tiny blemish or slight gouge on part of lens that I noticed immediately after inspecting on install.  My guess is it would probably be better to do it from here.  I do not notice the small scratch...  in fact... I might not think it was worth the effort to replace it.This also means that through wear and tear...  if you make a small scratch, it won't detract too much.In my opinion this is an amazing deal for a superior product.  In normal light, the display is crystal clear with no distortions.  It is rock solid, durable, forgiving, and reasonably easy to install.. for even a novice.  Just watch the video and patiently take your time... get it lined up, then tape it as you want it.  Despite any haze that happens in bright light, I can still see the screen just fine.  In retrospect... the finer details of a smartphone screen are lost in bright light anyway.  I wish I had tried the screen in sunlight before I put the screen on to have a comparison.

The digitizer I received works fine, no problems at all.Be aware this does not include adhesive. The screen was shattered on the Aria I repaired and it rendered the adhesive useless because it had glass shards stuck to it. I used some 3M double sided tape which was somewhat of a hassle to rim the long thin side pieces. If I need to do this job again I will be sure to get the correct pre-cut adhesive strips, well worth a couple of dollars in my opinion.Also be aware that the LCD must be CAREFULLY removed to replace this digitizer because the digitizer cable attaches under the LCD. The LCD is held in place with adhesive and it is suggested to use heat to remove it. Heat will make the adhesive release easier, but I did not use it on mine since the LCD and digitizer were already broken and I did not care if they got more broken.Also, when reassembling, before you replace any of the yellow covers, remember to install the speaker. At this point immediately after speaker installation I chose to test everything prior to reassembly. The speaker on my Aria has 2 springy prongs that push it away from the contacts that make it work. During this test the speaker did not initially sound. I then lightly(!) squeezed together the top of the phone and this made the speaker sound. Once the back cover and 4 tri-lobe screws are installed, this serves to squeeze everything into place so the speaker works as normal.

Very amazed by this "budget" phone! Works wonderfully for the price you pay. As soon as it was received and charged, powered it on and was greeted with the "Update" option to Loli 5.0.2 already ready to go! A great sign already. This case is not bad for an inexpensive waterproof case, but you do have to be a little careful. I tested it in a bowl of water with a papertowl in it so I could check for moisture. The first time I didn't quite have it clicked together perfectly so it leaked a bit. I then dried it off and tried again, this time resealing it better. And, I submerged in the bowl for about 10 minutes and things appeared dry inside afterwards. So, just be very, very careful that you have it properly clicked together and all of the ports closed properly. It would be easy to think you have everything snapped together properly only to find out your phone got wet. So, take plenty of time putting it on the phone.It's not very bulky for a waterproof case which is really nice. Using the buttons can be a little difficult, but that is the case with many water proof cases.My main complaint was it was really hard to take it apart after the test. Once I finally pried it open, if I had a phone in there instead of a paper towel it would have gone flying across the room. So, I knocked off a star for that. Be very careful getting the case off.It does do the job if you are careful getting your phone in and out of it. I was given a discount on this case in exchange for a fair and honest review. However, that did not influence my review. I have also attached a few photos for you as well.

It's a battery charger for a Galaxy Note 2. Slip the battery in the compartment and it charges.  It is also a stand for the Galaxy Note 2. I bought a used Sprint Samsung Galaxy S II Epic 4G Touch (SPH-D710), and, just as I expected, it shipped with a junk battery. It appeared to be an OEM Samsung battery, but with the way the knock-off batteries look these days it can be tricky to tell. In any event, it was toast. Slightly bulged...even smelled horrible. Two hours life, max.I didn't want to take a gamble on a battery purchase, knowing that odds are good that an "OEM" battery will turn out to be phony, and aftermarket batteries are generally less than great (try [...] for a quick comparison). So, naturally, I prepared myself for the hefty price sticker and called around for a genuine Samsung battery. I wasn't able to procure one from a local Best Buy, Fry's, RadioShack, or two Sprint retailers (one of the corporate stores said they could have one shipped, but I wanted a battery NOW).On account of the positive Amazon reviews for this battery, I elected to order a couple Anker batteries as backups, knowing that I had a second Epic Touch (used) on the way for my fianc, and that there would be a good chance the OEM batteries would be unacceptable  anyway (due to age and because I have a hunch the ICS OTA update trashed a lot of batteries, encouraging people to offload their handsets on auction sites).Luckily, my Anker batteries came a day ahead of schedule, so I only had to suffer the stinky old Samsung battery for a day (plenty of time for me to install AOSP Jelly Bean 4.1.1, alpha 6).At first glance, this is an impressive product for the money spent. Packaging is worthy of display at a brick-and-mortar, FWIW (I will upload a couple pics). But if you're looking for a resale opportunity, note there are a couple Amazon-branded inserts inside the box. As others have written, the battery is a hair fatter than the OEM, but this is of no consequence to me as my cover snapped right back on with little fuss.There's an informative instructional sheet included that I think would be very useful for someone with a limited understanding of battery idiosyncrasies.I didn't purchase the phone new, so I have no basis for comparisons outside of what I've read on several online forums. This review isn't even pseudoscientific, just bear in mind. I'm not even running a stock ROM, so what do I know about anything?After only a day, I have to state I'm well pleased. With moderate usage, the phone ran more than 16 hours (about seven of which appeared to be in a deep sleep mode, while I was also in deep sleep). By morning, the phone showed better than 20% life remaining. I watched twenty minutes of videos on YouTube, then decided to put her back on the ol' charger with 15% remaining.If this is anything like most batteries, I'd bet it won't attain its max capacity until it's been through two or three charge/discharge cycles (100% to ~ 15-20% charge, back to 100%, cycle repeat). Phone calibration over time may also factor in, too. Ultimately, I think my experience will improve slightly in the shorter term, before the battery starts to diminish capacity in the longer term. I will be sure to update my experiences in the coming months by editing or adding to this review.I just checked my battery (I'm only on my second full charge) and pulled out my calculator; my trending suggests I will see just over 21 hours with limited usage, assuming no deep sleep state, and a 100-to-0 discharge. That should translate into real-world results that are perfectly acceptable to me.10/28/2012 update:A month later, and I still like this battery. After about a week of use, I flashed the phone back to the latest stock ROM (FH13). Battery life improved considerably. Epic Touch now runs about twice as long as my old Moto Photon, even though the Photon was originally rated with a greater talk time (though the phone does spend the bulk of its time in a standby state). Fianc's Epic Touch, with its stock Samsung battery, seems to get similar battery life. Still nothing scientific about my review, but I can get through the day very, very easily. Often times I have ~50% battery life after 15 hours off charger.

ProsThe shell the phone performs very well.  It is built with a hard plastic and a rubbery layer. It has 2 pieces; a front side and a back side that snugly fits over the edges of the front side.  The fit is so snug that i am confident it will come off in an everyday accidental drop.  To remove the case you'd need to pry around the edge of the  seems with a narrow tool like a flat screwdriver or credit card.The headphone jack and usb port are covered with flaps to protect them from dust and water.ConsThe screen protector reduces the phone's touch sensitivity.The screen protector does not sit flush against the phone.The screen protector has a hole in it for the phone's speaker and front facing camera.  There is a small gap, and because of that dust, rain and sand can get in under the screen.  I've reinstalled the case several times and was unable to get rid of the gap.Real-World UsageI've taken the phone hiking and I had no fear any any shock damage. I have dropped the phone while standing up and it survived with no problems.I've used the phone in rain and water has gotten behind it... the water causes your phones touch screen to be even less sensitive until the water is removed.Dust also gets behind the screen protector.  Initially, it is just behind the hold, but as you use your phone more and more, you'll notice the dust slowly spread over time.I've used this case at the beach and this was where sand got behind the screen protector.  I was just relaxing on a beach towel when a few grains got behind the screen protector.  Its impossible to shake out the sand completely.Final ThoughtsSince rain, dust and sand can get behind the screen protector, you'll find yourself periodically removing the case to clean your screen.  Water is the worst because it prevents you from using your phone.  Removal of the case is a pain because of its incredibly snug fit around the edges of your phone.  The phone's greatest strength now becomes an annoyance.  Regular removal of the case slowly loosens its snug fit around the edges, which is not good.I would not recommend this case.  Personally, I've gone back to using a simple silicon case and a screen protector sheet.

My wife has been asking me to find for her a Bluetooth hands-free set so she can connect her cell phone to the car stereo system and I have been researching for a good solution to handle this. I was given an opportunity to test this Bluetooth receiver and so far it has worked out well for us.This is a very small device, a little more than 1.5" (4cm to be more exact) in diameter, and very simple in operation. Theres a single multi-function button that handles everything - powering on/off, broadcasting its Bluetooth ID for pairing and answering/disconnecting the phone. It does not get much simpler than that in terms of how to operate once it is set up.Setting up is simple too. It pairs easily via Bluetooth and identifies itself as GD-BT30. I had no difficulty with it being recognized and working with my cell phone. In the box is a 3.5mm cable for connecting to a sound output like a car radio or a speaker system (if the source does not have a 3.5mm port or you do not have a conversion plug this device will not work out for you). Thats it - once paired and connected on one end and physically plugged in on the other, theres nothing more required.For my first test I connected this little device to a higher-end set of speakers and streamed music from my phone to the speakers, using the receiver as an intermediary. The music sounded just fine and there was no evidence of distortion, lag or noise in the sound at all - I could have just as easily directly connected my phone to the speakers and gotten the same sound, but I would have lost flexibility with the range. I moved the phone to a position 20'-30' from the receiver and had no degradation in sound or connection.My next test was to connect it to the car stereo system and use it to make a call. Setting the sound source to AUX on the car stereo system after plugging in the receiver I made a call and was able to speak and be heard as well as listen to the other person on the line, using the microphone in the receiver. With the receiver placed not far from my mouth (on the dashboard to be specific) the other person on the call told me I was clear and understandable. It should be understood that the position of the receiver is important when speaking - it cannot work miracles and turn muffled sounds into clear sounds.I can think of only one thing that I would change about this receiver. I would have included a clip, or a piece of Velcro, so that it could be attached to a specific location in the vehicle where it would be secure and accessible. A clip would allow it to stay attached to a sun visor, and Velcro would allow it to stay attached to the dashboard. This is a minor thing and its easy enough to find ways to attach it; my point is that it would be a nice touch to include such in the box.This is a nice Bluetooth receiver that works as advertised and allows for Bluetooth connectivity to devices that aren't otherwise Bluetooth-capable. It also works fine as a hands-free set-up for connecting a phone to a car speaker system. Its not very expensive either which makes this receiver a nice solution.Disclaimer: I was provided a sample of this product in exchange for a fair, honest and thorough review. Even though I received this product at no cost I have rated and reviewed this product without bias, based solely on my experience with it.

i bought this case as a more durable option to my Ozaki O!Coat bumper case for use in the wilderness. what attracted me to this one specifically was the integration of a front facing sound port for the iPhone's speaker. the iPhone has a decently loud speaker but is crippled by it's location, focusing the sound downward and away from the user. watching short video clips require turning up the volume or cupping your hand around the speaker port.all of the basic boxes are ticked for a mid-range case advertised to be durable: TPU surround for flexible fit, reinforced polymer body, clickable buttons, openings for silent switch, headphones, lightning port and speaker. it's unique not only by the front facing speaker port but also in it's 2-piece design. a TPU gasket features all of the proper openings and buttons, placed onto the iPhone before clipping the polymer shell onto the backside. most designs of this nature feature a full TPU body comolded with the polymer shell (eg, Speck Candyshell) or separately (eg, Incipio), but very few feature only a gasket style like this. the result is the protection and durability of a comolded case but in a much thinner package. it can be argued from a mechanical perspective that a layer of impact protection between the back of an iPhone and a rigid case is unnecessary, hence the perspective this design chooses to follow.however, there's just one issue: Maxboost advertises this case as compatible with the new iPhone 6s Plus. having both phones, that's not completely true after trying it on both of them. the latest iPhone is just ever so larger in all dimensions, practically unnoticeable. unfortunately (or fortunately) this case is molded to such tight tolerances that the newer iPhone 6s Plus doesn't completely fit, bulging at the case's weakest point: the top edge features attachment points between gasket and case that are smaller than those along the sides and bottom. this results in a slight gap between the two parts that could cause the two to separate upon an impact directed along that edge. last year's iPhone 6 Plus, however, fits perfectly.as for everything else, the polymer shell has a nice, grippy rubberized finish that adds sureness to your grip on the larger iPhone. the button covers are clicky and don't wiggle excessively and finished in a nicely understated matte black, a pleasant departure from all other manufacturers using a tacky, polished chrome. the redirected speaker ports work flawlessly, amplifying all sounds that emanate from the speaker by directing them toward the user. the inclusion of an attached cover for the lightning port is of questionable utility; you may think i'm mad but realize there is no like cover, attached or otherwise, for the headphone jack. besides, anything that would foul up the lightning port would similarly do so the headphone jack, mic and speaker. worst of all, it gets in the way of attaching a lightning cable for charging.so, 2 strikes. most wouldn't even notice the fitment issue i speak of and the lightning port cover is ridiculous. for the price, it's an excellent option compared to the competition and it's noticeably more compact. if you have an iPhone 6s Plus and a slight gap along the top seam isn't a bother, the benefits of having the speaker redirected forward is awesome and a design choice more manufacturers should adopt. how Maxboost included it in no way adds to the length of the case.

Let me start by saying that I own almost all of Cambridge Soundworks Radio's. I think there is one particular unit that I do not own. I have never owned any of their speakers so, the OontZ speaker was the first for me.I now have the OontZ XL speaker and I am equally pleased with this larger speaker.I received the XL yesterday evening and immediately plugged it into charge. It charged rather quickly and I paired it to my Motorola XOOM Tablet. I listened to Classical, Jazz and even some Country music. Classical music can really give a set of speakers a good workout and so can Jazz.Bluetooth does not have a reputation of having the best audio quality but, Cambridge Soundworks has certainly pulled out all the stops in this speaker. It does not sound as if it is being sent via Bluetooth. The sound is very clear, crisp and clean. No clipping or distortion what so ever. I turned the volume all the way up on the Xoom tablet and adjusted the volume on the OontZ speaker. Low, medium or high, the sound was very good.I placed the speaker on a table outside of my garage entrance this morning and listened to WSBZ 106.3 The Seabreeze.(via internet radio) They play a good mix of Jazz and the OontZ did a very good job of reproducing the sound of all of the instruments. A particular piece had an abundance of bass and the speaker handled it without any problems. Some speakers seem to rattle when low frequencies are played but, this speaker had absolutely no problems with the low bass sounds. The high and middle range is also very pleasing and well balanced. The spoken word is very clean and clear also. Not the usual nasal sound of some small speakers.A neighbor came over and immediately asked what I had and when I showed it to him, he said "I need one of those"! He enjoys listening to rock and heavy metal music. He had his iphone with him so, I asked him if he would like to hear "his" music on the speaker? He said yes, so we paired his phone with the OontZ XL. When the music started to play, the volume was very low so, I asked him to turn up the volume on his phone. He did and the music just seemed to come alive from the OontZ speaker. He looked at me and said, that is amazing. I want my wife to hear that. He went and got her and she could not believe the sound was coming from the speaker and wanted to know where the wires were? She said, well, I know what you will be getting for Christmas...My neighbor had already heard the smaller OontZ and wanted it. He said I could just sell mine to him and I had to tell him NO, NO, No. I am keeping it.I am listening to the speaker as I type this and I just turned to a Classical station in London England, UK. Classical FM. What a sound this speaker is producing.I am very happy with this speaker and I know I will really enjoy listening to it.I still like the smaller OontZ and will use it also.If you are in the market for a great sounding portable speaker system, then this should be it.Not to mention the ability to use it almost anywhere. I haven't tried the speaker phone yet but, that is the next thing I will try.This is a great product at a very reasonable price point. Well made and nice looking also.Ron BTyler, Texas

I purchased this product (TechMatte 10000mAh Dual-Port Portable Charger) back in Dec 2015, mainly for its LCD battery status display. Most other power banks in this price range only employ a crude 4-LED status indicator, so at best you can estimate the battery status to 25% intervals. In contrast, the TechMatte unit has a digital display between 100% down to 0%, in 1% steps.[Mechanical Design]The TechMatte unit looks slick, but slightly larger than I expected for a 10,000mAh battery pack. When compared to the MoKo 9,000mAh Dual-Port charger, for example, the TechMatte unit is 40% taller (140mm vs. 100mm).The USB power does not auto-on when an USB device is connected. You must press and hold the power button for 3 sec to turn it on. Another long press turns off the power. Two short presses turns on/off the LED flashlight. The light is quite wimpy, but should be handy in case of emergency.I checked the battery status of my TechMatte unit after 6 months of storage. It says 93%, which means the charge retention rate is very good.[Output Current]The two USB ports are rated 1A and 2.1A, respectively. While testing with my Barnes & Noble NOOK HD+ Tablet, the '2.1A' port can only charge the tablet at 0.45A, while the '1A' is able to charge at ~1.3A. It is possible that the charger can only provide 2.1A for Apple products.Output voltage is poorly regulated. When I try to draw a combined current of 2.1A from two ports, the output voltage drops from 5V down to just 4.6V[Battery Capacity]I connected a 2.5 Ohm power resistor to the 2.1A port. The unit puts out ~1.78A at ~4.82V for 216 minutes. The total output energy is thus 31Wh. This corresponds to 10,000mAh internal battery, if we assume conversion efficiency of 84%For comparison, the AmazonBasics PB10 power bank can produce 35Wh or 13% more energy, even though both are rated 10,000mAh. That means either the PB10 contains higher capacity battery, or is better in conversion efficiency.Ranked in order of Total Output Energy:- AmazonBasics PB10 Dual-Port (10,000mAh): 35Wh- TechMatte 10,000mAh Dual-Port charger: 31Wh- Yoobao SP2 (10,000mAh): 30.9Wh- KMASHI MP816 (10,000mAh): 30.0Wh- MoKo Dual-Port charger (9,000mAh): 29.5Wh[Battery Status Display]The LCD panel has a blue backlight and is very easy to read. During my capacity test, I noticed that the digital battery status readout is very inaccurate in the mid-range. See the chart I uploaded for details. For example, when the LCD display shows '50%', the actual battery status is close to 70%. So whats the purpose to show 1% resolution, when the error may be as large as 20%? My guess is that the display is simply a linear estimate based on battery voltage, and not been calibrated to reflect remaining battery capacity.[Recharge Time]Recharging current is 1.7A initially. A full charge from empty takes about 7 hrs. Total input energy=44Wh.One disappointment I noticed: while the battery is being recharged, both USB output ports are turned off. The Moko unit, on the other hand, is able to support pass-thru charging.[Conclusion]I liked the LCD battery status display on this TechMatte unit. Too bad it is not as accurate as it should be. Overall I consider it a Good, but not Excellent product, hence my 4-star rating.

I like this as a small back-up phone. But there really wasn't much in the way of instructions that come with this device. You get a small three-panel Quick Start Guide - but IMO it doesn't give you as much information as you might need. For example, you have to install the battery, sim card, and a micro-SD memory card (optional) - but the Quick Start Guide doesn't tell you how to get the back off the phone to do that. I was a bit hesitant to start just prying at the back, for all I knew the back had to slide off. I eventually figured it out by some trial-and-error (luckily I didn't damage the plastic back). You need to start at the top of the phone and pry the back a bit off, and then go along the sides to pop the small plastic clips. Once you get the back off it's easy to see what needs to be done. The only reason for my four-stars is that the instructions didn't cover this, otherwise I would have given the phone five-stars.The phone is pretty basic as you might expect. One of the reasons I picked this model to buy was that it could also play music. I put in a 16GB mirco-SD card that I had loaded up with music (MP3s). The phone didn't see any of the music. Removing the card and using my computer I reorganized the files. I had just dropped a bunch of folders (albums) on the card. Apparently you need to put the MP3 files in a folder that the phone creates titled 'My Music'. Once I did that the phone was able to see the music. You can just hook the phone up to the computer via the USB cable - but I pulled the card and did that directly. You can make playlists with the phone. The sound quality is pretty decent, much better than most MP3 players costing about the same as this phone did.I also like that this phone has a built-in FM radio feature, I still like to listen to the local terrestrial radio stations. Scanning and setting stations is very easy to do.I'm probably going to use this phone more as an audio device than as a phone. I'm very happy with my purchase.

Cases for smartphones can be a very subjective thing, where all too often we're offered a stylish bargain that doesn't hold up beyond the first few weeks. Then the start for looking for another begins. If you've ever been through this, you'll know exactly what I'm talking about, and experience can be a good teacher.Getting this OtterBox Prefix Series Hybrid Case for Samsung Galaxy S III in its Carbon color was the result of trying about a half dozen cases at a friendly local vendor before I received my new phone. Came very close to getting the Urban Armor Gear Composite Hybrid Case as it was so rugged. One from the OtterBox Defender Series was highly recommended, but in the end decided that as good as it was, it's two-piece design was not what I needed; I was looking more for function than form. After a couple of weeks of use, here are my subjective likes and dislikes regarding the OtterBox Prefix:Pros:+ Simplicity; a single-layer case that fits the phone perfectly+ Rugged; silicone case that protects from impact damage+ Unibody design; internal polycarbonate backbone adds additional protection+ Screen protector; comes standard, no need to buy another+ Frustration-free packaging; easy to remove from box+ Ports line up perfectly; no hasslesCons:- Patience is required when installing the Galaxy S III the first timeIn Use:The Samsung Galaxy S III 4G Android Phone follows the trend for making smartphones thinner with each new generation, and though it's much larger than my old BlackBerry 9330 that it replaced, it's about half as thick. But with this OtterBox Prefix Series Case, it adds a feeling of ruggedness to a phone that's only 0.34" (8.6 mm) thick... in subjective terms, an excellent combination for this user.This case comes in a true frustration-free package, and when first unboxed, I installed the screen protector. It comes with the small microfiber cloth the clean the phone's screen, and using the supplied squeegee, I slowly worked the bubbles out. You probably know the routine. The next step was to try and fit the phone to the case, and after a few minutes of fiddling, found that it was fairly easy to do from the top or the bottom; your choice. Once it was installed, with a couple of small wiggles, found that the fit was excellent. There are slight protrusions in the case for the power button on one side and the volume +/- controls on the other, and these have worked quite well once one becomes used to the 'feel' of them.Cleaning the case is easy. All that's needed is some mild soap and water, and then drying it lightly with a paper towel.As far as protection goes, my Galaxy S III usually has the vibrate feature on for calls and other functions, and one time it vibrated itself off of a dining room table, dropping about three feet right before my eyes. This case protected it, and the raised rib around the screen area does a decent job of protecting the screen face.It should be noted that if you use a docking station (I do not) you may wish to reconsider getting this case, as it probably won't work. The OtterBox Reflex Series would be a better choice if you have a Samsung Galaxy S III, as its bottom half slides off to allow for easy docking access.Summary:The OtterBox Prefix Series Hybrid Case for Samsung Galaxy S III has proven to be a good choice for medium protection of this phone. There are thinner cases on the market, and some that may be more rugged, but from a subjective perspective, this one is a good balance between protection and ergonomics. It does the job, and does not disappoint.1/16/2013

I spent most of last two years picking out an activity tracker that worked for me, I tried just about every product on the market: Polar Loop, Fitbit Flex, Fitbit Force, Jawbone Up, Jawbone Up24. Every different company that was making them was producing a product that had pros and cons, but the original Vivofit hit all of the best pros with the least cons, so I stuck with it for the last 6 months. Recently, I noticed my band had broken on the underside (This was not a manufacturers issue, I honestly fridget with the tracker in the band when bored and watching TV sometimes, bending it in ways it would never bend if used properly.) When I went to get replacement bands on Amazon, I noticed that Garmin had released this update, and thankfully it removed the few cons of the original Vivofit!To spell it out, the best features of an activity tracker are as follows (in my opinion):*Long Battery Life (original Vivofit and not the Vivofit2 both have this. Every other tracker I've tried (Polar Loop, Fitbit Flex, Fitbit Force, Jawbone Up, Jawbone Up24) effectively requires charging weekly if not more often, this was a huge disappointment to me, as I much more prefer a 'set it and forget it' mentality. For several of them, I'd find the device was dead or nearly dead just as I was about to exercise, or sleep or do ANYTHING which basically prevented me from using it when I needed to.*Band Closure/Switching - on the new Vivofit, the band closes much more securely than the original. Its actually a bit difficult to get off. This helps to prevent the accidental removals I had with the Up, Up24, Flex and Force. Also stops me from playing with it so much while I watch TV! Being able to switch bands was always a huge bonus to the Vivofit/Flex trackers. It helps keep the device going much longer than the others (Jawbone bands always broke eventually and there was that whole Rash issue with the Force that finally went away after not wearing it for a few months). The options available for the Vivofit2 make it an OK choice for just about every environment.*Display - Invaluable. My biggest gripe with the Up, Up24 and Flex were the lack of actual screen. Seeing that you have 200 steps to go to reach your target is much better motivator than seeing you're 4 dots out of 5 and not knowing until you sync later how close that actually is to your goal.*Timer - New Feature to the Vivofit2 - one of my biggest cons with the original Vivofit, so Im glad it was addressed. There are many activities that you can do which will not move your arms. These will lead to deceptively low activity numbers for a day (easiest example is biking/stationary cyling). Having a timer on the band to mark when you begin/end the activity is an awesome way to track how long you were going for a more accurate calorie count later. It is also helpful when you're working towards a time goal such as lowering your time on a 5k. Yes you could do the math yourself or use your cellphone - but if you're OK doing that, then you should just be using your cellphones pedometer instead of this!*Integration with MFP. If you're working out or dieting and NOT using MyFitnessPal, you're missing out. Having this device sync directly to MFP is a huge boon. The system will automatically tell MFP what you're exercise has been and MFP will send it your calorie intake for the day. I cannot express how convenient and awesome this is. Thankfully most of the bands do this now :)*Waterproof - Polar Loop and Vivofit/Vivofit2 are the only two that I tried which have enough water proofing to be worn when swimming. While you may not swim when working out, keep in mind that your band can also get pretty wet when you shower, if it happens to rain when you're wearing it, or if you do dishes - or a dozen other normal every day activities. All of the bands i listed are 'splash proof' but for the Jawbone products especially, this didn't alway mean they would work right when they got 'splashed'. I've gone in a pool with the Vivofit on - not a single issue. Also note that the Polar Loops screen has a tendency to go nuts when water hits it, which can be very distracting in the shower or washing dishes (it interprets every drop of water hitting it as a finger press and cycles its screen accordingly)*Sleep Tracking - though not a requirement per se, it can be interesting to see how much youve been sleeping in cold numbers. Every band tracked it, and none did exceptionally better than others. However, I much more preferred the Vivofit/Jawbone button press option to put the band in sleep mode, was much easier than the Fitbit Flexs screen tapping.

Things got worse.I purchased this phone June 8, 2018 and it arrived about a week later (I think the 14th but I honestly can't remember). I bought this phone as a trial run/in between phones phone, if that makes sense. Basically, I had a Galaxy S8 that was awful, I had a ton of issues, and I was making payments on it to ATT. I have always had android phones, and I was so fed up with my Samsung that i decided to try Apple. I didn't want to sign a new contract though in case I didn't like it. In the meantime I could return the Samsung so I wouldn't have to make any more payments and save up a bit for the taxes I'd have to pay when I got the new phone.Immediately I was disappointed to find that I paid 180 bucks for just the phone - it didn't come with anything else. I thought for sure it would have come with a charger. So I had to go buy a $30 charging cable just to charge it up and turn it on (I had to go to best buy or wait a few days for shipping). Now I'm out 200 bucks, had I known this I would have purchased the certified refurbished unit, because apparently they are the only ones that come with a charge cable. Lesson #1 in this saga...Once I get it charged up, I set up the phone and get everything situated. I noticed the battery condition is 70% of new, so I send an email to the buyer. All I wanted was to be reimbursed the 35 bucks or whatever it is to have the Apple store swap out the battery. Considering the phone was listed as "Very Good" I figured this was reasonable. I then returned my Samsung (mistake #2 on my part, the first was buying the phone to begin with) so now I only have this phone and no backup. The seller eventually gets back to me after I return the Samsung that I can return the phone for a refund. Well, I can't go a week without a phone, and I can't afford to buy another because I just bought this one. So my money and my life are tied up in this phone at this point in the story.Fast forward to mid July, and the phone won't hold enough of a charge to even turn on. Now I have to go get a new phone - credit card debt here I come! Needless to say I'm extremely frustrated. I'll take a moment to point out that Amazon lists very good items as "A well-cared-for item that has seen limited use but remains in great condition. The item is complete, unmarked, and undamaged, but may show some limited signs of wear. Item works perfectly." That last line was clearly ignored in my case. Amazon also lists unacceptable items as "Items in any of the following conditions are unacceptable for listing on Amazon:Item does not work perfectly in every regard.Item is not clean, including signs of mold, heavy staining, or corrosion.Item is damaged in a way that renders it difficult to use.Item is missing essential accompanying material or parts. (This does not necessarily include instructions.)Item requires repair or service.Item was not created by the original manufacturer or copyright holder. This includes copies, counterfeits, replicas, and imitations.Item was originally distributed as a promotional copy, promotional bundle, product sample, or advance reading copy. This includes uncorrected proofs of in-print or not-yet-published books.Any aspect of the item is obscured and not able to be read or viewed because of markings, stickers, or other damage.Item has passed the expiration date (includes "best by" and "sell by" dates), has an unacceptable portion of its shelf life remaining, or has had the expiration date removed or tampered with.Item is prohibited for sale on Amazon."In my opinion this item should never have been listed. The seller should have known about the battery issue too, because the phone literally tells you the condition it's in. In this case, diminished performance that ultimately led to failure.So I contacted Amazon at this point, and to my dismay they sided with the seller. After all of this I was still willing to work with them, but they said I couldn't return it anymore (even though that's an option in my order summary page). The seller said I could return it, but I'm not sending a phone with all my personal information on it to a random group of people I don't know. They won't reimburse for a new battery. I was willing to pay for that in order to wipe the phone and then send it back, provided they reimburse me for the phone AND the battery replacement. I was willing to accept a partial refund to cover the cost of a battery replacement. If there is a way to do it, I was willing to let them pay the apple store for a battery replacement to get the information off, then send it back for a refund... the response I got was that I can only return it within the first 14 days.TL;DR: Bought a phone seller knew was defective, can't wipe phone, seller will take back but won't cover cheap repair costs so I can wipe phone. Amazon sided with seller and will only help you if item fails within 14 days.

I care (probably too much) about phone cases, specifically in this case button sensitivity, and so I ordered 4 - The SUPCASE, Cruzerlite, Diztronic, and J&D Kickstand case.  This review is for the Cruzerlite case, and how it compares to the other 3.The Cruzerlite case is ideal for people that care about button sensitivity as the buttons are uncovered. This is my main issue for day-to-day use and as such it is the winner for me.  However, it is true that the material around the buttons is quite thin, and I can see how that would potentially concern or annoy some people.  Also, while the back is a design that I find neat, I could see other people being less impressed, and also it provides a texture which is more rough to the touch.  This is good from the standpoint of not sliding when on a table, but potentially could put off some people when they grab it from their pocket.  Aside from that, the protection seems adequate for standard indoor use with an occasional drop (I've already dropped it in the case and it handled it fine).  It would not compare to the protection provided by the J&D case.  I had no problems with any cutouts.Ultimately, I am using it as my normal day-to-day case and would recommend it for anyone who isn't looking for extra protection and cares about button feedback.

I have owned all three Otterbox models for the note 5 for extended periods: Symmetry, Commuter and Defender. I originally purchased the Commuter case locally and then returned it for the Defender due to the ridiculously slick exterior of the commuter. I picked up the Symmetry from Amazon Warehouse Deals soon after getting the Defender. I still have the Symmetry and Defender but both are mixed bags. I have purchased a few other cases that cost 1/2 as much as either of the Otterboxes and they are as good or better.The Defender case is made from good quality materials, fit is generally okay and it doesn't look too bad. The SPen is easily accessible as well as all the other ports. I originally had a tempered glass screen protector but removed it since the Defender has a built-in one. The case is pretty bulky, due to the size of the phone itself, making it impractical to carry the phone in anything but the included belt clip or a jacket pocket.There are a few design oddities that make it seem of lessor quality than the previous Defenders I have purchased for other phones. The screen protector has a crossbar near the earpiece that partially blocks access to the very top of the touch screen, so it always takes multiple tries to activate the notification bar. The protector also hovers at least a millimeter over the screen making the device unpleasant to use -- especially with the SPen. The cut out for the SPen is huge, much larger than required. It leaves a large area of the corner exposed on the back side. If you place a straight edge across the SPen cut out it will actually touch the corner of the phone, no pressure required. It really looks like if dropped at the right angle, from any height, that corner of the phone would definitely be vulnerable. The charging flap causes the outer cover to unseat from the rim of the front plastic frame every time it is flipped up, so you have to re-seat it after each charge. Almost every feature you interact with seems poorly conceived. All-in-all not the best use experience I have had with a case. Disappointing, especially for the price. Otterbox has always been my go to case for my phones but they came up a little short on the cases for this particular phone.The screen protector issue caused me to retire the case after a few months -- using the device with it was very irritating. There is really no excuse for such a poor fit. It is just careless design. I have another vendors case with an integrated screen protector and it sits flat and you can barely tell it is there.
