It comes with the bluetooth hands free receiver, car charger, magnetic suction base and user manual. It is very easy to install. Just plug the car charger to the cigarette lighter of your car. The car charger has 2 ports, left side for the receiver and the right side for charging your gadgets. Then plug the audio connector into the AUX then set the car audio system to AUX. This unit only supports car with AUX audio input. Then activate the bluetooth and pair it with your android phone. A blue light indicator will appear to your device that your bluetooth successfully done. You can even pair 2 phones to your device. Just turn off the bluetooth of your first phone then do the the same pairing bluetooth procedure on your 2nd phone. Then you have 2 phones connected on your device. You can alternate the two phones. You can't play the music at the same time, you must turn off the music on your first phone then play it on your 2nd phone. One at a time. It is totally awesome so even your car does not come with built in bluetooth you can now enjoy the convenience of it with this amazing kit. Got this device on a discounted price for testing and very much happy to share my opinion with this amazing product.

This unit has one fundamental (and slightly dumb) design flaw.  The diodes on the side are very bright and they light-up if there is just a tiny amount of light reaching the sensor.  This means that indoors, ambient light will light the bright diodes, but the light energy coming in is less than what is consumed by the diodes.  So when it could/should be charging, it is actually depleting it's power.  I've tested it, it really does deplete it's own power when in low indoor light.  But you can always flip it over- problem solved. It isn't a problem when charging from direct sunlight, but you seldom have direct sunlight on the unit.  I recommend just charging it from the computer.  Then it will charge fully and fast.

Just received this Nano Sim Card cutter, I have read the both negative 1 star reviews and the 5 star reviews and I wanted to say, my feeling is mixed.Because, like many 1 Star reviewer mentioned. If you are not careful, you will cut onto the gold contact and damage the SIM card and you will need a replacement once that is damaged. The reason behind this is because there is a little bit of play between the SIM card holder and the cutter itself. So if you are not careful thinking it will cut perfect every time you are wrong, you will need to carefully look at the gold contact and make sure they are not too close to the border and are even on all four sides, then you will get a good cut on the SIM card, otherwise if one side is closer to the border than the other, you will cut into the gold contact and ruin that SIM card.Bottom line, does it work? Yes it does! However you will have to wiggle the SIM card holder to make sure you have everything aligned before you cut it.

Fantastic portable charger  I always read a few reviews before I write mine so prospective buyers know what to look for.This is a 26800mAh battery pack.  It's purpose is to store power so that you can recharge your items on the go.It is *specifically* designed to charge portable USB powered devices.  iSmart is designed to prevent your devices from being damaged by over charging.  I've used RavPower iSmart devices for over a year now, with Samsung phones, Motorola Phones, Ipads, Galaxy pads... I've used them professionally (I'm an IT technician) as well as personally and I've been nothing but impressed.This comes with 2 Micro USB to USB cables, a carry bag and the battery.Like most batteries, it does not come with a charger, as the buyer is assumed to have a USB based charger for their phone or tablet that they can use with this to charge.If you find yourself needing a charger with multiple ports I recommend the RP-PC028 (6 port) or RP-UC07 (4 port) chargers, as these will charge all devices at the optimum charging amps.  iSmart doesn't appear to kick on the "turbo charge" or "fast charge" mode for Motorola or Samsung, but it also is your standard charge speed and not the lower .5a speed you get with normal USB ports.  At the same time it won't overload smaller devices such as Bluetooth headsets etc.  If you're looking for a solid car charger I recommend the 4 port RP-VC003 from Ravpower2 Micro USB to std USB are included, so this should work with most OEM phone and tablet chargers.I use industrial velcro to attach these to my nightstands and work bench at work. I can charge a Ipad Air2, Galaxy Tab4, Motorola Turbo and Motorola Nexus 7 without any drop in power to any of the devices.  Again, for charging any USB based electronics, this is a BEAST.I fully recharged my Nexus 7 from 15% twice in one day then topped off my tablet from 80% and it still had over 75% capacity.I can play my games on my phone while plugged in (Spendor, Ticket to Ride, Pandemic) and it took it all and kept charging.I've recommended RavPower to my friends and I'm not afraid to recommend them to my co-workers because they have never failed me.

Before opting for this, I was concerned about size. My current backup charger for my phone takes up a bit of room due to both the cord and the battery in my purse.  The original cord broke (due to pressure on the back of the connector and wire) and the cord I use now is a bit too long.  Both are in a baggie in my purse.This charger looked big in the picture but seemed to have a retractable cord and non-integrated wall plug that could be detached, so the size I thought was comparable.OUT OF THE BOX:This item comes in a durable plastic clamshell for shipping.  Going to keep that for other items.  Seems like a blister pack would be more cost effective.  The cord retracts and there is a storage spot for the iOS end. So it is a pretty clean setup.  Retracting was a bit spotty as the cord has a tendency to twist.  Odd seeing a cloth covered cord like this.  I associate that kind of cord with my grandmothers old toaster (but a lot thinner).  I assume that is done so it does not stick to itself or get twisted on the reel. I have verified that the unretracted cord is a full 4 feet.This charger is big. 5.8 x 3.1 x 0.8 inches.  About 1-1.2 the size of a set of playing cards and slightly wider and thicker.  Weight wise it is similar to my prior charger, but it is about 2X as thick and maybe a  wider.  The end of this charger slides off and can be left at home when not charging.  Then it is shorter (~5)Per the literature it comes ready to charge, but this charger only had one light lit indicating about 1/3rd charge.  I connected it to my phone which was at 30% and after about 1 hour, it brought it up to 64%.  Not bad, but not blindingly fast either.USAGE:Unfortunately I soon had a need for this charger in having to spend some time in the ER with my Mom.  The integrated cord made it nice as I could only pull out as much cord as I needed.  Sometimes just putting the charger under my phone, or leaving the charger in my purse and the phone on the exterior.  I used the charger 2X before it appeared to be low.  Each time it took my phone from under 20% to over 60% (my choice).  So right off the bat this was good.In the past (going to the ER), I have brought a charging cable, but if you have ever scoped out an ER for a wall socket, most are occupied or inconvenient. There your phone is your communication center.  Hospital rooms are a bit better to use a charger, as are nursing home rooms.I used it a second time, where it took my phone from <20% to 100% and had plenty of juice left.  It seemed faster than my older charger.OVERALL:I had reservations initially, but with the integrated cord, this charger is more handy than the older one.  Including the end for an iOS device and a spot to store is a nice touch.  It seems to hold more charge than prior charges as well. You apparently can charge the unit and your phone at the same time and has an extra slot to add another phone as well.  In case there is competition for outlets (as in most hotels).  The amount of charge opportunities seems to be greater than other chargers as well.  As I indicated above, I got 2 near full charges and it may have been possible to full charge 3 times.  That is that the info that came with it said, so given what I did, this seems likely.

My teenage son recently bought his own cell phone for the first time, and of course, he needed a case to help protect it. He spotted this one, and decided *it was the one*. He's had this on his phone for about a month now, and he really likes it. He's dropped his phone a couple of times, and the case has protected said phone with no damage. He said that it does feel a bit flimsy in a few spots, but the case fits well and has never come off. The Spigen case I had for the iPhone 6 I loved, and it looked like this was the same one. The benefits are that you get to show off the iPhone 6 Plus most closely to the way they made it. No shabby looking plastic bunkers wrapped around a beautiful device. The Air Cushions in the corners are an innovative feature I have yet to test, but they are based on sound logic. The bulk and thickness of the case overall is excellent - I feel it's appropriate and slim enough not to make an already hefty phone a behemoth. Also to be appreciated are the slight raised edges on the front as well as the tiny risers on the back, which keep the phone from ever resting on the front glass or flush against the whole back of the case. This prevents scratches on the back and as of now (2 months in) everything looks freakishly new. I love it. I do have to subtract two stars though for an oversight in the design. The power and volume buttons are covered rather than cut out. If you can't see the button, then you have to feel for the shallow lettering over the buttons, and since you can't feel the button, frequently you miss hitting it and have to keep pressing the case until you finally get it. It's not enough to ruin the whole case, but for such an otherwise considerate case, the design flaw makes the experience so-so.

As someone who travels quite a bit, I am always on the lookout for portable power. I don't like competing for "outlet space" at the airport, and sometimes find myself in places where there are no public outlets available at all. I have an old iPad mini, but I am usually most interested in keeping my phone charged when I am on the road.Because this might be confusing, I will put this at the top of my review: THIS PACK DOES NOT CONTAIN A WALL ADAPTER. You must have something that plugs into a wall or cigarette/automobile power outlet and provides a USB port in order to charge this pack. A computer with a powered USB port will also work, but may charge more slowly, depending on the characteristics of that port.This is a typical power pack and I don't see anything bad or really notable about it from that aspect. It does what it says: charges your devices.There are two USB ports on the pack: one is 5V/2.4A for iPads and the other is 5V/1.0A for iPhones/iPods, and other devices. If you don't know what input power your device can take, look at its specifications, just to be safe, but a lot of devices will step down the higher amperage. You can charge two phones at once, or a phone and a tablet... just not two tablets at the same time (unless you have a smaller tablet that will charge at 1.0A)From a capacity perspective, I charged my phone, my watch, and a Kindle Fire, all from about 10% battery to 100% before I had to recharge this. The box indicates that it comes "pre-charged," which is technically true, but I think mine arrived with ~50% charge, not fully charged. That's fine as it should be plenty to charge up your phone completely at least once, but I think it is worth noting.There is a microUSB port for input (charging) and a slot on the side that keeps a short cable -- USB to either the microUSB or the Apple Lightning. This is convenient, but a little less so for the Apple users since the Lightning adapter can't be used to charge the pack. The microUSB adapter can be turned around and used as an "input," thus only requiring the single cable to charge the device from a USB port or wall adapter. Apple users need to carry another cable, or, at least, the other short cable that comes with this pack, to be able to charge from a USB port or wall adapter. As a user of Apple devices, that knocks a star off for me. It may not matter to you.There is a single button with 4 green LEDs to show capacity. Push the button to have them light up. They also light up during charging so you can see how full the pack is as it is filling up.It is offered in a variety of colors -- I guess you can match the color of your phone (?) or your favorite outfit (?) -- or, you can easily tell them apart if your kids each have one. They are nice-looking and you can tell some design work went into making them look less brick-like.This is a solid pack with good capacity and construction. The ability to carry the "output" cable is nice. It is a little big and heavy to carry around in a shirt pocket, but you may be able to carry it in the back pocket of jeans (I carry this one in my backpack). This one is good for "capacity" whereas there are smaller packs available for carrying in your pocket for "emergency charging." Those weigh about 1/3 and will get your phone 25-50% charged -- just enough to pay for your ber, get directions back to the hotel, or make a call.There is not much in the way of instructions, but, what would I expect? It is a battery... I plug my stuff into it. :)The pack has a $2500/1500/2000 warranty for connected equipment warranty. Basically, Belkin will repair or replace devices that are damaged by an electrical charge while "correctly connected" to the pack. The details are supposedly on their website, but I was not able to find them. The page that I did find had been retired. This is a new pack, so maybe the website hasn't caught up yet.

Professional reviewers will tell you that the HTC One X is simply the best Droid phone on the planet and that the upcoming Galaxy III will have to work hard to dethrone it.  While it is an excellent phone, some glaring flaws in the One X made me question the hype.  The ray of sunshine came through for me when I learned to stop worrying and love the Internet.  Read on...When it comes to the Pros, the One X has a whole lot going for it.  The International version has a quad-core processor and 32GB of storage onboard.  I was surprised and disappointed to hear that both the One X and the Galaxy III will be losing that quad-core in the American versions, but I've learned a bit since then.  Number of cores is important to a processor, there's no doubt:  but the internal architecture of the processor is important, too.  The manufacturing of the One X's newer dual-core is more efficient than the older quad-core processor, and sites benchmarking the two have come up with almost identical performance scores.  Using the phone, there's no doubt that everything is speedy and responsive--the browser is fast, apps like Facebook knock load-times out of the park, and games like Angry Birds don't stutter trying to render house-smashing physics.  The One X seems to be slightly less happy making the transition from one app to the other, but it's not a problem, it's more like, "getting here felt like 110% speed, closing out and opening the next program took a moment and then we were back at 110% again."  Call quality is quite good, and it seems like these days just about every smartphone makes good, clear calls given good coverage--but speakerphone modes will always be tiny and tinny.Where we have the downsides of AT&T start with the fact that the AT&T version for the US contains only 16GB of storage.  You don't get all of that 16GB by any stretch:  after separate partitions for the system and software, you get just shy of 10GB left for your use.  When you think about the fact that an hour of 1080p HD video clocks in at around 11GB, that's not good--even if you put no music or photos on the phone, you're already limited from the get-go thanks to the storage downgrade.  "No problem," says I, "I have my 32GB microSD!  I'll just pop it into the...what?  No slot?"  That's right:  no SD slot is a glaring error, and I criticize the pros who say, "10 GB is plenty!" or "they couldn't put a slot in the unibody design" (oh, really?  Then how did they do just that to hold the SIM card?).  This is the hard part about the One X:  the battery and memory are what they are, and cannot be upgraded or replaced.That said, the "Sunshine in the Cloud" comes in learning to wean yourself from copies of everything.  When I take pictures or video on my phone, the first thing I want to do is get them right off the phone and onto a PC, so I can do whatever I want to with them.  HTC have gone out of their way to make that possible for you.  It's amazing:  nearly everything in the interface will allow you to tether yourself to "a place for your stuff".  HTC One X users are gifted with 23GB of extra storage on DropBox, for a total of 25, so that takes care of my file storage needs.  Google Play allows you to upload 20,000 songs and stream them anytime, so there goes my obsession with copying MP3s onto an SD Card.  I can automatically upload camera pictures to any one of a number of places:  Flickr, Google+, Dropbox, Facebook, you name it--they can automatically go straight into the cloud and I can pull them down any time.  You might think that would run up your data bill, but everywhere I've looked I can set up how to transfer my files:  on 4G LTE, on Wi-Fi only, on either, or only when I manually push them.Playing music is important to me, so I put the One X through its paces before I settled in:  Google Play has streamed over Wi-Fi without a hiccup, and I even started a stream in 4G, attached to Wi-Fi, and turned 4G off without so much as a dropped note.  I was shocked.  I got in the car, tethered to my car audio, drove around with 4G running the show and all went well.  I also played with Internet Radio (worked great) and an FM app (FM radio is terrible in my area for any device).  The only drawback is that there is a music player native to the phone that expects files to be there, so I'm working through Google Play instead.  One of the professional sites recommended MOG, but they don't tell you that it's $10 a month for the service...no thanks, the One X is quite good with any of the other options!So then we come to the camera.  Yes, you would still be stuck with only being able to record an hour or less of 1080p before needing to offload it to the web (and you can automate that, too)...but I also thought about how I use the video feature on a phone:  in bits and pieces.  The one time I came close to an hour--for my daughter's piano recital--I still ended up recording it in segments, for the simple reason that I can't hold perfectly still for an hour on end with a phone held upright in both hands:  that's what the hand-shaped digital video cameras are for.  The rear camera is 8MP in still shots, and wow, is the shutter speed fast!  My wife brags about her Canon point-and-shoot, but covering my daughter's birthday I was out-shooting her time after time.  The flash is decent for a phone, but just like a regular camera your image quality is best when you shoot with plenty of lighting.  There is a lower-resolution front camera, but I would only imagine that would be useful for snapping thumbnail pics.  Video is also fairly smooth, and the One X not only promises 30fps of 1080p HD, it lets you take still pictures while the video is running.Next we come to the Network.  The HTC One X tethers quickly and it really does appear to make the most of the bandwidth (and there are sites that can give you benchmarks).  For me, I went to a Linksys WRT 610N on the N band, then on its 5N band, then a Netgear WNH-DE1000, and at work we have a brand-new Cisco system that's blazing fast.  Some people are finding that the One X is cranky with older routers, but I think I've figured out the problem.  For some reason, if you connect to a network and move away, the phone still thinks it's connected.  It will pretend to try to connect to other networks (and fail) and sometimes it simply won't see the other networks.  The problem will most likely be fixed in an update, but in the meantime there's a simple workaround:  either manually disconnect from the gone network, or just turn Wi-Fi off and on again:  the new available networks will show up and it will connect quickly.  I've now played with an AT&T hot spot, a Starbucks, and a Panera and all three connect fast.  When I'm on the 4G LTE network, I do notice a very marked improvement over my 3G Captivate:  it's close to broadband performance now.  Bluetooth pairing is also quick and easy--and you can use it to send things like Contact data from your old phone to your One X.One other thing that really impresses me with the HTC One X is Android 4.0, a.k.a. "Ice Cream Sandwich".  This new OS is a remarkable improvement in many small ways, such as being able to customize just about every feature (as I'd mentioned earlier with the upload settings).  For the HTC One X, there's a clever little extra bit in the way it unlocks:  drag the silver ring from the bottom across the screen.  Are you just going to make a call?  Or just going to take a picture?  No need to unlock the whole phone and re-load everything:  simply drag that ring across the icon you want and it'll open just that program for you.  I gather you can customize the unlock methods too but I haven't felt the need to do so.  The screen is really bright and clear, and while the Gorilla Glass is supposed to be unscratchable, it's also very smooth:  fingerprint buildup can slow you down, but it comes off really easily with a swipe across a shirtsleeve.  The camera on the back sticks up from the unit, which is weird:  it's like a glass pimple that's begging to get banged on something, but thus far I haven't had it get caught on a pocket or give me grief when handling the phone.  The smooth unibody design is slightly curved and feels comfortable in a medium-to-large hand like mine.  Last, while the phone comes with a quick-start guide, AC charger that splits into a USB cable, and some promo materials, it's worth it to hop out to HTC.com and set up your phone:  there are tons of free apps in the marketplace, you can still use the Play Store (which is Google's new Android Market), and I also use Amazon's App Store for Android.  The PDF manual available from the HTC site is friendly and easy-to-read:  if you're considering an HTC One X, I highly recommend you take the time to read the manual and see if you like these new functions, because there are many more I simply can't fit here.  AT&T does still load your phone with CRAPplications you'll likely never use (as many of them cost a monthly fee and can be had elsewhere for free), and they also still make it impossible to Root your phone, so if you really feel the need to do that, you should consider the Unlocked version (or another carrier).  However, I'm pleased to tell you that I'm not seeing any of that junk running in the background like it used to do on my equally-unrootable Samsung Captivate.If you want a new Smartphone and you like HTC, the One X is an excellent phone.  The lack of an SD slot and reduced storage size for AT&T customers are fairly huge flaws that almost made me return it, but a change in my habits has simplified my life.NOTE:  This review is for the HTC One X sold through AT&T.  If you are seeing it cross-posted under another HTC One X, be aware that Amazon does this and we reviewers have no control over it.

I own a Galaxy S5 and have a great holster with nice thick rubber padding and rear stand already. I bought a bunch of those for my gf, whose family also has S5's. Unfortunately, the belt clip on the holster one of them broke, so I needed a replacement. Instead of having all the eggs in one basket, I thought I would give this holster a try. The funny thing is, this looks very similar to the holster I had when I was using an S3. But when it arrived, I realized there were some good and bad features to this version.First, the good. It snaps in easily and tightly. Once in, the phone feels stable. The buttons are easy to press and all the openings are in the right place. The belt holster is secure and swivels, just like the one that broke. The rear stand is a plus, allowing you to rest the phone on the table as you watch videos. It does everything that it advertised itself to do.Now, the bad. I had assumed that the outside was slightly rubberized because my previous case for the S3 looked exactly the same. In fact, the pictures on this page gives it a sense like it might be rubberized. But it's not. There is no rubber anywhere on this case. It's hard plastic, right against your phone. There is a bit of felt on the holster to prevent your phone screen from getting scuffed, but that's it. That means this is not a great case for drop protection. That hard plastic transfer a lot of the impact right into your phone. It'll still be good for protect it from scratches, but don't expect much shock absorption. Now, the description actually said hard plastic, so I should have read it more carefully.Another negative is the tightness of the case. It's really tight. That's great so that your phone doesn't slip out of the case for any reason, but not great if you need to take out the phone. For example, to change batteries if you own a spare. I pried and pried to the point I was afraid the case itself would scratch up my phone. Eventually, it came out just fine.The holster works well. The belt clip is pretty much the same as the one that broke on the earlier holster. For most of the last decade there has been a huge outstanding question about cellphone radiation. Some studies say yes, while others say no. There is no doubt that radiation is emitted, but is that radiation unsafe? I'm not sure. But I've been telling my family for years to use headphones and not to put the phone up to their heads. You know, just in case. So if this case reduces radiation then that's a good thing regardless of whether or not is actually dangerous. Unfortunately I have no way to actually test that. So I'll assume the answer is yes, it does reduce exposure. That's the only reason to give this case any stars compared to other cases.Functionally, this case is not well designed. Compared to tried and true dial layer cases like Otterbox or LifeProof or single layer cases like UAG this cases is clunky in the extreme. The rubber inside fits but isn't tight. The hard plastic back connects on the top and each side but only via very small (less than one inch wide) plastic hooks. The bottom had nothing holding the hard case to the phone. The small hooks are so ridged and so small they are hard to get on the phone. (I'll post a picture of how they never really line up correctly.) What we are left with is a phone with very little coverage by the outside case, and a rubber case that's not that great in my opinion.Looks wise, this is close to ugly. With wood print is awkward on a mobile device. It's not a pretty wood either. The clear plastic on black rubber is also not pretty.I'll be clear: if this case didn't claim to reduce radiation exposure it would be a one star case because of the coverage and looks. I can't imagine this won't be updated in future versions of this case.I want this case to be successful. If radiation turns out to be dangerous then we will all have cases like this in the future.  But they will undoubtedly look better though and they will do a better job of covering.

We have a several weather-proof power bank batteries, and this is one that really seems to be done right. It is ruggedly stylish but has a functional design, with a rubberized covering that is easy to grip (even when damp) and rubber closures for the input and output ports.This power bank's 5000mAh capacity provides the juice needed for most situations, while its size (5 inches long x 3 inches wide and just 1/2 inch thick, weighing only 5 ounces) is perfect for stowing in your pack. About two hours later, the unit was fully charged by our 2A wall charger and we put it to immediate use.Operating this battery is simple -- open either rubber cover (one on each side) and just use your cable to connect the battery to your device, and let it work. This battery does come with one short micro-USB cable, so you have to use your own lightning cable if trying to charge an Apple device. It is just the right size to have around for emergencies, on day trips and even as part of our back-up gear when fishing (give the water-resistant covering), hiking and camping.Solar charging capability, affordable, rugged, water-resistant, portable, and did the job as described by the manufacturer. If you are looking for a battery pack of this size, functionality, rugged look and feel, and good capacity, for use as I have described above, then I highly recommend giving this power bank a try based on our experience with it.Recommended.Go for it.We received this item gratis from a seller, without obligation, and decided to objectively test and review it. I actually rather like this case... probably should have given it five stars instead of four, 'cause it really does (almost) deserve them.But I gave it four stars (and, again, maybe I'm going too harsh becuase execpt for what I'm about to describe, it's a darned nice case) because some of the cut-out holes in the TPU (for things like the phone's mic holes, its speaker, etc.) are not quite perfectly lined-up.I've had other high-quality TPU cases for this phone, and all manufacturers, to date, have managed to by-golly get the phone's holes perfectly lined-up with the TPU case's holes.  And while this TPU case pretty much accomplishes that, too, they're not perfect......and, in fact, the lower (and so most important, because it's the one used during phone calls) mic hole cut-out in the TPU case is so not-centered that the phone's mic hole is right on (and I mean RIGHT on) the edge of the TPU... such that if the TPU somehow stretched not even a millimeter, it would partially (or maybe even completely) cover the phone's lower mic hole.  I'm sorry, but that's just cutting it too close, in my opinion.The edge of the TPU case's plastic doesn't quite come as close to the top mic hole, but even it is far from perfectly centered, as I've had with so many other TPU cases.The speaker's not really centered in its TPU case cut-out hole, either, but it's nowhere near close to being covered.  The other TPU case cut-out holes are pretty much okay......except I'm worried that the edge of the TPU case's plastic might (and I stess that word, because so far it hasn't, with the headset plug that I have) impede a slightly wider plastic headset plug such that the contacts of the plug might not fully push down into the jack, and make good contact (or the friction of it all causes the plug to maybe pop-out or something).But -- and this is important -- the alignment of the TPU case's cut-outs are still okay... not blocking anything, and so not dysfunctional.  But they're just not perfect.  Other cases I've had, I'm sorry, were perfect.  What can I say.It's a darned nice-looking TPU case, though... interesting "S"-shaped pattern on the back.  And I really like the grippy sides... very, very nice.I also like -- and, seriously, I've learned that this is really important -- that the power and volume hardware buttons are covered.  I never used to like that, but then one day, after only four months of use of my Infuse 4G, I had the occasion to partially disassemble it, and as long as I was in there, I decided to clean things up a little.  I could not believe, after only four months of use, the caked-up combination of body oil and hair and dust -- the kind you find when you clean a computer mouse -- all around the mechanicals of the hardware buttons inside the phone's case (not the TPU case, but the case of the phone, itself, which I had partially disassembed).  Ugh!It taught me, right then and there, that no self-respecting TPU case should ever fail to cover the hardware buttons... in the case of the Infuse, the power and volume buttons (it's other buttons are all capacitive, on the glass).  I had a BODYGLOVE MIRAGE TPU case on the phone until I started using this one.  And if you don't know about the MIRAGE, trust me, it's one heck of a nice case... firmer and thinner TPU than any I've seen; easily one of the best (if not the best) TPU cases made (though they're no longer made, and fast selling-out, so if you want one, and can find one still in stock somewhere, get it while you can!).  But the MIRAGE doesn't cover the hardware volume and power buttons, which I used to think was best.  But then I saw the resulting crap caked-up around the mechanicals of the hardware buttons inside the phone, and it turned me around, but quick!Yes, covered (by TPU) hardware buttons takes some getting used to.  But after seeing what kind of crap gets in there......and after realizing that that, and probably not an actual failure of the button, is why some people complain, after a while, that their power button, or their volume buttons, no longer work, I decided to get used to it.  Failed power buttons is why there are "Screen Off" or "Screen Off & Lock" apps out there, which allow a person to lock the screen by pushing an icon on the glass rather than pressing the hardware power button... so that said hardware power button gets used just that much less over the life of the phone.  And while wear-and-tear of that button is, indeed, a concern......I now realize, after being inside the phone and seeing all the crap caked-up around the mechanicals of those hardware buttons, that that may actually be why people think they fail.  (Not that wear-and-tear doesn't make them fail, too... but I'm just sayin' that I'll bet some failures are not wear-and-tear but are, instead, just really dirty buttons inside the phone).And so, even though I'm not terribly fond of it, I've ordered this (and actually, another, too) case which covers the hardware power and volume buttons......and I'm bygod gonna' get used to using them; and I'm bygod never gonna' own another TPU case which doesn't cover them (so... anyone wanna' buy a slightly used MIRAGE case?).  I guarantee that when my phone's hardware buttons are still working when those of others' I know have stopped, I'll be glad I got a TPU case which covers the hardware buttons.Plus, what the heck:  Covered hardware buttons are also impervious to spills of liquid on them and stuff.  So it's all good, really.  Just takes a little getting used to.So, bottom line:  This is a good case... great, even.  Everything fits-up nicely, and all the cut-out holes are functional, but not everything is precisely centered in them.  And it's so close in the case of the mic holes that it has me worried about whether the TPU might one day stretch enough to cover them.  Plus the headset cut-out hole, I think, could have been a couple millimeters larger, all 'round.  But the cut-outs are still, generally, fine.So, this is a good case.  Definitely don't hesitate to buy it.  Nice looking... quite nice, actually.  Firm (good quality TPU; though thicker than the MIRAGE's TPU).  Shiny where it's supposed to be, and matte finish where it's supposed to be.  EXCELLENT GRIPPY THINGIES ON THE SIDES... love those.All-in-all, a good case.  Glad I got it.Hope that helps._________________________________Gregg L. DesElmsNapa, California USAgregg at greggdeselms dot com

I've used the Defender case before on an iPhone 4s (still on it after several years) and love the protection they provide for the delicate devices. This case works as well as the former one, and I was able to easily remove the built-in screen protector and use this case with a Tech Armor ballistic glass screen shield instead.What I like about this case:+ Best bounce protection I've found in ANY case for iPhone.+ Adds a rubberized texture to the case that makes it easier to handle.+ Apple logo is still visible on the back of the case, yet it's protected from scratches.+ Cameras (front and back) are not obstructed by the case.+ With or without the built-in screen protection, you can still use the fingerprint ID function.+ You can use a ballistic glass screen protector instead of the built-in protection without any fit-issues.+ Dual layer protection is fairly easy to install and keeps dirt out with everyday use.+ Speakers are not covered.+ Holster is a nice accessory and the phone is easy to put in and out.What I'm not crazy about, but can't imagine how I'd change the case to fix it:- It's bulky; you lose the cool, slim lines of the iPhone under all that protection.- Dual layer of protection drops the iPhone's reception. For example without any case I can have 3 bars at my house, but if I put this case on the phone; I have 1.- Port covers are sometimes hard to dislodge and with use become floppy.- Power button becomes a bit harder to depress.- Provides some protection against weather (light rain), but not waterproof.Overall, I think it's one of the best options for users who want the maximum amount of protection against everyday drops and who hope to keep their device in the best possible condition for the longest amount of time. It's not perfect however, as it can negatively affect the iPhone's reception ability.

Budget phones are often forgotten by the more famous case makers, so it is nice to see the Stylo 2 get some attention from Incipio. This is a practical dual layer case. Installation is easy, you first install the soft silicon core, and on top of that, you click into place the rigid outer layer. They align perfectly and the different color helps figure it out easily. To remove it, you gently pull/peel at the edges of rigid layer and then remove the silicon.You can keep the silicon layer on only, it works fine as it is, but you don't have the extra protection of the rigid layer on the back and the left and side edges of the phone. The rigid outer shell feels solid in the hand, does not make the phone feel bigger, and has a nice hold. The material is smooth, so if you are using it with wet or slippery hands and such, there are no ridges or grooves. I haven't dropped it or fumbled it.The various ports, camera, pen, speaker, micro USB, etc are all visible and usable through the case. One potential issue for some: if you have a headphone with an old stubby plug, it may not go all the way in the jack, so you need to get an extension or use a different headphone. I haven't had any issue with the ones I tried, but there are thousands of headphones out there.

OtterBox's reputation for producing "bomb-proof" smartphone cases is present in this, their latest iteration. It's a two-part product: the shell, which encases the phone, and a plastic plate that attaches on to the case and features a large belt clip. Together, it's a burly, hulking box, the phone case equivalent of a monster truck. Is your phone safe? Much more so than in a delicate, streamlined skin. But at the expense of space - space in your purse, space in your backpack, space in your pocket, even space on your belly when worn on a belt. I give it four stars because it keeps your phone much safer. But isn't there a happy medium?

Let's get to the nitty gritty:Pros:1- It's BIG. Big for gaming, big for videos, big for web browsing. Simply put, if you want a phone with the most real estate on the screen size, then there is no alternative. Don't be so mesmerized on the 1080p screens that will come out soon. Unless you want to destroy your eyes and use the phone from an inch away, you will not notice the difference in everyday use.2- It's fast. Yes, the Qualcomm S4 Pro quad-core beats this Tegra 3/Exynos 4 CPU in most benchmarks, but again, unless you are a hard-core geek that is OCD on numbers, you will not notice any difference in everyday use. There is no discernible lag anywhere (it's probably just your slow Internet connection).3- It's beautiful. Not HTC or Sony beautiful, but this is one sleek and stylish phone. The white is classic, but the Titanium Gray is also super slick. Just check out the back cover and you will see what I mean (yes, even with that cheap-feeling plastic material).4- Did I mention the screen? It's BIG, it's bright, and it's beee-ay-eeww-ti-ful! AMOLED screens have awesome color saturation and contrast (most times even overkill, actually). Some people say it's not good in daylight. Um, have you tried cranking up the brightness??5- The Samsung/Android touch. Not everyone may like Samsung's Touchwiz user interface compiled onto Android, but I dare you to find a phone with more features than this one (and similarly the Galaxy S3). Here are the main highlights:a. Samsung utilizes the front facing camera almost to perfection. You can use it to control your music player; it has facial recognition to sense you are directly in front of the screen so it won't dim and turn off the screen (for reading, web browing, video playback, etc.); it recognizes your facial orientation so that when you are lying down sideways looking at the screen, the accelerometer will not kick in to auto-rotate the screen in the wrong orientation. That is something that has always bothered me with other phones and FINALLY someone has added that feature! Granted, not all these features work flawlessly, but it's a great start.b. Integration with Google is finally starting to show off in good ways. It was always once that Apple had the superior ecosystem. But now we see the future is bright indeed. Not just evolution, but finally innovation!c. The S Pen and all the neat things you can do with it. Preview a point in the movie before skipping to that point, and other things like going old-school back to the PDA days!6- It accepts up to a 64GB microSD card. That turned out to be the dealbreaker for me. Explanation in "cons" section below.Cons:1- It's BIG. With my tiny hands(hey hey), I can hardly do anything one-handed with this phone. Pocketability rating: -2. Putting it up on your face to make a phone call is also a big fail if you're sensitive to public ridicule like me. Have your Bluetooth earpiece ready.2- It's quite heavy. I really wanted to switch to the Verizon Droid DNA - for a 5-inch screen, it was remarkably svelte and light. And if it wasn't for the fact of pi$$-poor Verizon customer service at their call centers, and that it only had 16GB of memory with no microSD card option, I would have made the switch. The Droid DNA is superior in every way except those things mentioned. I have absolutely no doubt that the Samsung Note 3 (and maybe even the Galaxy S4) will be a 1080p screen with the Exynos 5 quadcore - can't wait!3- The screen may be bright and colorful, but it's way over-saturated and unnatural. Fortunately, you can go to the screen options and set it to "Natural." "Dynamic" is overkill, and even so is "Standard."Conclusion:Even with its inherent flaws, I can't help but love it. It's not even a phone, it's a cellular tablet(cablet, anyone??). I'm going to keep it. Until the next big thing is already here....

The Native Union Play Video Memo Pad is designed to take the place of Dry Erase Boards in your house and eliminate the use of post-it-notes stuck as reminders on your refrigerator. It features a 2.4" color screen and lets you tape messages up to three minutes long. You can leave more than one message - up to four hours of messages. The memo pad has a camera but you don't need a computer to use it (it runs on 3 AAA batteries). It has a built in microphone and speaker and a light flashes when there are new messages (messages are recorded with the time and date). It has a magnetized back so you can stick it on your refrigerator or other metallic surface; it also comes with a magnetic wall-tape pad so you can mount it on non-magnetic surfaces if you want to.What I like about The Native Union Play Video Memo Pad:It is very easy to set up and use. The not needing to use a computer to set it up is a huge plus - you just put in the batteries and you're good to go. It is small so it doesn't take up a lot of room on the refrigerator - having it magnetized is a big help (you can also slip it into your pocketbook or pocket if you need to use it while you are out). I use it the most when rearranging my freezer to see what is in it. When I did so in the past, I made a list of whatever was in the freezer so I knew what I had; I also made a list of what I needed to buy. Now I use the Play to do both. This is so much more convenient since I don`t have to continually stop what I am doing to write down what I have or need. I also use this when going through the stuff I have stored in my basement. This is especially handy since I don't always have a place to put paper and pen (and my basement can be a bit too dirty to put down a piece of paper) and it makes taking inventory so much easier.What I don't like about The Native Union Play Video Memo Pad:It doesn't work as well as it could. While there are only three buttons (play, record, and delete), you have to press those buttons multiple times to get the device to do something. You have to press the Play button each time you want to hear a message - if there are multiple messages you have to hit the play button to listen to each message (you can't play them in a continuous string), and the machine doesn't tell you how many messages there are to be played back. It can be a bit confusing when you go to record a message, you have to push the record button several times. I could do without the camera - it doesn't take very flattering pictures. The sound is very tinny - family members have had to ask me in person what I said on the recording! And it is loud (I don't see an option to adjust the sound) - I can't imagine taking the device with me to go grocery shopping (unless I want to annoy everyone around me!).Bottom-line:The Native Union Play Video Memo Pad is a cute little gadget but has a few too many flaws for my liking.

I have the 128GB version of the iPhone 6 Plus, and need to protect it well since I can't splurge again. I upgraded from the iPhone 5, which I alternated between a Lifeproof and a Tech21 case. More on that later.I originally purchased a Tech21 for the 6 Plus. It looked cool (clear with orange border) and didn't add bulk to the huge 6 Plus. Bonus that it matched the iPhone 5 with same case that was handed down to my son :) I wasn't thrilled about sticking anything to my screen, so I never applied a screen protector film.I started looking for a tough case, and immediately went to Lifeproof which doesn't yet have one for the 6 Plus. Perhaps it was a blessing in disguise, because I was never absolutely thrilled with it on my 5; clients always complained that they couldn't hear me if I wasn't using a Bluetooth earpiece. The case muffled my voice, no matter how many times I adjusted it per the instructions of Lifeproof customer support.Since I couldn't find the case that I wanted, I started considering alternatives like a wallet / folio case. The concept was great; carry a few cards, maybe a couple of dollars and protect my screen when closed with the flap. I bought it. Epic failure. Epic. Avoid the Labato case; pretty, but ineffective protection. After a few weeks, my phone dropped from 3 feet with the folio case closed. Corner hit the wood floor. My screen wasn't cracked but it stoped working, and it cost $150 to fix the screen. I started looking for a rugged case that would protect the screen.I put the repaired phone back into the Tech21 and tried to feel better by reading the positive review of it, but I remained concerned about the exposed screen and lack of rigidity.After researching (too much) and watching (too many) YouTube drop test / review videos, I narrowed the choice to the Griffin All Terrain. It would not be waterproof like the Lifeproof, but it would protect my investment, including the screen. I had it in my cart for purchase, but was too concerned about the extra bulk and bad reviews about the screen (some complaints reported about both the Griffin and Otter that the gap between screen and case caused problems). I did a little more research and narrowed my choices after visiting mobilereviews-eh.ca then decided upon this Urban Armor. When I ordered it, I didn't fully understand that it would come with screen protection to stick to the screen, but thought it would somehow be held in place by the case. After opening the package and cleaning the screen with the included 'napkin', I carefully applied the screen protector. I was pleasantly surprised that it could be easily applied without smudges and perfectly aligned. In hindsight, my refusal to stick protection to my screen was silly.After applying the screen protector to my satisfaction (ie with a healthy dose of OCD), I placed the phone in the case. Easy installation. The edges of the case were just flexible enough to let the phone go in, but not slip out. The buttons and cut outs are aligned and function perfectly. The case is rigid and seemingly has zero flex, specifically with the phone installed. The rubber / plastic inserts along the edges of the case are ribbed and prevent slipping. The four corner guards have raised 'dots' on the back, keeping the case just above the surface (water on a counter, etc). The face has a slightly raised edge that doesn't seem any higher than my Tech21, but it is slightly less rounded, feels more rigid and seems to leave slightly more space for your finger to touch the edge of the screen. There is a very slight gap between the edge of the case and end / perimeter of the screen protector, but it doesn't impact performance and the screen is fully functional. The screen protector leaves the home button / sensor completely exposed, so it works perfectly. The volume buttons have a satisfying 'click' to them. The bottom cutouts are better than precise, as they leave a little extra room around the earphone jack, charger port and speaker. The indentations on the back look cool, but are also functional; they act as finger grooves to improve grip.Another user wrote a review and commented that s/he had installed a Velcro strap. I like the idea and intend to do the same. When I do, I will update this review and maybe upload a picture.[UPDATE 1] I gave up looking for the 0.5 inch wide hook & loop (ie cinch) Velcro strap that someone else posted. Instead, I am going to try a DIY approach. I intend to sew a loop (through the two holes on the back of the case) for my hand to squeeze through, keeping the sewing hidden behind the case. Hopefully, the strap will remain taut against the case so it won't add noticeable bulk to the back. If it stretches out with time, I can make another strap with what's left on the spool. 1cm Width White Black Nylon Coated Rubber Garment Stretch Bands 20M 65.6Ft for $9.25. As a guy, I may have more fun with my Dremel or chainsaw, but for this project, I'm going needle/thread.

This car mount doesn't seem particularly sturdy and comes with insufficient instructions.  There's a square plastic piece with a bunch of screws.  It appears to be some kind of mount but I have no idea what it does for sure since this piece is pictured nowhere on the instructions.  Another assembly issue--there are four plastic pegs that slide into the edges of the main phone mount, presumably to secure the legs that hold the phone in place, although it seems highly unlikely that they will actually do this.  It's more likely they will simply pop out if any pressure is supplied to them from the legs.  Three of the pegs slid right in with no problems or resistance (which is why I don't see how they could stop the legs from sliding right off the edges of the phone mount).  The fourth peg absolutely would not go in no matter how hard I tried.  It took me 45 minutes to get this one piece inserted and to do it I had to line it up perfectly and then apply a great deal of even pressure along the entire surface of the peg.  To me this represents poor quality control because a) it shouldn't be so hard to insert 1 of the 4 pegs and b) once the pegs are actually inserted they should snap into place so they can't slide right back out, which is undoubtedly what will happen should the legs get too close to the edge of the phone cradle.Then there's the sliding cradle that actually holds your phone.  The spring action feels very flimsy and like something that will not last long term.  Either much stronger springs or wheels that allow you to adjust and lock the phone grip instead of the included flimsy springs would have been a welcome improvement.  If this car mount actually lasts for a full year I would be genuinely shocked.The suction cup also seems to be weaker than the ones included with any other window car mount that I have ever previously purchased.  It took me several tries to get it to stick to my car windshield at all (and that was after following the instructions and cleaning the glass with the included alcohol wipe).  I'm concerned that the suction cup will not do a good job of actually holding the weight of my phone and that it will come tumbling down off the glass way more often than it did with my previous mount.  Is the company that makes this product going to pay to fix my phone if it gets damaged because of their shoddy materials and design?The most bizarre thing about this product is that if you exclude the suction cup and flimsy spring loading in the phone cradle the rest of this car mount actually seems to be well designed and made with sturdy materials.  There are several points of adjustment to control the phone angle, arm angle, and arm length.  The plastic used for the base and telescoping arm seems to be very solid and of good quality.  The design to let the arm rest against the dash of the car to relieve weight from the end of the phone mount is smart--although including rubber legs or rounded rubber where the telescoping arm makes contact with the dash surface to reduce car movement impact on the device screen and windshield mount itself would have been a welcome inclusion.  I might go to the hardware store and see if I can't find something to rig up myself since this seems like it would greatly improve the usability of the product.This car mount has some good points--the phone cradle will likely accommodate a very wide range of phone sizes without interfering with any buttons or jacks.  The telescoping arm makes the product suitable for both smaller vehicles and SUV's and trucks.  Unfortunately, the manufacturer seems to have skimped on quality in a couple of important areas which makes this car mount hard to recommend for the price.  I would be willing to bet the phone cradle doesn't last a year before the springs give out, which makes this far from the premium product that the product name claims.

Shameful that a majority of the reviews are "exchanged" for discounted products because those reviews completely missed the mark.Pros:Cheap and available for primeCons:Firstly, the screen protector is falsely advertised. The main product photo leads the buyer to believe there is a "strip" of screen protector that sits between the camera and the earpiece. Completely misrepresented, the strip isn't there, it's just one square cut out at the top. There's hardly any protection at all around the sensors, ear piece and camera.Next, although some shields do not offer full coverage and end just before the edge of the screen, (allows room for a full coverage case to "grab" around the front) this protector is so awkwardly sized that it hardly fits right at all. It will not sit perfectly flush with both verticle edges, one side always seems more off than the other which sets off a huge red flag for anyone with OCD. Plus, I can tell it doesn't reach the edge of the LCD display because when I full-screen a photo, I can see a thin verticle strip a few pixels wide on both sides of the display that look slightly distorted from the curvature of the edge of the screen protector.This caused a huge issue when the phone fell and it just so happened that the sharp object it hit, pierced right between the edge of the protector and my full coverage case, damaging the actual phone glass. Every other screen protector I've used has always sat flush with the edge of my phone case after it "grips around" the front.Lastly, the manufacturing of this product is so cheap, you can tell immediately after taking it out of the package that it won't last long. It lacks any real thickness to offer strength and stability. Thinner isn't always better. Don't be surprised if you notice thin spiderweb cracking even for a small bump to the screen...After reaching out to their customer service with my comments via Amazon message, I was sent a very unprofessional response lacking any proper grammar, punctuation, and even capitalization. Basically a horrible Chinese to English translation. They actually demanded I provide proof of my purchase by responding with the order number despite the fact that this information is already provided with a verified customer contact.When they finally got back to me, I was told that it was not possible for them to send a replacement as they only ship from China and thus if I wanted a replacement from the authorized warehouse, it would have to be processed through Amazon returns... Conveniently I received this information just 1 day after the return window closed.I'd much rather pay up double what this cost for a new one, from a different manufacturer, than buy from this company ever again.All in all:Would not recommend.Folks, you get what you pay for. I was just looking for a great deal and wound up with a headache.All of this hassle was not worth the 4 bucks I paid for the shield.Spend an extra $4 and get a more quality protector. You and your phone will both be thankful that you did.

switched to this Caseology Vault Series case after using a Spigen Neo Hybrid Carbon case for almost a month. Here is a comparison between the two:Pros:- The Caseology has a matte finish. The Spigen has a glossier skin, until it gets scuff marks from normal use, then it gets a few dull spots which can be distracting.- The Micro USB opening is larger on the Caseology than on the Spigen. About half of my charging cables won't fit the Spigen (the plug is blocked by the case or catches the lip of the case during plug-in). I still have a few old cables that won't fit the Caseology, but most do fit.Neutral:- The Caseology has two layers on the back (a softer layer inside and a hard plastic outside), while the Spigen has only one layer, with a bumper holding it around the edges. I don't see much of a thickness difference, and I think protection is about the same, good in both cases.- Sharp edges: Some reviewers mentioned that the Caseology has sharp edges. My case does have what I consider to be "raised edges" but they are not really sharp and they are not where you would normally touch them. But if that is important to you, go with the Spigen, which is definitely smooth all around.- Stiff buttons: The power and volume button covers are harder to push on the Caseology than on the Spigen, but that's a matter of preference. I tend to accidentally adjust the volume with the Spigen case, so I prefer the stiffer buttons on the Caseology.Overall, both cases are nice and have good fit and finish, but I prefer the Caseology because of the matte skin and the fact that I can use more of my charging cables

I am impressed with this phone. It is attractively designed and looks like an iPhone 6S Plus. The case is made of plastic and not as solid as an iPhone 6S Plus. It does have a nice display with good resolution. The rear camera is 13 MP and the front camera is 5 MP. They both take good photos and videos. The rear camera has a built in flash. I was impressed with the quality of the photos and of the videos that the phone would take. The battery is removable and replaceable by the user. The battery is a part number B01-E550.The phone uses an Android operating system version 4.4.2 which is KitKat. I asked the system to check for a system update and it advised me that there was no operating system update. This Android system is a bit older but it still works and allows good functionality of the phone. There was a new version update of the phones firmware which I installed. Installing the new update took about10 to 15 minutes as it also updated the apps and the SD card settings. Make sure that your phone is charged to 30% or more, better yet leave it plugged in and charging while updating.The unit can use a memory card with a capacity of up to 32 GIG. You can set the memory card to be the default memory storage for apps and other items. The phone comes with 8 GIG of built in ROM memory and while that is a reasonable amount I strongly recommend that you get and use a 32 GIG memory card. Fresh out of the box the phone had 2.25 GB of Operating System Storage and 5.75GB of free space.I set the phone to accept apps from unknown sources and I downloaded apps from Amazon and from the Google play store. I installed the following apps for my testing: Kindle reading app for Android Amazon Video App Amazon Music ANTUTU benchmarking app WIFI Analyzer App Amazon Underground AppAll of the apps worked well and the phone played videos very well. The ANTUTU benchmarking software tested the unit at 23290 which is OK but not top of the line performance. This is an 8 core CPU. That benchmarking score compared to an iPhone 6S Plus is 23290 for the Posh to 139999 for the iPhone 6S Plus. Please remember that the iPhone 6S Plus when released cost 9 times more than this POSH phone. The results are obvious that the iPhone 6S Plus is a better phone but for the money this is an OK Phablet sized phone.This POSH Phone has the following features: Nice display with 720 by 1280 resolution with a 5.5-inch diagonal screen. It has a good picture and video even when you are looking at the screen at an angle regardless of whether you tilt the camera in the vertical or horizontal movement. Good built in speaker quality 8 core CPU with up to 1.4 GHz clocking Good cameras for photos and videos. The rear camera is 13 MP quality and the front camera is a 5 MP quality. Both the front and rear cameras have flashes. 2400 mAh battery that has OK battery life. In phone use it lasted all day and the next day after 36 hours of being on it was still at over 30% charged. That was just making some calls, texting and emails. Watching videos, taking photos or doing a lot of web browsing will use up your battery much faster but I have that issue with every phone I have owned. Make sure that you have the screen brightness set on auto to conserve battery resources. If you are not using Bluetooth, I would also turn it off to extend the battery life. I was surprised that on standby the phone was dead after 72 hours. It states the phone battery would work for 240 hours on standby. Good solid phone quality and test quality for testing using a Simple Mobile SIM chip. (T-Mobile) Easy to set up with the Android system and it automatically connected to my Google account and imported my emails, contacts and other data. Bluetooth capable Text messaging was goodThe GPS works fine on the phone using Google maps.Whats in the box? The phone itself and it is 3.1 inches wide and 5.9 inches long and 0.3 inches thick. It weighs in at 4.94 ounces. A charging cable that is 45 inches long. One end is a USB and the other end is a Micro USB that you plug into the phone. A POSH User Guide manual. I found the manual to be very hard to read and the type in it is very small and the diagrams are also small. A simplistic case to put the phone in. It is a soft gel style of case. A front screen protector sheet that is clear plastic. An AC wall charger the operates on 100 to 240 VAC and outputs 5 VDC at 1 amp. A set of white stereo earphones that are a round cord design. The earphones have a built in microphone in them and a mute button on the small block where the headphones split and the block has a clip on it for your clothes. The headphones, when plugged in, also act as the antenna for the FM radio app. The headphones are 46 inches long. The phones battery which is a 2400 mAh battery and it is replaceable. The battery life could have been better and for that reason I deducted a star from the rating.Cons:+ It has a complete manual but the text is so small that it is difficult to read+ The battery life was not as good as I expected.Overall this is a good phone for the money and it has a lot of features and it comes with a lot of accessories. I rated it at 4 stars for the money as it performs well. It would have been 5 stars if the battery life was better. I just tested a similar phone and it had a 3000 mAh battery in it and this phone could use one of that size also.I was supplied a sample for test and evaluation. A review was not promised to the supplier in exchange for the sample but I stated that if I provided a review that it would be fair and honest.

This case by Haissky did deliver, and is water tight.  I submerged it with paper towels in a bowl for a while just to ensure there were no holes in it.  It did hold water. I could see the clip breaking off if you scraped a rock or something.  However, I really didn't buy this for submerging, that's more of an oops.  I just want it protected at the beach, in the rain, etc.  Also have realized that these cases are great when they're at risk of just getting dirty (fishing, cooking, gardening).  You can operate it with muddy hands, or fish guts, and not worry that your phone will stink or get clogged up. This is just a basic two USB port wall charger.It is compact and portable. The prongs that go into the outlet fold up when it's not in use, so it's easy to throw into a bag and transport it from, say, the home to the office.It has two outlets, both at 5V, but one puts out 1 Amp (just fine for charging most phones) and the other puts out 2.1 Amp (best for charging iPads and other more power hungry devices). That's just perfect because the two items I need to charge most often are my iPhone and my iPad. This gives a nice way to charge them both at once, without using up the USB ports on my computer, which I often have an external drive plugged into.One minor thing I don't like is that the "Seagle" logo lights up fairly brightly (in blue) to indicate when it's plugged in. I guess that's a good thing because you know it's getting power, but it also means I don't use this in my bedroom where I'd prefer to charge my devices, because I find lights distracting when I want to sleep. I guess I could just cover it up with tape, but I'd prefer if the "on" indicator were less obtrusive -- maybe a miniature green light.Overall, though, this is what they say it is: a compact and convenient two port USB wall charger. (Product received for the purpose of testing and review.)

I purchased my OtterBox iPhone 6 Plus Defender case directly from OtterBox (before they sold out). I bought the black case (my only choice at the time).  So far I am very satisfied.  The case of course made a large phone, even larger, but I knew that when I purchased it.  The outside dimensions are roughly 6.75" x 3.5" x .5".  The button controls work well.  The finger print scanner is now a little harder to use.  I am not sure what the other reviewers are talking about when they mention gaps with the screen protector and the case not fitting.  My case fits my phone like a glove, and there are no gaps in the case or screen protector, and the touch sensitivity is great.  This is our third OtterBox Defender case.  Our first was for my iPhone 4s, and second for my wife's iPhone 5.  These cases have protected our phones for years.  My iPhone 4s still looked like new when I removed it from the case.  We are hard on phones, the Defender cases have saved our phones from numerous drops, without a dent or cracked screen (even the cases still looked good).  OtterBox has good customer service and they stand by their product.  After I had owned my 4s case for around 3+ years, the outside cover was getting a little loose, so I called to order a new outside cover.  The OtterBox representative said that they had switched to a different cover material, and that they would replace my case at no charge, all I had to do was send them a photo of the original case.  They sent me a whole new case.  The new cover material, which is what they are using now, was a real improvement.  My only real gripe with this case, is trying to get one.  My wife's new iPhone 6+ should be here in the first week of November, and at this point, I cannot purchase a Defender case for her phone.  She usually gets a different color case, to avoid our grabbing the wrong phone.  At this time OtterBox cannot give me a date that more black cases, or the color cases will be available.

The following is the review for items New Trent (2PCS) Combo-Pack: Dual High-Speed USB Port Car Chargers w/ Free Full Speed Micro-USB Charging Cables - 1pc 15W and 1pc 10W (NT89T)I tested these items on a 4 day business trip to New Mexico earlier this week.15W Dual USB Car Charger New Trent Model NT88CThe charger worked perfectly and especially came in handy because my rental car only had 1 power port (cigarette plug) in the vehicle.The design is functional and practical in regards to the USB ports being on the end of the charger so I didn't have to worry about the USB plugs hitting the sides of the console in the rental car.Some key features of this charger, it has both a 1Aand 2.1A usb ports for charging. I can charge a phone and tablet at the same time. The charging speed of the 2.1A was equal to my stock charger for my Galaxy S4 and Galaxy Note 8, both were charged during my trip. I also used the 1A charger to recharge my Jawbone headset and an Olympus point and shoot camera.The built in LED's are easy to see during daytime use so I know it's plugged in properly.The ports are clearly labeled to show 2.1A and 1ACame with included charging cable.The box was easy to read and clearly shows the specs of each port on the back.It's a great charger, especially for the price. Why pay high prices for an OEM charger when you can pick one of these up for a fraction of the cost.New Trent 1A/2A Dual USB charger model number NT80CIt's a decent charger for the money and the diamond shaped end of the unit makes it easy to insert or remove from the power port in the car.I charged my Galaxy S4 and Galaxy Note 8 with this charger also during my trip. I was able to charge 2 smart phones at the same time (mine and an employees) but it won't allow you to charge a smart phone and tablet at the same time. This is not a complaint, I knew this prior to purchasing it, just including it for other users so they're aware of the charging limitations.While I like the shape of this charger, the built in LED's are almost impossible to see during daylight unless I cup my hand around the charger to see if they're lit which is opposite of the New Trent NT88C which is VERY easy to see during sunny days. The charger worked well but with some design changes (brighter, easier to see LED's and higher wattage to 15W) this would be an OUTSTANDING charger.It may sound picky, but It's hard to read the description on the back of the box, specifically the blue box area with white lettering.

When I review screen protectors, I take into consideration two main things:1. The best device screen protectors are made from durable material that will protect your screen from more than just day-to-day bumps and scratches. Whether the screen protector is thick or thin, it should be hard and scratchproof. It should also resist fingerprints and germs.2. The design of the device screen protector is almost as important as its durability. If it peels up, collects dirt around the edges, gets in the way of your device case or interferes with how your finger interacts with the screen, it probably won't last long on your device. The best screen protectors for models are smooth under your finger, responds to your touch and doesn't leave you feeling like your finger is floating above the screen  regardless of how thick it is. It also comes with a cleaning cloth to help you get all the fingerprints, oil and dust off your screen before you apply the device screen protector.I am happy to say that this screen protector pack has met them! I am very happy with the fit, quality, and form of it! Overall, I do recommend this screen protector if you are looking the market for a new one.Disclosure: As a fellow Amazon buyer, I have always researched items before spending hard earned money on purchases, and now I am able to test items and present my honest and unbiased reviews to assist others in making (or avoiding) purchases. I purchased this product at a discount or for free in exchange for my honest review. Thanks!
